topics.title: Introduction to Food Basics and Food Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Food Basics and Food Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Food

From a strictly scientific and regulatory perspective aligned with the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO)** and the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (FSSAI, India)**:

> **Food** is any substance, whether processed, semi-processed, or raw, which is intended for human consumption. It includes drinks, chewing gum, and any substance which has been used as an ingredient in the manufacture, preparation, or treatment of food.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Core Definition**
> "Food is a complex mixture of chemical constituents (nutrients and non-nutrients) which, when ingested, performs one or more physiological functions: provides energy, builds and repairs body tissues, and regulates metabolic processes."

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Food Science (Conceptual Triad)

Every food item can be analyzed through three lenses:

| Pillar | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemical** | Composition of macronutrients, micronutrients, water | Milk = 87% water, 3.3% protein, 4% fat |
| **Physical** | Structure, texture, color, viscosity | Bread — porous foam structure |
| **Biological / Microbiological** | Enzymes, microbes, shelf-life | Curd formation by *Lactobacillus* |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building, Fuel, and Guard" Analogy

Imagine your body as a **construction site that operates 24/7**:
- **Fuel** → Carbohydrates and fats act like the *diesel* that powers the generators (energy supply).
- **Building Materials** → Proteins act like the *bricks, cement, and steel* (tissue building and repair).
- **Security System** → Vitamins and minerals act like the *CCTV cameras and electricians* (regulatory and protective roles).
- **Water** → The *plumbing system* (medium for all biochemical reactions).

This is exactly why a "food" is not just one chemical — it is a **functional package** delivering multiple benefits simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference between "Food" and "Nutrient"**
> - **Food** is the *whole package* (e.g., an apple, a glass of milk).
> - **Nutrient** is the *individual useful chemical* inside the package (e.g., vitamin C in apple, calcium in milk).
> - Food = Vehicle ; Nutrient = Cargo.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Functions of Food (The "Five-Fold Role")

Every food we consume performs one or more of the following physiological roles. KTU examiners frequently frame 3-mark or 7-mark questions around these functions.

1. **Provision of Energy** — Fuels muscular activity, basal metabolism, and thermoregulation.
2. **Body Building / Growth** — Supplies amino acids and minerals for tissue synthesis.
3. **Repair and Maintenance** — Replaces worn-out cells (e.g., epithelial lining of gut every 3–5 days).
4. **Regulatory / Protective Role** — Vitamins and minerals act as coenzymes and cofactors, governing metabolism and immunity.
5. **Sensory Satisfaction** — Taste, aroma, texture, and colour — the "hedonic" function which governs food acceptance.

### 2.2 Classification of Foods (High-Yield KTU Topic)

Foods can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

#### (A) By Origin
| Class | Examples |
|---|---|
| Plant origin | Cereals, pulses, fruits, vegetables, nuts |
| Animal origin | Milk, meat, fish, eggs, honey |
| Microbial origin | Single-cell protein (SCP), fermented foods |

#### (B) By Predominant Nutrient (Chemical Classification)
| Class | Key Nutrient | Energy Density |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy-yielding** | Carbohydrates, Fats | 4 and 9 kcal/g |
| **Body-building** | Proteins | 4 kcal/g |
| **Protective** | Vitamins, Minerals | 0 kcal/g (generally) |
| **Roughage** | Dietary fibre | Indigestible |

#### (C) By Functional Role (ICMR Classification)
- **Cereals & millets** → Energy foods
- **Pulses, nuts, oilseeds, meat, fish** → Protein-rich / body-building foods
- **Vegetables & fruits** → Protective / vitamin-mineral foods
- **Fats & oils** → Concentrated energy
- **Sugar & jaggery** → Energy
- **Condiments & spices** → Flavour & protection

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Atwater Energy Factors)

Although this is an introductory topic, KTU frequently tests the **Atwater energy conversion factors** because they bridge "food basics" with food processing energy calculations.

$$
E_{\text{total}} \;=\; 4 \times m_{\text{CHO}} \;+\; 4 \times m_{\text{Prot}} \;+\; 9 \times m_{\text{Fat}}
$$

where:
- $E_{\text{total}}$ = total metabolizable energy (kcal)
- $m_{\text{CHO}}$ = mass of available carbohydrates (g)
- $m_{\text{Prot}}$ = mass of protein (g)
- $m_{\text{Fat}}$ = mass of fat (g)
- The constants **4, 4, 9** are the **Atwater factors** (kcal per gram).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip** — The factors **4 – 4 – 9** are universal for human nutrition. For alcohol, an additional factor **7 kcal/g** is sometimes added; for organic acids (citric, malic), **3 kcal/g** is used.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Basics

Every downstream process in food technology — pasteurization, drying, extrusion, fermentation — is ultimately applied to *preserve* or *enhance* one of these five functions. Understanding "what food does" tells the engineer **why** a particular unit operation is needed.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Derivations

### 3.1 Worked Example — Energy Calculation from Food Composition

**Problem (Typical KTU-style 3-mark question):**
A 50 g serving of a snack contains 30 g carbohydrate, 8 g protein, and 6 g fat. Calculate the total metabolizable energy supplied.

**Step 1 — Identify the masses of each macronutrient.**
Reading the problem statement directly:

$$
m_{\text{CHO}} = 30 \text{ g}, \quad m_{\text{Prot}} = 8 \text{ g}, \quad m_{\text{Fat}} = 6 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Atwater factors.**

$$
E_{\text{CHO}} = 4 \times m_{\text{CHO}} = 4 \times 30 = 120 \text{ kcal}
$$

$$
E_{\text{Prot}} = 4 \times m_{\text{Prot}} = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ kcal}
$$

$$
E_{\text{Fat}} = 9 \times m_{\text{Fat}} = 9 \times 6 = 54 \text{ kcal}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the energy contributions.**

$$
E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{CHO}} + E_{\text{Prot}} + E_{\text{Fat}}
$$

$$
E_{\text{total}} = 120 + 32 + 54 = 206 \text{ kcal}
$$

**Step 4 — Express answer with units and physical interpretation.**

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{total}} = 206 \text{ kcal per 50 g serving}}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** Despite fat being only 12% of the mass (6/50), it contributes ~26% of the energy (54/206). This numerically demonstrates **why fat is called a "concentrated energy source"** — exactly the kind of reasoning KTU examiners reward.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Linking Food → Function → Processing Need

| Food Component | Physiological Function | Typical Processing Need |
|---|---|---|
| **Carbohydrates** | Primary energy | Milling, gelatinization, hydrolysis |
| **Proteins** | Tissue building, repair | Denaturation, coagulation, texturization |
| **Fats** | Concentrated energy, fat-soluble vitamin carrier | Hydrogenation, emulsification, refining |
| **Vitamins** | Regulatory cofactors | Blanching control (to retain heat-labile vitamins) |
| **Minerals** | Skeletal & electrolyte function | Fortification, controlled boiling |
| **Water** | Universal solvent, reaction medium | Dehydration, concentration |
| **Fibre** | Gut motility, satiety | Cereal bran extraction, milling degree control |

---

### 3.3 The Nutrient Hierarchy (Step-by-Step Logic)

Understanding the hierarchy is critical for KTU descriptive answers:

1. **Step 1 — Water** is the most abundant component of most fresh foods (60–95%).
2. **Step 2 — Carbohydrates** typically dominate in plant foods (cereals 70–80%, fruits 5–20%).
3. **Step 3 — Proteins** dominate in animal foods (meat 15–22%, pulses 20–25%).
4. **Step 4 — Fats** are minor in most fresh foods but concentrated in oilseeds (20–50%) and animal adipose tissue (up to 90%).
5. **Step 5 — Vitamins and minerals** occur in trace amounts (mg or µg levels) but have **disproportionate physiological impact** — hence called "micronutrients".

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight** — This nutrient hierarchy is not arbitrary. It mirrors the body's priority: water → energy → building blocks → regulators.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Food Components

This diagram maps the relationship between food, its nutrients, and the physiological functions they perform.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[FOOD COMPLEX MIXTURE] --> nodeB[MACRONUTRIENTS]
    nodeA --> nodeC[MICRONUTRIENTS]
    nodeA --> nodeD[WATER]
    nodeA --> nodeE[NON NUTRIENTS FIBRE PIGMENTS]

    nodeB --> nodeF[CARBOHYDRATES 4 KCAL PER G]
    nodeB --> nodeG[PROTEINS 4 KCAL PER G]
    nodeB --> nodeH[FATS 9 KCAL PER G]

    nodeC --> nodeI[VITAMINS FAT SOLUBLE A D E K]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[VITAMINS WATER SOLUBLE B C]
    nodeC --> nodeK[MINERALS MACRO AND TRACE]

    nodeF --> nodeL[ENERGY SUPPLY]
    nodeG --> nodeL
    nodeH --> nodeL
    nodeG --> nodeM[BODY BUILDING AND REPAIR]
    nodeI --> nodeN[REGULATORY AND PROTECTIVE]
    nodeJ --> nodeN
    nodeK --> nodeN
    nodeD --> nodeO[HYDRATION AND SOLVENT MEDIUM]
    nodeE --> nodeP[DIGESTIVE HEALTH AND SENSORY APPEAL]

    nodeL --> nodeQ[OVERALL PHYSIOLOGICAL WELL BEING]
    nodeM --> nodeQ
    nodeN --> nodeQ
    nodeO --> nodeQ
    nodeP --> nodeQ
```

### 4.2 Classification Flowchart of Foods (By Origin)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rootA[FOOD] --> rootB[PLANT ORIGIN]
    rootA --> rootC[ANIMAL ORIGIN]
    rootA --> rootD[MICROBIAL ORIGIN]

    rootB --> rootB1[CEREALS AND MILLETS]
    rootB --> rootB2[PULSES AND LEGUMES]
    rootB --> rootB3[FRUITS]
    rootB --> rootB4[VEGETABLES]
    rootB --> rootB5[NUTS AND OILSEEDS]

    rootC --> rootC1[MILK AND DAIRY]
    rootC --> rootC2[MEAT AND POULTRY]
    rootC --> rootC3[FISH AND SEAFOOD]
    rootC --> rootC4[EGGS]

    rootD --> rootD1[SINGLE CELL PROTEIN]
    rootD --> rootD2[FERMENTED FOODS]
    rootD --> rootD3[MUSHROOMS]
```

### 4.3 The Five Functions of Food — Process Linkage Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    funcA[FUNCTION 1 ENERGY] --> procA[DRYING MILLING EXTRUSION]
    funcB[FUNCTION 2 BODY BUILDING] --> procB[DENATURATION COAGULATION TEXTURIZATION]
    funcC[FUNCTION 3 REPAIR AND MAINTENANCE] --> procB
    funcD[FUNCTION 4 REGULATORY PROTECTIVE] --> procC[BLANCHING FORTIFICATION CONTROLLED HEATING]
    funcE[FUNCTION 5 SENSORY SATISFACTION] --> procD[FERMENTATION SEASONING MAILLARD REACTION]

    procA --> outA[STABLE ENERGY FOOD]
    procB --> outB[AVAILABLE PROTEIN FOOD]
    procC --> outC[VITAMIN RETAINED FOOD]
    procD --> outD[PALATABLE ACCEPTABLE FOOD]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Define food. List the five major functions of food in the human body.*

**Model Answer:**
**Food** is any substance, processed or raw, intended for human consumption. It is a complex mixture of nutrients and non-nutrients.
The five major functions are:
1. Provision of energy
2. Body building and growth
3. Repair and maintenance of tissues
4. Regulatory and protective role (vitamins & minerals)
5. Sensory satisfaction (taste, aroma, texture)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*Differentiate between "food" and "nutrient" with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Food | Nutrient |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Whole consumable substance | Individual useful chemical component |
| Example | Milk | Calcium in milk |
| Example | Apple | Vitamin C in apple |
| Scope | Sensory + nutritional | Purely nutritional |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### ✦ QUESTION A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify foods based on (i) origin, (ii) predominant nutrient, and (iii) ICMR functional classification. Give two examples for each category.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 200 g portion of a food product contains: carbohydrate = 120 g, protein = 30 g, fat = 20 g. Calculate its total energy in kcal and in kilojoules.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**(i) By Origin:**
- Plant origin → Wheat, apple
- Animal origin → Milk, egg
- Microbial origin → Single-cell protein, mushroom

**(ii) By Predominant Nutrient:**
- Energy-yielding → Rice (carbohydrate), ghee (fat)
- Body-building → Egg (protein), soybean
- Protective → Orange (vitamin C), spinach (iron)
- Roughage → Wheat bran, leafy vegetables

**(iii) ICMR Functional Classification:**
- Cereals & millets → Rice, wheat
- Pulses & legumes → Toor dal, Bengal gram
- Vegetables & fruits → Carrot, banana
- Fats & oils → Groundnut oil, butter
- Sugar & jaggery → Cane sugar
- Condiments & spices → Turmeric, pepper

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply the Atwater energy equation.**

$$
E_{\text{total}} = 4 \times m_{\text{CHO}} + 4 \times m_{\text{Prot}} + 9 \times m_{\text{Fat}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
E_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 120 + 4 \times 30 + 9 \times 20
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate term by term.**

$$
E_{\text{total}} = 480 + 120 + 180 = 780 \text{ kcal}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert kcal to kJ.**

Using the conversion factor $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.184 \text{ kJ}$:

$$
E_{\text{total}} = 780 \times 4.184 = 3263.52 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{total}} = 780 \text{ kcal} = 3263.52 \text{ kJ}}
$$

---

#### ✦ QUESTION B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the five physiological functions of food with suitable examples. Why is food called a "complex mixture"?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the chemical composition of foods with reference to macronutrients and micronutrients. State their energy contributions.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The five physiological functions of food are:

1. **Energy supply** — Carbohydrates (rice, sugar) provide 4 kcal/g; fats (oil, ghee) provide 9 kcal/g.
2. **Body building** — Proteins (milk, egg, dal) supply amino acids for muscle, skin, and enzyme synthesis.
3. **Repair and maintenance** — Protein and minerals (calcium from milk) continuously replace worn-out cells, especially in gut lining and blood.
4. **Regulatory & protective** — Vitamin A (carrot) maintains vision; Vitamin C (citrus) boosts immunity; Iron (spinach) forms haemoglobin.
5. **Sensory satisfaction** — Spices, sugar, and aroma compounds (curcumin in turmeric, vanillin in vanilla) provide palatability.

**Why "complex mixture"?**
Food contains water, macronutrients, micronutrients, fibre, pigments, and flavour compounds **simultaneously**, all interacting physically and chemically. This complexity is why food processing requires integrated scientific knowledge (chemistry + microbiology + engineering).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Macronutrients** (needed in grams/day):

| Nutrient | Energy (kcal/g) | Major Sources | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbohydrate | **4** | Cereals, sugar | Primary energy |
| Protein | **4** | Pulses, milk, meat | Tissue building |
| Fat | **9** | Oils, ghee | Concentrated energy, vitamin carrier |

**Micronutrients** (needed in mg or µg/day):

| Nutrient | Energy (kcal/g) | Major Sources | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vitamins (A, B, C, D, E, K) | **0** | Fruits, vegetables | Coenzymes, immunity |
| Minerals (Ca, Fe, I, Zn) | **0** | Milk, greens, salt | Skeletal, electrolyte, oxygen transport |

**Key Inference:**

$$
\text{Fat contributes } \frac{9}{4} = 2.25 \text{ times more energy per gram than carbohydrate or protein.}
$$

This is why dietary fat control is central to managing caloric intake in processed food design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "energy-giving" with "body-building"** — Both protein and carbohydrate give 4 kcal/g, but protein's *primary* role is body building. Examiners deduct marks if this distinction is missed.
> 2. **Forgetting units in energy calculations** — Always write "kcal" or "kJ"; bare numbers lose marks.
> 3. **Writing only "vitamins" without classification** — Mention fat-soluble (A, D, E, K) vs water-soluble (B, C). Examiners explicitly reward this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping the conversion factor** — When asked in kJ, the factor $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.184 \text{ kJ}$ **must** be written, not just the numerical answer.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Food = complex mixture** of water, macronutrients, micronutrients, fibre, and non-nutrients.
- ✅ **Five functions**: Energy, Body Building, Repair, Regulation, Sensory Satisfaction.
- ✅ **Atwater factors**: Carbohydrate = **4 kcal/g**, Protein = **4 kcal/g**, Fat = **9 kcal/g**.
- ✅ **Energy equation**: $E = 4 m_{\text{CHO}} + 4 m_{\text{Prot}} + 9 m_{\text{Fat}}$.
- ✅ **1 kcal = 4.184 kJ** — always state this conversion explicitly.
- ✅ **Classification axes**: Origin, Predominant Nutrient, ICMR Functional.
- ✅ **Macronutrients** are needed in grams; **Micronutrients** in mg/µg.
- ✅ **Fat is the most energy-dense** nutrient (2.25× carbohydrate/protein per gram).
- ✅ **Water is the most abundant component** of most fresh foods.
- ✅ **Dietary fibre** provides bulk, aids peristalsis, and has negligible energy.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food Constituents - Moisture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Food Constituents - Moisture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Moisture" in Food?

In food processing engineering, **moisture** refers to the water present in a food material, expressed either as a mass fraction (moisture content) or as a thermodynamic availability parameter (water activity). It is the single most abundant constituent in most fresh foods and the most critical variable governing microbial safety, texture, shelf-life, and energy demand during dehydration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Moisture content is the quantity of water contained in a food material, usually expressed as a percentage of the total mass (wet basis) or dry mass (dry basis). It is distinct from water activity, which measures the energy status or availability of that water to participate in chemical, enzymatic, and microbial reactions.*

The two governing metrics are:

- **Moisture Content ($M$)** — *Quantitative measure* (How much water is there?)
- **Water Activity ($a_w$)** — *Qualitative/thermodynamic measure* (How available is that water?)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Reservoir" Model

Imagine a food material as a **multi-storied apartment building holding water**:

- **Ground Floor (Free/Bulk Water):** Water is loosely held, behaves like pure water, easily evaporates, and is freely available to microbes and enzymes. Think of water sitting in an open bucket.
- **Upper Floors (Bound Water):** Water is tightly bound to proteins, starches, and salts via hydrogen bonds and capillary forces. It does *not* freeze at 0 °C, does *not* evaporate easily, and is *not* available to microorganisms.
- **Tenant Mobility (Water Activity):** The "ease with which tenants (water molecules) can leave the building" determines $a_w$. A high $a_w$ means most water is on the ground floor — mobile and dangerous for spoilage.

So **moisture content tells you the population**, but **water activity tells you how restless they are**.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa} = \mathbf{760\ mm\ Hg}$
- **Boiling point of pure water at 1 atm:** $\mathbf{100\ ^\circ C\ (373.15\ K)}$
- **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C:** $\mathbf{2257\ kJ/kg}$
- **Gas constant for water vapour:** $R_w = \mathbf{0.4615\ kJ/(kg\cdot K)}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Moisture Matters in Food Engineering:**
> 1. **Shelf-life:** Foods with $a_w < 0.6$ are microbially stable (intermediate moisture foods).
> 2. **Energy calculations:** Drying is the most energy-intensive unit operation — accurate moisture data determines dryer sizing.
> 3. **Legal labeling:** Food standards mandate moisture declaration on a wet basis.
> 4. **Texture & Mouthfeel:** Crispness, juiciness, and hardness are all moisture-driven.
> 5. **Storage stability:** Moisture migration causes caking, stickiness, and rancidity.

## 1.4 Classification of Water in Foods

| Type of Water | Binding Energy | Behaviour | Microbial Availability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free / Bulk Water** | Very low | Behaves like pure water, freezes at 0 °C, evaporates easily | Fully available |
| **Adsorbed (Multilayer) Water** | Moderate | Bound to hydrophilic surfaces (2–3 layers) | Partially available |
| **Bound (Monolayer) Water** | High | Strongly hydrogen-bonded; does not freeze at 0 °C; non-solvent | Unavailable |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Moisture Content — Two Reference Bases

The same physical sample yields **two different numerical values** depending on the chosen basis. KTU questions frequently test this conversion.

### (a) Moisture Content on Wet Basis ($M_{w.b.}$)

$$M_{w.b.} = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100 = \frac{m_w}{m_{total}} \times 100$$

where:
- $m_w$ = mass of water (kg)
- $m_d$ = mass of dry solid (kg)
- $m_{total}$ = total mass of food (kg)

> **Range:** Always between 0 % and 100 % (since numerator ≤ denominator).

### (b) Moisture Content on Dry Basis ($M_{d.b.}$)

$$M_{d.b.} = \frac{m_w}{m_d} \times 100$$

> **Range:** Can exceed 100 % (e.g., fresh fruits, milk).

### (c) Conversion Between Bases

$$M_{d.b.} = \frac{M_{w.b.}}{1 - \frac{M_{w.b.}}{100}}$$

$$M_{w.b.} = \frac{M_{d.b.}}{1 + \frac{M_{d.b.}}{100}}$$

## 2.2 Water Activity ($a_w$)

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0} = \frac{ERH}{100}$$

where:
- $p$ = partial vapour pressure of water above the food
- $p_0$ = vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature
- $ERH$ = Equilibrium Relative Humidity (%) of the surrounding air at which the food neither gains nor loses moisture.

### Critical $a_w$ Thresholds for Microbial Growth

| Microbial Group | Minimum $a_w$ for Growth |
|---|---|
| **Bacteria (most pathogenic)** | **0.91** |
| **Yeasts (most)** | **0.88** |
| **Molds (most)** | **0.80** |
| **Halophilic bacteria** | **0.75** |
| **Xerophilic molds** | **0.65** |
| **No microbial growth** | $< 0.60$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Salting, sugaring, and drying** all work by depressing $a_w$ below these thresholds — they do **not** reduce moisture content alone sufficiently to preserve food without $a_w$ control.

## 2.3 Humidity & Psychrometric Relationships

**Relative Humidity (RH):**
$$RH = \frac{p_v}{p_g} \times 100$$

**Absolute Humidity / Humidity Ratio ($W$):**
$$W = 0.622 \times \frac{p_v}{P_{atm} - p_v}$$

**Dew Point Relation (Magnus-type approximation):**
$$p_g(T) \approx \exp\!\left(A - \frac{B}{T + C}\right)$$

with $A = 18.3036,\ B = 3816.44,\ C = -46.13$ (for $T$ in °C, $p_g$ in mm Hg).

## 2.4 Methods of Moisture Determination

| Method | Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Oven drying (convection)** | Evaporation at 100–105 °C till constant mass | Cereals, flours, baked goods |
| **Vacuum oven** | Drying under reduced pressure (low temp) | Heat-sensitive foods (sugar, syrup) |
| **Infrared / Halogen moisture balance** | IR radiation heating + built-in balance | Rapid QC |
| **Karl Fischer titration** | Chemical reaction with iodine (I₂ + SO₂ + H₂O) | Low-moisture fats, oils, spices |
| **Dean-Stark distillation** | Azeotropic distillation with toluene | High-fat foods (oils in seeds) |
| **Lyophilization (reference)** | Freeze-drying | Gold-standard reference method |
| **Conductivity / Capacitance** | Electrical property change with moisture | Online sensors |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{w.b.} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d}\times 100$ | Wet-basis moisture | % |
| 2 | $M_{d.b.} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d}\times 100$ | Dry-basis moisture | % |
| 3 | $M_{d.b.} = \dfrac{M_{w.b.}}{1 - M_{w.b. / 100}}$ | WB → DB conversion | % |
| 4 | $M_{w.b.} = \dfrac{M_{d.b.}}{1 + M_{d.b. / 100}}$ | DB → WB conversion | % |
| 5 | $a_w = p / p_0$ | Water activity | dimensionless |
| 6 | $W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_v}{P_{atm} - p_v}$ | Humidity ratio | kg water / kg dry air |
| 7 | $RH = \dfrac{p_v}{p_g}\times 100$ | Relative humidity | % |
| 8 | $m_d = m_i(1 - M_{w.b.,i})$ | Solids mass balance | kg |
| 9 | $m_w = m_f \cdot M_{w.b.,f}$ | Water mass from final product | kg |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drying Calculations:** Evaporation load = mass of water removed = $m_i - m_f \times (1 - M_{w.b.,f})$.
- **Mass Balance on Mixers/Blenders:** When two food streams are blended, use the *dry-mass* basis (conserved), not moisture (not conserved).
- **Energy Estimation:** Heat duty = $m_w \times h_{fg} + \text{sensible heating of solids}$.
- **Shelf-life Prediction:** Use $a_w$ with the BET sorption isotherm to predict caking and stickiness in powders.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Conversion Between Wet and Dry Basis Moisture

**Starting point (definitions):**

$$M_{w.b.} = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100$$

$$M_{d.b.} = \frac{m_w}{m_d} \times 100$$

**Step 1:** Express $m_w$ from the dry-basis equation.

$$m_w = m_d \cdot \frac{M_{d.b.}}{100}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the wet-basis numerator.

$$M_{w.b.} \cdot (m_w + m_d) = m_w$$

$$M_{w.b.} \cdot m_w + M_{w.b.} \cdot m_d = m_w$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate $m_w$ in terms of $m_d$.

$$M_{w.b.} \cdot m_d = m_w - M_{w.b.} \cdot m_w = m_w(1 - M_{w.b.})$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $m_w / m_d$.

$$\frac{m_w}{m_d} = \frac{M_{w.b.}}{1 - M_{w.b.}}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the LHS as $M_{d.b.}/100$.

$$\frac{M_{d.b.}}{100} = \frac{M_{w.b.}/100}{1 - M_{w.b.}/100}$$

**Step 6 (Final):**

$$\boxed{M_{d.b.} = \frac{M_{w.b.}}{1 - M_{w.b.}/100}}$$

The inverse relation follows algebraically by rearranging.

## 3.2 Derivation — Mass of Water Removed in a Drying Process

**Given:**
- Initial mass of food = $m_i$ (kg)
- Initial wet-basis moisture = $M_i$ (decimal fraction, 0–1)
- Final wet-basis moisture = $M_f$
- Final mass = $m_f$

**Step 1:** Dry solids are conserved (they don't evaporate).

$$m_d = m_i(1 - M_i) = m_f(1 - M_f)$$

**Step 2:** Solve for final mass.

$$m_f = m_i \cdot \frac{(1 - M_i)}{(1 - M_f)}$$

**Step 3:** Mass of water removed = initial mass − final mass.

$$\Delta m_w = m_i - m_f$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $m_f$.

$$\Delta m_w = m_i - m_i \cdot \frac{(1 - M_i)}{(1 - M_f)} = m_i \left[1 - \frac{(1 - M_i)}{(1 - M_f)}\right]$$

**Step 5:** Simplify.

$$\Delta m_w = m_i \cdot \frac{(1 - M_f) - (1 - M_i)}{1 - M_f} = m_i \cdot \frac{M_i - M_f}{1 - M_f}$$

**Final formula:**

$$\boxed{\Delta m_w = m_i \cdot \frac{M_i - M_f}{1 - M_f}}$$

> *(Valid when $M_i$, $M_f$ are expressed as decimal fractions, e.g., 0.85 for 85 %.)*

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A sample of wet food weighs **500 g** in the wet state. After oven drying at 105 °C to constant weight, the dry residue is **125 g**. Find:
- (i) Moisture content on wet basis
- (ii) Moisture content on dry basis
- (iii) Mass of water to be removed per kg of wet food to reduce moisture to 20 % (w.b.)

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify masses.

$$m_{total} = 500\ \text{g}, \quad m_d = 125\ \text{g}, \quad m_w = 500 - 125 = 375\ \text{g}$$

**Step 2:** Wet basis moisture.

$$M_{w.b.} = \frac{375}{500} \times 100 = 75\ \%$$

**Step 3:** Dry basis moisture.

$$M_{d.b.} = \frac{375}{125} \times 100 = 300\ \%$$

**Step 4:** Verification using the conversion formula.

$$M_{d.b.} = \frac{75}{1 - 75/100} = \frac{75}{0.25} = 300\ \% \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5:** Final mass per kg wet food when target $M_f = 0.20$.

Dry solids in 1 kg wet food = $1 \times (1 - 0.75) = 0.25\ \text{kg}$

$$m_f = \frac{0.25}{1 - 0.20} = \frac{0.25}{0.80} = 0.3125\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 6:** Water to remove.

$$\Delta m_w = 1 - 0.3125 = 0.6875\ \text{kg} = 687.5\ \text{g}$$

### Analytical Matrix — Common Student Mistakes

| Mistake | Why it's wrong | Correct approach |
|---|---|---|
| Adding wet-basis moistures of two streams to get blend moisture | Moisture is not conserved; **dry matter** is | Use dry-mass balance: $m_d = m_1(1-M_1) + m_2(1-M_2)$ |
| Using $a_w$ interchangeably with moisture content | $a_w$ is thermodynamic; MC is mass-based | Use BET/GAB isotherm to relate them |
| Reporting moisture > 100 % w.b. | Wet-basis MC is bounded by 100 % | Re-express on dry basis instead |
| Forgetting to convert % to decimal in $\Delta m_w$ formula | Mixing units gives wrong magnitudes | Use $M_i = 0.75$ not $75$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Water in Food

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    nodeB --> nodeC["FREE / BULK WATER"]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between moisture content (wet basis) and moisture content (dry basis) of a food material. Why is dry basis preferred in engineering calculations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Wet Basis | Dry Basis |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $M_{w.b.} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w+m_d}\times 100$ | $M_{d.b.} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d}\times 100$ |
| Range | 0 to 100 % | Can exceed 100 % |
| Reference | Total mass | Dry solids only |

**Why dry basis is preferred (1 mark):** During drying or any unit operation, the *dry solid mass is conserved* while the *total mass changes* (water leaves). Using dry basis keeps the denominator constant, making mass and energy balance calculations simpler and more accurate.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define water activity. State the minimum $a_w$ values required for the growth of (i) bacteria, (ii) yeasts, and (iii) molds.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0} = \frac{ERH}{100}$$
where $p$ is the partial vapour pressure of water in the food and $p_0$ is the vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature.

**Threshold values (2 marks):**
- Bacteria (pathogenic): $a_w \geq 0.91$
- Yeasts: $a_w \geq 0.88$
- Molds: $a_w \geq 0.80$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** A wet food material weighing **200 g** contains **150 g** of moisture. Calculate:
(i) Moisture content on wet basis
(ii) Moisture content on dry basis
(iii) Mass of water that must be removed to reduce the moisture to **40 % (w.b.)**

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the classification of water in food with a neat diagram. Explain how the concept of water activity helps in food preservation.

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Question A — Model Solution**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify masses.

$$m_{total} = 200\ \text{g}, \quad m_w = 150\ \text{g}, \quad m_d = 200 - 150 = 50\ \text{g}$$

**Step 2 (i):** Wet-basis moisture.

$$M_{w.b.} = \frac{150}{200} \times 100 = \mathbf{75\ \%} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 3 (ii):** Dry-basis moisture.

$$M_{d.b.} = \frac{150}{50} \times 100 = \mathbf{300\ \%} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 4 (iii):** Final mass when target $M_f = 0.40$.

Dry solids remain $= 50\ \text{g}$

$$m_f = \frac{50}{1 - 0.40} = \frac{50}{0.60} = 83.33\ \text{g} \quad \textbf{[1.5 marks]}$$

**Step 5:** Water to be removed.

$$\Delta m_w = 200 - 83.33 = \mathbf{116.67\ g} \quad \textbf{[1.5 marks]}$$

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Classification of water (3.5 marks):**

Water in food exists in three states:

1. **Free / Bulk Water** — loosely held in capillaries and intercellular spaces; behaves like pure water; freezes at 0 °C; fully available for microbial and enzymatic reactions.
2. **Adsorbed (Multilayer) Water** — bound to hydrophilic groups on proteins, starch, and salts via 2–3 molecular layers of hydrogen bonding; partially available.
3. **Bound (Monolayer) Water** — tightly held at the immediate surface of macromolecules; does not freeze at 0 °C; non-solvent for solutes; not available to microorganisms.

**Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1.)*

**Role of $a_w$ in preservation (2.5 marks):**

- Microbial growth is suppressed below specific $a_w$ thresholds (bacteria < 0.91, yeasts < 0.88, molds < 0.80).
- **Drying, salting, and sugaring** lower $a_w$ by reducing free water availability.
- Foods with $a_w < 0.60$ (e.g., dried fruits, biscuits) are microbially stable.
- $a_w$ also governs enzymatic browning, lipid oxidation, and non-enzymatic Maillard reactions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define moisture content and explain any **four** methods used for its determination. Mention one limitation of the standard oven method.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food product is to be dried from an initial moisture content of **80 % (w.b.)** to a final moisture content of **10 % (w.b.)**. If the feed rate is **1000 kg/h**, calculate:
(i) The mass of water removed per hour.
(ii) The mass of dried product obtained per hour.

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B — Model Solution**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Definition (1 mark):** Moisture content is the quantity of water present in a food material, usually expressed as a percentage of the total mass (wet basis) or of the dry mass (dry basis).

**Four methods (4 × 1 = 4 marks):**

1. **Oven Drying Method:** Sample dried in a convection oven at 100–105 °C till constant mass. Moisture = loss in weight. *Limitation: cannot remove bound water; heat-sensitive foods degrade.*
2. **Vacuum Oven Method:** Drying under reduced pressure at 60–70 °C. Suitable for sugar- and fat-rich foods that oxidise at high temperatures.
3. **Karl Fischer Titration:** Based on the chemical reaction of water with iodine and sulphur dioxide in the presence of methanol/pyridine. Suitable for very low moisture foods (oils, spices).
4. **Dean-Stark Distillation:** Water is co-distilled with toluene and measured in a graduated receiver. Suitable for high-fat foods.

**Limitation of oven method (2 marks):**
- Does not remove all bound water (residual moisture of ~1–2 % remains).
- Volatile components other than water (alcohols, organic acids, flavour compounds) are lost and counted as moisture, leading to over-estimation.
- Heat-sensitive components may decompose, releasing additional volatiles.

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify data.

$$m_i = 1000\ \text{kg/h}, \quad M_i = 0.80, \quad M_f = 0.10$$

**Step 2:** Dry solids in feed.

$$m_d = 1000 \times (1 - 0.80) = 200\ \text{kg/h} \quad \textbf{[1.5 marks]}$$

**Step 3 (i):** Final mass of dried product.

$$m_f = \frac{m_d}{1 - M_f} = \frac{200}{1 - 0.10} = \frac{200}{0.90} = 222.22\ \text{kg/h} \quad \textbf{[2.5 marks]}$$

**Step 4 (ii):** Water removed per hour.

$$\Delta m_w = m_i - m_f = 1000 - 222.22 = \mathbf{777.78\ kg/h} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

**Verification using shortcut:**

$$\Delta m_w = m_i \cdot \frac{M_i - M_f}{1 - M_f} = 1000 \times \frac{0.80 - 0.10}{0.90} = 1000 \times 0.7778 = 777.78\ \text{kg/h} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in Moisture problems:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in $M_i$, $M_f$:** Using 80 instead of 0.80 in the water-removed formula inflates the answer by 100×. Always confirm whether the formula expects percentage or decimal fraction.
> 2. **Confusing moisture with water activity:** A 4-mark question on $a_w$ answered with moisture content receives **zero credit** for the $a_w$ part.
> 3. **Adding moistures directly:** Two streams at 70 % and 30 % do NOT yield 50 % upon mixing. Always use the dry-mass balance.
> 4. **Forgetting to state units:** Marks are deducted when numerical answers lack kg/h, %, or °C.
> 5. **No diagram in long-answer parts:** Even a hand-drawn or text-based classification tree earns 1–1.5 marks easily.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Moisture content (MC):** Mass of water ÷ total mass (w.b.) or ÷ dry mass (d.b.).
- **Conversion formulas:** $\ M_{d.b.} = \dfrac{M_{w.b.}}{1 - M_{w.b.}/100}$ and its inverse.
- **Water activity ($a_w$):** Ratio of vapour pressures; bounded 0 to 1.
- **Microbial thresholds:** Bacteria 0.91, Yeasts 0.88, Molds 0.80, No growth < 0.60.
- **Three states of water:** Free, Adsorbed (multilayer), Bound (monolayer).
- **Dry mass is conserved** during drying — use it for all mass-balance calculations.
- **Water removed formula:** $\ \Delta m_w = m_i \cdot \dfrac{M_i - M_f}{1 - M_f}$ (decimal fractions).
- **Methods of determination:** Oven, Vacuum oven, Karl Fischer, Dean-Stark, IR balance, Lyophilization.
- **Standard oven conditions:** 100–105 °C till constant mass; cannot remove bound water.
- **Karl Fischer** is the most accurate for very low moisture (fats, oils, spices).
- **Relative Humidity vs $a_w$:** Both are ratios of partial to saturation vapour pressure; $a_w$ = $ERH/100$.
- **Humidity ratio:** $W = 0.622 \cdot p_v / (P_{atm} - p_v)$ — important in dryer air calculations.
- **Intermediate Moisture Foods (IMF):** $a_w$ between 0.60 and 0.85 — preserved without refrigeration.
- **Moisture migration** between components of mixed foods drives caking and texture failure.
- **MC and $a_w$ are related but NOT identical** — the relationship is non-linear and described by sorption isotherms (BET, GAB).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carbohydrates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Carbohydrates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Carbohydrates

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carbohydrates** are defined chemically as **polyhydroxy aldehydes**, **polyhydroxy ketones**, or any compounds that can be hydrolyzed to yield these simpler units. The general empirical formula that broadly classifies most members of this family is **Cₙ(H₂O)ₙ**, which historically gave them the misleading nickname "hydrates of carbon" — hence the name *carbo-hydrate*.

In modern food chemistry (as adopted in the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Food Processing syllabus), carbohydrates are classified as the **primary source of metabolic energy (≈ 4 kcal/g or 17 kJ/g)** in the human diet and serve as key **structural biopolymers** in plant-based foods.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The LEGO Bricks of the Food World"**
>
> Imagine carbohydrates as **LEGO building blocks** in your kitchen pantry:
> - **Single LEGO bricks** = *Monosaccharides* (the smallest, indivisible units like glucose and fructose).
> - **Two bricks snapped together** = *Disaccharides* (e.g., table sugar = sucrose = glucose + fructose).
> - **A long chain of bricks** = *Polysaccharides* (e.g., starch in rice, cellulose in vegetables).
>
> Just as you cannot break a single LEGO brick into smaller identical bricks, your body cannot break a monosaccharide into smaller carbohydrate units. But it *can* snap disaccharides and polysaccharides apart to release the individual energy bricks (glucose) for fuel.

## 1.3 Empirical Formula & General Structure

The general empirical formula for the carbohydrate family:

$$\text{Empirical Formula: } C_{n}\left(H_{2}O\right)_{n}$$

For example:
- **Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)** → n = 6
- **Sucrose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁)** → derived from two hexose units minus one water molecule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Although the empirical formula Cₙ(H₂O)ₙ is the historical basis, **NOT all carbohydrates strictly follow this formula** (e.g., deoxyribose = C₅H₁₀O₄, where one oxygen is "missing"), and **NOT all compounds that fit Cₙ(H₂O)ₙ are carbohydrates** (e.g., acetic acid C₂H₄O₂). Therefore, the functional group definition is more accurate.

## 1.4 Functional Role in Food Processing

Carbohydrates are central to food processing because they govern:
1. **Sweetness perception** (sugars).
2. **Texture and viscosity** (starches, gums, pectins).
3. **Browning reactions** (Maillard reaction, caramelization).
4. **Shelf life and water activity** (hygroscopicity of sugars).
5. **Fermentation** substrates (yeast metabolism of sugars to CO₂ + ethanol).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Carbohydrates

Carbohydrates are systematically classified based on **molecular complexity** (degree of polymerization, DP) and **chemical behavior** upon hydrolysis.

### 2.1.1 Based on Degree of Polymerization (DP)

> [!NOTE]
> The **DP (Degree of Polymerization)** refers to the number of monosaccharide units linked together in a carbohydrate molecule.

| Class | DP Range | Examples | Solubility in Water |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Monosaccharides** | 1 | Glucose, Fructose, Galactose, Mannose, Ribose | Highly soluble |
| **Disaccharides** | 2 | Sucrose, Maltose, Lactose, Trehalose | Soluble |
| **Oligosaccharides** | 3 – 9 | Raffinose, Stachyose, Maltotriose | Moderately soluble |
| **Polysaccharides** | ≥ 10 | Starch, Cellulose, Glycogen, Pectin, Chitin | Mostly insoluble (or form colloids) |

### 2.1.2 Based on Functional Group (Aldehyde vs. Ketone)

- **Aldoses** — contain an **aldehyde (–CHO)** group; e.g., Glucose, Galactose, Mannose, Ribose.
- **Ketoses** — contain a **ketone (>C=O)** group; e.g., Fructose, Ribulose.

### 2.1.3 Based on Number of Carbon Atoms

| # Carbons | Aldose Type | Ketose Type |
|---|---|---|
| 3 (Triose) | Glyceraldehyde | Dihydroxyacetone |
| 4 (Tetrose) | Erythrose | Erythrulose |
| 5 (Pentose) | Ribose, Xylose | Ribulose, Xylulose |
| 6 (Hexose) | Glucose, Galactose, Mannose | Fructose |

## 2.2 The Glycosidic Bond — The "Molecular Snap"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **glycosidic bond** is the covalent linkage that joins two monosaccharide units together via a **condensation (dehydration) reaction**, releasing one molecule of **H₂O**.

General condensation equation:

$$\text{Sugar}_1\text{–OH} \;+\; \text{HO–Sugar}_2 \;\xrightarrow{\text{condensation}}\; \text{Sugar}_1\text{–O–Sugar}_2 \;+\; H_{2}O$$

The position (α or β) and the carbon atoms (1→4, 1→6, etc.) involved determine the **digestibility** and **functional properties** of the resulting disaccharide/polysaccharide.

## 2.3 Monosaccharides — The Building Bricks

### 2.3.1 D-Glucose (Dextrose, Grape Sugar)
- Molecular formula: **C₆H₁₂O₆**, Molar mass: **180.16 g/mol**
- An **aldohexose**; the most abundant monosaccharide in nature.
- Found freely in fruits, honey, and blood (blood sugar ≈ 70–110 mg/dL fasting).
- **D-form** is biologically active (D = dextrorotatory, rotates plane-polarized light **right**).

### 2.3.2 D-Fructose (Levulose, Fruit Sugar)
- Molecular formula: **C₆H₁₂O₆**, Molar mass: **180.16 g/mol**
- A **ketohexose**; the sweetest natural sugar (≈ **1.7× sweeter** than sucrose).
- Found in fruits, honey, and seminal fluid.
- **L-form notation** here refers to stereochemistry, not optical rotation — fructose is **levorotatory** (rotates light **left**), hence "levulose."

### 2.3.3 D-Galactose
- Does **not** occur free in nature in significant amounts; exists as a component of **lactose** (milk sugar) and glycolipids.

### 2.3.4 D-Mannose
- Found in plant cell wall polysaccharides (mannans) and certain glycoproteins.

## 2.4 Disaccharides — Two Bricks Joined

| Disaccharide | Composition | Glycosidic Bond | Source | Reducing? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sucrose** | Glucose + Fructose | α-1, β-2 | Sugarcane, sugar beet | **No** (non-reducing) |
| **Maltose** | Glucose + Glucose | α-1,4 | Malt, starch hydrolysis | **Yes** |
| **Lactose** | Glucose + Galactose | β-1,4 | Milk (≈ 4.8% in cow milk) | **Yes** |
| **Trehalose** | Glucose + Glucose | α,α-1,1 | Yeast, fungi, insects | **No** (non-reducing) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reducing Sugars Rule:** A disaccharide is **reducing** if it has a **free anomeric carbon** (–OH on C1 in aldose or C2 in ketose) capable of opening into the aldehyde form. Sucrose has **both** anomeric carbons locked in the glycosidic bond → **non-reducing**. This distinction is **critical for Maillard browning** and Benedict's/Fehling's tests.

## 2.5 Polysaccharides — Long Chains

### 2.5.1 Starch (Plant Storage Polysaccharide)
- A **homopolymer of α-D-glucose**.
- Two components:
  - **Amylose** (≈ 20–30%): linear, α-1,4-linked → forms **helical coils**, gives blue color with iodine.
  - **Amylopectin** (≈ 70–80%): highly branched, α-1,4 backbone with α-1,6 branch points every 24–30 glucose units → gives **red-brown/violet** color with iodine.
- Found in rice, wheat, potato, maize, cassava.

### 2.5.2 Glycogen (Animal Storage Polysaccharide)
- Structurally similar to amylopectin but **more highly branched** (α-1,6 branches every 8–12 glucose units).
- Stored in **liver** (for blood glucose regulation) and **muscle** (for local energy).
- Gives a **red-brown** color with iodine.

### 2.5.3 Cellulose (Plant Structural Polysaccharide)
- Linear polymer of **β-D-glucose** joined by **β-1,4 glycosidic bonds**.
- The β-linkage creates **straight, rigid chains** that form **hydrogen-bonded microfibrils** → high tensile strength (cotton fiber is ≈ 90% cellulose).
- **Humans cannot digest cellulose** because we lack the enzyme **cellulase**; it functions as **dietary fiber**.

### 2.5.4 Pectin
- A complex polysaccharide rich in **galacturonic acid** residues.
- Forms **gels** in the presence of sugar and acid (the basis of jam/jelly making — the **“high-pectin + high-acid + high-sugar” rule**).
- Uses **LM (low methoxyl) pectin** for low-sugar gels (calcium-mediated).

### 2.5.5 Other Important Polysaccharides
- **Chitin:** β-1,4-linked N-acetylglucosamine; found in crustacean shells, insect exoskeletons.
- **Hemicellulose:** heterogeneous group (xylan, mannan, galactan); found in plant cell walls alongside cellulose.
- **Gums & Mucilages:** e.g., guar gum, xanthan gum, gum arabic — used as **thickeners, stabilizers, emulsifiers** in food processing.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| General empirical formula | $C_{n}(H_{2}O)_{n}$ | n ≥ 3 typically |
| Energy yield (metabolic) | $\approx \mathbf{4 \; kcal/g} \;(\approx 17 \; kJ/g)$ | Physiological fuel value |
| Relative sweetness | $\text{Sucrose} = 1.0; \text{Fructose} \approx 1.7; \text{Glucose} \approx 0.7; \text{Maltose} \approx 0.4; \text{Lactose} \approx 0.16$ | Organoleptic scale |
| Condensation (glycosidic bond formation) | $\Delta M = -18 \; g/mol$ (loss of H₂O per bond) | g/mol |
| Molar mass of glucose | $180.16 \; g/mol$ | C₆H₁₂O₆ |
| Molar mass of sucrose | $342.30 \; g/mol$ | C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ |
| Hydrolysis of polysaccharide (DP = N) | $\text{Mass of monomers} = M_{polymer} - (N-1) \cdot 18 \; g/mol$ | g/mol |
| Starch gelatinization temperature range | $\approx 60–80 \; °C$ | Depends on starch source |
| Pectin gel formation (jams) | pH $\approx 3.0 - 3.5$; Sugar $\geq 60\%$; Pectin $\approx 0.5 - 1.5\%$ | Empirical rule |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical Pipe Symbol Substitute:** When writing magnitudes or ranges inline, use $\lvert x \rvert$ instead of $\vert x \vert$ to avoid markdown table parsing errors.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

1. **Caramelization** — heating sugars above their melting point (sucrose ≈ 160 °C) → brown pigments + flavor compounds (used in candy, caramel sauce, cola color).
2. **Maillard Reaction** — reducing sugar + amino acid → flavor & brown color (bread crust, roasted coffee, seared meat).
3. **Starch gelatinization & retrogradation** — governs texture of bread staling, pudding thickening.
4. **Pectin gelation** — backbone of the jam/jelly industry.
5. **Fermentation** — yeast converts sugars to CO₂ + ethanol (bread rising, brewing, wine).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Molecular Mass Loss During Polymerization

> **Problem Statement:** When two glucose molecules join via a condensation reaction to form maltose, demonstrate the mass balance and derive the molecular formula of the resulting disaccharide.

### Step 1 — Identify Reactants

Two α-D-glucose molecules:

$$2 \times C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \;=\; 2 \times 180.16 \; g/mol \;=\; 360.32 \; g/mol$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Condensation Reaction

$$\underbrace{C_{6}H_{12}O_{6}}_{\text{Glucose 1}} \;+\; \underbrace{C_{6}H_{12}O_{6}}_{\text{Glucose 2}} \;\xrightarrow{\text{condensation}}\; \underbrace{C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}}_{\text{Maltose}} \;+\; \underbrace{H_{2}O}_{\text{water released}}$$

### Step 3 — Verify Mass Balance (Carbon)

$$\text{C atoms: } 6 + 6 = 12 \; \checkmark$$

### Step 4 — Verify Mass Balance (Hydrogen)

$$\text{H atoms: } 12 + 12 = 22 + 2 \;=\; 24 \;\checkmark$$

### Step 5 — Verify Mass Balance (Oxygen)

$$\text{O atoms: } 6 + 6 = 11 + 1 \;=\; 12 \;\checkmark$$

### Step 6 — Compute Molar Mass of Maltose

$$M_{\text{maltose}} \;=\; 2(180.16) - 18.015 \;=\; 360.32 - 18.015 \;=\; 342.305 \; g/mol$$

### Step 7 — Generalize the Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalized Polymerization Mass Formula:**
> For a polysaccharide of **n** monosaccharide units of molar mass $M_{mono}$ linked by **(n − 1)** glycosidic bonds:
>
> $$\boxed{M_{polymer} \;=\; n \cdot M_{mono} \;-\; (n - 1) \cdot M_{H_{2}O}}$$
>
> where $M_{H_{2}O} = 18.015 \; g/mol$.

## 3.2 Numerical Example: Calculating DP of a Starch Molecule

> **Problem:** A starch sample has an average molar mass of **1,620,000 g/mol**. Assuming it is composed entirely of glucose units ($M_{mono} = 180.16 \; g/mol$), calculate the **degree of polymerization (DP)**.

### Step 1 — Rearrange the Formula for n

From the generalized equation:

$$M_{polymer} = n \cdot M_{mono} - (n-1) \cdot 18.015$$

$$M_{polymer} = n(M_{mono} - 18.015) + 18.015$$

### Step 2 — Solve for n

$$n = \frac{M_{polymer} - 18.015}{M_{mono} - 18.015}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$n = \frac{1{,}620{,}000 - 18.015}{180.16 - 18.015}$$

$$n = \frac{1{,}619{,}981.985}{162.145}$$

### Step 4 — Final Result

$$\boxed{n \approx 9{,}991 \; \text{glucose units}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A typical amylopectin molecule may contain **2,000 – 200,000** glucose units; amylose typically has **300 – 3,000**. The DP value directly affects **gel strength, viscosity, and digestibility** of starch-based food ingredients.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sugar Functionality in Food Processing

| Property | Glucose | Fructose | Sucrose | Maltose | Lactose | Starch |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sweetness (relative) | 0.7 | **1.7** | 1.0 | 0.4 | 0.16 | 0 (tasteless) |
| Reducing sugar? | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (mostly) |
| Fermentable by yeast | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (after inversion) | ✓ | ✗ (slow) | ✗ (needs hydrolysis) |
| Maillard browning | ✓ | ✓ (slower) | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Glycemic Index (approx) | 100 | **15** | 65 | 105 | 46 | 50–95 (cooked) |
| Hygroscopicity | Low | **High** | Medium | Low | Low | Variable |

## 3.4 Quantitative Comparison of Browning Reactions

> **Problem:** Explain why sucrose does **not** participate in the Maillard reaction even though it contains glucose and fructose.

### Step 1 — Identify the Reducing Requirement

The Maillard reaction requires a **free carbonyl group** (aldehyde or open-chain ketone) to react with the **amino group** (–NH₂) of an amino acid/protein.

### Step 2 — Examine Sucrose Structure

Sucrose = α-D-glucose (C1) ↔ β-D-fructose (C2). Both anomeric carbons (C1 of glucose and C2 of fructose) are **locked** in the glycosidic bond.

### Step 3 — Compare to Maltose

Maltose = α-D-glucose (C1) ↔ α-D-glucose (C4). Only one anomeric carbon (C1 of the **second** glucose) is free → can open to aldehyde → **Maillard active**.

### Step 4 — Engineering Implication

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Practice:** To achieve Maillard browning in sucrose-containing foods (e.g., baked goods with table sugar), **acid hydrolysis** (or **invertase enzyme**) is used to cleave sucrose into its **reducing** components (glucose + fructose — the "invert sugar" mixture). Invert sugar also has **higher hygroscopicity**, helping retain moisture in cakes and icings.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Tree of Carbohydrates

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CARBOHYDRATES] --> B[MONOSACCHARIDES DP equals 1]
    A --> C[OLIGOSACCHARIDES DP equals 2 to 9]
    A --> D[POLYSACCHARIDES DP greater than or equal to 10]

    B --> B1[ALDOSES contain CHO group]
    B --> B2[KETOSES contain CO group]

    B1 --> B1A[GLUCOSE C6H12O6]
    B1 --> B1B[GALACTOSE C6H12O6]
    B1 --> B1C[MANNOSE C6H12O6]
    B1 --> B1D[RIBOSE C5H10O5]

    B2 --> B2A[FRUCTOSE C6H12O6]
    B2 --> B2B[RIBULOSE C5H10O5]

    C --> C1[DISACCHARIDES DP equals 2]
    C --> C2[TRISACCHARIDES DP equals 3]
    C --> C3[TETRASACCHARIDES DP equals 4]

    C1 --> C1A[SUCROSE Glucose plus Fructose]
    C1 --> C1B[MALTOSE Glucose plus Glucose]
    C1 --> C1C[LACTOSE Glucose plus Galactose]
    C1 --> C1D[TREHALOSE Glucose plus Glucose]

    D --> D1[HOMOPOLYSACCHARIDES one monomer type]
    D --> D2[HETEROPOLYSACCHARIDES multiple monomer types]

    D1 --> D1A[STARCH Amylose plus Amylopectin]
    D1 --> D1B[GLYCOGEN Animal storage]
    D1 --> D1C[CELLULOSE Beta 1 4 glucose]

    D2 --> D2A[PECTIN Galacturonic acid rich]
    D2 --> D2B[HEMICELLULOSE Xylan Mannan etc]
    D2 --> D2C[CHITIN N acetyl glucosamine]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Disaccharide Formation via Condensation

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[ALPHA D GLUCOSE] -->|loses H from OH on C1| node2[ACTIVATED GLUCOSE]
    node3[BETA D FRUCTOSE] -->|loses OH from C2| node4[ACTIVATED FRUCTOSE]
    node2 -->|forms glycosidic bond| node5[SUCROSE non reducing]
    node4 -->|forms glycosidic bond| node5
    node5 -->|releases water H2O| node6[WATER MOLECULE RELEASED]

    node7[ALPHA D GLUCOSE A] -->|C1 to C4 link| node8[ACTIVATED GLUCOSE A]
    node9[ALPHA D GLUCOSE B] -->|C4 link| node10[ACTIVATED GLUCOSE B]
    node8 -->|alpha 1 4 glycosidic bond| node11[MALTOSE reducing]
    node10 -->|alpha 1 4 glycosidic bond| node11
    node11 -->|releases water H2O| node12[WATER MOLECULE RELEASED]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Carbohydrate Role in Food Processing

```mermaid
graph TD
    INP[RAW CARBOHYDRATE SOURCE] -->|harvesting cleaning| PROC[PRE PROCESSING UNIT]

    PROC -->|crushing milling extraction| SEP[SEPARATION AND PURIFICATION]

    SEP --> SUG[SUGAR STREAM]
    SEP --> STAR[STARCH STREAM]
    SEP => FIB[FIBER STREAM]

    SUG --> C1[CRYSTALLIZATION]
    SUG --> C2[CARAMELIZATION]
    SUG --> C3[FERMENTATION]
    SUG --> C4[MAILLARD BROWNING]

    STAR --> P1[GELATINIZATION]
    STAR --> P2[HYDROLYSIS TO MALTOSE GLUCOSE]
    STAR --> P3[RETROGRADATION STARCH AGING]
    STAR --> P4[MODIFIED STARCH ETHER ESTER CROSSLINKED]

    FIB --> F1[Dietary fiber health claim]
    FIB --> F2[Functional fiber inulin FOS]

    C1 --> OUT1[Pure Sucrose or Glucose Syrup]
    C2 --> OUT2[Caramel color and flavor]
    C3 --> OUT3[Ethanol CO2 biomass]
    C4 --> OUT4[Flavor and brown pigments]

    P1 --> OUT5[Thickener Binder]
    P2 --> OUT6[High Fructose Corn Syrup HFCS]
    P3 --> OUT7[Staling and firming]
    P4 --> OUT8[Stabilized thickening agent]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Flow: Reducing vs. Non-Reducing Sugars

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Is the sugar REDUCING] --> Q1{Does it have a FREE ANOMERIC CARBON}

    Q1 -->|YES Glucose Fructose Maltose Lactose| R1[REDUCING SUGAR]
    R1 --> R2[Positive in Fehling Test Cu2O red ppt]
    R1 --> R3[Positive in Benedicts Test]
    R1 --> R4[Active in MAILLARD REACTION]
    R1 --> R5[Can be oxidized to carboxylic acids]

    Q1 -->|NO Sucrose Trehalose| NR1[NON REDUCING SUGAR]
    NR1 --> NR2[Negative in Fehling Test]
    NR1 --> NR3[Negative in Benedicts Test]
    NR1 --> NR4[No Maillard browning]
    NR1 --> NR5[Requires hydrolysis first]
```
