<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024 Scheme]`
**Q: Define carbohydrates. Give two examples each for monosaccharides and disaccharides.**

**Model Answer:**
Carbohydrates are polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones, or compounds that yield these on hydrolysis. They generally follow the empirical formula Cₙ(H₂O)ₙ and serve as the primary energy source (≈ 4 kcal/g) in the human diet.

- **Monosaccharides examples:** Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆), Fructose (C₆H₁₂O₆).
- **Disaccharides examples:** Sucrose (glucose + fructose), Maltose (glucose + glucose).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024 Scheme]`
**Q: Differentiate between reducing and non-reducing sugars with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Reducing Sugars | Non-Reducing Sugars |
|---|---|---|
| Free anomeric –OH | Present | Both anomeric carbons locked |
| Fehling's / Benedict's test | Positive (red Cu₂O ppt) | Negative |
| Maillard reaction | Active | Inactive |
| Examples | Glucose, Maltose, Lactose | Sucrose, Trehalose |

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU ESE Module Choice Format]** — Answer **ONE** of the following:

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2025 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify carbohydrates with suitable examples. Explain the structure and biological significance of any two polysaccharides.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is a glycosidic bond? Describe the formation of sucrose and maltose with chemical equations. Explain why sucrose is a non-reducing sugar while maltose is a reducing sugar.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Classification of Carbohydrates:**
Carbohydrates are classified on the basis of **degree of polymerization (DP)**:

1. **Monosaccharides (DP = 1):** Simplest sugars; cannot be hydrolyzed further.
   - Examples: Glucose, Fructose, Galactose, Mannose.
2. **Disaccharides (DP = 2):** Two monosaccharides linked by one glycosidic bond.
   - Examples: Sucrose, Maltose, Lactose.
3. **Oligosaccharides (DP = 3–9):** Examples: Raffinose, Stachyose.
4. **Polysaccharides (DP ≥ 10):** Long-chain polymers.
   - Examples: Starch, Glycogen, Cellulose, Pectin.

**Structure & Significance of Two Polysaccharides:**

**(i) Starch:**
- Polymer of α-D-glucose with two components: **amylose** (linear α-1,4) and **amylopectin** (branched α-1,4 with α-1,6 branches).
- Storage polysaccharide in plants (rice, wheat, potato).
- Easily digested by humans via α-amylase → maltose → glucose.
- Industrial use: thickener, binder, gelling agent (e.g., custard powder, sauces).

**(ii) Cellulose:**
- Polymer of β-D-glucose joined by β-1,4 glycosidic bonds.
- Forms straight, rigid chains stabilized by intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonds → structural microfibrils.
- Principal component of plant cell walls (cotton ≈ 90% cellulose).
- Indigestible by humans (no cellulase enzyme) → acts as **dietary fiber**, aiding bowel movement.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Glycosidic Bond:**
A glycosidic bond is a covalent linkage between the anomeric carbon (C1 in aldose, C2 in ketose) of one sugar and a hydroxyl group of another sugar, formed by a **condensation reaction** releasing one molecule of water.

**Formation of Sucrose:**

$$C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \;(\alpha\text{-glucose}) \;+\; C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \;(\beta\text{-fructose}) \;\longrightarrow\; C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} \;(\text{sucrose}) \;+\; H_{2}O$$

The linkage is **α-1, β-2** (i.e., C1 of glucose bonded to C2 of fructose).

**Formation of Maltose:**

$$2 \; C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \;(\text{glucose}) \;\longrightarrow\; C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} \;(\text{maltose}) \;+\; H_{2}O$$

The linkage is **α-1,4** (C1 of first glucose to C4 of second glucose).

**Reducing vs Non-Reducing Nature:**

In **sucrose**, *both* anomeric carbons (C1 of glucose AND C2 of fructose) are engaged in the glycosidic bond → **no free –OH on anomeric carbon** → cannot open to the free aldehyde/ketone form → **non-reducing**.

In **maltose**, only the C1 of the *first* glucose is locked; the C1 of the *second* glucose remains **free** → can open into the aldehyde form → can reduce Cu²⁺ to Cu⁺ → **reducing sugar**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2025 Scheme]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Maillard reaction. Discuss the role of carbohydrates in food browning and the factors affecting it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the structure of starch. Differentiate between amylose and amylopectin.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Maillard Reaction — Definition:**
The Maillard reaction is a **non-enzymatic browning reaction** between a **reducing sugar** (carbonyl group) and the **amino group** of an amino acid, peptide, or protein, producing brown-colored **melanoidins** and complex flavor compounds.

**Simplified Reaction Pathway:**

$$\text{Reducing Sugar} \;+\; \text{Amino Acid} \;\xrightarrow{\text{heat}}\; \text{Glycosylamine} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Amadori rearrangement}}\; \text{Amadori Product} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Melanoidins} \;+\; \text{Flavor compounds}$$

**Role of Carbohydrates in Browning:**
1. **Reducing sugars** (glucose, fructose, lactose, maltose) are the carbonyl donors.
2. The reaction governs color and flavor in **bread crust, roasted coffee, seared meat, baked cookies, toast**.
3. Non-reducing sugars (sucrose) cannot participate **directly** — must first be hydrolyzed.

**Factors Affecting Maillard Reaction:**
- **Temperature:** Reaction rate roughly doubles per 10 °C rise (Q₁₀ ≈ 2–3).
- **pH:** Faster at **alkaline pH** (≈ 8–10); suppressed at low pH.
- **Water activity (a_w):** Optimal at a_w ≈ 0.6–0.8; very dry or very wet conditions slow it.
- **Type of reducing sugar:** Pentoses (ribose) > hexoses (glucose) > disaccharides.
- **Type of amino acid:** Lysine (free ε-amino group) is highly reactive.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Structure of Starch — Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
AMYLOSE (Linear α-1,4 chain):
  G — G — G — G — G — G — G — G — G — G  (coiled into helix)
  |________________________________________________|
        (Forms blue complex with iodine / KI reagent)

AMYLOPECTIN (Branched α-1,4 backbone + α-1,6 branches):
  G — G — G — G — G — G — G — G — G — G
            |                       |
            G — G — G               G — G — G
            (branch every 24–30 glucose units)
```

**Differences between Amylose and Amylopectin:**

| Feature | Amylose | Amylopectin |
|---|---|---|
| Proportion in starch | 20 – 30% | 70 – 80% |
| Structure | Linear, unbranched | Highly branched |
| Glycosidic bonds | Only α-1,4 | α-1,4 backbone + α-1,6 branches |
| Branch points | None | Every 24–30 glucose units |
| Iodine test color | **Deep blue** | **Red-brown / Violet** |
| Solubility in water | Lower (retrogrades easily) | Higher |
| Gel strength | Strong, firm gels | Soft, viscous pastes |
| Film-forming | Yes (edible films) | No |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing Maillard reaction as "reaction between sugar and protein only" — **wrong**; the actual reactive species is the **free amino group (–NH₂)** of amino acids/lysine residues.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **caramelization** (sugar alone, no amino group, requires ≈ 160 °C for sucrose) with **Maillard** (sugar + amino group, occurs at lower temps ≈ 140 °C).
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Stating "sucrose is reducing" — **fatal error**; both anomeric carbons are locked.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In maltose structure, students often misidentify the **free anomeric carbon** — remember only the **second glucose's C1** is free.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Writing the empirical formula as a *defining* feature rather than a *historical observation*; KTU expects the **functional group definition** as primary.
> - **Common Mistake 6:** Omitting units (g/mol, kcal/g) in numerical questions — KTU deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit consistently.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Carbohydrates — KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Cheat-Sheet**

- ✅ **Definition:** Polyhydroxy aldehydes/ketones; empirical formula Cₙ(H₂O)ₙ; energy = **4 kcal/g**.
- ✅ **Classification by DP:** Mono (1) → Di (2) → Oligo (3–9) → Poly (≥10).
- ✅ **Classification by functional group:** **Aldoses** (CHO) vs. **Ketoses** (C=O).
- ✅ **Glucose** = aldohexose, blood sugar, **D-form** biologically active.
- ✅ **Fructose** = ketohexose, **sweetest** natural sugar (1.7× sucrose), levorotatory.
- ✅ **Sucrose** = glucose + fructose (α-1, β-2 link), **NON-reducing**, table sugar.
- ✅ **Maltose** = glucose + glucose (α-1,4 link), **reducing**, malt sugar.
- ✅ **Lactose** = glucose + galactose (β-1,4 link), **reducing**, milk sugar.
- ✅ **Starch** = α-glucose polymer (amylose + amylopectin); **blue** with iodine (amylose), **red-brown** (amylopectin).
- ✅ **Glycogen** = highly branched α-glucose polymer; animal storage.
- ✅ **Cellulose** = β-glucose polymer (β-1,4); **indigestible** dietary fiber.
- ✅ **Pectin** = galacturonic acid-rich; forms gels with **sugar + acid** (jam-making).
- ✅ **Reducing sugars** = have **free anomeric –OH** → positive Fehling/Benedict → **Maillard active**.
- ✅ **Glycosidic bond** = condensation linkage → loss of **H₂O (18 g/mol)** per bond.
- ✅ **Maillard reaction** = reducing sugar + amino acid → flavor + brown color; favored by **heat, alkaline pH, a_w 0.6–0.8**.
- ✅ **Caramelization** = sugar alone (no amino group) → brown pigments; sucrose at ≈ **160 °C**.
- ✅ **Invert sugar** = sucrose hydrolyzed into glucose + fructose; higher sweetness, more hygroscopic.
- ✅ **Glycemic Index** ranking (basic): Fructose (15) < Lactose (46) < Sucrose (65) < Glucose (100) < Maltose (105).
- ✅ **High Fructose Corn Syrup (HFCS)** = industrially produced via enzymatic isomerization of glucose to fructose.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proteins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Proteins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Proteins** are high-molecular-weight, nitrogen-containing biological macromopolymers composed of long chains of **α-amino acids** linked together by **peptide bonds** (also called amide bonds). The word *protein* is derived from the Greek word *proteios*, meaning "of the first rank," reflecting their fundamental importance in biological systems.

Chemically, proteins are characterized by the recurring peptide backbone:

$$\text{—NH—CHR—CO—NH—CHR—CO—}$$

Proteins are the second most abundant macromolecule in food systems (after water) and the principal nitrogenous component of the human diet. They supply the essential **α-amino acids**, which the human body cannot synthesize *de novo* and must obtain from food.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Structural Feature:** Every amino acid possesses at least one **amino group (—NH₂)** and one **carboxyl group (—COOH)** attached to the same **α-carbon atom**, making them "α-amino acids." This is the universal building block of all proteins.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Origami of Life"

Imagine proteins as **long strings of colored beads** (the beads = amino acids, 20 different types/colors). By themselves, these strings are floppy and useless. But each bead has tiny magnetic patches that pull the string into specific loops, folds, and twists. The result is a uniquely shaped 3-D object (the *folded protein*) — like a piece of **molecular origami** — that performs a specific job:

- **Enzymes** = molecular scissors that cut other molecules
- **Globin in hemoglobin** = a taxi that ferries oxygen
- **Gluten in wheat** = stretchy rubber bands that trap gas bubbles in bread
- **Actin/Myosin in meat** = tiny motors that make muscles contract

The "fold" matters: when heat or acid "unfolds" the protein (a process called **denaturation**), it loses its job — this is exactly what happens when an egg white turns from clear to white while boiling.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics

| Metric | Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Average nitrogen content in proteins** | **16% (≈ 6.25 conversion factor)** | Used to estimate crude protein by Kjeldahl analysis |
| **Standard conversion factor (N to protein)** | **6.25** | Multiplying measured N by 6.25 gives crude protein |
| **Number of standard α-amino acids** | **20** | Universally encoded by DNA |
| **Average residue weight of amino acid** | **110 Da** | Used in molecular weight estimation |
| **Reference daily protein intake (adult)** | **0.83 g/kg body weight** | FAO/WHO/UNU recommendation |
| **Peptide bond length (C–N)** | **~1.32 Å** | Partial double-bond character |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 6.25?** Most proteins contain ~16% nitrogen (1/0.16 = 6.25). However, this factor varies: milk = 6.38, wheat = 5.70, rice = 5.95, soybean = 5.71, eggs = 6.25. Always use the appropriate factor for accurate protein estimation.

## 1.4 Why Proteins Matter in Food Processing

Proteins are not just nutrients — they are **functional ingredients** that determine the texture, color, flavor, and stability of food. From the elasticity of cheese to the foam of meringue and the creaminess of ice cream, proteins are the master performers behind many desirable food qualities.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Focus Point:** In Module 1, questions on proteins typically test (1) amino acid chemistry, (2) levels of protein structure, (3) denaturation, and (4) functional properties. Master these four pillars.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 α-Amino Acid — The Building Block

Every α-amino acid has the general structure:

$$\text{H}_2\text{N—CHR—COOH}$$

where **R** is the variable side chain (the "identity card" of the amino acid) and the central carbon is the **α-carbon**. At physiological pH (~7), the amino acid exists predominantly as a **zwitterion** (dipolar ion):

$$\text{H}_3\text{N}^{+}\text{—CHR—COO}^{-}$$

### 2.1.1 Classification of α-Amino Acids

**A. By Nutritional Essentiality (most important for KTU)**

- **Essential amino acids (9):** Valine, Leucine, Isoleucine, Lysine, Methionine, Phenylalanine, Threonine, Tryptophan, Histidine (semi-essential for adults, essential for infants).
- **Conditionally essential:** Arginine, Cysteine, Tyrosine, Glutamine, Glycine, Proline, Serine.
- **Non-essential:** Alanine, Aspartic acid, Glutamic acid, Asparagine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic for Essential Amino Acids (9):** *"PVT TIM HALL"*
> **P**henylalanine, **V**aline, **T**hreonine, **T**ryptophan, **I**soleucine, **M**ethionine, **H**istidine, **A**rgine… wait — let me correct: **"LIM PVTTH K"** is unreliable. Use the standard chart.

**B. By Side-Chain Polarity (drives folding):**
- Non-polar (hydrophobic): Ala, Val, Leu, Ile, Pro, Phe, Trp, Met
- Polar uncharged: Ser, Thr, Cys, Tyr, Asn, Gln
- Positively charged (basic): Lys, Arg, His
- Negatively charged (acidic): Asp, Glu

**C. By Metabolic Fate:**
- **Glucogenic** — give rise to pyruvate / TCA intermediates
- **Ketogenic** — give rise to acetyl-CoA / ketone bodies
- Both: Ile, Phe, Trp, Tyr, Leu, Lys

## 2.2 The Peptide Bond

The **peptide bond** is formed by a **condensation reaction** between the α-carboxyl group of one amino acid and the α-amino group of another, releasing one molecule of water:

$$\text{H}_2\text{N—CHR}_1\text{—COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N—CHR}_2\text{—COOH} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N—CHR}_1\text{—CO—NH—CHR}_2\text{—COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Key Properties of the Peptide Bond:
1. **Planar** — The C, O, N, H atoms of the peptide group lie in a single plane (steric hindrance from R-groups enforces this).
2. **Partial double-bond character** — The C–N bond length is ~1.32 Å (shorter than a single bond, longer than a true C=N double bond) due to **resonance delocalization**.
3. **Trans configuration** — In most proteins, peptide bonds are *trans* (R-groups on opposite sides), except for proline.
4. **Rigid, not freely rotatable** — Stabilizes the protein backbone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Consequence:** The peptide backbone is essentially a series of rigid planar units joined by rotatable bonds at the α-carbon. This restricts possible conformations and is critical to protein folding.

## 2.3 Hierarchy of Protein Structure

### 2.3.1 Primary Structure (1°)
- The **linear sequence of amino acids** linked by peptide bonds.
- Determined by covalent (peptide) bonds.
- Genetically specified by DNA codons.
- Example: Insulin (51 amino acids in two chains).

### 2.3.2 Secondary Structure (2°)
- Regular, recurring spatial arrangements of the polypeptide backbone.
- **Two principal types:**
  - **α-helix** — right-handed helix; H-bonds between *i* and *i+4* residues; 3.6 residues per turn; stabilized by intra-chain H-bonds.
  - **β-sheet (β-pleated sheet)** — extended strands lying side-by-side; H-bonds between strands; can be *parallel* or *anti-parallel*.
- Stabilized by **hydrogen bonds** of the backbone C=O and N–H groups.

### 2.3.3 Tertiary Structure (3°)
- The overall 3-D folding of a single polypeptide chain into a compact, functional shape (globule).
- Stabilized by:
  - Hydrophobic interactions (R-groups burying inside)
  - Hydrogen bonds (side chains)
  - Ionic (salt) bridges
  - **Disulfide bridges (S–S)** between two cysteine residues
  - Van der Waals forces

### 2.3.4 Quaternary Structure (4°)
- Association of **two or more polypeptide subunits** into a functional oligomeric protein.
- Examples: **Hemoglobin** (4 subunits — 2α, 2β), Lactalbumin, Immunoglobulins.
- Stabilized by the same non-covalent forces as 3°, plus subunit interfaces.

## 2.4 Classification of Food Proteins

| Class | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple proteins** | Yield only α-amino acids on hydrolysis | Albumins, Globulins, Glutelins, Prolamines, Histones, Protamines, Scleroproteins |
| **Conjugated proteins** | Protein + non-protein (prosthetic) group | Glycoproteins, Lipoproteins, Phosphoproteins, Nucleoproteins, Chromoproteins, Metalloproteins |
| **Derived proteins** | Formed by modification of native proteins | Proteans, Metaproteins, Peptones, Peptides |

### 2.4.1 Osborne Classification of Plant Proteins (Cereal Chemistry)
- **Albumins** — soluble in water; coagulable by heat. (e.g., leucosin in wheat)
- **Globulins** — soluble in dilute salt; insoluble in pure water. (e.g., legumin in peas, glycinin in soybean)
- **Prolamines** — soluble in 70–80% ethanol. (e.g., gliadin in wheat, zein in maize)
- **Glutelins** — soluble in dilute acid or alkali. (e.g., glutenin in wheat, oryzenin in rice)

## 2.5 Protein Denaturation

**Denaturation** is the disruption of the *native* 3° and 4° structure of a protein (and partially 2°) **without breaking peptide bonds**. The primary structure is preserved, but the protein loses its biological activity and functional properties.

### Agents of Denaturation
| Physical | Chemical |
|---|---|
| Heat (most common) | Acids and alkalis (pH change) |
| Pressure (high-pressure processing) | Organic solvents (ethanol, acetone) |
| Shear / mechanical agitation | Heavy metals (Hg²⁺, Pb²⁺) |
| Freezing / ice crystals | Urea, guanidine HCl |
| Radiation (UV, ionizing) | Detergents (SDS) |
| Ultrasonication | Reducing agents (cleave S–S) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Denaturation ≠ Hydrolysis.** Denaturation does *not* break peptide bonds; hydrolysis does. Boiling an egg = denaturation (white turns opaque but bonds intact). Acid hydrolysis of casein = peptide bond cleavage into free amino acids.

## 2.6 Functional Properties of Proteins in Foods

These are the *physico-chemical* properties that determine how proteins behave in a food system:

1. **Hydration** — water absorption, swelling, water-holding capacity (WHC), solubility
2. **Solubility** — pH-dependent; minimum at **isoelectric point (pI)**
3. **Viscosity** — thickeners in soups, sauces, gravies
4. **Gelation** — formation of 3-D network trapping water (e.g., egg white gel, tofu)
5. **Emulsification** — stabilize oil-in-water emulsions (e.g., egg yolk, milk proteins)
6. **Foaming / Whipping** — form stable air-in-water foams (e.g., egg white foam, meringue)
7. **Elasticity / Texturization** — dough formation (gluten in wheat)
8. **Coagulation / Precipitation** — curd formation (cheese), tofu

## 2.7 Protein Quality Evaluation (Nutritional Metrics)

| Method | Formula / Concept | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemical Score (CS)** | $\text{CS} = \dfrac{\text{mg of limiting AA per g test protein}}{\text{mg of same AA per g reference (FAO) protein}} \times 100$ | Doesn't account for digestibility |
| **Biological Value (BV)** | $\text{BV} = \dfrac{\text{N retained}}{\text{N absorbed}} \times 100$ | Requires rat bioassay |
| **Net Protein Utilization (NPU)** | $\text{NPU} = \dfrac{\text{N retained}}{\text{N intake}} \times 100 = \text{BV} \times \text{Digestibility}$ | Includes digestibility factor |
| **Protein Efficiency Ratio (PER)** | $\text{PER} = \dfrac{\text{Weight gain (g)}}{\text{Protein consumed (g)}}$ | Rat growth assay; no maintenance measurement |
| **PDCAAS** | $\text{PDCAAS} = \text{Chemical score} \times \text{True digestibility}$, capped at **1.0 (100%)** | Currently preferred by WHO/FAO; uses ileal digestibility |
| **DIAAS** | Digestible Indispensable Amino Acid Score — uses ileal digestibility of each AA | Newer standard (2013 FAO), replacing PDCAAS |

## 2.8 Maillard Reaction (Non-Enzymatic Browning)

A critical reaction between **reducing sugars** (glucose, fructose, lactose) and the **free ε-amino group of lysine** (in proteins). It generates flavor, color, and toxic advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).

**Three stages:**
1. **Early stage:** Schiff base → Amadori (or Heyns) rearrangement
2. **Intermediate stage:** Dehydration, fragmentation → reductones, furfurals, pyrazines
3. **Final stage:** Polymerization → **Melanoidins** (brown pigments)

> [!WARNING]
> **Lysine loss in Maillard browning** is the single most important nutritional concern in food processing. Toasted bread, baked milk, and roasted coffee all suffer this — even if total protein is unchanged, the *available lysine* falls dramatically.

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Crude protein (Kjeldahl) | $\text{Protein (\%)} = \text{N (\%)} \times F$ | $F = 6.25$ (general); 5.70 (wheat), 6.38 (milk), 5.71 (soy) |
| Chemical Score | $\text{CS} = \dfrac{\text{AA in test}}{\text{AA in reference}} \times 100$ | Limiting AA = lowest CS |
| Biological Value | $\text{BV} = \dfrac{\text{N retained}}{\text{N absorbed}} \times 100$ | Animal bioassay |
| NPU | $\text{NPU} = \text{BV} \times D$ | $D$ = digestibility (decimal) |
| PER | $\text{PER} = \dfrac{\Delta W}{P_{\text{intake}}}$ | Rat growth assay |
| PDCAAS | $\text{PDCAAS} = \text{CS} \times D$, capped at 1.0 | Modern gold standard |
| True digestibility | $D = \dfrac{I - (F - F_k)}{I}$ | $I$=N intake, $F$=fecal N, $F_k$=endogenous fecal N |
| Isoelectric point (neutral AA) | $\text{pI} = \dfrac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}$ | Average of two dissociable H⁺ constants |
| Isoelectric point (acidic AA) | $\text{pI} = \dfrac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{aR}}{2}$ | $K_{aR}$ = side-chain COOH |
| Isoelectric point (basic AA) | $\text{pI} = \dfrac{\text{p}K_{a2} + \text{p}K_{aR}}{2}$ | $K_{aR}$ = side-chain NH₃⁺ |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the analytical machinery that KTU examiners expect students to *apply* — not just memorize — especially in 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Problem 1 — Chemical Score & Limiting Amino Acid

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A food protein contains the following essential amino acids (mg per g of protein): Lysine = 28, Methionine = 14, Threonine = 32, Tryptophan = 10. The FAO reference pattern is: Lysine = 55, Methionine = 35, Threonine = 40, Tryptophan = 10. Calculate the **chemical score**, identify the **limiting amino acid**, and the **PDCAAS** if the true digestibility is 0.88.

### Step 1 — Set up the Chemical Score formula
The chemical score of any amino acid is the ratio of its concentration in the test protein to that in the reference, expressed as a percentage:

$$\text{CS}_{\text{AA}} = \frac{\text{mg of AA in 1 g test protein}}{\text{mg of same AA in 1 g reference protein}} \times 100$$

### Step 2 — Compute CS for each amino acid
For **Lysine**:

$$\text{CS}_{\text{Lys}} = \frac{28}{55} \times 100 = 50.91\%$$

For **Methionine**:

$$\text{CS}_{\text{Met}} = \frac{14}{35} \times 100 = 40.00\%$$

For **Threonine**:

$$\text{CS}_{\text{Thr}} = \frac{32}{40} \times 100 = 80.00\%$$

For **Tryptophan**:

$$\text{CS}_{\text{Trp}} = \frac{10}{10} \times 100 = 100.00\%$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Limiting Amino Acid
The **limiting amino acid** is the one with the *lowest* chemical score — it is the bottleneck of the protein's nutritional quality.

Comparing: Lysine (50.91), **Methionine (40.00)** ← lowest, Threonine (80.00), Tryptophan (100.00).

$$\boxed{\text{Limiting AA} = \text{Methionine}, \quad \text{CS} = 40.00\%}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate PDCAAS
PDCAAS combines chemical score with true digestibility, capped at 1.0 (or 100%):

$$\text{PDCAAS} = \text{CS}_{\text{limiting}} \times \text{Digestibility}$$

$$\text{PDCAAS} = 0.40 \times 0.88 = 0.352$$

$$\boxed{\text{PDCAAS} = 0.352 \;\; (\text{or } 35.2\%)}$$

This protein is therefore of **low nutritional quality** because methionine is severely limiting and the digestibility is moderate.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem 2 — Isoelectric Point of Glycine

**Background:** Glycine is the simplest amino acid. It has **two dissociable protons**: the α-carboxyl (pKₐ₁ ≈ 2.34) and the α-ammonium (pKₐ₂ ≈ 9.60). There is no ionizable side chain, so glycine is a "neutral" amino acid.

### Step 1 — Write the three protonation states

$$\text{H}_3\text{N}^{+}\text{—CH}_2\text{—COOH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{p}K_{a1}=2.34]{\text{H}^{+}} \text{H}_3\text{N}^{+}\text{—CH}_2\text{—COO}^{-} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{p}K_{a2}=9.60]{\text{H}^{+}} \text{H}_2\text{N—CH}_2\text{—COO}^{-}$$

The middle species is the **zwitterion** (net charge = 0).

### Step 2 — Apply the pI formula for neutral amino acids
At the isoelectric point, the molecule has zero net charge. The relevant pKₐ values straddling the zwitterion are pKₐ₁ and pKₐ₂:

$$\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2} = \frac{2.34 + 9.60}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{pI}_{\text{glycine}} = 5.97}$$

This means at pH 5.97, glycine has minimum solubility and will not migrate in an electric field.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem 3 — Acidic vs Basic Amino Acid pI

### 3.3.1 For Aspartic Acid (acidic, has extra —COOH in side chain)
- pKₐ₁ (α-COOH) = 1.99
- pKₐR (β-COOH side chain) = 3.90
- pKₐ₂ (α-NH₃⁺) = 9.90

Acidic AA's zwitterion spans the two lowest pKₐ values:

$$\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{aR}}{2} = \frac{1.99 + 3.90}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{pI}_{\text{aspartic acid}} = 2.95}$$

### 3.3.2 For Lysine (basic, has extra —NH₃⁺ in side chain)
- pKₐ₁ (α-COOH) = 2.18
- pKₐ₂ (α-NH₃⁺) = 8.95
- pKₐR (ε-NH₃⁺ side chain) = 10.53

Basic AA's zwitterion spans the two *highest* pKₐ values:

$$\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a2} + \text{p}K_{aR}}{2} = \frac{8.95 + 10.53}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{pI}_{\text{lysine}} = 9.74}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Recognition for KTU:**
> - **Neutral AA** → pI = (pKₐ₁ + pKₐ₂)/2 (pI around 5–6)
> - **Acidic AA** → pI = (pKₐ₁ + pKₐR)/2 (pI < 5, often ~3)
> - **Basic AA** → pI = (pKₐR + pKₐ₂)/2 (pI > 7, often ~9–10)

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Functional Properties and Food Applications

| Functional Property | Structural Basis | Key Food Application | Typical Protein Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solubility | Hydrophilic surface residues | Beverages, infant formula | Whey protein, caseinates |
| Water-holding capacity | Hydrogen bonding with H₂O | Sausages, bakery, meat | Soy, whey, egg white |
| Viscosity | Asymmetric, hydrated molecules | Soups, gravies, dressings | Gelatin, caseinates |
| Gelation | Network formation via cross-links | Tofu, surimi, cheese, dessert gels | Egg white, gelatin, soy |
| Emulsification | Amphiphilic structure (hydrophobic + hydrophilic regions) | Mayonnaise, milk, sausages, cake batters | Egg yolk, whey, soy |
| Foaming | Rapid surface denaturation, film formation | Meringue, mousses, bread | Egg white, whey |
| Dough formation | Viscoelastic gluten network via S–S bonds | Bread, pasta, noodles | Wheat gluten (gliadin + glutenin) |
| Coagulation / Curdling | pH or enzyme-induced aggregation | Cheese, paneer, tofu | Casein (rennet/acid), soy (Mg²⁺) |
| Browning (Maillard) | ε-NH₂ of lysine + reducing sugar | Toasted bread, baked milk, seared meat | All proteins with lysine |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Reasoning — Why Heat Cooks an Egg

This is a classic KTU conceptual question. Let's break it down step-by-step:

1. **Native state of ovalbumin** — folded into a compact tertiary structure with hydrophobic residues buried inside.
2. **Heat input** — increases molecular vibration, disrupts non-covalent forces (H-bonds, hydrophobic interactions) holding 3° structure.
3. **Denaturation** — protein unfolds, exposing hydrophobic residues and free —SH groups to the aqueous environment.
4. **Aggregation** — exposed hydrophobic patches of one molecule interact with another → **coagulation** of unfolded proteins into an opaque, insoluble 3-D network.
5. **Result** — transparent, runny albumen becomes white, opaque, firm solid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Peptide bonds are *not* broken during cooking. Cooking is denaturation + aggregation. The protein is still protein; it's just rearranged physically.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Reasoning — Why Milk Curdles with Lemon Juice

1. Casein micelles in fresh milk carry a **net negative charge** at pH ~6.6 (above pI of casein ≈ 4.6).
2. Like charges on adjacent micelles **repel** each other, keeping them dispersed.
3. Adding lemon juice (citric acid) **lowers the pH** toward the pI of casein.
4. At the pI, net charge = 0 → no electrostatic repulsion → **micelles aggregate** → curd forms.
5. Whey (the watery greenish-yellow liquid) is expelled.
6. The principle: **minimum solubility at pI** → used industrially in paneer, cheese, and tofu manufacture.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Define the following terms:**
(a) Isoelectric point
(b) Denaturation
(c) Essential amino acid

### **Model Answer:**

**(a) Isoelectric point (pI):**
The pH at which a protein (or amino acid) carries **no net electrical charge** and therefore has minimum solubility, maximum tendency to precipitate, and zero electrophoretic mobility. It is calculated as the average of the two pKₐ values that flank the zwitterionic form. For neutral amino acids:

$$\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}$$

**(b) Denaturation:**
Denaturation is the disruption of the native secondary, tertiary, and/or quaternary structure of a protein **without cleavage of peptide bonds**, leading to loss of biological activity and changes in physical properties (solubility, viscosity). It is caused by heat, pH extremes, organic solvents, heavy metals, and urea.

**(c) Essential amino acid:**
An amino acid that the human body **cannot synthesize at all (or in sufficient quantity)** and therefore must be supplied through the diet. The 9 essential amino acids are: **Phenylalanine, Valine, Threonine, Tryptophan, Isoleucine, Methionine, Histidine, Leucine, and Lysine** (PVT TIM HALL is unreliable — memorize the standard list).

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Briefly explain the Maillard reaction. Why is it nutritionally significant in processed foods?**

### **Model Answer:**

The **Maillard reaction** is a non-enzymatic browning reaction between the **carbonyl group of a reducing sugar** (e.g., glucose, lactose) and the **free amino group of an amino acid, peptide, or protein** (most importantly the ε-amino group of **lysine**). It proceeds in three stages:

1. **Condensation** → Schiff base → Amadori rearrangement
2. **Intermediate stage** → Dehydration, fragmentation → pyrazines, furfurals, reductones
3. **Final stage** → Polymerization → **melanoidins** (brown pigments)

**Nutritional significance:**
- The most reactive amino acid is **lysine**, whose ε-NH₂ is "consumed" in the Schiff base formation.
- This results in a **loss of bioavailable lysine** even though total protein content is unchanged by the Kjeldahl method.
- Affected products: toasted bread, baked milk, evaporated milk, seared meat, roasted coffee, sterilized milk powder.
- Severe heat treatment can cause lysine loss of 10–50%, drastically reducing protein quality.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students describe Maillard as "protein + sugar = brown color" without mentioning the **loss of available lysine**. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Answer any ONE of the following:**

**(a)** Describe the **classification of α-amino acids** based on (i) the polarity of the side chain, and (ii) nutritional essentiality. List the 9 essential amino acids and explain why lysine is the most limiting amino acid in cereal-based diets. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **four levels of protein structure** in detail, including the types of bonds and forces that stabilize each level. Give **one food-industry example** for each level. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Answer (a) — Classification of α-Amino Acids:**

**Definition:** α-Amino acids are the monomeric units of proteins, all sharing the general structure H₂N–CHR–COOH, differing only in the side chain (R-group).

**(i) Classification by Side-Chain Polarity:**

| Type | Property | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-polar (hydrophobic)** | Aliphatic or aromatic R; insoluble in water | Gly, Ala, Val, Leu, Ile, Pro, Phe, Trp, Met |
| **Polar uncharged** | Hydrophilic; can H-bond with water | Ser, Thr, Cys, Tyr, Asn, Gln |
| **Positively charged (basic)** | Carry +ve charge at pH 7 | Lys, Arg, His |
| **Negatively charged (acidic)** | Carry –ve charge at pH 7 | Asp, Glu |

This classification is critical because the distribution of polar and non-polar residues determines how the polypeptide chain **folds in aqueous environments** (hydrophobic residues bury inward; polar residues face outward).

**(ii) Classification by Nutritional Essentiality:**

- **Essential (9):** Phenylalanine, Valine, Threonine, Tryptophan, Isoleucine, Methionine, Histidine, Leucine, Lysine.
- **Conditionally essential:** Arginine, Cysteine, Tyrosine, Glutamine (needed in disease, growth, prematurity).
- **Non-essential:** The remaining 11 (synthesized in the body from intermediates of carbohydrate metabolism).

**Why is Lysine the limiting amino acid in cereal-based diets?**

- Cereal grains (wheat, rice, maize, sorghum) are the staple of large populations.
- The storage proteins of cereals — **prolamines and glutelins** (e.g., gliadin, glutenin, zein) — are exceptionally **deficient in lysine** but rich in methionine.
- Legumes (soy, beans, lentils) compensate by being **rich in lysine but low in methionine**.
- This complementarity is the scientific basis of the traditional **"rice + dal"** or **"wheat + bean"** combinations in Indian diets.
- Lysine deficiency leads to impaired growth, poor bone development, reduced immunity, and altered fat metabolism.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often list "essential amino acids" without explaining *why* lysine is limiting in cereals. Always connect the biochemistry to the food system — KTU examiners reward applied understanding.

---

### **Model Answer (b) — Four Levels of Protein Structure:**

The three-dimensional architecture of proteins is organized into four hierarchical levels:

**1. Primary Structure (1°):**
The **linear sequence** of amino acids in a polypeptide chain, determined by the gene. The link is the **covalent peptide bond** formed by condensation between the —COOH of one amino acid and the —NH₂ of the next.
*Food-industry example:* The amino acid sequence of **bovine insulin** (51 residues in two chains) determines its biological activity; even a single substitution (e.g., Glu → Val at position 6 of the β-chain) causes sickle-cell anaemia.

**2. Secondary Structure (2°):**
Local regular folding patterns of the backbone, stabilized by **hydrogen bonds** between the C=O of one peptide and the N–H of another.
- **α-helix:** right-handed; 3.6 residues per turn; H-bond between residue *i* and *i+4*.
- **β-sheet:** extended strands lying side-by-side (parallel or anti-parallel).
*Food-industry example:* **α-Keratin** in hair and wool (rich in α-helices cross-linked by disulfide bonds) determines wool's elasticity and texture.

**3. Tertiary Structure (3°):**
The overall 3-D folding of a **single** polypeptide chain into a compact globule. Stabilized by:
- Hydrophobic interactions (R-group burial)
- Hydrogen bonds (side chains)
- Ionic (salt) bridges between charged residues
- **Disulfide bridges (—S—S—)** between two cysteines
- Van der Waals forces
*Food-industry example:* **Myoglobin** — a single-chain globular protein whose tertiary fold creates a hydrophobic pocket housing the heme group; unfolding (denaturation) turns red meat brown on cooking.

**4. Quaternary Structure (4°):**
The non-covalent association of **two or more folded polypeptide subunits** into a functional oligomer. Subunits may be identical (homomeric) or different (heteromeric).
*Food-industry example:* **Hemoglobin** — four subunits (2α + 2β) each binding one heme; the famous "T to R" allosteric transition. Also, **immunoglobulins** (4 chains — 2 heavy + 2 light).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common 2-mark loss is the failure to mention the **type of bond** stabilizing each level. Memorize: **1° → covalent peptide; 2° → H-bonds; 3° → all four; 4° → non-covalent subunit interfaces.**

---

### **Question B-B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Answer any ONE of the following:**

**(a)** What is **protein denaturation**? Discuss the various **physical and chemical agents** causing it. Explain the consequences of denaturation in food processing with **at least four examples**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **functional properties of proteins** in food systems. Explain how these properties are exploited in the manufacture of (i) bread, (ii) mayonnaise, and (iii) paneer. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Answer (a) — Protein Denaturation:**

**Definition:**
Denaturation is the disruption of the native secondary, tertiary, and/or quaternary structure of a protein **by physical or chemical means, without breaking the peptide (covalent) backbone**, resulting in loss of biological activity and changes in physical properties (solubility, viscosity, optical density).

### **Agents of Denaturation:**

**Physical agents:**
1. **Heat** — most common; increases kinetic energy, disrupts H-bonds and hydrophobic interactions.
2. **High pressure** — disrupts hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions.
3. **Mechanical shear** — whipping, homogenization, high-speed mixing.
4. **Freezing** — ice crystals mechanically disrupt structure; freeze-concentration of solutes causes pH/salt shifts.
5. **Radiation** — UV and ionizing radiation break H-bonds and form radicals.
6. **Ultrasonication** — cavitation-induced shear and free radicals.

**Chemical agents:**
1. **pH extremes (acid/alkali)** — alters ionizable groups, disrupts salt bridges.
2. **Organic solvents** (ethanol, acetone) — disrupt hydrophobic interactions.
3. **Heavy metals** (Hg²⁺, Pb²⁺) — bind to —SH and —S–S– groups.
4. **Urea and guanidine HCl** — disrupt H-bonds.
5. **Detergents (SDS)** — bind to hydrophobic regions, unfold the chain.
6. **Reducing agents (β-mercaptoethanol, DTT)** — cleave disulfide bonds.

### **Consequences in Food Processing (with examples):**

| Example | Process | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Boiled egg** | Heat (70–80 °C) | Ovalbumin and conalbumin denature and coagulate; albumen turns from clear to opaque white. |
| **2. Curdled milk (paneer)** | Acid (lemon juice / lactic acid) | Casein micelles reach pI (~4.6), lose charge, aggregate into curd; whey is expelled. |
| **3. Yogurt / Cheese** | Fermentation by lactic cultures | Acid produced lowers pH → casein denatures and precipitates; texture set. |
| **4. Whipped cream / Meringue** | Mechanical shearing | Air incorporation and partial denaturation at the air-water interface → stable foam. |
| **5. Baked bread** | Heat | Gluten denatures and cross-links; starch gelatinizes; crust forms via Maillard. |
| **6. UHT milk** | Ultra-high temperature | Whey protein denaturation; slight cooked flavor. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse **denaturation** with **hydrolysis**. Denaturation preserves peptide bonds (primary structure intact); hydrolysis breaks them (producing peptides/free amino acids). Also, denaturation is *not always* undesirable — it is essential for making cheese, yogurt, tofu, bread, and meringue.

---

### **Model Answer (b) — Functional Properties of Proteins:**

**Definition:** Functional properties are the *physico-chemical* characteristics of proteins that determine their behavior in food systems during processing, storage, and consumption (texture, stability, appearance, etc.).

**Key Functional Properties:**
1. **Hydration** — water absorption / WHC (driven by polar and charged residues)
2. **Solubility** — pH-dependent; minimum at pI
3. **Viscosity** — thickening of liquids
4. **Gelation** — formation of 3-D networks holding water
5. **Emulsification** — stabilization of oil-in-water dispersions
6. **Foaming** — formation and stabilization of air-in-water dispersions
7. **Dough formation / Elasticity** — viscoelastic networks
8. **Coagulation / Curdling** — pH or enzyme-induced aggregation

### **Application in Three Foods:**

**(i) Bread Making — Dough Formation & Elasticity:**
- The two principal wheat storage proteins — **gliadin (prolamine)** and **glutenin (glutelin)** — combine with water during mixing to form **gluten**, a viscoelastic network.
- **Gliadin** contributes *extensibility* (flow); **glutenin** contributes *elasticity* (spring-back).
- **Disulfide bonds (—S—S—)** between glutenin chains cross-link the network, trapping CO₂ produced by yeast.
- On baking, **gluten denatures and sets**, locking in the porous crumb structure.

**(ii) Mayonnaise — Emulsification:**
- Mayonnaise is an oil-in-water emulsion (~70–80% oil, ~10–15% water, ~5–8% egg yolk).
- **Egg yolk proteins** (especially low-density lipoproteins and livetins) act as **emulsifiers** — they are *amphiphilic*, with hydrophobic regions anchoring the oil phase and hydrophilic regions projecting into water.
- During homogenization, proteins adsorb at the oil-water interface, lower interfacial tension, and form a stable film that prevents droplet coalescence.
- The end result: a thick, opaque, stable emulsion.

**(iii) Paneer — Coagulation (Acid-Induced Curdling):**
- Milk contains casein micelles stabilized by κ-casein and colloidal calcium phosphate.
- Adding citric acid (lemon juice) lowers pH toward the **isoelectric point of casein (pH ~4.6)**.
- At pI, casein micelles lose their net negative charge → no electrostatic repulsion → micelles **aggregate** into a curd.
- Heating (~80 °C) accelerates aggregation.
- The curd is filtered and pressed into **paneer**; the greenish-yellow liquid drained off is **whey** (containing whey proteins, lactose, minerals).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked for "examples," students list the property but forget the *molecular basis*. KTU examiners expect you to name the protein involved (e.g., glutenin + gliadin for gluten; κ-casein for casein micelle) and the *specific mechanism* (disulfide cross-links; pI precipitation; amphiphilic adsorption).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Proteins**

**1. Chemistry Basics**
- All proteins are polymers of **20 standard L-α-amino acids** linked by **peptide bonds**.
- General formula: H₂N–CHR–COOH; at neutral pH, amino acids exist as **zwitterions**.
- Protein contains ~**16% N**; conversion factor for crude protein = **6.25** (varies by food).

**2. Essential Amino Acids (9)**
- Phe, Val, Thr, Trp, Ile, Met, His, Leu, Lys
- **Limiting AAs:** Lysine in cereals, Methionine in legumes, both combinations give complete protein.

**3. Peptide Bond Properties**
- **Planar, partial double bond, trans configuration, rigid.**
- Formed by **condensation** (releases H₂O); cleaved by **hydrolysis** (requires H₂O).

**4. Four Levels of Structure**
- 1° = sequence (peptide bond)
- 2° = α-helix, β-sheet (H-bonds)
- 3° = 3-D fold (H-bonds, ionic, hydrophobic, **disulfide**)
- 4° = subunit assembly (non-covalent interfaces)
- **Denaturation** disrupts 2°, 3°, 4° — but **NOT 1°** (no peptide bond cleavage).

**5. Classification**
- Simple (albumin, globulin, prolamine, glutelin)
- Conjugated (glycoprotein, lipoprotein, phosphoprotein, metalloprotein)
- Derived (peptones, peptides)

**6. Functional Properties — Memorize the 8**
- Hydration, Solubility, Viscosity, Gelation, Emulsification, Foaming, Elasticity, Coagulation
- Each property is **exploited** in a real food (bread = elasticity; mayonnaise = emulsification; paneer = coagulation; meringue = foaming).

**7. Nutritional Quality Metrics**
- **Chemical Score** = (AA in test / AA in reference) × 100
- **BV** = N retained / N absorbed × 100
- **NPU** = BV × Digestibility
- **PER** = weight gain / protein intake (rat assay)
- **PDCAAS** = CS × Digestibility, **capped at 1.0**

**8. Isoelectric Point (pI)**
- pH at which net charge = 0; minimum solubility.
- Neutral AA: pI = (pKₐ₁ + pKₐ₂)/2
- Acidic AA: pI = (pKₐ₁ + pKₐR)/2
- Basic AA: pI = (pKₐ₂ + pKₐR)/2

**9. Maillard Reaction**
- **Reducing sugar + free amino group** (ε-NH₂ of **lysine**) → brown **melanoidins**.
- **Causes loss of bioavailable lysine** — major nutritional concern in heated milk, baked products, and roasted foods.
- Three stages: Schiff base → Amadori product → melanoidins.

**10. Examiner-Validated High-Yield Sentences**
- *"Denaturation does not break peptide bonds."*
- *"Lysine is the most limiting amino acid in cereals; methionine is limiting in legumes."*
- *"Proteins are amphoteric — they migrate toward cathode at pH < pI and toward anode at pH > pI."*
- *"At pI, protein has minimum solubility and maximum tendency to aggregate."*

> [!TIP]
> **Final Board Strategy:** In a 14-mark answer, dedicate ~**1 page each** to (i) definition, (ii) classification, (iii) structure/denaturation/functional, and (iv) food example. Always include at least one **numbered table** — KTU examiners reward visual organization in valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lipids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lipids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Lipids** are a chemically heterogeneous group of naturally occurring organic substances that are **soluble in non-polar organic solvents** (such as diethyl ether, chloroform, benzene, hexane, and acetone) but are **insoluble or only sparingly soluble in water**. They serve as concentrated energy reserves, structural components of biological membranes, carriers of fat-soluble vitamins, and precursors of several biologically active molecules such as prostaglandins and steroid hormones.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEFTT831)**, lipids are treated as a foundational macronutrient group distinct from carbohydrates and proteins because of their unique hydrophobic character and high caloric density (**approximately 9 kcal/g or 37 kJ/g**).

> [!NOTE]  
> **Historical Note for Examiners:** The classical Bloor (1943) definition emphasizes *solubility behavior* rather than chemical structure, which is why steroids, fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), terpenes, and even cholesterol are classified as lipids even though their structures differ markedly from triglycerides.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Body's Diesel Fuel"

Imagine your kitchen pantry containing two types of fuel for your body:

| Fuel Type | Source | Energy per kg | Burning Speed |
|-----------|--------|---------------|---------------|
| 🔥 **Petrol (Carbohydrates)** | Rice, bread, sugar | ~4 kcal/g | Burns fast, leaves ash (water + CO₂) |
| 🛢️ **Diesel (Lipids)** | Ghee, oil, butter | **~9 kcal/g** | Burns slow, dense, water-resistant |

**Lipids are the body's "diesel reserve"** — they pack **more than twice the energy** of carbohydrates per gram, they do not dissolve in water (the body's main solvent), and they are stored compactly in adipose tissue the way diesel is stored in fuel tanks.

> [!TIP]  
> **Real-world analogy for FFA vs Triglyceride:** Think of a triglyceride as a *tripod lamp* (glycerol = the central pole; three fatty acids = the three legs). Free Fatty Acids (FFAs) are like *loose table legs* lying on the floor — chemically useful but no longer serving their structural role. The process of *lipolysis* (enzymatic breakdown) chops off the legs, releasing FFAs.

## 1.3 Functional Roles of Lipids in Food Systems

1. **Energy source** — highest caloric density among macronutrients (**9 kcal/g**).
2. **Structural role** — phospholipids form bilayer membranes of every cell.
3. **Thermal insulation** — subcutaneous fat layer in animals.
4. **Organ protection** — cushioning around vital organs.
5. **Carrier of fat-soluble vitamins** — A, D, E, K require lipid media for absorption.
6. **Sensory quality** — texture, mouthfeel, flavor, and aroma (e.g., buttery notes from diacetyl, meaty notes from lipid-Maillard interactions).
7. **Satiety** — slow gastric emptying enhances the feeling of fullness.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Classification of Lipids

Lipids are classified into **three principal groups** based on their chemical constitution and hydrolysis behavior:

```
I. SIMPLE LIPIDS
   ├── Acylglycerols (Fats & Oils) — Glycerol + Fatty Acids
   │       ├── Monoacylglycerols (Monoglycerides)
   │       ├── Diacylglycerols (Diglycerides)
   │       └── Triacylglycerols (Triglycerides) ← MOST ABUNDANT
   └── Waxes — Long-chain alcohol + Long-chain fatty acid

II. COMPOUND (CONJUGATED) LIPIDS
   ├── Phospholipids — contain phosphate group
   │       ├── Glycerophospholipids (Lecithin, Cephalin)
   │       └── Sphingophospholipids (Sphingomyelin)
   ├── Glycolipids (Cerebrosides, Gangliosides)
   └── Lipoproteins (Chylomicrons, VLDL, LDL, HDL)

III. DERIVED LIPIDS
    ├── Fatty Acids (saturated, unsaturated)
    ├── Sterols (Cholesterol, Ergosterol)
    ├── Terpenes & Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)
    └── Hydrolysis products (Mono-, Di-glycerides)
```

## 2.2 Fatty Acids — The Building Blocks

A **fatty acid** is a carboxylic acid with a long aliphatic (typically 4–28 carbons) chain. The general formula is:

$$
\text{CH}_3-(\text{CH}_2)_n-\text{COOH}
$$

where $n$ usually ranges from 2 to 24.

### 2.2.1 Saturated vs Unsaturated — The "Why" and "How"

| Property | Saturated FA | Unsaturated FA |
|----------|--------------|----------------|
| C=C double bonds | **None** | One (MUFA) or more (PUFA) |
| Molecular geometry | Linear, can pack tightly | **Bent at each C=C** (cis-form) |
| Physical state at 25 °C | **Solid** (e.g., butter, ghee) | **Liquid** (e.g., sunflower oil) |
| Source | Animal fats, coconut, palm | Most plant oils, fish oils |
| Hydrogen count | Maximum possible | Lower (H-deficient at C=C) |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why does unsaturation lower melting point?**  
> Each *cis* double bond introduces a **~30° kink** in the hydrocarbon chain. These kinks prevent close molecular packing, weakening van der Waals forces. Less energy (heat) is required to disrupt these weaker interactions, hence the melting point drops.

### 2.2.2 Essential Fatty Acids (EFA) — Must Remember

The human body **cannot biosynthesize** linoleic acid (omega-6) and α-linolenic acid (omega-3), so they must come from the diet.

| Fatty Acid | Symbol | ω-Family | Importance |
|------------|--------|----------|------------|
| Linoleic acid | **C18:2 (n-6)** | ω-6 | Precursor of arachidonic acid |
| α-Linolenic acid | **C18:3 (n-3)** | ω-3 | Precursor of EPA & DHA |

## 2.3 Triglycerides (Triacylglycerols, TAG)

A triglyceride is an **ester of glycerol (a trihydric alcohol) with three fatty acid molecules**. General structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_2-\text{O-CO-R}_1 \\
\text{CH}-\text{O-CO-R}_2 \\
\text{CH}_2-\text{O-CO-R}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$ represent the alkyl chains of the three fatty acids. The bond —O—CO— is an **ester linkage**.

> [!TIP]  
> **KTU Board Favorite:** If the three fatty acids are identical → *simple triglyceride* (e.g., tripalmitin). If different → *mixed triglyceride* (e.g., 1-palmito-2-oleo-3-stearin). Naturally occurring fats are virtually always mixed triglycerides.

## 2.4 Phospholipids — The "Amphipathic Workhorses"

Phospholipids contain a **phosphate group** in addition to fatty acids and glycerol. They are *amphipathic* — having both a hydrophilic head (phosphate + polar group) and hydrophobic tails (fatty acids).

The most common example is **Phosphatidylcholine (Lecithin)**, found abundantly in egg yolk and soybeans — used widely as a natural emulsifier in food (e.g., chocolate, mayonnaise).

## 2.5 Sterols

Rigid, polycyclic structures. **Cholesterol** is the most biologically significant sterol in animal fats. Plant equivalents are called **phytosterols** (β-sitosterol, stigmasterol). Sterols lack fatty acid chains but are classified as lipids because of their solubility behavior.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Quality Indices of Fats & Oils

The following analytical parameters are **mandatory** for any KTU examination question on lipid chemistry. Memorize these formulas — they are tested almost every semester.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Convention used in all formulas below:**  
> *V* = volume of titrant (mL) ; *N* = normality of titrant ; *W* = weight of sample (g) ; *B* = blank reading (mL) ; *56.1* = equivalent weight of **KOH** ; *1000* = conversion factor from mol to mmol.

| # | Quality Index | Formula | Standard Used | What It Indicates |
|---|---------------|---------|---------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Acid Value (AV)** | $\text{AV} = \dfrac{(V - B) \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | alcoholic KOH | Free fatty acid content (hydrolytic rancidity) |
| 2 | **Saponification Value (SV)** | $\text{SV} = \dfrac{(V - B) \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | alcoholic KOH (excess) | Average molecular weight; long-chain ⇒ low SV |
| 3 | **Iodine Value (IV)** | $\text{IV} = \dfrac{(B - V) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$ | Wijs solution (ICl) | Degree of unsaturation (C=C bonds) |
| 4 | **Peroxide Value (PV)** | $\text{PV (meq/kg)} = \dfrac{(V - B) \times N \times 1000}{W}$ | Sodium thiosulfate | Oxidative rancidity; primary oxidation products |
| 5 | **Reichert-Meissl Value (RMV)** | distillation + titration | volatile fatty acids (C4–C6) | Detection of butter adulteration |
| 6 | **Polenske Value (PV)** | same distillate, after RMV | water-insoluble volatile FA | Detects coconut / palm kernel oil |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Critical sign convention:** For Acid Value and Saponification Value, the formula uses **V − B** (sample − blank). For Iodine Value, it is **B − V** because iodine is *consumed* by the sample, so the blank titre is **greater** than the sample titre. Reversing this is the **#1 mark-killer** in KTU lipid numerical problems.

### 2.6.1 Engineering Interpretation of These Indices

| Index | High Value Means | Low Value Means |
|-------|------------------|------------------|
| **Iodine Value** | Highly unsaturated oil (good for paints/drying oils) | Saturated / hydrogenated fat (good for margarine) |
| **Saponification Value** | Short-chain fatty acids (e.g., coconut oil ~ 250) | Long-chain fatty acids (e.g., rapeseed oil ~ 175) |
| **Acid Value** | Hydrolytic spoilage, poor storage | Fresh, well-stored oil |
| **Peroxide Value** | Oxidative deterioration, off-flavor risk | Fresh oil, intact antioxidants |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Why Saponification Value Inversely Relates to Molecular Weight

### 3.1.1 The Underlying Chemistry

When a triglyceride reacts with **excess KOH**, the ester bonds are cleaved, releasing glycerol and the potassium salts of fatty acids (soaps):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OOCR})_3 \; + \; 3\,\text{KOH} &\longrightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 \; + \; 3\,\text{RCOOK} \\
\text{(Triglyceride)} \quad \quad \; \; & \quad \quad \text{(Glycerol)} \quad \; \text{(Soap)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step explanation:** One mole of triglyceride consumes exactly **three moles of KOH**. The KOH *consumed* is what we measure by back-titration with standard HCl.

### 3.1.2 From Moles to Saponification Value

Let $M_T$ = molecular weight of the triglyceride, and 1 g of fat contains $\dfrac{1}{M_T}$ moles of triglyceride.

Moles of KOH consumed per gram of fat:

$$
\text{Moles KOH / g} = \dfrac{1}{M_T} \times 3 = \dfrac{3}{M_T}
$$

Mass of KOH consumed per gram of fat:

$$
\text{g KOH / g fat} = \dfrac{3}{M_T} \times 56.1
$$

By definition, Saponification Value = **mg KOH required to saponify 1 g of fat**:

$$
\text{SV} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{M_T} = \dfrac{168300}{M_T}
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Standard Style

> **Q:** Calculate the saponification value of pure tripalmitin. (M.W. of tripalmitin = 807.3 g/mol)

**Step 1 — Identify equivalents:**
Tripalmitin has **3 ester linkages** ⇒ each mole consumes 3 mol KOH.

**Step 2 — Moles in 1 g:**

$$
n_{\text{tripalmitin}} = \dfrac{1 \text{ g}}{807.3 \text{ g/mol}} = 1.2387 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Moles of KOH consumed:**

$$
n_{\text{KOH}} = 3 \times 1.2387 \times 10^{-3} = 3.7161 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of KOH in mg:**

$$
m_{\text{KOH}} = 3.7161 \times 10^{-3} \times 56.1 \times 1000 = 208.47 \text{ mg}
$$

**Step 5 — Saponification Value:**

$$
\boxed{\text{SV} = 208.5 \text{ mg KOH / g fat}}
$$

> **Verification using derived formula:**
>
> $$\text{SV} = \dfrac{168300}{807.3} \approx 208.5 \;\checkmark$$

The **literature value** for tripalmitin is 208–210, confirming our derivation. ✓

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Acid Value

> **Q:** 5.0 g of an oil sample was titrated against 0.1 N alcoholic KOH. The titre value was 12.5 mL. Blank reading = 0.5 mL. Calculate the Acid Value.

**Step 1 — Apply formula:**

$$
\text{AV} = \dfrac{(V - B) \times N \times 56.1}{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{AV} = \dfrac{(12.5 - 0.5) \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{5.0}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$
12.0 \times 0.1 \times 56.1 = 67.32
$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:**

$$
\boxed{\text{AV} = 13.46 \text{ mg KOH / g oil}}
$$

> [!TIP]  
> **Quality grade (BIS standards for edible oils):** AV should be < **4 mg KOH/g** for refined oils and < **0.6 mg KOH/g** for cold-pressed / virgin oils.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Iodine Value

> **Q:** 0.30 g of an oil was treated with Wijs solution. Blank titre = 45.2 mL of 0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃. Sample titre = 28.6 mL. Calculate IV.

**Step 1 — Identify the *B − V* direction:**

Unsaturation consumes ICl ⇒ blank consumes *more* thiosulfate. So:

$$
\text{IV} = \dfrac{(B - V) \times N \times 12.69}{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{IV} = \dfrac{(45.2 - 28.6) \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.30}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$
16.6 \times 0.1 \times 12.69 = 21.0654
$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:**

$$
\boxed{\text{IV} = 70.22 \text{ g I}_2 / 100\text{g oil}}
$$

**Classification (KTU standard):**

| IV Range | Classification | Examples |
|----------|---------------|----------|
| < 90 | Non-drying | Coconut, palm |
| 90 – 130 | Semi-drying | Cottonseed, sesame |
| > 130 | Drying | Linseed, soyabean |

## 3.4 Derivation of Iodine Value — Why 12.69?

Iodine Value is defined as **grams of I₂ absorbed by 100 g of fat**. The thiosulfate titration involves:

$$
\text{I}_2 + 2\,\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6
$$

1 mol thiosulfate ≡ 0.5 mol I₂ ≡ 0.5 mol I₂ ≡ **127 g I₂** (since atomic mass of I = 127).

For 1 mL of 1 N Na₂S₂O₃, the equivalent I₂ = $\dfrac{127}{1000} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$ mol... simplifying step-by-step:

$$
1 \text{ mL of 1 N Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \equiv 0.001 \text{ equivalent} \equiv 0.001 \times 127 \text{ g I}_2 \equiv 0.127 \text{ g I}_2
$$

But IV is expressed per 100 g sample, so multiplying by 100/W:

$$
\boxed{\text{IV} = \dfrac{(B-V) \times N \times 12.7 \times 100}{W \times 1000 / 1000} = \dfrac{(B-V) \times N \times 12.69}{W}}
$$

(12.69 vs 12.7 reflects atomic weight standard adopted: I = 126.9)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Diagram — Lipids

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Lipids] --> B[Simple Lipids]
    A --> C[Compound Lipids]
    A --> D[Derived Lipids]

    B --> B1[Triacylglycerols Fats and Oils]
    B --> B2[Waxes]

    C --> C1[Phospholipids]
    C --> C2[Glycolipids]
    C --> C3[Lipoproteins]

    C1 --> C1a[Glycerophospholipids Lecithin]
    C1 --> C1b[Sphingolipids Sphingomyelin]

    D --> D1[Fatty Acids]
    D --> D2[Sterols Cholesterol]
    D --> D3[Fat Soluble Vitamins A D E K]

    D1 --> D1a[Saturated FA]
    D1 --> D1b[Unsaturated FA]

    D1b --> D1b1[MUFA Oleic]
    D1b --> D1b2[PUFA Linoleic]
    D1b --> D1b3[Essential FA]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of Lipid Reactions

```mermaid
graph LR
    TG[Triacylglycerol] -->|Hydrolysis Lipase or Acid or Alkali| FFAG[Free Fatty Acids + Glycerol]
    TG -->|Hydrogenation Ni catalyst 180C| HYG[Hydrogenated Fat Margarine]
    TG -->|Oxidation Atmospheric O2| PRD[Peroxides Primary]
    PRD -->|Further oxidation| SEC[Secondary products Aldehydes Ketones]
    SEC -->|Off flavors| RANC[Rancidity]

    FFAG -->|With alkali KOH NaOH| SOAP[Soap Saponification]
    HYG -->|Used in baking| SF[Shortening Function]
    RANC -->|Antioxidant BHA BHT| STAB[Stabilized Oil]

    FFAG[Free Fatty Acids] -->|Titration with KOH| ACIDV[Acid Value Measured]
    TG -->|With excess KOH| SAPV[Saponification Value Measured]
    TG -->|With Wijs solution ICl| IV[Iodine Value Measured]
```

## 4.3 Fatty Acid Structure Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    FA[Fatty Acid CH3 CH2 n COOH] --> SFA[Saturated FA No double bond]
    FA --> USFA[Unsaturated FA Contains C=C]

    SFA --> SFA1[Short Chain C4 to C10 Butyric Capric]
    SFA --> SFA2[Medium Chain C12 to C14 Lauric Myristic]
    SFA --> SFA3[Long Chain C16 C18 Palmitic Stearic]

    USFA --> MUFA[MUFA One double bond Oleic C18:1]
    USFA --> PUFA[PUFA Multiple double bonds Linoleic C18:2 Linolenic C18:3]

    PUFA --> EFA1[Linoleic Acid Omega 6 Essential]
    PUFA --> EFA2[Alpha Linolenic Acid Omega 3 Essential]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Phospholipid

```mermaid
graph TD
    PL[Phospholipid Molecule] --> HD[Hydrophilic Head Polar]
    PL --> TL[Hhydrophobic Tails Non polar]

    HD --> PG[Phosphate Group]
    HD --> PR[Polar Alcohol Choline Ethanolamine Serine Inositol]

    TL --> FA1[Fatty Acid 1 Saturated]
    TL --> FA2[Fatty Acid 2 Unsaturated]

    PL --> MEM[Forms Bilayer Membrane]
    PL --> EMU[Acts as Emulsifier Lecithin]
```
