# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define lipids. Why are they classified on the basis of solubility rather than chemical structure?**

**Model Answer:**

Lipids are a heterogeneous group of organic substances that are **soluble in non-polar solvents** (such as ether, chloroform, benzene) but **insoluble in water**. They include fats, oils, waxes, phospholipids, sterols, and fat-soluble vitamins.

They are classified on the basis of **solubility rather than structure** because their chemical structures vary widely — ranging from simple triglycerides to complex steroids and terpenes. What unifies them is their *common solubility behavior* in non-polar solvents. This definition was given by **Bloor (1943)** and is universally accepted in food science.

> **Mark Distribution (3 M):** Definition (1M) + Examples list (1M) + Reason for solubility-based classification (1M).

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between fats and oils with suitable examples. Mention the role of unsaturation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Fats | Oils |
|----------|------|------|
| Physical state at room temperature (25 °C) | **Solid** | **Liquid** |
| Degree of unsaturation | Low | High |
| Iodine Value | Low (e.g., 30–50) | High (e.g., 80–200) |
| Source | Mostly animal (ghee, lard, butter) | Mostly plant (sunflower, groundnut, mustard) |
| Hydrogenation | Already mostly saturated | Can be hydrogenated to make vanaspati / margarine |

**Role of unsaturation:** Unsaturated fatty acids contain one or more **cis double bonds** that introduce kinks in the chain, preventing close molecular packing. This **lowers the melting point**, keeping oils liquid at room temperature. The greater the unsaturation, the more liquid the lipid.

> **Mark Distribution (3 M):** Tabular differences (2M) + Role of unsaturation with cis-kink concept (1M).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each module in the End Semester Exam carries **14 marks**, distributed as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both parts must be answered. Below, we provide TWO complete alternative questions, allowing students to choose.

---

### ⭐ **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Classify lipids in detail with suitable examples. Add a note on the biological significance of phospholipids.**

**Model Answer:**

**I. Classification of Lipids:**

**1. Simple Lipids** — Esters of fatty acids with alcohols.
   - **Acylglycerols:** Esters of glycerol with fatty acids. Include mono-, di-, and **triacylglycerols (triglycerides)**. Triglycerides are the principal form of lipid storage in plants and animals.
     - Examples: Tripalmitin, Triolein.
   - **Waxes:** Esters of long-chain fatty acids with long-chain monohydric alcohols.
     - Examples: Beeswax, Carnauba wax, Lanolin (wool wax).

**2. Compound (Conjugated) Lipids** — Simple lipids + additional groups.
   - **Phospholipids:** Contain phosphate + nitrogenous base. Amphipathic.
     - Examples: Lecithin (phosphatidylcholine), Cephalin (phosphatidylethanolamine), Sphingomyelin.
   - **Glycolipids:** Contain carbohydrate moiety.
     - Examples: Cerebrosides, Gangliosides.
   - **Lipoproteins:** Lipid + protein (apolipoprotein).
     - Examples: Chylomicrons, VLDL, LDL, HDL.

**3. Derived Lipids** — Hydrolysis products of simple/compound lipids.
   - Fatty acids (saturated & unsaturated).
   - Sterols (cholesterol, ergosterol).
   - Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
   - Mono- and diglycerides.

**II. Biological Significance of Phospholipids:**

1. **Membrane structure:** Form the **phospholipid bilayer** of every biological membrane, providing a selectively permeable barrier.
2. **Amphipathic nature:** Hydrophilic head + hydrophobic tail enables lipid–water interfaces (e.g., micelle, liposome formation).
3. **Emulsification in food:** Lecithin (from egg yolk, soy) is a natural emulsifier used in mayonnaise, chocolate, and bakery shortenings.
4. **Cell signaling:** Phosphatidylinositol acts as a precursor for second messengers (IP₃, DAG).
5. **Lung surfactant:** Dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine prevents alveolar collapse.
6. **Myelin sheath:** Sphingomyelin provides electrical insulation in nerves.

> **Mark Distribution (7M):** Three classes with 2 examples each (4M) + Significance of phospholipids (3M with 2 points explained).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain saponification and hydrogenation of oils. Mention the industrial relevance of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**I. Saponification:**

Saponification is the **alkaline hydrolysis of triglycerides** to yield glycerol and salts of fatty acids (soaps).

**Reaction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OOCR})_3 \; + \; 3\,\text{KOH} &\longrightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 \; + \; 3\,\text{RCOOK} \\
(\text{Triglyceride}) \quad \quad \quad & \quad \quad (\text{Glycerol}) \quad \; (\text{Soap — potassium salt})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Process conditions:**  
- Reactant: Oil/fat + NaOH (for hard soap, e.g., bathing soap) or KOH (for soft soap, e.g., shaving cream).  
- Temperature: ~80–100 °C with continuous stirring.  
- By-product: Glycerol is recovered, purified, and used in cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, explosives (nitroglycerin), and food.

**Industrial relevance:**  
- Soap manufacturing (toilet soaps, laundry soaps).  
- Glycerol recovery — used in food, pharma, and chemical industries.  
- Quality control of oils via **Saponification Value** measurement.

---

**II. Hydrogenation:**

Hydrogenation is the **addition of hydrogen across C=C double bonds** of unsaturated fatty acids in the presence of a metal catalyst (Ni, Pt, Pd).

**Reaction (simplified):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)_7\text{CH}=\text{CH}(\text{CH}_2)_7\text{COOH} \; + \; \text{H}_2 &\xrightarrow{\text{Ni, 180 °C, 5 atm}} \text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)_{16}\text{COOH} \\
(\text{Oleic acid}) \quad \quad \quad \quad & \quad \quad (\text{Stearic acid})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Process conditions:**  
- Catalyst: finely divided **nickel** (0.05–0.2 % by weight).  
- Temperature: 140–225 °C.  
- Pressure: 5–50 psi H₂.  
- Reactor: closed vessel with vigorous stirring for uniform H₂ contact.

**Industrial relevance:**  
- Production of **vanaspati** and **margarine** from liquid vegetable oils.  
- Production of **shortenings** for baking (raises melting point, improves texture and shelf life).  
- Improvement of **oxidative stability** (less unsaturation ⇒ less prone to rancidity).  
- Raw material for **soap, candle, and cosmetic industries**.

> **Mark Distribution (7M):** Saponification — reaction + process + 2 industrial uses (3.5M) + Hydrogenation — reaction + conditions + 2 industrial uses (3.5M).

---

### ⭐ **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is rancidity? Differentiate hydrolytic and oxidative rancidity. Explain with reactions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Rancidity is the **deterioration of fats and oils** resulting in the development of unpleasant odors, flavors, and toxic compounds, rendering the lipid unfit for consumption.

---

**1. Hydrolytic Rancidity:**

- **Cause:** Enzymatic (lipase) or microbial hydrolysis of triglycerides, releasing **free fatty acids (FFAs)**.
- **Especially affects:** Short-chain fatty acids — e.g., **butyric acid** (in butter) gives a *rancid, cheesy* odor; caproic and caprylic acids produce goaty / cheesy notes.
- **Common in:** Butter, ghee, coconut oil.
- **Indicator:** **Acid Value** increases with hydrolytic rancidity.

**Reaction:**

$$
\text{Triglyceride} \; + \; \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{lipase}} \text{Glycerol} \; + \; \text{Free Fatty Acids}
$$

---

**2. Oxidative Rancidity:**

- **Cause:** Atmospheric **oxygen** attacks the C=C double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids, forming **peroxides** (primary products), which decompose into **aldehydes, ketones, and hydrocarbons** (secondary products) responsible for off-flavors.
- **Mechanism:** Free-radical chain reaction — Initiation → Propagation → Termination.
- **Especially affects:** Polyunsaturated oils (linseed, soyabean, fish oils).
- **Indicators:** **Peroxide Value** (primary) and **TBA value / anisidine value** (secondary).

**Reaction (simplified chain):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RH} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{}^{\bullet}\text{OOH} \quad \text{(Initiation)} \\
\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{ROO}^{\bullet} \\
\text{ROO}^{\bullet} + \text{RH} &\rightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{R}^{\bullet} \quad \text{(Propagation)} \\
\text{ROO}^{\bullet} + \text{ROO}^{\bullet} &\rightarrow \text{Stable products} \quad \text{(Termination)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where **ROOH = hydroperoxides** (primary oxidation products).

---

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Hydrolytic Rancidity | Oxidative Rancidity |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Cause | Water + lipase | Atmospheric O₂ |
| Main products | Free fatty acids | Peroxides → aldehydes/ketones |
| Affects | All fats/oils | Polyunsaturated oils |
| Indicator | Acid Value | Peroxide Value |
| Prevention | Reduce water activity, inactivate lipases | Use antioxidants (BHA, BHT), nitrogen flushing |

> **Mark Distribution (7M):** Definition (1M) + Hydrolytic — cause, reaction, indicator (2M) + Oxidative — mechanism, reaction, indicator (3M) + Comparison (1M).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Define Acid Value, Saponification Value, and Iodine Value. A sample of oil (4.5 g) gave saponification value 195. Calculate the average molecular weight of the fatty acids present.**

**Model Answer:**

**I. Definitions:**

- **Acid Value (AV):** The number of **mg of KOH** required to neutralize the **free fatty acids** present in **1 g** of fat/oil.
- **Saponification Value (SV):** The number of **mg of KOH** required to completely saponify **1 g** of fat/oil.
- **Iodine Value (IV):** The number of **grams of iodine (I₂)** absorbed by **100 g** of fat/oil.

---

**II. Calculation of Average Molecular Weight from SV:**

**Step 1 — Recall the relationship:**

We derived earlier (Section 3.1) that for a triglyceride of molecular weight $M_T$:

$$
\text{SV} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{M_T}
$$

The triglyceride contains **three fatty acid molecules**. Average molecular weight of one fatty acid:

$$
M_{\text{FA}} = \dfrac{M_T}{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for M_T using given SV:**

$$
195 = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{M_T}
$$

$$
M_T = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{195} = \dfrac{168300}{195}
$$

$$
M_T = 863.08 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Average molecular weight of one fatty acid:**

$$
M_{\text{FA}} = \dfrac{863.08}{3} \approx 287.7 \text{ g/mol}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{\text{FA}} \approx 287.7 \text{ g/mol}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This value falls in the range typical of long-chain fatty acids (stearic acid = 284.5; oleic acid = 282.5; arachidic acid = 312.5), confirming the oil is a long-chain triglyceride source, e.g., groundnut or mustard oil.

> **Mark Distribution (7M):** Three definitions (3M) + Calculation with formula (3M) + Final answer with interpretation (1M).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]  
> **Top 5 ways KTU students lose marks in Lipid questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Iodine Value formula** — Using (V − B) instead of (B − V). Always confirm: blank titre is *larger* because the sample consumes iodine.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 1000** in Peroxide Value — The standard expression is in **milliequivalents of O₂ per kg**, not per gram.
>
> 3. **Confusing RMV with Polenske Value** — RMV detects *water-soluble* volatile fatty acids (butter adulteration); Polenske detects *water-insoluble* volatile fatty acids (coconut/palm kernel oil adulteration). The reagent sequence is critical.
>
> 4. **Writing *cis* and *trans* incorrectly** — *cis* C=C keeps the molecule bent and liquid; *trans* C=C straightens it (as in *vanaspati*, formed during partial hydrogenation). Examiner deducts 1 mark for the wrong isomer naming.
>
> 5. **Not converting units** — IV is in g I₂/100 g sample (not per gram); SV is in mg KOH/g (not grams). Always state units explicitly in the final answer.

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Lipids" (Module 1)**

- ☐ **Definition:** Bloor's solubility-based definition (soluble in non-polar solvents, insoluble in water).
- ☐ **Three classes:** Simple (TAG, waxes), Compound (phospholipids, glycolipids, lipoproteins), Derived (FFA, sterols, vitamins).
- ☐ **Triglyceride structure:** Glycerol + 3 fatty acids via **ester bonds** (-O-CO-).
- ☐ **Saturated FA examples:** Palmitic (C16:0), Stearic (C18:0).
- ☐ **Unsaturated FA examples:** Oleic (C18:1), Linoleic (C18:2 ω-6), Linolenic (C18:3 ω-3).
- ☐ **Essential Fatty Acids:** Linoleic (ω-6) & α-Linolenic (ω-3).
- ☐ **Energy density:** **9 kcal/g** (highest among macronutrients).
- ☐ **Acid Value** → hydrolytic rancidity indicator.
- ☐ **Saponification Value** → average molecular weight indicator.
- ☐ **Iodine Value** → unsaturation indicator (sign: B − V).
- ☐ **Peroxide Value** → oxidative rancidity indicator (in meq/kg).
- ☐ **Saponification reaction** = alkali hydrolysis → soap + glycerol.
- ☐ **Hydrogenation** = Ni catalyst, 180 °C → solid fat from oil.
- ☐ **Hydrolytic rancidity** = lipase + water → FFA (Acid Value ↑).
- ☐ **Oxidative rancidity** = O₂ + free radicals → peroxides → aldehydes/ketones (Peroxide Value ↑).
- ☐ **Antioxidants:** BHA, BHT, TBHQ, Vitamin E (tocopherol) — chain breakers.
- ☐ **cis vs trans:** cis = bent (natural oils); trans = straight (partial hydrogenation, harmful).
- ☐ **Memorize:** SV formula $= (V-B) \times N \times 56.1 / W$; IV formula $= (B-V) \times N \times 12.69 / W$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vitamins and Minerals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vitamins and Minerals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vitamins — Formal Definition

A **vitamin** is defined as an *organic micronutrient* that an organism requires in **trace quantities** for normal metabolic functioning but cannot synthesize in adequate amounts endogenously. Vitamins must therefore be obtained **exogenously** through the diet. They act primarily as **coenzymes**, **cofactors**, or **antioxidants**, enabling catalytic reactions essential for growth, energy metabolism, cellular repair, and immunity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Vitamins are *not* a source of calories (do not provide energy) and are *not* structural components of the body. They are **catalytic helpers** that unlock the energy stored in macronutrients (carbohydrates, fats, proteins).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Ignition Key" Metaphor

Imagine your car engine (the body's metabolism) has a full tank of petrol (food/calories) but no key to start it. The car simply will not move. Vitamins act as the **ignition key** — without them, even an abundant diet remains biologically "unusable." They are not fuel, but they *unlock* the fuel.

## 1.2 Minerals — Formal Definition

A **mineral** (dietary mineral / nutritional element) is an *inorganic chemical element* required by living organisms for proper physiological function. Unlike vitamins, minerals cannot be destroyed by heat, light, or cooking — they are **ash-forming** elements that remain after combustion of organic matter.

Minerals are classified by the quantity required by the human body:

- **Macrominerals (Major minerals)** — required in amounts **> 100 mg/day**
- **Microminerals (Trace minerals)** — required in amounts **< 100 mg/day**

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Bricks & Spark Plugs" Metaphor

If vitamins are the ignition keys, then minerals are the **spark plugs and steel frame** of the car. Some minerals (like **calcium** and **phosphorus**) are *structural bricks* building bones and teeth. Others (like **iron** and **zinc**) are *spark plugs* facilitating enzymatic reactions and oxygen transport.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction Table for Quick Recall**
>
> | Feature | Vitamins | Minerals |
> |---|---|---|
> | Chemical nature | Organic | Inorganic |
> | Energy yield | **0 kcal/g** | **0 kcal/g** |
> | Destroyed by heat | Yes (especially Vit C, B1) | No (heat stable) |
> | Destroyed by light | Some (B2, A) | No |
> | Daily requirement | Micrograms to milligrams | Milligrams to grams |
> | Source | Plants, animals, microbes | Soil → plants → animals |

## 1.3 The 13 Recognized Vitamins

There are exactly **13 essential vitamins** universally recognized in human nutrition. They split into two physicochemical families:

1. **Fat-Soluble Vitamins (FSV):** A, D, E, K — stored in adipose tissue and liver, no daily intake required strictly.
2. **Water-Soluble Vitamins (WSV):** Vitamin C + 8 B-complex vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B5, B6, B7, B9, B12) — minimal storage, daily intake essential.

## 1.4 Essential Minerals List (Major + Trace)

**Major minerals (7):** Calcium (Ca), Phosphorus (P), Potassium (K), Sulfur (S), Sodium (Na), Chlorine (Cl), Magnesium (Mg)

**Trace minerals (8 essential + several ultra-trace):** Iron (Fe), Zinc (Zn), Copper (Cu), Manganese (Mn), Iodine (I), Selenium (Se), Molybdenum (Mo), Fluoride (F), Chromium (Cr), Cobalt (Co)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Processing Significance:** Vitamins are the **most heat-labile** nutrients. Losses during processing (blanching, pasteurization, drying, extrusion) are a major concern in food technology. This topic is central to retention studies, **fortification policies**, and **shelf-life prediction**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic — Why Two Solubility Groups?

The fat-soluble vs water-soluble classification is **not arbitrary** — it dictates:
- **Absorption pathway** (lymphatic vs portal blood)
- **Storage capacity** (months vs hours)
- **Toxicity potential** (hypervitaminosis A/D possible; B/C toxicity rare)
- **Susceptibility to processing losses** (FSV protected in food matrix; WSV easily leached)

## 2.2 Vitamin A (Retinol / Provitamin Carotenoids)

### Chemistry
- **Active form:** Retinol (alcohol), Retinal (aldehyde), Retinoic acid
- **Provitamin:** β-carotene (plants) — cleaved by intestinal **β-carotene oxygenase 1 (BCO1)**

### Conversion Equation

$$1 \text{ RAE} = 1 \mu g \text{ retinol} = 2 \mu g \text{ supplemental }\beta\text{-carotene} = 12 \mu g \text{ dietary }\beta\text{-carotene} = 24 \mu g \text{ dietary }\alpha\text{-carotene}$$

> **RAE** = Retinol Activity Equivalent

### Physiological Roles
1. **Vision:** Component of *rhodopsin* (retinal + opsin) in rod cells of retina
2. **Epithelial differentiation:** Maintains mucous membranes
3. **Growth & development:** Critical for embryonic morphogenesis
4. **Immunity:** Enhances T-cell function
5. **Antioxidant (β-carotene):** Quenches **singlet oxygen** ($\ce{^1O2}$)

### Deficiency — Clinical Spectrum
- **Night blindness (Nyctalopia)** — earliest sign
- **Xerophthalmia** — dryness of conjunctiva
- **Bitot's spots** — foamy patches on sclera
- **Keratomalacia** — corneal softening → blindness
- **Follicular hyperkeratosis** — goose-flesh skin

### KTU Formula Sheet — Vitamin A Retinol Equivalents

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Retinol Activity Equivalent | $\text{RAE} = \mu g_{\text{retinol}} + \dfrac{\mu g_{\beta\text{-carotene}}}{12} + \dfrac{\mu g_{\text{other carotenoids}}}{24}$ | $\mu g$ |
| IU to RAE | $\text{RAE} = \dfrac{IU}{3.33}$ (for retinol) | $\mu g$ |
| Daily RDA (adult male) | **900 $\mu g$ RAE** | $\mu g$/day |
| Daily RDA (adult female) | **700 $\mu g$ RAE** | $\mu g$/day |

## 2.3 Vitamin D (Calciferol) — The Sunshine Vitamin

### Chemistry
Two physiologically relevant forms:
- **D3 (Cholecalciferol)** — animal source, synthesized in skin from 7-dehydrocholesterol
- **D2 (Ergocalciferol)** — fungal/plant source

### Photolysis Reaction in Skin

$$\text{7-dehydrocholesterol} \xrightarrow{\text{UV-B (290–315 nm)}} \text{previtamin D}_3 \rightarrow \text{vitamin D}_3$$

### Activation Pathway (Two Hydroxylations)

$$\text{D}_3 \xrightarrow[\text{liver}]{\text{25-hydroxylase (CYP2R1)}} 25(\text{OH})\text{D}_3 \xrightarrow[\text{kidney}]{\text{1α-hydroxylase (CYP27B1)}} 1,25(\text{OH})_2\text{D}_3$$

The final **1,25(OH)₂D₃ (calcitriol)** is the hormonally active form.

### Physiological Roles
- **Calcium homeostasis:** Increases intestinal Ca²⁺ absorption via **calbindin-D9k** protein
- **Bone mineralization:** Works with PTH to maintain serum Ca²⁺
- **Cell differentiation & immunity:** Modulates > 200 genes

### Deficiency
- **Rickets** (children) — bowed legs, rachitic rosary
- **Osteomalacia** (adults) — soft bones
- **Hypocalcemic tetany**

## 2.4 Vitamin E (Tocopherols & Tocotrienols)

### Chemistry
Eight natural forms: **α, β, γ, δ** × **(tocopherol/tocotrienol)**. **α-tocopherol** is biologically most active.

### Antioxidant Mechanism — Lipid Peroxidation Chain Breakage

$$\text{ROO}^{\bullet} + \alpha\text{-TOH} \rightarrow \text{ROOH} + \alpha\text{-TO}^{\bullet}$$

The tocopheroxyl radical is **resonance-stabilized** and is regenerated by ascorbate (Vitamin C):

$$\alpha\text{-TO}^{\bullet} + \text{AscH}^- \rightarrow \alpha\text{-TOH} + \text{Asc}^{\bullet -}$$

This is the basis of the **vitamin E-vitamin C antioxidant cycle** in biomembranes.

### Functions
1. **Chain-breaking antioxidant** in lipid bilayers
2. **Membrane stabilizer** via lipid-lipid packing
3. **Modulator of platelet aggregation** (anti-thrombotic)

### Deficiency
- **Hemolytic anemia** in preterm infants
- **Spinocerebellar ataxia** (rare)
- **Muscle weakness**

## 2.5 Vitamin K (Phylloquinone / Menaquinone)

### Forms
- **K1 (Phylloquinone)** — plant source, leafy greens
- **K2 (Menaquinone)** — bacterial synthesis (gut flora, fermented foods)
- **K3 (Menadione)** — synthetic, water-soluble

### γ-Carboxylation Reaction

$$\text{Glu-residue} + \text{O}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{Vitamin KH}_2 \xrightarrow{\gamma\text{-glutamyl carboxylase}} \text{Gla-residue} + \text{Vitamin K epoxide}$$

Gla (γ-carboxyglutamate) residues enable **Ca²⁺ binding** in clotting factors II, VII, IX, X, protein C, protein S, osteocalcin.

### Deficiency
- **Hemorrhagic disease of newborn (HDN)** — prophylactic IM injection at birth
- **Prolonged PT (prothrombin time)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Newborns have sterile guts and low vitamin K stores → mandatory prophylaxis at birth is a public-health milestone question.

## 2.6 Water-Soluble Vitamins — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Vitamin | Active Coenzyme Form | Key Function | Deficiency Disease |
|---|---|---|---|
| **B1 — Thiamine** | Thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP) | Decarboxylation of α-keto acids | **Beriberi** (wet/dry), Wernicke-Korsakoff |
| **B2 — Riboflavin** | FAD, FMN | Redox reactions | Cheilosis, angular stomatitis |
| **B3 — Niacin** | NAD⁺, NADP⁺ | H-transfer, ~ 400 enzymes | **Pellagra** (4 D's: Dermatitis, Diarrhea, Dementia, Death) |
| **B5 — Pantothenic acid** | Coenzyme A (CoA) | Acyl group transfer | Burning feet syndrome (rare) |
| **B6 — Pyridoxine** | PLP (pyridoxal-5'-phosphate) | Amino acid metabolism (transamination) | Microcytic anemia, peripheral neuropathy |
| **B7 — Biotin** | Biocytin (lysyl-biotin) | Carboxylation reactions | Dermatitis, alopecia |
| **B9 — Folate** | THF (tetrahydrofolate) | One-carbon transfer | **Megaloblastic anemia**, neural tube defects |
| **B12 — Cobalamin** | Methylcobalamin, Adenosylcobalamin | Methylation, odd-chain FA metabolism | **Pernicious anemia**, subacute combined degeneration |
| **C — Ascorbic acid** | Ascorbate (AscH⁻) | Hydroxylation, antioxidant | **Scurvy** (bleeding gums, poor wound healing) |

## 2.7 Minerals — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.7.1 Calcium (Ca) — Most Abundant Body Mineral

- **Body content:** ~ **1.0–1.2 kg** (99% in bones/teeth as hydroxyapatite)
- **Hormonal control:** PTH ↑ Ca²⁺, Calcitonin ↓ Ca²⁺, Vitamin D ↑ absorption

$$\text{Ca}^{2+}_{\text{intake}} \xrightarrow[\text{D}_3\text{-dependent}]{\text{Calbindin-D9k}} \text{Ca}^{2+}_{\text{blood}} \xrightarrow{\text{PTH/calcitonin}} \text{Bone} \rightleftarrows \text{Blood}$$

**Deficiency:** Osteoporosis, tetany, rickets
**RDA:** **1000 mg/day** adults

### 2.7.2 Iron (Fe) — Critical Trace Element

Two dietary forms:
- **Heme iron** (animal foods: hemoglobin, myoglobin) — bioavailability **15–35%**
- **Non-heme iron** (plant foods: Fe³⁺ salts) — bioavailability **2–20%**, enhanced by vitamin C

**Enhancers of non-heme Fe absorption:**
- Vitamin C (reduces Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺)
- Organic acids (citric, malic)

**Inhibitors:**
- **Phytates** (whole grains, legumes)
- **Polyphenols** (tea, coffee)
- **Calcium** (competes with iron)

$$\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{AscH}^- \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{Asc}^{\bullet -} + \text{H}^+$$

**Deficiency:** **Iron-deficiency anemia** — most common nutritional deficiency worldwide (WHO estimate: > 1.6 billion affected).

### 2.7.3 Iodine (I) — Thyroid Hormone Cofactor

$$\text{Tyrosine} \xrightarrow{\text{Iodination (TPO)}} \text{MIT} \rightarrow \text{DIT} \rightarrow \text{T}_3 / \text{T}_4$$

**Deficiency spectrum (Iodine Deficiency Disorders — IDD):**
- Goiter (enlarged thyroid)
- **Cretinism** (severe maternal deficiency → child)
- Hypothyroidism, myxedema

**Public-health intervention:** Universal **iodized salt** (KI or KIO₃ added at **7–15 ppm**)

### 2.7.4 Zinc (Zn)

- Cofactor for **> 300 enzymes** (e.g., alcohol dehydrogenase, carbonic anhydrase, superoxide dismutase)
- Required for **DNA polymerase** activity → cell division, immunity, wound healing
- **Deficiency:** Growth retardation, hypogonadism, dysgeusia, acrodermatitis enteropathica

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula & Conversion Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use these units precisely in KTU numerical/problem-solving questions.**

| Concept | Formula / Value | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Vitamin A | $1 \text{ IU} = 0.3 \mu g \text{ retinol}$ | $\mu g$ RAE |
| Vitamin D | $1 \text{ IU} = 0.025 \mu g \text{ D}_3$ | $\mu g$ |
| Vitamin E | $1 \text{ IU} = 0.67 \text{ mg }\alpha\text{-tocopherol}$ (natural) | mg α-TE |
| Niacin equivalence | $1 \text{ NE} = 1 \text{ mg niacin} = 60 \text{ mg tryptophan}$ | mg |
| Folate | DFE = $\mu g_{\text{food folate}} + 1.7 \times \mu g_{\text{fortified folic acid}}$ | $\mu g$ DFE |
| Energy from B-vitamins role | $\text{Energy released} = \Delta G'^{\circ}_{\text{ATP hydrolysis}} \approx \mathbf{-30.5 \text{ kJ/mol}}$ | kJ/mol |
| Daily Water requirement | **2.5–3.0 L** (including food moisture) | L/day |
| Na⁺/K⁺ ideal ratio | **< 1.0** (modern diets often 2–3) | molar |
| Iron absorption enhancer | $\%\text{abs} \uparrow \text{ with Vit C} = 100 \times \left(\dfrac{\text{abs}_{\text{+C}}}{\text{abs}_{\text{baseline}}} - 1\right)$ | % |
| Mineral bioavailability | $\text{BV} = \dfrac{\text{mineral absorbed}}{\text{mineral ingested}} \times 100$ | % |

## 2.9 Engineering Utility in Food Processing

| Processing Operation | Vitamin Impact | Mineral Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Blanching (80–100°C, 1–10 min)** | 10–60% loss of WSV (B1, C, folate) by leaching | Minimal — water-soluble minerals leach 5–30% |
| **Pasteurization (72°C, 15 s)** | Vitamin C loss 5–20% | Negligible |
| **Sterilization (121°C, 15 min)** | B1 loss up to **50%**; C loss up to **90%** | Negligible |
| **Dehydration** | Photosensitive vitamins (A, B2) degraded by light/heat | No change |
| **Extrusion (high T, high shear)** | Significant FSV loss (A, E) | Slight mineral loss |
| **Fermentation** | Microbial *synthesis* of B-vitamins (B2, B12) | Bioavailability ↑ (phytate hydrolysis) |
| **Fortification** | Adds vitamins externally to compensate losses | Adds minerals (Fe, I, Ca) to staples |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Loss calculations in blanching, effect of pH on vitamin stability, and the **phytate-mineral chelation** concept are recurring **14-mark** question areas.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Vitamin A — β-Carotene to Retinol Conversion (Stoichiometric Derivation)

### Problem Setup
A serving of cooked carrots provides **4.5 mg β-carotene**. Calculate the **retinol activity equivalent (RAE)** delivered to the body.

### Step 1 — Identify Conversion Factor

From the KTU Formula Sheet, dietary β-carotene has a bioconversion factor of **1:12** (i.e., 12 $\mu g$ β-carotene → 1 $\mu g$ retinol).

$$\text{RAE} = \dfrac{\mu g \text{ dietary }\beta\text{-carotene}}{12}$$

### Step 2 — Convert mg to $\mu g$

$$\begin{aligned}
4.5 \text{ mg} &= 4.5 \times 1000 \text{ }\mu g \\
&= 4500 \text{ }\mu g \text{ of }\beta\text{-carotene}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Conversion

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RAE} &= \dfrac{4500 \text{ }\mu g}{12} \\
&= 375 \text{ }\mu g \text{ retinol activity}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compare to RDA

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RDA (adult male)} &= 900 \text{ }\mu g \text{ RAE} \\
\text{\% RDA met} &= \dfrac{375}{900} \times 100 \\
&= 41.67 \%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conclusion:** One serving of cooked carrots (~ 100 g) delivers approximately **42% of the daily vitamin A requirement** — biologically significant, despite being a plant source.

---

## 3.2 Niacin Equivalent Derivation from Dietary Protein

### Problem Setup
A 70-kg adult consumes a diet providing **80 g of high-quality protein** per day. Tryptophan constitutes **1.1%** of this protein. How much niacin (in Niacin Equivalents, NE) is derived endogenously?

### Step 1 — Calculate Tryptophan Intake

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Tryptophan intake} &= 80 \text{ g} \times 0.011 \\
&= 0.88 \text{ g} \\
&= 880 \text{ mg}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Niacin Conversion Factor

The body converts **60 mg tryptophan → 1 mg niacin**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Niacin from tryptophan} &= \dfrac{880 \text{ mg}}{60} \\
&\approx 14.67 \text{ mg NE}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Add Preformed Niacin (Assume 5 mg from diet)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total NE} &= 14.67 + 5 \\
&= 19.67 \text{ mg NE/day}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compare to RDA

$$\text{RDA Niacin (adult male)} = 16 \text{ mg NE/day}$$

$$\text{\% RDA met} = \dfrac{19.67}{16} \times 100 \approx 122.9 \%$$

> **Conclusion:** Even with marginal niacin intake, tryptophan-rich protein prevents pellagra — this is why **maize-based** populations (low tryptophan, bound niacin) historically suffered pellagra.

---

## 3.3 Iron Absorption — Vitamin C Enhancement Derivation

### Problem Setup
A vegetarian meal provides **4 mg non-heme iron**. Without enhancers, absorption is **5%**. With **50 mg vitamin C**, absorption rises to **12%**. Calculate the **enhancement factor** and **absolute iron absorbed**.

### Step 1 — Baseline Absorption

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Fe absorbed (no Vit C)} &= 4 \text{ mg} \times 0.05 \\
&= 0.20 \text{ mg}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Enhanced Absorption

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Fe absorbed (+ Vit C)} &= 4 \text{ mg} \times 0.12 \\
&= 0.48 \text{ mg}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Enhancement Factor

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Enhancement factor} &= \dfrac{0.48}{0.20} \\
&= 2.4 \text{ (i.e., 140\% increase)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compare to Daily Requirement

$$\text{RDA Fe (adult male)} = 8 \text{ mg/day; females (reproductive)} = 18 \text{ mg/day}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{\% RDA met (female)} &= \dfrac{0.48}{18} \times 100 \approx 2.67\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Insight:** A single meal is insufficient — repeated meals and enhancers at every serving are essential. This is why **public-health programs** pair iron fortification with vitamin C awareness.

---

## 3.4 Vitamin C (Ascorbic Acid) Thermal Degradation Kinetics

### Problem Setup
Ascorbic acid in orange juice degrades following **first-order kinetics**:

$$C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$$

During pasteurization at **85°C**, the rate constant is **k = 0.092 min⁻¹**. Initial concentration **C₀ = 50 mg/100 mL**. Calculate retention after **5 minutes**.

### Step 1 — Substitute Values

$$\begin{aligned}
C_t &= 50 \times e^{-(0.092)(5)} \\
&= 50 \times e^{-0.46}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate Exponential

$$\begin{aligned}
e^{-0.46} &\approx 0.631 \\
C_t &= 50 \times 0.631 \\
&\approx 31.55 \text{ mg/100 mL}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate % Retention

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Retention} &= \dfrac{31.55}{50} \times 100 \\
&= 63.1\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conclusion:** A 5-minute pasteurization at 85°C retains **~ 63% of ascorbic acid** — a critical value for shelf-life prediction and nutrient labeling.

---

## 3.5 Iodized Salt Concentration Calculation

### Problem Setup
A salt iodization plant uses **potassium iodate (KIO₃)** at level **10 ppm iodine equivalent**. The molecular weight of KIO₃ is **214 g/mol**; Iodine atomic weight is **127 g/mol**.

**Calculate:** Mass of KIO₃ required per **metric ton (1000 kg)** of salt.

### Step 1 — Calculate Required Iodine

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Iodine needed} &= 1000 \text{ kg} \times 10 \text{ ppm} \\
&= 1000 \times 10 \text{ mg} \\
&= 10000 \text{ mg} \\
&= 10 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Stoichiometric Ratio

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{KIO}_3 \text{ required} &= 10 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{214}{127} \\
&= 10 \times 1.685 \\
&= 16.85 \text{ g of KIO}_3
\end{aligned}$$

> **Practical Answer:** Add **~ 16.85 g of KIO₃ per metric ton of salt** to achieve 10 ppm iodine fortification.

---

## 3.6 Energy from Macronutrient — Vitamin Coenzyme Coupling (B1 Role)

### Step-by-Step Energy Yield of Glucose (Illustrating TPP role)

$$\text{Glucose} \xrightarrow{\text{Glycolysis}} 2 \text{ Pyruvate} + 2 \text{ NADH} + 2 \text{ ATP}$$

**Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (requires TPP, lipoate, FAD, NAD⁺):**

$$\text{Pyruvate} + \text{CoA} + \text{NAD}^+ \xrightarrow{\text{TPP}} \text{Acetyl-CoA} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{NADH}$$

**TPP mechanism (step 1 — carbanion formation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{TPP} &\rightleftharpoons \text{TPP-ylide (carbanion)} \\
\text{TPP-ylide} + \text{Pyruvate} &\rightarrow \text{Hydroxyethyl-TPP} + \text{CO}_2
\end{aligned}$$

> **Insight:** Without B1 (TPP), pyruvate cannot be decarboxylated → pyruvate accumulates in blood → **beriberi**. This is why **polished rice** (B1-stripped) caused beriberi epidemics.

---

| Nutrient | Heat | Light | O₂ | pH Acid | pH Alkaline | Leaching (Water) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vitamin A | Moderate | **High** | High | Stable | Moderate | None |
| Vitamin D | Stable | Stable | Moderate | Stable | Moderate | None |
| Vitamin E | Stable | **High** | **High** | Stable | Stable | None |
| Vitamin K | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Stable | Moderate | None |
| Vitamin C | **High** | Moderate | **High** | Stable | **High** | **High** |
| Thiamine (B1) | **High** | Moderate | Moderate | Stable | **High** | **High** |
| Riboflavin (B2) | Moderate | **High** | Stable | Stable | Moderate | **High** |
| Folate | **High** | Moderate | Moderate | Stable | Moderate | **High** |
| B12 | Moderate | Stable | Stable | Stable | Moderate | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Drawing/writing such a stability matrix and explaining ONE example in depth is a frequently awarded 14-mark question.

## 3.8 Phytate-Mineral Chelation Derivation

### Theoretical Basis
Phytic acid (myo-inositol hexaphosphate, **IP6**) is the principal storage form of phosphorus in cereals, legumes, and oilseeds.

$$\text{IP6} + \text{M}^{n+} \rightarrow \text{IP6-M complex (insoluble)}$$

Where Mⁿ⁺ represents Ca²⁺, Zn²⁺, Fe²⁺, Mg²⁺.

### Mass-Action Calculation (Conceptual)
At pH 6.5, the binding constant for Zn²⁺ to IP6 is ~ 10⁸·⁵. This drives:

$$\text{Zn}^{2+}_{\text{diet}} \rightleftharpoons \text{Zn}^{2+}_{\text{free}} + \text{IP6}^{12-} \rightarrow \text{Zn-IP6 complex (excreted)}$$

### Practical Mitigation Strategies
1. **Soaking** (8–12 h) — activates endogenous **phytase**
2. **Germination (sprouting)** — phytase activity ↑↑↑
3. **Fermentation** — microbial phytase
4. **Thermal processing** (popping, roasting) — partial phytate hydrolysis

> **Engineering Insight:** These are the rationale behind traditional Indian food processing — **idli/dosa batter fermentation**, **sprouted moong**, **popped rice** — which improve mineral bioavailability.
