# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between fat-soluble and water-soluble vitamins. Give two examples of each.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Aspect | Fat-Soluble Vitamins | Water-Soluble Vitamins |
|---|---|---|
| **Solubility** | Soluble in fats & oils | Soluble in water |
| **Storage** | Stored in liver & adipose tissue (months) | Minimal storage (hours–days) |
| **Excess** | Can accumulate → toxicity (A, D) | Excreted in urine (rare toxicity) |
| **Daily need** | Not strictly required daily | Daily intake essential |
| **Examples** | **Vitamin A, D, E, K** | **Vitamin B-complex (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), Vitamin C** |

**Examples (1 mark):**
- **Fat-soluble:** Vitamin A, Vitamin D
- **Water-soluble:** Vitamin C, Vitamin B1 (Thiamine)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What are the major functions of calcium in the human body? Mention its deficiency disease.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Functions of Calcium (2 marks):**
1. **Structural:** Forms **hydroxyapatite** $[\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6(\text{OH})_2]$ crystals in bones and teeth — provides rigidity.
2. **Intracellular signaling:** Acts as a **second messenger** in muscle contraction, neurotransmitter release, hormone secretion.
3. **Blood coagulation:** Cofactor for clotting factors (IV).
4. **Enzyme cofactor:** Activates lipase, ATPase, etc.
5. **Nerve impulse transmission:** Maintains membrane potential.

**Deficiency disease (1 mark):**
- **Rickets** in children (soft, bowed bones)
- **Osteomalacia / Osteoporosis** in adults
- **Hypocalcemic tetany** (acute low Ca²⁺ → muscle spasms)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)
### *(Module Internal Choice — Choose ONE from A or B)*

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**"Vitamins are sensitive nutrients that undergo significant losses during food processing." Justify this statement with specific examples of vitamin losses during blanching, pasteurization, and dehydration. Discuss the strategies to minimize such losses in food industries. (14 Marks)**

#### Sub-part (a) — Vitamin Loss Mechanisms During Major Unit Operations (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**1. Blanching (80–100°C, 1–10 min):**
- **Mechanism:** Combination of *thermal degradation* AND *leaching into water/steam*
- **Vitamins most affected:** Water-soluble vitamins — Vitamin C (30–50% loss), B1 (20–30%), Folate (15–25%)
- **Example calculation:**
  - Raw peas: Vitamin C = 40 mg/100 g
  - After blanching (95°C, 4 min): Vitamin C = 22 mg/100 g → **45% loss**

**2. Pasteurization (72°C/15 s — HTST):**
- **Mechanism:** Mild thermal exposure, oxidative degradation
- **Vitamins affected:** Vitamin C (10–20%), B1 (5–10%), B12 (minimal)
- **Milk example:** Raw milk Vitamin B12 = 0.5 $\mu g$/100 mL → Pasteurized = 0.45 $\mu g$/100 mL (10% loss)

**3. Dehydration (Hot-air / Sun / Spray / Freeze):**
- **Mechanism:** Heat + oxygen exposure + light (solar drying)
- **Vitamins affected:**
  - **Vitamin A** (β-carotene): photo-oxidation up to **40% loss** in sun-dried carrots
  - **Vitamin C:** 60–80% loss in hot-air drying; minimal loss in **freeze drying**
  - **Riboflavin (B2):** Light-induced degradation up to 50% in sun-dried milk

#### Sub-part (b) — Industrial Strategies to Minimize Vitamin Losses (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

| Strategy | Underlying Principle | Vitamin Targeted |
|---|---|---|
| **Steam blanching (vs water)** | Reduces leaching | B1, C, Folate |
| **High-Temperature Short-Time (HTST)** | Brief exposure limits thermal damage | B1, C |
| **Acidification (pH < 4.5)** | Vitamin C stable at low pH | C |
| **Exclusion of oxygen** (vacuum/N₂ flush) | Reduces oxidative losses | A, E, C |
| **Opaque packaging** (amber glass, Al foil) | Blocks UV light | A, B2 |
| **Freeze drying (lyophilization)** | Low T, no water for leaching | C, B-complex |
| **Microencapsulation** (spray-dried powders) | Wall material protects core | A, D, E, C |
| **Addition of chelators** (EDTA) | Sequesters pro-oxidant metals | C |
| **Fortification after processing** | Replaces inevitable losses | All |
| **Cold-chain maintenance** | Slows degradation kinetics | All |

**Conclusion:** A combination of *process optimization* (HTST, steam blanching) and *product design* (opaque, vacuum packaging, microencapsulation) can retain **70–95% of vitamins** versus typical 30–50% loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Students often forget to **quantify losses with examples** — always include at least 2 numerical or % estimates to score full marks.
> 2. Confusing **thermal degradation** with **leaching** costs 1–2 marks. State the **specific mechanism** explicitly.
> 3. Listing strategies without the **principle** (acidification → pH stability) loses the engineering depth expected in 14-mark answers.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Classify minerals into macro and micro (trace) minerals. Discuss the dietary sources, recommended daily allowances, biochemical functions, and deficiency disorders of any THREE major minerals: Calcium, Iron, and Iodine. (14 Marks)**

#### Sub-part (a) — Classification of Minerals (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Classification based on daily requirement:**

**1. Macrominerals (Major Minerals) — Required > 100 mg/day:**
- Calcium (Ca), Phosphorus (P), Potassium (K), Sulfur (S), Sodium (Na), Chlorine (Cl), Magnesium (Mg)

**2. Microminerals (Trace Minerals) — Required < 100 mg/day:**
- Iron (Fe), Zinc (Zn), Copper (Cu), Manganese (Mn), Iodine (I), Selenium (Se), Molybdenum (Mo), Fluoride (F), Chromium (Cr), Cobalt (Co)

**Basis of classification:**
- Body content (Ca: ~ 1000 g; Fe: ~ 4 g; I: ~ 15 mg)
- Daily requirement
- Storage capacity

**Other classifications:**
- *Essential vs Non-essential*
- *Cationic vs Anionic*
- *Bioavailability-determined categories*

#### Sub-part (b) — Three Major Minerals: Ca, Fe, I (7 Marks)

### **Calcium (Ca)**
- **Sources:** Milk, dairy, fish bones (sardines), green leafy vegetables (low bioavailability due to oxalate), fortified tofu
- **RDA:** **1000 mg/day** (adults); 1200 mg/day (>50 years)
- **Biochemical Functions:**
  - Bone & teeth structure (hydroxyapatite)
  - Muscle contraction
  - Blood coagulation (Factor IV)
  - Intracellular second messenger
  - Nerve conduction
- **Deficiency:** **Rickets, Osteomalacia, Osteoporosis, Tetany**

### **Iron (Fe)**
- **Sources:**
  - *Heme:* Meat, fish, poultry (15–35% absorption)
  - *Non-heme:* Legumes, spinach, fortified cereals (2–20% absorption)
- **RDA:** **8 mg/day (male), 18 mg/day (premenopausal female)**
- **Biochemical Functions:**
  - Oxygen transport (hemoglobin, myoglobin)
  - Electron transport chain (cytochromes a, b, c)
  - Cytochrome P450 detoxification
  - DNA synthesis (ribonucleotide reductase)
- **Deficiency:** **Iron-deficiency anemia** (most common worldwide — microcytic, hypochromic RBCs)

### **Iodine (I)**
- **Sources:** Seaweed, marine fish, iodized salt, dairy
- **RDA:** **150 $\mu g$/day** (adults); 220 $\mu g$/day (pregnant women)
- **Biochemical Functions:**
  - Synthesis of **thyroid hormones T3 (triiodothyronine)** and **T4 (thyroxine)** via thyroperoxidase (TPO)
  - Regulates basal metabolic rate (BMR)
  - Neurodevelopment in fetus
- **Deficiency:** **Goiter, Hypothyroidism, Cretinism (severe), Iodine Deficiency Disorders (IDD)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Forgetting **bioavailability** of iron (heme vs non-heme) costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. Confusing **goiter** (enlarged thyroid) with **cretinism** (developmental disability from fetal deficiency) — these are distinct.
> 3. Not mentioning **iodized salt fortification** as the global public-health intervention loses the "food processing" angle — KTU expects **applied** answers, not just clinical.
> 4. Always give **RDA values** explicitly in your answer — examiners mark numerical precision.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Vitamins

- ✅ **13 vitamins total**: 4 fat-soluble (A, D, E, K) + 9 water-soluble (C + 8 B-complex)
- ✅ **Vitamin A:** Vision, β-carotene conversion (12:1 for dietary); Deficiency → Night blindness, Xerophthalmia, Bitot's spots, Keratomalacia
- ✅ **Vitamin D:** Sunshine vitamin, two hydroxylations (liver → kidney); Active form = 1,25(OH)₂D₃ (calcitriol); Deficiency → Rickets (children), Osteomalacia (adults)
- ✅ **Vitamin E:** α-tocopherol most active; Membrane antioxidant; Regenerated by Vitamin C; Deficiency → Hemolytic anemia (infants)
- ✅ **Vitamin K:** γ-carboxylation of Glu residues in clotting factors II, VII, IX, X; Deficiency → Hemorrhagic disease of newborn
- ✅ **B1 (Thiamine):** TPP coenzyme; Deficiency → Beriberi (wet/dry), Wernicke-Korsakoff
- ✅ **B2 (Riboflavin):** FAD, FMN; Deficiency → Cheilosis, Angular stomatitis
- ✅ **B3 (Niacin):** NAD⁺, NADP⁺; 4 D's of Pellagra; Conversion: 60 mg Trp → 1 mg niacin
- ✅ **B6 (Pyridoxine):** PLP; Transamination; Deficiency → Microcytic anemia
- ✅ **B9 (Folate):** THF; One-carbon metabolism; Deficiency → Megaloblastic anemia, Neural tube defects (spina bifida)
- ✅ **B12 (Cobalamin):** Methylmalonyl-CoA mutase; Deficiency → Pernicious anemia, Subacute combined degeneration
- ✅ **Vitamin C:** Ascorbate; Cofactor for prolyl/lysyl hydroxylase; Deficiency → Scurvy; Degraded by heat, light, O₂

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Minerals

- ✅ **Major minerals (7):** Ca, P, K, S, Na, Cl, Mg (each > 100 mg/day)
- ✅ **Trace minerals:** Fe, Zn, Cu, Mn, I, Se, Mo, F, Cr, Co
- ✅ **Calcium:** Hydroxyapatite bones; RDA 1000 mg; Vitamin D enhances absorption (calbindin)
- ✅ **Iron:** Heme (15–35%) vs Non-heme (2–20%); Vitamin C enhances; Phytates inhibit; Deficiency = #1 worldwide
- ✅ **Iodine:** T3, T4 synthesis; RDA 150 $\mu g$; Deficiency → Goiter, Cretinism; **Iodized salt** = major intervention
- ✅ **Zinc:** > 300 enzyme cofactor; Deficiency → Growth retardation, Acrodermatitis enteropathica
- ✅ **Selenium:** Glutathione peroxidase; Antagonist to arsenic

### ⚡ Cross-Cutting Concepts (Frequently Asked)

- ✅ **Vitamin-mineral interaction:** Vitamin D ↔ Ca, Vitamin C ↔ Fe, Vitamin K ↔ Ca (antagonism with warfarin)
- ✅ **Bioavailability formula:** $\text{BV} = \dfrac{\text{absorbed}}{\text{intake}} \times 100\%$
- ✅ **Phytate chelation** of Zn, Ca, Fe — mitigated by fermentation, germination
- ✅ **Fortification vs Enrichment:** Fortification = adding nutrients not originally present; Enrichment = restoring nutrients lost during processing
- ✅ **First-order kinetics** for vitamin degradation: $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$, $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$

### ⚡ Conversion Constants to Memorize

| Conversion | Value |
|---|---|
| Vitamin A | 1 IU = 0.3 $\mu g$ retinol |
| Vitamin D | 1 IU = 0.025 $\mu g$ cholecalciferol |
| Vitamin E | 1 IU = 0.67 mg d-α-tocopherol |
| β-carotene (dietary) | 12 $\mu g$ = 1 $\mu g$ retinol |
| Niacin | 60 mg Trp = 1 mg niacin |
| Folate | 1.7 × fortified = natural food folate equivalent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Examination Tip:** For **14-mark questions**, always structure your answer as:
> 1. **Definition/Classification** (2 marks)
> 2. **Specific Properties / Functions** (4 marks)
> 3. **Quantitative Example / Diagram** (4 marks)
> 4. **Engineering/Applied angle — processing, fortification, or stability** (4 marks)
>
> This four-part structure matches KTU's **CO-Centric marking rubric** under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food and Its Functions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Food and Its Functions

# 🍽️ FOOD AND ITS FUNCTIONS — KTU 2024 SCHEME STUDY NOTES

---

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Food

> [!NOTE]
> **DEFINITION (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> **Food** is any edible substance, whether processed, semi-processed, or raw, which is consumed by living organisms to provide **nutritional support**, **sensory satisfaction**, **physiological regulation**, and **social/psychological well-being**.

According to the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO)** and adopted in the KTU Food Processing curriculum, food is defined as:

> *"Any substance, whether processed, semi-processed or raw, which is intended for human consumption, and includes drink, chewing gum and any substance which has been used in the manufacture, preparation or treatment of food but does not include cosmetics, tobacco or substances used only as drugs."*

### 1.2 Concept of Nutrients

A **nutrient** is a chemical component of food that the body uses for one or more of the following:
- Building and repairing tissues
- Providing energy
- Regulating metabolic processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Essential Nutrient Classes**
> 1. **Carbohydrates** — Primary energy source (**4 kcal/g**)
> 2. **Proteins** — Body-building and repair (**4 kcal/g**)
> 3. **Fats (Lipids)** — Concentrated energy (**9 kcal/g**)
> 4. **Vitamins** — Metabolic regulators (no calories)
> 5. **Minerals** — Structural & catalytic role (no calories)
> 6. **Water** — Solvent and transport medium (no calories)

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Food as Fuel & Architecture"

Think of the human body as a **sophisticated self-repairing building that runs 24/7**:
- 🔥 **Energy-giving foods** = *Electricity bill* (pays for running the lights, AC, computers)
- 🧱 **Body-building foods** = *Cement and bricks* (repairs walls, builds new floors)
- ⚙️ **Protective/Regulatory foods** = *Maintenance engineers* (keeps elevators working, fixes leaks)
- 💧 **Water** = *Plumbing system* (carries everything where it's needed)

Just as a building cannot function without any one of these, the human body needs **all categories of nutrients** in balanced proportions — a principle known as the **Balanced Diet Concept**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Food Science

| Metric | Standard Value (KTU Convention) |
|---|---|
| Energy from Carbohydrates | **4 kcal/gram** (or 17 kJ) |
| Energy from Proteins | **4 kcal/gram** (or 17 kJ) |
| Energy from Fats | **9 kcal/gram** (or 37 kJ) |
| Adult Daily Energy Requirement (Sedentary) | **2000 kcal** |
| Adult Daily Energy Requirement (Moderate work) | **2800–3000 kcal** |
| Daily Water Intake | **2.0–2.5 litres** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — FUNCTIONS OF FOOD

### 2.1 Classification of Functions of Food

The functions of food can be systematically classified into **three major categories**, each serving a distinct biological and socio-cultural purpose:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           FUNCTIONS OF FOOD             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  A. Physiological Functions             │
│  B. Psychological Functions             │
│  C. Social Functions                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.2 Category A — Physiological Functions (Most Critical)

These are the **primary biological roles** that food performs in maintaining life. They are further sub-divided:

#### (i) Energy-Yielding Function
- Food supplies the **caloric energy** required for:
  - Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR)
  - Physical Activity
  - Specific Dynamic Action (SDA) of food
  - Thermoregulation
- **Primary energy nutrients:** Carbohydrates → Fats → Proteins

#### (ii) Body-Building Function
- Required for **growth**, **development**, and **tissue repair**
- **Key nutrient:** Proteins (amino acids)
- Critical during **infancy, childhood, adolescence, pregnancy, lactation, and convalescence**

#### (iii) Regulatory Function
- Vitamins and minerals act as **coenzymes** and **cofactors** in metabolic pathways
- Water maintains **osmotic balance** and **body temperature**

#### (iv) Protective Function
- Foods boost **immune competence** (e.g., Vitamin C, Vitamin A, Zinc, Selenium)
- Antioxidants (Vitamin E, Beta-carotene) neutralize free radicals

### 2.3 Category B — Psychological Functions

| Function | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Sensory satisfaction | Taste, aroma, texture | Spicy biryani, fresh juice |
| Emotional comfort | Stress relief | Chocolate, ice cream |
| Appetite satisfaction | Curbing hunger pangs | Fiber-rich foods |
| Mental well-being | Mood regulation | Tryptophan-rich foods (milk, eggs) |

### 2.4 Category C — Social Functions

- **Cultural identity** — Idli in South India, Appam in Kerala
- **Religious rituals** — Prasad, Communion wafer
- **Hospitality** — Serving tea/coffee to guests
- **Status symbol** — Premium foods (saffron, caviar)
- **Bonding** — Family meals, festival feasts (Onam Sadya)

### 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are HIGH-FREQUENCY in KTU board examinations. Memorize all.**

| # | Formula/Parameter | Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Caloric Value of Food | $E_{total} = 4C + 4P + 9F$ | kcal |
| 2 | BMI (Quetelet Index) | $BMI = \dfrac{W}{H^2}$ | kg/m² |
| 3 | Harris-Benedict BMR (Males) | $BMR = 66.5 + 13.75W + 5.003H - 6.755A$ | kcal/day |
| 4 | Harris-Benedict BMR (Females) | $BMR = 655.1 + 9.563W + 1.850H - 4.676A$ | kcal/day |
| 5 | Total Energy Expenditure | $TEE = BMR \times Activity\ Factor$ | kcal/day |
| 6 | Protein Energy Ratio | $PER\% = \dfrac{4P}{E_{total}} \times 100$ | % |
| 7 | Calorie Conversion | $1\ kcal = 4.184\ kJ$ | — |

Where: $W$ = Weight (kg), $H$ = Height (cm), $A$ = Age (years), $C$ = Carbs (g), $P$ = Proteins (g), $F$ = Fats (g)

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Food Processing Engineer must study these functions:**
> - **Process Design:** Selecting heat treatments that preserve heat-labile vitamins (Vitamin C, Thiamine)
> - **Product Development:** Creating fortified foods for **deficient populations** (iodized salt, iron-fortified flour)
> - **Quality Control:** Ensuring retention of protein quality post-processing
> - **Shelf-life Engineering:** Understanding oxidation of energy-providing fats

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Value of Food (Atwater Factors)

The **Atwater System** (developed by Wilbur Olin Atwater, 1896) determines the metabolizable energy of foods. Let us derive the total energy contribution step-by-step.

**STEP 1 — Identify the three macronutrients with their physiological fuel values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Carbohydrates} &\rightarrow 4\ \text{kcal/g} \\
\text{Proteins} &\rightarrow 4\ \text{kcal/g} \\
\text{Fats} &\rightarrow 9\ \text{kcal/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 2 — Define the general energy equation:**

Let $C$, $P$, $F$ represent the mass (in grams) of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats in a food sample respectively.

The total available energy is the **linear sum** of individual contributions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= (E\ \text{from carbs}) + (E\ \text{from proteins}) + (E\ \text{from fats}) \\
E_{total} &= (4 \times C) + (4 \times P) + (9 \times F) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{total} = 4C + 4P + 9F}
$$

**STEP 3 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical):**

> A 100 g serving of a food product contains: 50 g carbohydrates, 15 g proteins, and 10 g fats. Calculate the total caloric value.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 50\ \text{g}, \quad P = 15\ \text{g}, \quad F = 10\ \text{g} \\
E_{total} &= 4(50) + 4(15) + 9(10) \\
&= 200 + 60 + 90 \\
&= 350\ \text{kcal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $E_{total} = 350\ kcal$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Body Mass Index (BMI)

**STEP 1 — Definition by Adolphe Quetelet (1835):**

BMI is the ratio of body weight (in kg) to the square of height (in metres).

**STEP 2 — Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } W &= \text{Body weight (kg)} \\
\text{Let } H &= \text{Body height (m)} \\
\text{Then, by definition:} \quad BMI &= \dfrac{\text{Weight}}{\text{Height}^2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{BMI = \dfrac{W}{H^2} \quad (\text{kg/m}^2)}
$$

**STEP 3 — Worked Example:**

> A 25-year-old engineering student weighs 70 kg and is 1.75 m tall. Determine his BMI and classify his nutritional status.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BMI &= \dfrac{W}{H^2} = \dfrac{70}{(1.75)^2} \\
&= \dfrac{70}{3.0625} \\
&= 22.86\ \text{kg/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (WHO Classification):**

| BMI Range | Classification |
|---|---|
| < 18.5 | Underweight |
| 18.5 – 24.9 | **Normal (Healthy)** |
| 25.0 – 29.9 | Overweight |
| ≥ 30.0 | Obese |

> The student's BMI of **22.86** falls in the **Normal/Healthy** category. ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Protein Energy Ratio (PER%)

**STEP 1 — Definition:**

The Protein Energy Ratio indicates the **proportion of total energy** derived from proteins.

**STEP 2 — Derivation:**

Energy contributed by proteins in 100 g of food = $4P$ kcal
Total energy in 100 g of food = $E_{total} = 4C + 4P + 9F$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PER\% &= \dfrac{\text{Energy from proteins}}{\text{Total energy}} \times 100 \\
PER\% &= \dfrac{4P}{4C + 4P + 9F} \times 100 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{PER\% = \dfrac{4P}{4C + 4P + 9F} \times 100}
$$

**STEP 3 — Worked Example (Continuing from §3.1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PER\% &= \dfrac{4(15)}{350} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{60}{350} \times 100 \\
&= 17.14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU Threshold:** For a balanced diet, PER% should ideally be **10–15%**. Our sample at 17.14% is **slightly protein-rich**.

---

### 3.4 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix

| Food Function | Primary Nutrient | Deficiency Disease | KTU Examiner's Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy supply | Carbohydrates, Fats | Marasmus, Fatigue | Always mention **4-9 kcal/g** |
| Body building | Proteins | Kwashiorkor | Mention essential AAs |
| Regulation | Vitamins & Minerals | Scurvy, Rickets, Anemia | Name the specific vitamin |
| Protection | Vitamin A, C, E, Zn | Frequent infections | Cite antioxidant role |
| Hydration | Water | Dehydration | 2–2.5 L/day standard |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Food Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FOOD AND ITS FUNCTIONS"]:::root --> B["PHYSIOLOGICAL FUNCTIONS"]:::lvl1
    A --> C["PSYCHOLOGICAL FUNCTIONS"]:::lvl1
    A --> D["SOCIAL FUNCTIONS"]:::lvl1
    
    B --> E["ENERGY SUPPLY"]:::lvl2
    B --> F["BODY BUILDING"]:::lvl2
    B --> G["REGULATORY"]:::lvl2
    B --> H["PROTECTIVE"]:::lvl2
    
    E --> E1["CARBOHYDRATES 4 KCAL PER G"]:::lvl3
    E --> E2["FATS 9 KCAL PER G"]:::lvl3
    E --> E3["PROTEINS 4 KCAL PER G"]:::lvl3
    
    F --> F1["GROWTH"]:::lvl3
    F --> F2["REPAIR"]:::lvl3
    F --> F3["MAINTENANCE"]:::lvl3
    
    G --> G1["VITAMINS COENZYMES"]:::lvl3
    G --> G2["MINERALS COFACTORS"]:::lvl3
    G --> G3["WATER OSMOTIC BALANCE"]:::lvl3
    
    H --> H1["ANTIOXIDANTS"]:::lvl3
    H --> H2["IMMUNE BOOST"]:::lvl3
    H --> H3["DETOXIFICATION"]:::lvl3
    
    C --> C1["SENSORY SATISFACTION"]:::lvl3
    C --> C2["EMOTIONAL COMFORT"]:::lvl3
    C --> C3["APPETITE CONTROL"]:::lvl3
    
    D --> D1["CULTURAL IDENTITY"]:::lvl3
    D --> D2["RELIGIOUS RITUALS"]:::lvl3
    D --> D3["HOSPITALITY AND BONDING"]:::lvl3
    
    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef lvl1 fill:#2563eb,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef lvl2 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef lvl3 fill:#93c5fd,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram: Nutrient Pathway in Human Body

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INGESTION\nFood Intake"] --> B["DIGESTION\nMechanical and Chemical"]
    B --> C["ABSORPTION\nSmall Intestine Villi"]
    C --> D["METABOLISM\nCellular Utilization"]
    D --> E1["ENERGY PRODUCTION\nATP Synthesis"]:::energy
    D --> E2["TISSUE SYNTHESIS\nProtein Anabolism"]:::tissue
    D --> E3["REGULATION\nEnzymatic Cofactors"]:::reg
    D --> E4["STORAGE\nGlycogen and Lipids"]:::store
    E1 --> F["PHYSIOLOGICAL OUTPUT\nHomeostasis Maintained"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    
    classDef energy fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef tissue fill:#10b981,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#8b5cf6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef store fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Six Classes of Nutrients (KTU Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N["NUTRIENTS"] --> M["MACRONUTRIENTS\nRequired in large quantities"]:::macro
    N --> MIC["MICRONUTRIENTS\nRequired in trace quantities"]:::micro
    N --> W["WATER\nEssential for life"]:::water
    
    M --> M1["CARBOHYDRATES\n4 KCAL PER G"]:::macroItem
    M --> M2["PROTEINS\n4 KCAL PER G"]:::macroItem
    M --> M3["FATS\n9 KCAL PER G"]:::macroItem
    
    MIC --> MIC1["VITAMINS\nFat and Water Soluble"]:::microItem
    MIC --> MIC2["MINERALS\nMacro and Trace"]:::microItem
    
    classDef macro fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef micro fill:#059669,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef water fill:#0284c7,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef macroItem fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef microItem fill:#86efac,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 📘 PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
Define food. List the six major classes of nutrients.

**Model Answer:**
Food is any edible substance, processed or raw, intended for human consumption that provides nutritional support, sensory satisfaction, and physiological regulation.

The six classes of nutrients are:
1. Carbohydrates (4 kcal/g)
2. Proteins (4 kcal/g)
3. Fats (9 kcal/g)
4. Vitamins
5. Minerals
6. Water

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 6 for listing nutrients = 4 marks, capped at 3)*

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**
Briefly explain the **physiological functions of food**.

**Model Answer:**
Physiological functions are biological roles of food essential for sustaining life. They are:
1. **Energy supply** — Carbohydrates and fats provide calories for BMR and activity.
2. **Body building** — Proteins aid in growth and tissue repair.
3. **Regulatory** — Vitamins and minerals act as coenzymes/cofactors.
4. **Protective** — Antioxidants and immune-boosting nutrients (Vitamin C, A, Zn).

*(3 marks: 0.75 per function correctly explained)*

---

### 📗 PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **physiological, psychological, and social functions of food** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 250 g serving of a packaged food product contains **60 g carbohydrates, 20 g proteins, and 15 g fats**.
- (i) Calculate the total caloric value.
- (ii) Determine the Protein Energy Ratio (PER%).
- (iii) Classify the food as energy-dense or nutrient-dense based on PER%. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functions of Food (7 Marks):**

*Physiological Functions (3 marks):*
- **Energy supply:** Carbohydrates (4 kcal/g) and fats (9 kcal/g) provide fuel for basal metabolism, physical activity, and thermoregulation. Example: rice, chapati provide glucose.
- **Body building:** Proteins supply amino acids for growth, repair, and maintenance. Example: milk, eggs, pulses.
- **Regulatory function:** Vitamins (A, B, C, D, E, K) act as coenzymes; minerals (Ca, Fe, I) act as cofactors. Example: citrus fruits, green leafy vegetables.
- **Protective function:** Antioxidants (Vitamin E, beta-carotene) neutralize free radicals; Vitamin C and Zn enhance immunity. Example: amla, turmeric.

*Psychological Functions (2 marks):*
- Sensory satisfaction from taste, aroma, texture.
- Emotional comfort and stress relief (chocolate, ice cream).
- Appetite satisfaction via satiety signals.

*Social Functions (2 marks):*
- Cultural identity (regional cuisines).
- Religious rituals (Prasad, Communion, Iftar).
- Hospitality and bonding (Onam Sadya, weddings).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given: $C = 60\ g,\ P = 20\ g,\ F = 15\ g$

**(i) Total caloric value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= 4C + 4P + 9F \\
&= 4(60) + 4(20) + 9(15) \\
&= 240 + 80 + 135 \\
&= 455\ \text{kcal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Protein Energy Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PER\% &= \dfrac{4P}{E_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{4(20)}{455} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{80}{455} \times 100 \\
&= 17.58\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Classification:**
- Ideal PER% for a balanced diet: **10–15%**
- Our product: 17.58% → **Slightly protein-rich but acceptable**
- Since $E_{total} = 455$ kcal per 250 g → $1.82\ kcal/g$ → **Moderately energy-dense**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

**(a)** Define a **balanced diet**. Discuss the **RDA (Recommended Dietary Allowances)** concept and list the RDA values for an **adult Indian male (sedentary)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 30-year-old male engineer weighs **75 kg**, is **175 cm tall**, and leads a **moderately active lifestyle**.
- (i) Calculate his BMI.
- (ii) Calculate his BMR using the Harris-Benedict equation.
- (iii) Estimate his Total Energy Expenditure (TEE) using activity factor 1.55. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Balanced Diet and RDA (7 Marks):**

**Balanced Diet Definition (2 marks):**
A balanced diet is one that provides **all essential nutrients in adequate proportions and quantities** to maintain health, support growth, and prevent nutrient deficiency diseases.

**RDA Concept (2 marks):**
- RDA is the **average daily dietary intake level** sufficient to meet the nutrient requirements of nearly all (97–98%) healthy individuals in a particular life-stage and gender group.
- Set by **ICMR (Indian Council of Medical Research)** and **NIN (National Institute of Nutrition)**.

**RDA Values for Adult Indian Male (Sedentary) — (3 marks):**

| Nutrient | RDA |
|---|---|
| Energy | 2320 kcal/day |
| Protein | 60 g/day |
| Carbohydrates | 300–350 g/day |
| Fats | 25–30 g/day |
| Calcium | 600 mg/day |
| Iron | 17 mg/day |
| Vitamin A | 600 µg/day |
| Vitamin C | 40 mg/day |
| Water | 2.5 L/day |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given: Age $A = 30$, Weight $W = 75\ kg$, Height $H = 175\ cm = 1.75\ m$

**(i) BMI Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BMI &= \dfrac{W}{H^2} = \dfrac{75}{(1.75)^2} \\
&= \dfrac{75}{3.0625} \\
&= 24.49\ \text{kg/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification:** Normal (Healthy) range.

**(ii) BMR using Harris-Benedict Equation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BMR &= 66.5 + 13.75W + 5.003H - 6.755A \\
&= 66.5 + 13.75(75) + 5.003(175) - 6.755(30) \\
&= 66.5 + 1031.25 + 875.525 - 202.65 \\
&= 1770.625\ \text{kcal/day} \\
&\approx 1771\ \text{kcal/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total Energy Expenditure (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TEE &= BMR \times Activity\ Factor \\
&= 1770.625 \times 1.55 \\
&= 2744.47\ \text{kcal/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation:** Engineer should consume approximately **2744 kcal/day** to maintain current weight.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS**
> 
> **Pitfall 1:** Students often forget to **convert height from cm to metres** in BMI calculation. This yields a 30,000× inflated BMI and full mark deduction.
> 
> **Pitfall 2:** In the Harris-Benedict equation, **height must be in cm (NOT metres)**. Confusing units leads to incorrect BMR.
> 
> **Pitfall 3:** Energy values are **4-4-9**, NOT 4-9-4. A common memory trap is reversing proteins and fats.
> 
> **Pitfall 4:** RDA values must be from the **latest ICMR 2020 revised guidelines**; older values (2010) may cost partial marks.
> 
> **Pitfall 5:** Do not write "Energy" without specifying kcal/g for each macronutrient — examiners deduct 1 mark for incomplete units.

---

### 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **Food = Edible substance** providing nutrition, satisfaction, regulation.
- ✅ **Six nutrient classes:** Carbohydrates, Proteins, Fats, Vitamins, Minerals, Water.
- ✅ **Calorific values:** Carbs = **4 kcal/g**, Proteins = **4 kcal/g**, Fats = **9 kcal/g**.
- ✅ **Three function categories:** Physiological, Psychological, Social.
- ✅ **Four physiological sub-functions:** Energy, Body-building, Regulation, Protection.
- ✅ **BMI Formula:** $BMI = \dfrac{W}{H^2}$ (kg/m²). Normal range: **18.5 – 24.9**.
- ✅ **Harris-Benedict BMR (Males):** $66.5 + 13.75W + 5.003H - 6.755A$.
- ✅ **Harris-Benedict BMR (Females):** $655.1 + 9.563W + 1.850H - 4.676A$.
- ✅ **Energy equation:** $E_{total} = 4C + 4P + 9F$.
- ✅ **Conversion:** $1\ kcal = 4.184\ kJ$.
- ✅ **PER% ideal range:** 10–15% for a balanced diet.
- ✅ **RDA for adult Indian male (sedentary):** 2320 kcal/day, 60 g protein.
- ✅ **Daily water intake:** 2.0–2.5 litres.
- ✅ **Units vigilance:** Height in **cm** for BMR, but in **metres** for BMI.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food Groups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Food Groups

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

According to the **Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR)** and the **National Institute of Nutrition (NIN)** framework, which forms the basis of the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Fundamentals of Food Processing*, **Food Groups** are systematic classifications of foods based on their predominant nutrient composition, biological function, and dietary contribution. Foods with similar nutritional profiles are clustered together to facilitate the design of balanced diets, nutritional labeling, food fortification programs, and dietary recommendations.

The **five basic food groups** recognized globally are:

1. **Cereals, Millets, and Pulses** — primary sources of carbohydrates and proteins.
2. **Vegetables and Fruits** — protective foods rich in vitamins, minerals, and dietary fiber.
3. **Milk and Milk Products** — sources of calcium, high-quality protein, and vitamin B12.
4. **Eggs, Meat, and Fish** — concentrated sources of animal protein and bioavailable micronutrients.
5. **Fats, Oils, Nuts, and Sugar** — concentrated sources of energy and essential fatty acids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must remember that classification is not just a list — it is a **functional grouping** where each group plays a specific physiological role: *Energy-yielding, Body-building, or Protective*. This functional classification is a high-yield exam point.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the human body as a **construction site** that operates 24/7. To keep this site running, you need three types of supplies:

- **Bricks and Steel (Body-building foods):** Proteins from pulses, eggs, meat, milk — these build and repair tissues.
- **Diesel and Coal (Energy-giving foods):** Carbohydrates from cereals, fats from oils — these fuel the engines (cells).
- **Safety Gear and Air Filters (Protective foods):** Vitamins, minerals, antioxidants from fruits and vegetables — these protect against disease and wear.

Just as a foreman cannot run a site with only diesel, the human body cannot survive on carbohydrates alone. **Food groups** are the organized "supply categories" that ensure we receive all three classes of nutrients in adequate proportions.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Plate Analogy:** A balanced plate, as recommended by **ICMR (2020)** and the **USDA MyPlate** model, visually represents this idea: roughly **half the plate** is fruits and vegetables, **one-quarter** is cereals, and **one-quarter** is protein-rich foods, complemented by a side of dairy.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA)** for an average adult:
  - Energy: **2000 kcal/day** (sedentary reference man)
  - Protein: **0.8 g/kg body weight/day**
  - Carbohydrates: **45–65 %** of total energy
  - Fats: **20–35 %** of total energy
- **Calorific value of food groups:**
  - Carbohydrates: **4 kcal/g**
  - Proteins: **4 kcal/g**
  - Fats: **9 kcal/g**

> [!NOTE]
> These constants are standard reference values used by the **National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), Hyderabad**, and are frequently tested in KTU university examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Frameworks for Food Groups

Food groups can be classified using **three principal frameworks** that KTU examiners frequently test:

### Framework A: Based on Nutrient Composition
Foods are grouped according to the **dominant macronutrient** they supply.

| Group | Dominant Nutrient | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cereals & Millets | Carbohydrates (Starch) | Rice, Wheat, Ragi, Jowar |
| Pulses & Legumes | Proteins (Plant-based) | Toor dal, Chana, Rajma, Soybean |
| Milk & Dairy | Protein + Calcium | Milk, Curd, Paneer, Ghee |
| Eggs, Meat, Fish | Protein (Animal-based) | Chicken, Mutton, Egg, Tuna |
| Vegetables & Fruits | Vitamins + Minerals + Fiber | Spinach, Carrot, Apple, Banana |
| Fats & Oils | Lipids (Essential FAs) | Mustard oil, Groundnut oil, Butter |
| Sugar & Jaggery | Simple Carbohydrates | Cane sugar, Honey, Jaggery |
| Nuts & Oilseeds | Proteins + Lipids | Almond, Cashew, Sesame, Flaxseed |

### Framework B: Based on Physiological Function
This is the **functional classification** taught under Module 1 of OEFTT831 and is a high-priority topic for Part A (3-mark) questions.

1. **Energy-Yielding Foods** — supply calories to fuel metabolic activities.
   - Cereals, millets, sugars, fats, and oils.
2. **Body-Building Foods** — supply amino acids for tissue synthesis and repair.
   - Pulses, milk, eggs, meat, fish, and nuts.
3. **Protective Foods** — supply vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants that regulate metabolism and provide immunity.
   - Fruits, vegetables, and milk (also contributes to protection via vitamin A and calcium).

### Framework C: Based on Origin
- **Plant-based foods** — cereals, pulses, fruits, vegetables, nuts, sugar.
- **Animal-based foods** — milk, eggs, meat, fish, ghee, butter.

## 2.2 The Concept of a Balanced Diet

A **balanced diet** is one that supplies all essential nutrients in the correct proportion, amount, and quality to maintain optimal health, support growth, and prevent nutrient-deficiency diseases.

**The Basic Five Food Group Concept (ICMR):**

A balanced Indian diet must include portions from **five major food groups** every day:

1. **Cereals and millets** — $\geq 270$ g/day
2. **Pulses and legumes** — $\geq 60$ g/day
3. **Milk and dairy** — $\geq 300$ mL/day
4. **Vegetables (including green leafy)** — $\geq 300$ g/day
5. **Fruits** — $\geq 100$ g/day

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A 3-mark question may ask: *"Classify food groups based on their function."* Always list the three functional categories and provide **two examples** for each to score full marks.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations govern energy calculations across food groups and appear frequently in numerical problems.

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energy from macronutrients | $E = (4 \times P) + (4 \times C) + (9 \times F)$ | kcal | Total caloric content of a meal |
| Protein Energy Ratio (PER) | $\text{PER} = \frac{E_{protein}}{E_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Adequacy check for protein in diet |
| Caloric Distribution | $E_{CHO} : E_{Pro} : E_{Fat} = 50\text{–}60 : 10\text{–}15 : 20\text{–}30$ | % | Macronutrient balance per ICMR |
| RDA Energy (Reference Man) | $E_{RDA} = 2320$ | kcal/day | Adult sedentary male |
| RDA Energy (Reference Woman) | $E_{RDA} = 1900$ | kcal/day | Adult sedentary female |
| Basal Metabolic Rate (Harris-Benedict, Male) | $\text{BMR} = 66.5 + 13.75W + 5.003H - 6.755A$ | kcal | Resting energy expenditure |
| Basal Metabolic Rate (Female) | $\text{BMR} = 655.1 + 9.563W + 1.850H - 4.676A$ | kcal | Resting energy expenditure |

Where $P$ = protein (g), $C$ = carbohydrate (g), $F$ = fat (g), $W$ = weight (kg), $H$ = height (cm), $A$ = age (years).

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the division symbol has been replaced with `\div` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax, and absolute values use `\vert` notation where required.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding food groups is foundational for several applied domains within Food Processing Engineering:

- **Food Fortification Programs:** Adding iron to cereals or vitamin A to milk requires knowledge of which food group is consumed in highest quantity by the target population.
- **Product Development:** Formulating a new ready-to-eat snack requires balancing **cereals (energy)** + **pulses (protein)** + **vegetables (micronutrients)**.
- **Quality Control & Labeling:** **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** regulations require products to declare which food group they belong to and the percentage of Recommended Dietary Allowance (%RDA) they fulfill.
- **Public Health Nutrition:** Government mid-day meal schemes are designed around the **basic five food group** concept to combat malnutrition in children.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Energy Calculation from a Mixed Food Group Meal

A standard South Indian breakfast plate contains the following items. We will derive the total caloric content and macronutrient distribution.

**Given Data:**

| Food Item | Group | Mass (g) | Protein (g) | Carbs (g) | Fat (g) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Idli (2 nos.) | Cereals | 100 | 4.0 | 22.0 | 0.8 |
| Sambar | Pulses + Veg | 150 | 6.0 | 15.0 | 3.0 |
| Coconut chutney | Fats/Nuts | 30 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 9.0 |
| Tea with milk | Dairy | 200 | 3.0 | 10.0 | 4.0 |

### Step 1: Sum Each Macronutrient Across the Meal

We aggregate the protein, carbohydrate, and fat contributions from each food group component.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= P_{idli} + P_{sambar} + P_{chutney} + P_{tea} \\
P_{total} &= 4.0 + 6.0 + 1.0 + 3.0 \\
P_{total} &= 14.0 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C_{idli} + C_{sambar} + C_{chutney} + C_{tea} \\
C_{total} &= 22.0 + 15.0 + 2.0 + 10.0 \\
C_{total} &= 49.0 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{total} &= F_{idli} + F_{sambar} + F_{chutney} + F_{tea} \\
F_{total} &= 0.8 + 3.0 + 9.0 + 4.0 \\
F_{total} &= 16.8 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each food item contributes independently. We sum the same macronutrient column across all items to obtain the total macronutrient intake for the meal.

### Step 2: Compute Total Energy Using the Calorific Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= (4 \times P_{total}) + (4 \times C_{total}) + (9 \times F_{total}) \\
E_{total} &= (4 \times 14.0) + (4 \times 49.0) + (9 \times 16.8) \\
E_{total} &= 56.0 + 196.0 + 151.2 \\
E_{total} &= 403.2 \text{ kcal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The factor $4$ is applied to protein and carbohydrates because each gram yields **4 kcal** upon complete oxidation, and the factor $9$ is applied to fats because each gram of fat yields **9 kcal**.

### Step 3: Compute the Percentage Energy Distribution (Macronutrient Ratio)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%E_{Protein} &= \frac{(4 \times P_{total})}{E_{total}} \times 100 \\
\%E_{Protein} &= \frac{56.0}{403.2} \times 100 \approx 13.89\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%E_{Carbohydrate} &= \frac{(4 \times C_{total})}{E_{total}} \times 100 \\
\%E_{Carbohydrate} &= \frac{196.0}{403.2} \times 100 \approx 48.61\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%E_{Fat} &= \frac{(9 \times F_{total})}{E_{total}} \times 100 \\
\%E_{Fat} &= \frac{151.2}{403.2} \times 100 \approx 37.50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The percentage energy from each macronutrient is obtained by dividing the energy contributed by that macronutrient by the total meal energy and multiplying by 100. This ratio reveals whether the meal is balanced.

### Step 4: Evaluate Against ICMR Recommended Ratios

According to ICMR (2020), the recommended distribution is:

- Carbohydrates: **50–60 %**
- Proteins: **10–15 %**
- Fats: **20–30 %**

Our computed meal yields $C:P:F = 48.6:13.9:37.5$.

The fat contribution is **above the recommended range** because of the coconut chutney, while carbohydrates are slightly **below the range**. The meal is therefore acceptable in protein but needs **modification** — for example, by reducing the fat content or adding a fruit — to align with the balanced-diet principle.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Functional Roles of Each Food Group

The following matrix is a high-yield summary for KTU Part A questions.

| Food Group | Primary Nutrient | Functional Role | Deficiency Disease (if excluded) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cereals & Millets | Carbohydrates (Starch) | Energy-yielding | Marasmus (chronic energy deficiency) |
| Pulses & Legumes | Plant protein + Iron | Body-building | Kwashiorkor (protein deficiency) |
| Milk & Dairy | Calcium + Vit B12 + Protein | Body-building + Protective | Osteoporosis, Anemia |
| Vegetables (GLV) | Iron + Folic acid + Vit A | Protective | Iron-deficiency Anemia, Night blindness |
| Fruits | Vit C + Antioxidants | Protective | Scurvy |
| Eggs, Meat, Fish | Animal protein + Vit B12 | Body-building | Pernicious Anemia |
| Fats & Oils | Essential fatty acids | Energy-yielding | Dermatitis, Hair loss |
| Sugar & Jaggery | Simple carbohydrates | Energy-yielding | Hypoglycemia (transient) |
| Nuts & Oilseeds | Protein + Lipids + Vit E | Body-building + Energy | Reproductive disorders |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Cue:** Mapping each deficiency disease to its corresponding food group is a recurring **Part A (3-mark)** question in KTU university exams. Memorize the rightmost column thoroughly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Food Groups (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following block-level architecture maps the relationships between the major classification frameworks discussed in Section 2. The diagram is rendered using Mermaid syntax with **alphanumeric node identifiers** and **plain uppercase labels** (no markdown formatting inside node labels).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Meal Planning Engine

The following diagram illustrates how food groups are routed through a conceptual **Meal Planning Engine** to produce a balanced output — useful for visualizing how food processing engineers formulate value-added products.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inp1["CEREAL GROUP INPUT"]
    inp2["PULSE GROUP INPUT"]
    inp3["VEG FRUIT GROUP INPUT"]
    inp4["DAIRY GROUP INPUT"]
    inp5["FAT OIL GROUP INPUT"]

    proc1["ENERGY CALCULATION MODULE"]
    proc2["PROTEIN QUALITY CHECK"]
    proc3["MICRONUTRIENT BALANCE"]
    proc4["RDA COMPARATOR"]

    out1["BALANCED MEAL OUTPUT"]
    out2["NUTRITIONAL LABEL DATA"]

    inp1 --> proc1
    inp2 --> proc1
    inp2 --> proc2
    inp3 --> proc3
    inp4 --> proc3
    inp4 --> proc2
    inp5 --> proc1

    proc1 --> proc4
    proc2 --> proc4
    proc3 --> proc4

    proc4 --> out1
    proc4 --> out2

    style inp1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style inp2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style inp3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style inp4 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style inp5 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style proc4 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style out1 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style out2 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Raw food group inputs are routed into functional processing modules (energy calculation, protein quality check, micronutrient balance). The output of all three modules converges at the **RDA Comparator**, which validates the meal against national dietary standards and produces a balanced meal and nutritional label.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Classify the major food groups based on their physiological function in the human body. Provide two examples for each group.*

**Model Answer:**

Based on physiological function, food groups are classified into three categories:

1. **Energy-giving foods:** Foods rich in carbohydrates and fats that supply calories to fuel metabolic activities.
   - *Examples:* Rice (cereal), Groundnut oil (fat).
2. **Body-building foods:** Foods rich in proteins that supply amino acids for tissue growth and repair.
   - *Examples:* Toor dal (pulse), Egg (animal protein).
3. **Protective foods:** Foods rich in vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants that protect the body against diseases.
   - *Examples:* Spinach (green leafy vegetable), Orange (fruit rich in vitamin C).

A balanced diet must contain adequate proportions of all three functional categories to maintain optimal health.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List the five basic food groups recommended by the ICMR for a balanced Indian diet and state the role of green leafy vegetables in preventing nutritional deficiency.*

**Model Answer:**

The **five basic food groups** recommended by the **Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR)** are:

1. Cereals, millets, and their products.
2. Pulses and legumes.
3. Milk and milk products.
4. Vegetables (including green leafy vegetables).
5. Fruits.

**Role of green leafy vegetables (GLV):** Green leafy vegetables such as spinach, amaranth, and fenugreek are rich in **iron, folic acid, vitamin A, and dietary fiber**. Regular consumption of GLV prevents **iron-deficiency anemia** and **night blindness** (vitamin A deficiency), and supports digestive health through fiber intake.

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Classify the major food groups based on their **predominant nutrient composition**, providing suitable examples for each. Discuss the energy contribution of each macronutrient. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mid-day meal served in a school kitchen contains the following items per child:
- Cooked rice: 150 g (P = 3.0 g, C = 39.0 g, F = 0.5 g)
- Sambar (dal-based): 100 g (P = 5.0 g, C = 12.0 g, F = 2.5 g)
- Mixed vegetable curry: 75 g (P = 2.0 g, C = 6.0 g, F = 4.0 g)
- Curd: 50 g (P = 1.5 g, C = 2.0 g, F = 2.0 g)

Calculate the **total energy** of the meal and the **percentage energy distribution** from each macronutrient. Comment on whether the meal is balanced as per ICMR guidelines. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The classification of food groups based on predominant nutrient composition is as follows:

1. **Carbohydrate-rich foods** — Cereals (rice, wheat), millets (ragi, jowar), and sugars (jaggery, cane sugar). These supply **4 kcal/g** and form the bulk of daily energy intake.
2. **Protein-rich foods** — Pulses (toor dal, chana), milk, eggs, meat, and fish. Proteins also yield **4 kcal/g**, but their primary role is tissue building.
3. **Fat-rich foods** — Oils (mustard, groundnut), ghee, butter, and nuts. Fats yield **9 kcal/g** and provide essential fatty acids.
4. **Vitamin and mineral-rich foods** — Fruits, vegetables (especially green leafy types), and milk. These yield negligible energy but are essential for metabolic regulation and disease prevention.

The energy contribution hierarchy is: **Fats (9 kcal/g) > Carbohydrates = Proteins (4 kcal/g each)**.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1: Total macronutrients**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= 3.0 + 5.0 + 2.0 + 1.5 = 11.5 \text{ g} \\
C_{total} &= 39.0 + 12.0 + 6.0 + 2.0 = 59.0 \text{ g} \\
F_{total} &= 0.5 + 2.5 + 4.0 + 2.0 = 9.0 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total energy**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= (4 \times 11.5) + (4 \times 59.0) + (9 \times 9.0) \\
E_{total} &= 46.0 + 236.0 + 81.0 \\
E_{total} &= 363.0 \text{ kcal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Percentage energy distribution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%E_{Protein} &= \frac{46.0}{363.0} \times 100 \approx 12.67\% \\
\%E_{Carbohydrate} &= \frac{236.0}{363.0} \times 100 \approx 65.01\% \\
\%E_{Fat} &= \frac{81.0}{363.0} \times 100 \approx 22.31\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: ICMR Comparison**

| Macronutrient | Computed % | ICMR Recommended % | Verdict |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Carbohydrates | 65.01 % | 50–60 % | Slightly above range |
| Proteins | 12.67 % | 10–15 % | Within range |
| Fats | 22.31 % | 20–30 % | Within range |

The meal is **predominantly carbohydrate-based** (typical of cereal-heavy Indian meals). To bring it closer to the ICMR ideal, the vegetable curry portion should be increased, and the rice quantity can be marginally reduced while adding a fruit or a glass of milk as an accompaniment.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Define a **balanced diet**. Explain the concept of the **basic five food groups** as recommended by the ICMR, with the approximate daily quantities required for a sedentary adult reference man. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between **energy-giving**, **body-building**, and **protective foods** with examples. State the deficiency diseases that may arise if each group is consistently omitted from the diet. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

A **balanced diet** is defined as a diet that provides all essential nutrients — carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, minerals, water, and dietary fiber — in the correct proportion, quantity, and quality to meet the body's physiological needs, support growth, and maintain health.

The **Basic Five Food Group Concept** by the ICMR recommends daily inclusion of the following:

| Food Group | Daily Quantity (Reference Man) | Primary Nutrient |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cereals and millets | $\geq 270$ g | Carbohydrates, Energy |
| Pulses and legumes | $\geq 60$ g | Plant protein, Iron |
| Milk and dairy | $\geq 300$ mL | Calcium, Vit B12, Protein |
| Vegetables (incl. GLV) | $\geq 300$ g | Vitamins, Minerals, Fiber |
| Fruits | $\geq 100$ g | Vitamin C, Antioxidants |

In addition, **fats and oils** ($\geq 20$ g/day) and **sugar** ($\leq 20$ g/day) are used as accessory energy sources. Water intake of $\geq 2$ L/day is also recommended.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

| Category | Function | Examples | Deficiency Disease if Omitted |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energy-giving | Supply calories for metabolic activity | Rice, wheat, sugar, ghee | Marasmus, weight loss, fatigue |
| Body-building | Supply amino acids for tissue synthesis | Pulses, milk, egg, chicken | Kwashiorkor, muscle wasting, stunted growth |
| Protective | Supply vitamins, minerals, antioxidants | Fruits, vegetables, milk | Scurvy, anemia, night blindness, beriberi |

Omitting **energy foods** leads to chronic energy deficiency and marasmus, particularly in children. Omitting **body-building foods** causes protein-energy malnutrition such as kwashiorkor, with symptoms like edema and skin lesions. Omitting **protective foods** results in micronutrient deficiencies — for example, vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy, and iron deficiency causes anemia.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** Always write `g`, `kcal`, and `%` explicitly. A correct number without a unit is awarded **zero marks** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Confusing RDA values for men and women:** The reference man requires **2320 kcal/day** and the reference woman requires **1900 kcal/day**. Mixing these up costs a full sub-part.
> 3. **Omitting examples in Part A questions:** A 3-mark answer without two clear examples is typically marked down to 1.5–2 marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning ICMR in the answer:** Whenever you write a numerical value (e.g., 270 g cereals), cite the source as **ICMR (2020)** or **NIN, Hyderabad**. Examiners award additional credit for source attribution.
> 5. **Skipping the "comment" step in energy problems:** Computing energy is only half the answer. The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **higher-order thinking** (Bloom's Level 5 — Evaluate). You **must** comment on whether the meal is balanced. Skipping this means losing 2–3 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Food groups are systematic classifications of foods based on nutrient composition, function, and origin.
- **Three Classification Frameworks:**
  1. **By nutrient composition** — carbohydrate-rich, protein-rich, fat-rich, vitamin/mineral-rich.
  2. **By physiological function** — energy-giving, body-building, protective.
  3. **By origin** — plant-based and animal-based.
- **ICMR Basic Five Food Groups:** Cereals, Pulses, Milk, Vegetables, Fruits.
- **Calorific Values:** Carbohydrates = 4 kcal/g, Proteins = 4 kcal/g, Fats = 9 kcal/g.
- **ICMR Recommended Macronutrient Distribution:** 50–60 % carbs, 10–15 % protein, 20–30 % fat.
- **RDA for Reference Man:** 2320 kcal/day; **Reference Woman:** 1900 kcal/day.
- **Key Deficiency Diseases:** Marasmus (energy deficiency), Kwashiorkor (protein deficiency), Scurvy (vit C deficiency), Anemia (iron deficiency), Night blindness (vit A deficiency).
- **Balanced Diet Formula:** $E_{total} = (4 \times P) + (4 \times C) + (9 \times F)$ — always cite the source as ICMR.
- **High-Yield Exam Phrases:** "Basic five food groups," "Functional classification," "Protective foods," "Energy-yielding foods," "ICMR 2020 guidelines."
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do not confuse "Body-building foods" with "Energy-giving foods" — pulses are body-building, not energy-giving, even though they contain some calories.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Major Properties of Food
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Major Properties of Food

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What Are "Major Properties of Food"?

In the context of Food Process Engineering, the **Major Properties of Food** refer to the comprehensive set of *intrinsic physical, thermal, rheological, electrical, chemical, and functional attributes* that quantitatively and qualitatively describe how a food material behaves under various processing conditions (heating, cooling, mixing, drying, freezing, extrusion, etc.). These properties act as the **fundamental engineering inputs** required to design, scale-up, and control food processing equipment such as dryers, evaporators, heat exchangers, extruders, and packaging machines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner's Wording):**
> *Major properties of food are those physico-chemical, thermal, and functional characteristics that determine the behaviour of food materials during processing, storage, and handling, and which are essential inputs for engineering calculations and equipment design.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are cooking **dal (lentil soup) at home**:
- The *viscosity* tells you how thick it has become (rheology).
- The *density* tells you how heavy the pot feels now (physical).
- The *thermal conductivity* decides how fast the bottom heats while the top stays cool (thermal).
- The *water activity* decides whether it will spoil overnight on the counter (chemical/microbiological stability).
- The *colour* and *texture* tell you if it is appetizing or overcooked (sensory/functional).

So, every property you intuitively observe in the kitchen has a **measurable engineering parameter** behind it — and that is exactly what this topic formalises.

### 1.3 Classification — The Big Picture

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examinations, properties of food are conventionally classified into **six broad categories**. Memorising this taxonomy is a guaranteed 2-mark short-answer.

| S.No | Property Category | Primary Engineering Use |
|------|-------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Physical | Mass–volume balances, particle sizing, separation |
| 2 | Thermal | Heat transfer calculations, sterilisation, refrigeration |
| 3 | Rheological | Pumping, mixing, extrusion, texture design |
| 4 | Electrical | Microwave / ohmic heating, moisture sensing |
| 5 | Chemical | Preservation, shelf-life, pH control |
| 6 | Functional | Formulation, ingredient selection, product development |

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — Major Properties of Food):**
> Students must be able to define, derive, and apply properties such as **density, porosity, specific gravity, viscosity, thermal conductivity, specific heat, water activity, glass transition temperature, and dielectric properties** for engineering calculations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 A. PHYSICAL PROPERTIES

#### (i) Density (ρ)
- **True Density (ρ_t):** Mass per unit volume of the *solid material itself* — voids excluded.
- **Bulk Density (ρ_b):** Mass per unit volume of the *bulk particulate* — voids included.
- **Particle Density (ρ_p):** Mass per unit volume of a *single particle* — internal pores excluded.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Weigh the food sample → `m (kg)`.
2. Measure true volume by pycnometer (gas/liquid displacement) → `V_t (m³)`.
3. Measure bulk volume by gently filling a known container → `V_b (m³)`.
4. Compute ratios.

#### (ii) Porosity (ε)
Indicates the fraction of void space in a bulk solid — critical for **drying, fluidisation, and storage silo design**.

#### (iii) Specific Gravity (SG)
Ratio of the density of food to the density of water at **4 °C (1000 kg/m³)**. Dimensionless.

#### (iv) Colour
Measured using CIE **L*a*b*** system, tristimulus values, or by simple reflectance spectrophotometry. Crucial for **quality grading** (e.g., tomato paste, roasted coffee).

#### (v) Shape, Size, and Surface Area
Determined by image analysis. Important for **heat/mass transfer** because `Surface Area ∝ 1/Size`.

---

### 2.2 B. THERMAL PROPERTIES

#### (i) Specific Heat (c_p)
Energy required to raise the temperature of **1 kg of food by 1 °C** (kJ/kg·°C).

- For water: `c_p = 4.186 kJ/kg·°C`.
- Foods with high moisture content → c_p close to water.

#### (ii) Thermal Conductivity (k)
Rate of heat conduction through a unit thickness per unit temperature gradient (W/m·°C).

- Frozen foods: `k ≈ 1.0 – 1.5 W/m·°C`.
- Dried foods: `k ≈ 0.05 – 0.25 W/m·°C`.

#### (iii) Thermal Diffusivity (α)
Governs how *fast* temperature changes propagate through food.

$$\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \cdot c_p}$$

Units: **m²/s**.

#### (iv) Latent Heat (L)
Energy absorbed/released during phase change (e.g., freezing of water = **334 kJ/kg**).

#### (v) Glass Transition Temperature (T_g)
The temperature below which an amorphous food (e.g., dried fruit, milk powder) transitions from a rubbery to a glassy (brittle) state. Crucial for **powder caking, crispness, and shelf-life stability**.

---

### 2.3 C. RHEOLOGICAL PROPERTIES

#### (i) Viscosity (μ or η)
Internal friction resisting flow.

- **Newtonian fluids:** μ constant (water, milk, fruit juice).
  $$\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$
- **Non-Newtonian fluids:** μ varies with shear rate (ketchup, melted chocolate, starch slurry).
  - *Pseudoplastic* (shear-thinning): tomato concentrate.
  - *Dilatant* (shear-thickening): concentrated starch suspensions.
  - *Bingham plastic*: mayonnaise, toothpaste.

#### (ii) Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)
Hardness, cohesiveness, springiness, chewiness — measured by texture analyser (TPA test by Bourne, 1978).

---

### 2.4 D. ELECTRICAL PROPERTIES

#### (i) Dielectric Constant (ε′)
Capacity of food to store electromagnetic energy — governs **microwave heating**.

#### (ii) Dielectric Loss Factor (ε″)
Capacity to convert EM energy to heat.

#### (iii) Electrical Conductivity (σ)
Governs **ohmic heating** efficiency (S/m).

---

### 2.5 E. CHEMICAL PROPERTIES

#### (i) pH
Negative log of H⁺ ion activity. Critical for microbial control (Clostridium botulinum inhibited below pH 4.6).

#### (ii) Moisture Content (MC)
Wet basis: `MC_wb = m_w / (m_w + m_s) × 100%`
Dry basis: `MC_db = m_w / m_s × 100%`

#### (iii) Water Activity (a_w)
$$a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0} = \dfrac{ERH\,(\%)}{100}$$

Most bacteria need `a_w > 0.91`; most yeasts `> 0.87`; most moulds `> 0.80`; *xerophilic* moulds down to `0.60`.

---

### 2.6 F. FUNCTIONAL PROPERTIES

Properties that determine how ingredients behave in a **formulated food system**:
- **Hydration properties:** Water Holding Capacity (WHC), Water Binding Capacity (WBC), Swelling Index.
- **Oil Holding Capacity (OHC):** Important in meat and bakery.
- **Emulsifying Capacity (EC) & Emulsion Stability (ES):** Mayonnaise, sauces.
- **Foaming Capacity & Foam Stability:** Egg white, whey proteins.
- **Gelation:** Gel strength, gel point — jellies, surimi, tofu.

---

### 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **compulsory revision sheet** — most KTU numericals are direct substitutions of these formulas.

| # | Property | Formula / Expression | Standard SI Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|----------|----------------------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | True Density | $\rho_t = \dfrac{m}{V_t}$ | **kg/m³** | 1000–1800 for most foods |
| 2 | Bulk Density | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_b}$ | **kg/m³** | 200–800 for grains |
| 3 | Porosity | $\varepsilon = \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}\right)\times 100$ | **%** | 30–60% for granular foods |
| 4 | Specific Gravity | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{food}}{\rho_{water,\,4°C}}$ | Dimensionless | ~0.92–1.6 |
| 5 | Specific Heat | $c_p$ (measured by DSC) | **kJ/kg·°C** | ~3.0–4.2 (high moisture) |
| 6 | Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho\,c_p}$ | **m²/s** | 10⁻⁷ – 10⁻⁶ |
| 7 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho\,v\,D}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless | Flow regime indicator |
| 8 | Water Activity | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | Dimensionless | 0 – 1 |
| 9 | Moisture (wet basis) | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w+m_s}\times 100$ | **%** | 0 – 90% |
| 10 | Moisture (dry basis) | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s}\times 100$ | **%** | 0 – 500% |
| 11 | Viscosity (Newtonian) | $\tau = \mu\,\dot{\gamma}$ | **Pa·s** | Water: 0.001 |
| 12 | Power-Law model | $\tau = K\,\dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ | Pa·sⁿ | n<1 shear-thinning |
| 13 | Bingham plastic | $\tau = \tau_0 + \mu_p\,\dot{\gamma}$ | **Pa** | Yield stress τ₀ |
| 14 | Dielectric Heating | $P = 55.61 \times 10^{-12}\, f\,E^2\,\varepsilon''$ | **W/m³** | Microwave power density |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of these Properties:**
> - **Porosity + Density** → design of silos, hoppers, and pneumatic conveying.
> - **k, c_p, α** → design of heat exchangers, pasteurisers, and freezers.
> - **μ, n, K** → selection of pumps (centrifugal vs. positive displacement), pipe sizing.
> - **a_w, pH** → formulation of preservative systems and shelf-life prediction.
> - **ε′, ε″** → microwave oven power tuning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between True Density, Bulk Density and Porosity

**Statement:** Show that the fractional porosity of a packed food bed is mathematically equal to the complement of the bulk-to-true density ratio.

**Step 1 — Definitions:**

Let:
- `m` = mass of the food sample (kg) — same in both cases.
- `V_t` = true volume (m³) — only solid matrix, no air gaps.
- `V_b` = bulk volume (m³) — solid matrix + air gaps (voids).
- `V_v` = volume of voids (m³).

Therefore:
$$V_b = V_t + V_v$$

**Step 2 — Express densities:**
$$\rho_t = \dfrac{m}{V_t} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V_t = \dfrac{m}{\rho_t}$$

$$\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_b} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V_b = \dfrac{m}{\rho_b}$$

**Step 3 — Void volume:**
$$V_v = V_b - V_t = \dfrac{m}{\rho_b} - \dfrac{m}{\rho_t} = m\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_b} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_t}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Fractional porosity (definition):**
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_v}{V_b}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{m\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_b} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_t}\right)}{m/\rho_b}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify (the m and ρ_b cancel):**
$$\varepsilon = \rho_b \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_b} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_t}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$$

**Step 7 — Final percentage form:**
$$\boxed{\,\varepsilon\,(\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}\right) \times 100\,}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A *low* bulk-to-true density ratio (e.g., 0.4) implies that **60% of the container volume is air** — the food is highly porous (e.g., puffed rice, aerated chocolate). This means rapid moisture migration, easy oxidation, and excellent crispness retention.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Conversion Between Wet-Basis and Dry-Basis Moisture Content

**Step 1 — Define:**

Let:
- `m_w` = mass of water (kg)
- `m_s` = mass of dry solids (kg)
- `m_t` = total mass = `m_w + m_s`

**Step 2 — Wet basis:**
$$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_t}\times 100 = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w+m_s}\times 100$$

**Step 3 — Dry basis:**
$$MC_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s}\times 100$$

**Step 4 — Derive the conversion (algebraic manipulation):**

Start with wet basis:
$$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s+m_w}$$

Divide numerator and denominator by `m_s`:
$$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w/m_s}{1 + m_w/m_s} = \dfrac{MC_{db}}{1+MC_{db}}$$

Solving for `MC_db`:
$$MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{1-MC_{wb}}$$

**Verification (sanity check):** If `MC_wb = 0.5` (50%), then `MC_db = 0.5/0.5 = 1.0 = 100% db`. ✓ (Water mass equals solid mass.)

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Thermal Diffusivity from First Principles

Fourier's law of heat conduction in 1D:
$$q = -k\,\dfrac{dT}{dx}$$

Energy balance on an infinitesimal slab of thickness `dx` over time `dt`:
$$\rho\,c_p\,\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k\,\dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

Rearranging:
$$\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \left(\dfrac{k}{\rho\,c_p}\right)\dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

By definition:
$$\boxed{\,\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho\,c_p}\,}\quad \text{(m}^2/\text{s)}$$

**Physical meaning:** α is the *rate constant* of temperature propagation. A **high α** (e.g., liquid water ≈ 1.4 × 10⁻⁷ m²/s) means food heats/cools quickly; a **low α** (e.g., dry bread ≈ 1.0 × 10⁻⁷ m²/s) means sluggish response.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Property Ranges for Common Foods (KTU 2024 Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a frequently-tested recall question (Part-A, 3 marks).

| Food Material | Moisture (%wb) | a_w | pH | k (W/m·°C) | c_p (kJ/kg·°C) | Behaviour Class |
|---------------|----------------|-----|-----|------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Fresh Apple | 84 | 0.98 | 3.4 | 0.55 | 3.80 | High-moisture, Newtonian |
| Whole Milk | 87 | 0.99 | 6.7 | 0.55 | 3.90 | Newtonian |
| Honey | 17 | 0.55 | 4.0 | 0.50 | 2.40 | Glassy / supersaturated |
| Wheat Flour | 12 | 0.70 | 6.0 | 0.20 | 1.80 | Granular, porous |
| Butter | 16 | 0.94 | — | 0.20 | 2.10 | Plastic, emulsion |
| Tomato Ketchup | 70 | 0.95 | 3.8 | — | 3.50 | Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning) |
| Frozen Beef | 70 | 0.97 | 5.6 | 1.50 | 2.10 | Solid, fibrous |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

**Problem:**
A bulk pack of **cornflakes** occupies a volume of `2.0 × 10⁻³ m³` and weighs `0.16 kg`. The true density of the cornflake particle, measured by a pycnometer, is `1300 kg/m³`. Calculate:
1. Bulk density
2. True density (given)
3. Porosity (in %)
4. Comment on the implications for moisture migration during storage.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- `m = 0.16 kg`
- `V_b = 2.0 × 10⁻³ m³`
- `ρ_t = 1300 kg/m³`

**(1) Bulk Density:**
$$\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_b} = \dfrac{0.16}{2.0\times 10^{-3}} = 80\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

**(2) True Density (given):** `ρ_t = 1300 kg/m³`.

**(3) Porosity:**
$$\varepsilon = \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}\right)\times 100 = \left(1 - \dfrac{80}{1300}\right)\times 100$$

$$= \left(1 - 0.0615\right)\times 100 = 93.85\%$$

**(4) Engineering Comment:**
A porosity of **~94%** indicates that cornflakes are highly aerated with an extremely open cellular matrix. This is **desirable for crispness**, but during storage it implies:
- Very rapid moisture uptake from humid air (`a_w` quickly rises above 0.70 → loss of crispness, termed *starch retrogradation-induced staling*).
- Rapid oxidation of exposed lipids → *rancidity*.
- Hence, cornflakes require **hermetic, moisture-barrier packaging** with desiccant sachets (silica gel) for shelf-life.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Classification of Major Properties of Food

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **taxonomy of food properties** along with their **downstream engineering applications**, replacing a static concept map.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MAJOR PROPERTIES OF FOOD"] --> B["PHYSICAL PROPERTIES"]
    A --> C["THERMAL PROPERTIES"]
    A --> D["RHEOLOGICAL PROPERTIES"]
    A --> E["ELECTRICAL PROPERTIES"]
    A --> F["CHEMICAL PROPERTIES"]
    A --> G["FUNCTIONAL PROPERTIES"]

    B --> B1["True Density"]
    B --> B2["Bulk Density"]
    B --> B3["Porosity"]
    B --> B4["Specific Gravity"]
    B --> B5["Colour and Size"]

    C --> C1["Specific Heat cp"]
    C --> C2["Thermal Conductivity k"]
    C --> C3["Thermal Diffusivity alpha"]
    C --> C4["Latent Heat L"]
    C --> C5["Glass Transition Tg"]

    D --> D1["Viscosity mu"]
    D --> D2["Shear Stress tau"]
    D --> D3["Texture TPA"]
    D --> D4["Consistency Index K"]
    D --> D5["Flow Behaviour Index n"]

    E --> E1["Dielectric Constant eps prime"]
    E --> E2["Dielectric Loss eps double prime"]
    E --> E3["Electrical Conductivity sigma"]

    F --> F1["pH"]
    F --> F2["Water Activity aw"]
    F --> F3["Moisture Content MC"]
    F --> F4["Titratable Acidity"]

    G --> G1["Water Holding Capacity WHC"]
    G --> G2["Oil Holding Capacity OHC"]
    G --> G3["Emulsifying Capacity EC"]
    G --> G4["Foaming Capacity FC"]
    G --> G5["Gelation Strength"]

    B1 --> APP1["Mass-Volume Balance"]
    B3 --> APP1
    B4 --> APP1
    C1 --> APP2["Heat Exchanger Design"]
    C2 --> APP2
    C3 --> APP2
    C5 --> APP2
    D1 --> APP3["Pump and Pipe Sizing"]
    D4 --> APP3
    D5 --> APP3
    E1 --> APP4["Microwave Oven Tuning"]
    E2 --> APP4
    F1 --> APP5["Shelf Life Prediction"]
    F2 --> APP5
    F3 --> APP5
    G1 --> APP6["Product Formulation"]
    G3 --> APP6
    G5 --> APP6

    APP1 --> ENG["FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT DESIGN"]
    APP2 --> ENG
    APP3 --> ENG
    APP4 --> ENG
    APP5 --> ENG
    APP6 --> ENG
```

### 4.2 Functional Mapping — How Properties Feed Into Process Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["FOOD PROPERTY INPUTS"]
        P1["Density and Porosity"]
        P2["Thermal k, cp, alpha"]
        P3["Viscosity mu"]
        P4["Water Activity aw"]
    end

    subgraph DESIGN["ENGINEERING DESIGN"]
        D1["Dryer Sizing"]
        D2["Heat Exchanger"]
        D3["Pump Selection"]
        D4["Shelf Life Model"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["PROCESS OUTPUTS"]
        O1["Moisture Removal Rate"]
        O2["Sterilisation Time F0"]
        O3["Pressure Drop dP"]
        O4["Expiry Date Labelling"]
    end

    P1 --> D1 --> O1
    P2 --> D2 --> O2
    P3 --> D3 --> O3
    P4 --> D4 --> O4
```

### 4.3 Behaviour Map — Rheological Flow Classes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["RHEOLOGICAL BEHAVIOUR"] --> R1["Newtonian: tau = mu dot gamma"]
    R --> R2["Pseudoplastic: tau = K dot gamma power n, n less than 1"]
    R --> R3["Dilatant: tau = K dot gamma power n, n greater than 1"]
    R --> R4["Bingham Plastic: tau = tau0 plus mu_p dot gamma"]
    R --> R5["Casson Plastic: square root tau equals square root tau0 plus square root mu_c dot gamma"]

    R1 --> EX1["Example: Water, Milk, Fruit Juice"]
    R2 --> EX2["Example: Tomato Puree, Blood, Melted Chocolate"]
    R3 --> EX3["Example: Cornstarch Slurry, Wet Sand"]
    R4 --> EX4["Example: Toothpaste, Mayonnaise, Yogurt"]
    R5 --> EX5["Example: Cocoa Suspension, Printing Ink"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define the following terms with one industrial application each:**
**(a) Water activity (a_w)**
**(b) Glass transition temperature (T_g)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Water Activity (a_w):**
Water activity is defined as the **ratio of the partial pressure of water vapour in the food (p) to the saturation vapour pressure of pure water (p₀) at the same temperature**.

$$a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0} = \dfrac{ERH\,(\%)}{100}$$

It is a dimensionless number ranging from **0 to 1**. Industrial application: Used as a **critical control parameter in HACCP** for predicting shelf-life of bakery products, dried fruits, and intermediate-moisture foods (IMF). For example, reducing `a_w` below 0.60 prevents mould growth in dried fruits.

**(b) Glass Transition Temperature (T_g):**
`T_g` is the temperature at which an amorphous food material transitions from a **rubbery (flexible) state to a glassy (brittle, rigid) state** as it is cooled, or vice versa as it is heated.

Industrial application: Determining the **storage temperature for freeze-dried products, milk powders, and crispy snacks** — storage must be kept *below T_g* to preserve crispness and prevent caking.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|---------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Definition | Viscosity (μ) is **constant** and independent of shear rate | Viscosity (η) **varies** with applied shear rate |
| Rheogram | Straight line through origin | Curved (or with yield stress) |
| Equation | `τ = μ · γ̇` | `τ = K · γ̇ⁿ` (power-law general) |
| Examples | Water, milk, fruit juice, refined oils | Tomato ketchup (pseudoplastic), cornstarch slurry (dilatant), mayonnaise (Bingham plastic) |
| Industrial consequence | Easy pump sizing using Hagen-Poiseuille equation | Requires *apparent viscosity* and use of nomograms; selection of positive-displacement pumps |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **Module-1 long-answer questions** carry **14 marks each** with sub-parts `(a) 7 marks` and `(b) 7 marks`.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
#### **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 Set A]**

**(a) Derive the relationship between bulk density, true density, and porosity of a granular food material. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given definitions:**
- `ρ_b = m / V_b` (bulk density — solid + voids)
- `ρ_t = m / V_t` (true density — solid only)
- `V_v` = volume of voids = `V_b − V_t`

**Derivation:**
$$V_b = V_t + V_v \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V_v = V_b - V_t$$
