```mermaid
    I --> K
    J --> L[BY PRODUCT OIL CAKE]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the three outflow channels — **Bran (fiber + vitamins)**, **Endosperm product (flour/rice)**, and **Germ (oil)**. This represents how milling **fractionates** the grain into distinct commercial products.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    F --> G[Polishing]
    G --> H[Grading and Sorting]
    H --> I[White Rice Final Product]

    E -.->|Byproduct| J[Rice Husk]

    K -.->|Further Processing| L[Rice Bran Oil]
```

> **Engineering Insight:** Each block represents a **unit operation**. The **paddy separator** is critical — it recovers unhusked grains using a **gravity table** based on density differences between paddy (heavier) and brown rice (lighter).

> **Application Note:** The **HTST extrusion process** (block G) operates at **150–200°C** for **15–30 seconds** under high pressure, instantly gelatinizing starch and producing a porous, crunchy texture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C[Tempering Conditioning]
    C --> D[Break Rolls First Stage]
    D --> E[Sifting and Classification]

    F --> G[Reduction Rolls]
    G --> H[Final Flour Stream]
    H --> I[Packaging]

    E -.->|Oversized| J[Germ and Bran Streams]
    J --> K[Animal Feed Byproduct]
```

> **Tempering Note:** The **conditioning step (Block C)** adds **2–4% moisture** to the wheat and lets it rest for **12–24 hours**. This **plasticizes the bran** (making it tough and flexible) and **loosens the endosperm** (making it brittle), thereby improving flour separation efficiency.

# 🎯 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Cereals with Examples** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Cereals are edible grains belonging to the **Gramineae (Poaceae) family** of monocotyledonous plants. They are characterized by a **starchy endosperm** as the major edible portion and form the staple food of a major part of the world's population. 

**Examples:** Rice (*Oryza sativa*), Wheat (*Triticum aestivum*), Maize (*Zea mays*), Barley (*Hordeum vulgare*), Sorghum (*Sorghum bicolor*), and Pearl Millet (*Pennisetum glaucum*).

**Classification based on growing season:**
- **Kharif (Monsoon):** Rice, Maize, Jowar (Sorghum), Bajra (Pearl Millet)
- **Rabi (Winter):** Wheat, Barley, Oats, Rye

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate Between Endosperm and Germ in a Cereal Grain** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Feature** | **Endosperm** | **Germ (Embryo)** |
|-------------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Position** | Central, bulk of the grain | Small, attached to the base |
| **Composition** | **70–80% starch**, 8–12% protein | **30% lipid**, vitamins, enzymes |
| **Function in grain** | Energy reserve for the seedling | Future plant (embryo) |
| **Function in food** | Main source of flour, semolina | Source of edible oil and vitamin E |
| **Processing** | Retained in refined flour | Removed during milling to extend shelf life |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** divided into sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. Students must answer either the **OR (a)/OR (b)** alternative fully.

---

### **📌 Question A: Comprehensive Question on Cereal Structure & Composition** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. A (a) Describe the anatomical structure of a typical cereal grain with the help of a neat labeled diagram. List the major chemical components present in each part. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The cereal grain (e.g., wheat) is anatomically divided into **three main parts**: the **pericarp** (outer covering), the **endosperm** (starchy bulk), and the **germ** (embryo).

**(i) Pericarp (Husk/Hull):** The outermost protective layer comprising the epicarp, mesocarp, and endocarp. It is rich in **cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin**. In some cereals (rice, oats), it is firmly attached and must be removed by dehulling.

**(ii) Seed Coat (Testa):** A thin pigmented layer beneath the pericarp. In red sorghum, it contains **tannins** which influence taste and nutritional quality.

**(iii) Aleurone Layer:** A single-celled protein-rich layer beneath the seed coat, containing **15–20% protein, B-vitamins (thiamine, niacin), and minerals**. It is often classified as part of the "bran" in milling.

**(iv) Endosperm (Starchy):** The largest portion (about **80–85%** of the grain weight), composed of **large and small starch granules** embedded in a protein matrix (containing **glutenin and gliadin** in wheat). It is the principal source of refined flour.

**(v) Embryo (Germ):** A small portion (**2–3%** of grain weight) comprising the **scutellum, plumule, and radicle**. It is rich in **lipids (30%), proteins, and Vitamin E**. It is usually removed during milling to prevent **oxidative rancidity** in flour.

> **Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 (Mermaid block diagram of grain anatomy).

---

**Q. A (b) Explain the proximate composition of cereals. Why is the protein content of wheat and rice different from that of millets? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Proximate Composition** refers to the major macronutrient and micronutrient constituents of food, including **moisture, carbohydrates, proteins, fats, fiber, and minerals (ash)**.

**Typical proximate composition of major cereals (per 100 g):**
- **Moisture:** 10–14%
- **Carbohydrates (mainly starch):** 65–78%
- **Proteins:** 7–12%
- **Fats:** 1.5–5%
- **Crude Fiber:** 0.9–2.8%
- **Minerals (ash):** 1.2–2.3%

**Difference in Protein Content — Why?**

- **Wheat (11.8%) and Rice (7.5%):** These are "**elite cereals**" with most of the protein concentrated in the **endosperm** in the form of **gluten (gliadin + glutenin)**. The protein is primarily **prolamin-type**, optimized for **baking quality** in wheat and **easy digestibility** in rice.

- **Millets (11.6%) and Sorghum (10.4%):** These are "**coarse cereals**" with proteins concentrated in the **aleurone and germ layers** rather than the endosperm. The protein is primarily **kafirin** (in sorghum) and **pennisetin** (in pearl millet) — **prolamin-type but lacking gluten-forming properties**.

> **Conclusion:** Millets have **higher overall protein content** but lack the **functional gluten network** of wheat, making them unsuitable for bread but excellent for **gluten-free products and diabetic-friendly foods**.

---

### **📌 Question B: Comprehensive Question on Cereal Processing** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. B (a) Explain the parboiling of rice in detail. What are the advantages and disadvantages of parboiling? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Parboiling** is a **hydrothermal treatment** of paddy rice aimed at improving its **milling quality, nutritional value, and storage life**. It is widely practiced in **India, Bangladesh, and Southeast Asia**.

**Steps of Parboiling:**

1. **Soaking:** Paddy is soaked in water at **65–70°C** for **3–4 hours** (hot soaking) or at room temperature for **24–36 hours** (cold soaking) until moisture reaches **30–35%**.

2. **Steaming:** The soaked paddy is then **steamed at atmospheric pressure** (or under 1–2 kg/cm² pressure) for **10–20 minutes**. This **gelatinizes the starch** in the endosperm and **migrates nutrients from the bran to the endosperm**.

3. **Drying:** The steamed paddy is dried back to **12–14% moisture** (w.b.) using **sun drying or mechanical dryers**.

**Advantages:**
- **Higher milling yield (5–7% more)** because the gelatinized endosperm becomes **hard and resistant to breakage**.
- **Improved nutritional quality** — vitamins (especially thiamine) and minerals **migrate from bran to endosperm** during steaming and are retained after milling.
- **Reduced breakage** during dehusking.
- **Better storage stability** due to reduced oil migration from germ to endosperm.
- **Improved cooking quality** — the rice is **less sticky** and has a **firmer texture**.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Color change** — the rice develops a **yellowish/amber hue** (due to Maillard browning) which is undesirable for premium white rice markets.
- **Longer processing time** and **higher energy cost**.
- **Cracking** if drying is not done carefully.

---

**Q. B (b) Define milling. Compare dry milling and wet milling of maize with suitable applications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Milling** is the process of **removing the husk, bran, and germ** from a cereal grain and **reducing the endosperm to a granular or powdered form** suitable for human consumption.

**Dry Milling of Maize:**
- Whole maize is cleaned, conditioned, and passed through **dehullers, roller mills, and sifters**.
- Products: **Maize grits, maize flour, maize meal, and germ** (separated for oil extraction).
- **Applications:** Snacks, breakfast cereals, tortillas, animal feed.

**Wet Milling of Maize:**
- Maize is **steeped in dilute sulfurous acid (0.1–0.2% SO₂)** at **48–52°C for 24–48 hours** to soften the kernel and separate components.
- The steeped kernels are then **cracked, germ-separated, screened, centrifuged, and dried**.
- Products: **Pure maize starch, maize gluten meal, maize oil, and corn syrup / high-fructose corn syrup**.
- **Applications:** Starch industry, sweeteners, brewing adjunct, bioethanol, biodegradable plastics.

**Comparative Table:**

| **Feature** | **Dry Milling** | **Wet Milling** |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Process** | Mechanical size reduction after conditioning | Steeping + fractionation |
| **End Products** | Grits, flour, meal, germ | Pure starch, gluten, oil, syrup |
| **Capital Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Energy Use** | Lower | Higher |
| **Purity of Starch** | Lower (60–70%) | Higher (>99%) |
| **Application** | Food, feed, brewing | Industrial starch, sweeteners |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Cereals" with "Millets":** Students often write millets as cereals only. **Millets ARE cereals** (they belong to Poaceae), but they are classified separately as "**coarse cereals**" or "**nutri-cereals**" in the Indian National Food Security Act.
> 
> 2. **Gluten Myth:** Writing "rice contains gluten" is a **fatal mistake**. Rice, maize, and millets are **gluten-free**.
> 
> 3. **Proximate Composition:** Students forget to mention **moisture** as a proximate component. The standard proximate analysis always includes **moisture, ash, fat, protein, fiber, and carbohydrates**.
> 
> 4. **Unit Errors in Numerical:** Always specify whether moisture content is on **wet basis or dry basis**. KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** if this is not mentioned.
> 
> 5. **Diagram Labeling:** In Part B (a), a **neat labeled diagram is mandatory** for full 7 marks. Always draw the **three main parts** (husk, endosperm, germ) with sub-layers.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this section for last-minute revision before the KTU exam.**

### ✅ Core Concepts to Memorize
- Cereals belong to the **Poaceae (Gramineae) family** — monocotyledonous.
- **Big Three:** Rice, Wheat, Maize (account for ~80% of global cereal production).
- A cereal grain has **3 main parts**: Pericarp (husk), Endosperm (starchy), Embryo (germ).
- **Gluten** = Gliadin (extensibility) + Glutenin (elasticity) — **unique to wheat** (and related species like rye, barley).
- **Proximate Composition** (typical): 12% moisture, 70–78% starch, 7–12% protein, 1.5–5% fat.
- **Parboiling** increases milling yield by **5–7%** and retains vitamins in the endosperm.
- **Malting** activates **α-amylase and β-amylase** enzymes in barley for brewing.
- **Safe storage moisture** for cereals: **≤ 13% (w.b.)** to prevent fungal growth.
- **Conversion formulas:** $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{MC_{db}}{1 + MC_{db}}$ and $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}}$.
- **Kjeldahl protein formula:** Uses a **conversion factor of 5.7 for wheat** and **6.25 for general foods**.

### 🎯 KTU 2024 Likely Short-Answer Topics
1. Define cereals with two examples.
2. Difference between rice and wheat flour.
3. Define parboiling — list two advantages.
4. What is gluten? Why is rice gluten-free?
5. Draw a labeled diagram of a wheat grain.
6. List four primary processing techniques of cereals.
7. Mention the critical moisture content for safe storage of cereals.
8. Differentiate between dry and wet milling.

### 🏆 KTU 2024 Likely Long-Answer Topics
1. Structure of a cereal grain with composition of each layer.
2. Parboiling — process, advantages, and disadvantages.
3. Milling of wheat/rice with flow diagram.
4. Proximate composition of cereals with nutritional significance.
5. Malting of barley — process and applications.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Tip for KTU Aspirants:** Always conclude your Part B answers with a **practical application line** (e.g., "These properties make parboiled rice ideal for export markets in Africa and the Middle East"). This demonstrates **application-oriented thinking** and earns **extra marks** in KTU 2024 scheme ESE (End Semester Examination).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pulses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pulses** are the dried edible seeds of plants belonging to the family **Fabaceae (Leguminosae)**. They are harvested solely for their dry grain, distinguishing them from other legumes that are consumed in their immature, green, or vegetable form (e.g., green peas, fresh beans).

As per the **Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO)** and the **Codex Alimentarius**, pulses are defined as **"annual leguminous crops yielding grains harvested exclusively in their dry form"**, with moisture content typically below **12-14%**. This excludes green beans, soybeans (used for oil), groundnuts, and fresh peas, which are botanically legumes but functionally classified as vegetables or oilseeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pulse processing forms the **second-largest segment** of India's food processing industry, after cereals. The country contributes approximately **23-25%** of global pulse production and consumes nearly **30%** of the world's pulses, making this a high-weightage module.

## 1.2 Botanical Classification

The primary pulse crops studied under KTU Module 1 include:

| Pulse Type | Botanical Name | Family |
|---|---|---|
| Chickpea (Bengal Gram) | *Cicer arietinum* | Fabaceae |
| Pigeon Pea (Red Gram) | *Cajanus cajan* | Fabaceae |
| Green Gram (Moong) | *Vigna radiata* | Fabaceae |
| Black Gram (Urad) | *Vigna mungo* | Fabaceae |
| Lentil (Masoor) | *Lens culinaris* | Fabaceae |
| Cowpea | *Vigna unguiculata* | Fabaceae |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of pulses as nature's protein capsules.** Just like a pharmaceutical capsule protects its medicinal powder inside a hard shell, a pulse grain wraps its high-protein cotyledon inside a tough, fibrous seed coat (husk). Before we can "digest" the nutrition effectively, we need to **process** (mill/dehull) it — analogous to breaking open a capsule to release the active ingredient.

In real-world terms, the **seed coat** is the protective packaging, the **cotyledons** are the nutrient warehouse, and the **germ** is the embryonic sprout. Industrial pulse processing essentially separates these three components and transforms the cotyledons into **dal (split pulse)** for human consumption.

## 1.4 Nutritional Significance

Pulses are globally recognized as **"poor man's meat"** due to their:

- **Protein content:** Typically **20-30%** (much higher than cereals at 7-12%)
- **Lysine-rich amino acid profile:** Complements cereal proteins (lysine is the limiting amino acid in rice/wheat)
- **Dietary fiber:** **15-25%** in raw form
- **Low glycemic index:** **29-38** (suitable for diabetic diets)
- **Micronutrients:** B-vitamins, folate, iron (3-8 mg/100g), zinc, and potassium

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **complementary protein concept** is critical: when cereals (low in lysine) are combined with pulses (low in methionine/cystine), the resulting amino acid profile closely matches animal protein quality. This is why **dal-roti** is the cornerstone of Indian nutrition.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Pulse Grain

A pulse grain has three distinct morphological parts, each with distinct composition and processing relevance:

### A. Seed Coat (Husk/Testa)
- **Composition:** Primarily **cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin**
- **Function:** Protective barrier against insects, microbes, and mechanical damage
- **Color determinants:** Pigmented varieties (black, red, brown) contain polyphenols and tannins
- **Processing relevance:** Removed during dehulling to improve digestibility and palatability

### B. Cotyledons (Storage Tissue)
- **Composition:** **Starch granules embedded in a protein matrix**
- **Storage proteins:** Globulins (legumin and vicilin) account for **70-80%** of total pulse proteins
- **Processing relevance:** Forms the major edible portion (dal)

### C. Embryo (Germ)
- **Composition:** High in **lipids, enzymes, and vitamins**
- **Processing relevance:** Often removed during milling; contains anti-nutritional factors

## 2.2 Anti-Nutritional Factors (ANFs)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner Alert:** Questions on ANFs are consistently asked (2-3 marks). Memorize the factor, its effect, and the processing method that destroys it.

| ANF | Chemical Class | Effect | Destruction Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trypsin Inhibitor** | Proteinaceous | Blocks protein digestion | **Heat (boiling/autoclaving)** |
| **Phytic Acid** | Phosphorus compound | Binds minerals (Zn, Fe, Ca) | **Soaking + fermentation, malting** |
| **Lectins/Haemagglutinins** | Glycoprotein | Causes RBC agglutination | **Wet heat (boiling 10 min)** |
| **Tannins / Polyphenols** | Phenolic | Reduces protein digestibility | **Dehulling, soaking** |
| **Saponins** | Glycosides | Bitter taste | **Soaking, cooking** |
| **Oligosaccharides (Raffinose, Stachyose)** | Carbohydrates | Causes flatulence | **Germination, fermentation** |
| **L-DOPA** | Amino acid derivative | Causes toxicity in *Mucuna* | **Soaking + boiling** |

## 2.3 Pulse Processing Operations

### 2.3.1 Primary Processing (Cleaning & Grading)
1. **Cleaning:** Removal of foreign matter, stones, immature grains
2. **Grading:** Separation based on size (using indented cylinders, gravity separators)
3. **Drying:** Reduction of moisture to safe storage level (<12%)

### 2.3.2 Secondary Processing (Milling)

**Pulse milling** is the most critical industrial operation and involves:

```
Step 1: Pre-conditioning → Step 2: Pitting → Step 3: Dehulling → Step 4: Splitting
```

**Key Sub-Operations Explained:**

1. **Pre-conditioning:** 
   - **Drying to 10-12% moisture** (or sun-drying)
   - **Loosening of husk** via tempering with water (1-3% addition)
   - **Oil/water application** for difficult-to-mill pulses (e.g., pigeon pea)

2. **Pitting:**
   - Mechanical abrasion using **emery rollers** or **stone rollers**
   - Cracks the seed coat without breaking the cotyledon
   - Critical for **lathyrus (khesari)** and **tur** (pigeon pea)

3. **Dehulling:**
   - Removal of loosened husk via **aspiration** or **centrifugal separators**
   - Achieved through **rubber rollers** rotating at differential speeds

4. **Splitting:**
   - Cotyledons split along the natural cleavage plane
   - Produces **dal** (split pulse) and **chilka** (split with husk)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU University Exam-relevant.** Master the units and boundary conditions.

| Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units / Normal Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Moisture Content (Wet Basis)** | $MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | **% w.b.** |
| **Moisture Content (Dry Basis)** | $MC_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | **% d.b.** |
| **Hydration Capacity** | $HC = \frac{W_s - W_0}{W_0}$ | **g water/g dry pulse** (typical: 0.8-1.2) |
| **Hydration Index** | $HI = \frac{HC_{sample}}{HC_{control}}$ | **Dimensionless** |
| **Swelling Capacity** | $SC = \frac{V_s - V_0}{V_0}$ | **mL/g** |
| **Cooking Time** | $t_c$ until soft | **minutes** (15-90 min) |
| **Protein Content (Kjeldahl)** | $\%N = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 14}{W} \times 100$ | **% protein = %N × 6.25** |
| **Dehulling Efficiency** | $\eta_d = \frac{\text{Mass of dehulled grains}}{\text{Total mass input}} \times 100$ | **%** (target: >85%) |
| **Dal Yield** | $Y = \frac{\text{Mass of dal}}{\text{Mass of whole pulse}} \times 100$ | **%** (typical: 70-78%) |
| **Hard-to-Cook (HTC) Defect** | Cooking time increase | **>2× normal = HTC** |
| **Water Absorption Ratio** | $WAR = \frac{W_{hydrated}}{W_{dry}}$ | **Ratio** (typical: 1.8-2.2) |
| **1000-Grain Weight (Test Weight)** | $TW = \frac{W_{1000\,grains}}{1000}$ | **g** |
| **Bulk Density** | $\rho_b = \frac{M}{V}$ | **kg/m³** |

### Conversion Relationships

The relationship between wet and dry basis moisture content:

$$MC_{db} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{1 - \frac{MC_{wb}}{100}}$$

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{MC_{db}}{1 + \frac{MC_{db}}{100}}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pulse processing technology has direct applications in:

- **Industrial dal mills** (capacity: 1-100 tonnes/day)
- **Public Distribution System (PDS)** quality control
- **Export commodity grading** under AGMARK standards
- **Ready-to-eat food formulations** (instant dal, canned pulses)
- **Protein fortification** programs for cereal-based diets
- **Astronaut and military rations** (pre-cooked pulse powders)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion of Wet Basis to Dry Basis Moisture Content

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**. Understand every step.

### Given:
- Let $W_w$ = weight of wet sample (g)
- Let $W_d$ = weight of dry sample (g)
- Weight of water = $W_w - W_d$

### Step 1: Define Wet Basis Moisture

By definition, moisture content on a wet basis expresses moisture as a percentage of the **total wet weight**:

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$

### Step 2: Define Dry Basis Moisture

Dry basis moisture expresses moisture as a percentage of the **dry solid weight only**:

$$MC_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$$

### Step 3: Establish the Ratio

Dividing the two expressions:

$$\frac{MC_{db}}{MC_{wb}} = \frac{\dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}}{\dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w}} = \frac{W_w}{W_d}$$

### Step 4: Express W_w in terms of W_d

From the ratio above:

$$W_w = W_d \times \frac{MC_{db}}{MC_{wb}}$$

### Step 5: Substitute and Solve

We know the moisture content on dry basis can also be written as:

$$MC_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}$$

Substituting $W_w$:

$$\begin{aligned}
MC_{db} &= \frac{\left(W_d \times \frac{MC_{db}}{MC_{wb}}\right) - W_d}{W_d} \\
MC_{db} &= \frac{W_d \left(\dfrac{MC_{db}}{MC_{wb}} - 1\right)}{W_d} \\
MC_{db} &= \frac{MC_{db}}{MC_{wb}} - 1
\end{aligned}$$

Rearranging:

$$1 + MC_{db} = \frac{MC_{db}}{MC_{wb}}$$

$$\boxed{MC_{wb} = \frac{MC_{db}}{1 + \dfrac{MC_{db}}{100}}}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\boxed{MC_{db} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{1 - \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{100}}}$$

### Step 6: Verification Example

**Problem:** A pulse sample has moisture content of **10% (w.b.)**. Find the moisture content on dry basis.

**Solution:**

$$MC_{db} = \frac{10}{1 - \dfrac{10}{100}} = \frac{10}{0.9} = 11.11\% \text{ (d.b.)}$$

**Interpretation:** As expected, dry-basis moisture is always numerically higher than wet-basis moisture because the denominator is smaller (dry weight vs. total weight).

## 3.2 Derivation: Hydration Capacity Relationship

### Given:
- $W_0$ = initial dry weight of pulse grain (g)
- $W_s$ = weight after soaking for time $t$ (g)
- $W_{water}$ = absorbed water = $W_s - W_0$

### Step 1: Definition
$$HC = \frac{W_s - W_0}{W_0}$$

### Step 2: Time-Dependent Hydration Model

Pulse hydration follows the **Peleg model** (an empirical two-parameter model):

$$M(t) = M_0 + \frac{t}{k_1 + k_2 \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $M(t)$ = moisture at time $t$ (g water/g dry solid)
- $M_0$ = initial moisture content
- $k_1$ = Peleg rate constant (min · g dry / g water)
- $k_2$ = Peleg capacity constant (g dry / g water)

### Step 3: Limit Analysis

As $t \to \infty$:

$$M_{\infty} = M_0 + \frac{1}{k_2}$$

Therefore, the **equilibrium hydration capacity** is:

$$\boxed{HC_{eq} = \frac{1}{k_2} = M_{\infty} - M_0}$$

### Step 4: Worked Example

**Problem:** 100 g of tur dal is soaked. After 4 hours, weight becomes 195 g. Calculate hydration capacity.

**Solution:**

$$HC = \frac{195 - 100}{100} = \frac{95}{100} = 0.95 \text{ g water/g dal}$$

This falls within the normal range for pigeon pea (0.85-1.05).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Quality Parameters Across Common Pulses

| Parameter | Chickpea | Pigeon Pea | Green Gram | Black Gram | Lentil |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Protein (%)** | 19-22 | 20-22 | 21-24 | 22-25 | 23-26 |
| **Carbohydrate (%)** | 60-62 | 58-62 | 56-60 | 58-62 | 60-65 |
| **Fiber (%)** | 4-6 | 5-7 | 4-5 | 4-5 | 3-4 |
| **Fat (%)** | 5-7 | 2-3 | 1-1.5 | 1-1.5 | 1-2 |
| **Calcium (mg/100g)** | 105-150 | 70-100 | 75-90 | 110-130 | 25-35 |
| **Iron (mg/100g)** | 4-5 | 2.5-3.5 | 3-4 | 3.5-4.5 | 5-7 |
| **Cooking Time (min)** | 60-90 | 45-60 | 25-35 | 30-45 | 15-25 |
| **Hydration Capacity** | 0.95-1.10 | 0.85-1.05 | 0.90-1.05 | 0.85-1.00 | 0.80-0.95 |
| **Dal Yield (%)** | 75-78 | 70-74 | 76-80 | 74-78 | 78-82 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the **inverse relationship** between cooking time and dal yield — lentil cooks fastest but has the lowest protein density among common pulses.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pulse Grain Anatomy (Functional Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **anatomical structure of a pulse grain** and maps the functional components relevant to food processing:

```mermaid
graph TD
    PG[Pulse Grain]
    PG --> SC[Seed Coat Husk]
    PG --> HY[Hilum]
    PG --> EM[Embryo Germ]
    PG --> CT1[Cotyledon 1]
    PG --> CT2[Cotyledon 2]

    SC --> SC1[Cellulose]
    SC --> SC2[Hemicellulose]
    SC --> SC3[Lignin]
    SC --> SC4[Polyphenols Pigments]

    EM --> EM1[Lipids]
    EM --> EM2[Enzymes]
    EM --> EM3[Vitamins]

    CT1 --> CT1A[Starch Granules]
    CT1 --> CT1B[Storage Proteins Globulins]
    CT1 --> CT1C[Dietary Fiber]

    CT2 --> CT2A[Starch Granules]
    CT2 --> CT2B[Storage Proteins Globulins]
    CT2 --> CT2C[Mineral Salts]

    CT1B --> GL1[Legumin 11S]
    CT1B --> GL2[Vicilin 7S]

    style PG fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style SC fill:#D2B48C,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px
```
