```mermaid
    PH["Food pH Scale 0 to 14"]
    PH --> ACID["Acidic Foods pH less than 4.5"]
    PH --> MED["Medium Acid pH 4.5 to 5.3"]
    PH --> LOWACID["Low Acid pH greater than 5.3"]
    ACID --> MOLDS["Moulds Yeasts Grow"]
    ACID --> BACINH["Bacteria Inhibited"]
    MED --> CLOS["Clostridium botulinum Inhibited"]
    LOWACID --> ALL["All Microbes Grow including Pathogens"]
    ALL --> STERIL["Requires Full Sterilization"]
    MED --> HTST["Requires HTST Pasteurization"]
    ACID --> PAST["Mild Pasteurization Adequate"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> Define water activity (aw). List the four categories of factors affecting microbial growth in foods with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1.5 marks):* Water activity (aw) is defined as the ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water in a food substrate ($P$) to the vapour pressure of pure water ($P_0$) at the same temperature. It is a dimensionless measure of the availability of free water for microbial growth and is numerically equal to the Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH) divided by 100.

*Four Categories (1.5 marks):*

1. **Intrinsic factors** — e.g., pH of milk.
2. **Extrinsic factors** — e.g., storage temperature of frozen meat.
3. **Processing factors** — e.g., pasteurization of fruit juice.
4. **Implicit factors** — e.g., competitive flora in fermented foods.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Differentiate between psychrotrophs, mesophiles, and thermophiles with respect to their optimum growth temperatures. Give one food-spoilage example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Group | Optimum Growth Temp | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Psychrotrophs** | 20 – 30 °C, grow at ≤ 7 °C | *Pseudomonas fluorescens* in refrigerated milk |
| **Mesophiles** | 20 – 45 °C | *Salmonella* spp. in eggs / meat |
| **Thermophiles** | 45 – 80 °C | *Bacillus stearothermophilus* in canned foods |

(1 mark per row of comparison + example.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question A (14 marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **intrinsic factors** that affect microbial growth in foods. Highlight the role of water activity with a suitable diagram of the microbial growth spectrum vs. aw. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A milk sample contains $5 \times 10^5$ CFU/mL of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*. The pasteurization process at 72 °C reduces this to 50 CFU/mL in 30 seconds. Calculate: **(i)** the D-value at 72 °C, and **(ii)** the time required at 72 °C to deliver a 5-log reduction in count. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The intrinsic factors are the inherent physico-chemical properties of the food that influence microbial proliferation:

1. **Water Activity (aw):** The most critical intrinsic factor. Microorganisms require *free* water for metabolism. Different microbial groups have minimum aw thresholds:

   - Bacteria: $a_w \ge 0.91$
   - Yeasts: $a_w \ge 0.88$
   - Moulds: $a_w \ge 0.80$
   - Halophiles: $a_w \ge 0.75$
   - Xerophiles: $a_w \ge 0.60$

2. **pH (Acidity):** Most bacteria grow in the pH range 4.5 – 9.0; yeasts and moulds tolerate 1.5 – 8.5. Foods with pH < 4.5 (e.g., citrus juice) are microbiologically stable.

3. **Nutrient Content:** Foods rich in proteins, carbohydrates, lipids, vitamins, and minerals (e.g., meat, milk) support diverse microflora.

4. **Oxidation-Reduction Potential (Eh):** Aerobic bacteria (e.g., *Pseudomonas*) need positive Eh; anaerobes (e.g., *Clostridium*) need negative Eh. Vacuum-packed or canned foods have low Eh.

5. **Antimicrobial Constituents:** Naturally occurring inhibitors such as lysozyme (egg white), eugenol (cloves), allicin (garlic), and lactoferrin (milk) suppress bacterial growth.

6. **Biological Structures:** Intact skin of fruits, eggshell, and husk protect internal tissues from microbial invasion.

**Diagram (Microbial Growth Spectrum vs. aw):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AW0["aw 0.00 to 0.60"] --> NO["No Growth Instant Coffee Milk Powder"]
    AW1["aw 0.60 to 0.80"] --> XER["Xerophilic Moulds Osmophilic Yeasts"]
    AW2["aw 0.80 to 0.88"] --> MOULD["Most Moulds and Yeasts"]
    AW3["aw 0.88 to 0.91"] --> BACT["Most Spoilage Bacteria"]
    AW4["aw 0.91 to 1.00"] --> ALL["All Microbes including Pathogens"]
```

**(7 marks breakdown: 1 mark definition + 1.5 marks for water activity discussion + 4.5 marks for remaining five intrinsic factors + 0 mark for diagram if not on paper; the diagram is bonus.)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Given:** $N_0 = 5 \times 10^5$ CFU/mL, $N_t = 50 = 5 \times 10^1$ CFU/mL, $t = 30$ seconds.

**Part (i) — D-value at 72 °C:**

$$D_{72} = \frac{t}{\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N_t)} = \frac{30}{\log_{10}(5 \times 10^5) - \log_{10}(5 \times 10^1)}$$

$$\log_{10}(5 \times 10^5) = \log_{10}(5) + 5 = 0.699 + 5 = 5.699$$

$$\log_{10}(5 \times 10^1) = \log_{10}(5) + 1 = 0.699 + 1 = 1.699$$

$$D_{72} = \frac{30}{5.699 - 1.699} = \frac{30}{4} = 7.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Part (ii) — Time for 5-log reduction:**

$$t_{5D} = 5 \times D_{72} = 5 \times 7.5 = 37.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Final Answer:** $D_{72} = 7.5$ s; $t_{5D} = 37.5$ s.

**(7 marks: 3.5 marks for part (i) with complete steps, 3.5 marks for part (ii).)**

---

> **Question B (14 marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **extrinsic factors** affecting microbial growth in foods. How does modified atmosphere packaging (MAP) influence spoilage flora? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The D-value of *Clostridium botulinum* spores at 121.1 °C is 0.21 minutes. If a canned food has an initial spore load of $10^9$ spores per can, calculate the time required at 121.1 °C to achieve a **12-D reduction** (commercial sterility). What is the F-value of the process? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

Extrinsic factors are those associated with the storage environment external to the food:

1. **Storage Temperature:** Classifies microorganisms into psychrotrophs (≤ 7 °C growth), mesophiles (20 – 45 °C), thermophiles (45 – 80 °C), and hyperthermophiles (≥ 80 °C). Refrigeration (≤ 4 °C) prevents growth of mesophilic pathogens; freezing (≤ –18 °C) halts most microbial activity.

2. **Relative Humidity (RH):** High surface RH promotes mould and bacterial growth. Low RH favours drying and reduces surface aw.

3. **Gaseous Atmosphere:**
   - **Oxygen concentration:** High O₂ favours aerobic spoilage (moulds, pseudomonads); vacuum/anaerobic storage favours anaerobes.
   - **CO₂ concentration:** Elevated CO₂ (> 20 %) inhibits many spoilage bacteria and moulds.
   - **N₂:** Inert filler gas; displaces O₂ in MAP.

**MAP Influence:** Modified Atmosphere Packaging (typically 60 – 80 % CO₂ + 20 – 40 % N₂ for red meat) suppresses *Pseudomonas* (psychrotrophic spoilage bacteria), extends shelf life 2 – 3×, and shifts flora to lactic acid bacteria. High CO₂ dissolves in the food matrix, lowering pH and inhibiting decarboxylation enzymes.

**(7 marks: 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 marks for the three extrinsic factors + 2.5 marks for MAP mechanism.)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Given:** $D_{121.1} = 0.21$ minutes, $N_0 = 10^9$ spores/can.

**Step 1 — Time for 12-D reduction (commercial sterility):**

$$t_{12D} = 12 \times D_{121.1} = 12 \times 0.21 = 2.52 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 — F-value of the process:**

For constant-temperature holding at the reference temperature, the F-value equals the holding time at $T_{\text{ref}} = 121.1\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$:

$$F_0 = 12 \times D_{121.1} = 2.52 \text{ minutes}$$

**Final Answer:** $t_{12D} = 2.52$ min; $F_0 = 2.52$ min.

**(7 marks: 4 marks for 12-D calculation, 3 marks for F-value definition and computation.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse aₒ and water content.** aₒ is *availability* of water, not the *total* water content. A dry food (low moisture) can have high aₒ if water is loosely bound; conversely, high-moisture foods may have low aₒ if solutes bind water.
> 2. **D-value sign errors:** A D-value is *always positive*. A negative sign in the formula arises from the slope convention; magnitude only is used.
> 3. **Confusing D-value with z-value:** D-value is *time* (min or s); z-value is *temperature* (°C). Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Misidentifying psychrotrophs vs. psychrophiles:** Psychrotrophs grow at refrigeration temperature but have *mesophilic optima* (e.g., 25 – 30 °C). True psychrophiles are rare in foods.
> 5. **Failing to convert CFU/mL to log₁₀ before applying the D-value formula.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- 🔑 **Four Factor Categories:** Intrinsic · Extrinsic · Processing · Implicit.
- 🔑 **Water Activity Equation:** $a_w = P / P_0 = ERH / 100$.
- 🔑 **Microbial Minimum aw:** Bacteria 0.91 · Yeasts 0.88 · Moulds 0.80 · Xerophiles 0.60.
- 🔑 **pH Thresholds:** Most bacteria need pH ≥ 4.5; moulds and yeasts tolerate 1.5 – 8.5.
- 🔑 **D-value formula:** $D_T = t / [\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N_t)]$.
- 🔑 **z-value formula:** $z = (T_2 - T_1) / \log_{10}(D_1 / D_2)$.
- 🔑 **F-value:** $F_0 = 12 D$ for commercial sterility of low-acid canned foods.
- 🔑 **Hurdle Concept (Leistner):** Synergistic multi-factor inhibition.
- 🔑 **Q₁₀ rule:** Microbial growth rate approximately doubles per 10 °C rise (within mesophilic range).
- 🔑 **Eh (Redox Potential):** Aerobes need positive Eh; anaerobes need negative Eh.
- 🔑 **Most important extrinsic factor:** Storage temperature.
- 🔑 **Most important intrinsic factor:** Water activity.
- 🔑 **MAP gases:** CO₂ (inhibitory) + N₂ (inert filler); O₂ exclusion favours anaerobes.
- 🔑 **Commercial sterilization:** 12-D reduction of *C. botulinum* = *botulinum cook* = $F_0 \ge 2.52$ min.
- 🔑 **Psychrotrophs ≠ Psychrophiles:** Psychrotrophs are the *food-relevant* cold-growers.
- 🔑 **Arrhenius equation** relates microbial growth rate to absolute temperature (K).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles and Methods of Food Preservation - Preservation by Low Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles and Methods of Food Preservation - Preservation by Low Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preservation by Low Temperature** is defined as a *physical method of food preservation* in which the storage temperature of a perishable food product is reduced to a level that **suppresses microbial proliferation, retards enzymatic activity, and slows down chemical/biochemical deterioration reactions**, thereby extending the shelf life of the food while retaining its nutritional, sensory, and functional quality.

The method is broadly classified into two regimes based on the final product temperature:

| Regime | Temperature Range | Physical State of Water | Typical Shelf-Life Extension |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Chilling (Refrigeration)** | **0 °C to +8 °C** | Unfrozen (liquid) | Few days to few weeks |
| **Freezing** | **0 °C to −40 °C** | Mostly frozen (solid ice) | Several months to years |
| **Deep Freezing** | **≤ −18 °C** | > 95 % water as ice | 6–24 months |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Course Outcome (CO2) specifically maps this topic to *"Describe the principles and methods of preservation of foods."* Hence, students must clearly distinguish between **chilling** and **freezing** as two fundamentally different preservation mechanisms, not merely different temperatures.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pause Button" of Food

Imagine you are watching a movie (your food is fresh, full of activity — microbes are multiplying, enzymes are chopping proteins, fats are oxidising). Now imagine you press the **"Pause" button** on the remote:

- The characters (microbes) stop walking around → they cannot reproduce rapidly.
- The kitchen work (enzymatic reactions like browning) slows down drastically.
- The lighting changes (chemical oxidation) become barely noticeable.

This is exactly what **low temperature does to food**:

- **Microbes** are like the "characters": most spoilage bacteria thrive at **20–40 °C** (the *thermophilic* and *mesophilic* zones). Lowering the temperature pushes them into a sluggish state or kills the psychrotrophs.
- **Enzymes** are the "kitchen staff": they have an *optimum temperature* (often 30–50 °C) and slow down by a factor for every 10 °C drop (the **Q₁₀ rule**).
- **Chemical reactions** such as lipid oxidation, non-enzymatic browning (Maillard), and vitamin degradation also slow down exponentially with temperature.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Students:** "Low Temperature = Lower Kinetic Energy = Slower Molecular Collisions = Slower Reactions." This is the **single most important principle** behind preservation by cooling and freezing.

---

## 1.3 Underlying Scientific Principles (Three Pillars)

1. **Reduction of Microbial Growth Rate:** Most bacteria, yeasts, and moulds have an *optimum temperature*. Cooling shifts them away from their optimum, increasing their *lag phase* and decreasing their *specific growth rate (μ)*.
2. **Retardation of Enzymatic Reactions:** Endogenous enzymes (e.g., *polyphenol oxidase*, *lipase*, *pectin methylesterase*) and microbial enzymes cause spoilage. The Arrhenius relationship shows that reaction rates roughly *halve* for every 10 °C drop.
3. **Conversion of Free Water to Ice (Freezing only):** Unfreezable water (*bound water*) at **−30 °C ≈ 0.1 g water/g dry matter**. Freezing immobilises water, making it unavailable for microbial metabolism.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Q₁₀ (Temperature Coefficient):** Typically **2 to 3** for biological/enzymatic reactions; **1.5 to 2** for chemical spoilage reactions.
> - **Latent Heat of Fusion of Water (Lf):** **334 kJ/kg** or **80 kcal/kg**.
> - **Specific Heat of Water (cp):** **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** or **1 kcal/(kg·K)**.
> - **Specific Heat of Ice (cp,ice):** **2.093 kJ/(kg·K)** or **0.5 kcal/(kg·K)**.
> - **Specific Heat of Food (cp,food):** ≈ **3.35 kJ/(kg·K)** (typical mean value).
> - **Freezing Point of Pure Water:** **0 °C (273.15 K)**.
> - **Recommended Cold Storage for Frozen Foods (KTU/Codex):** **−18 °C or below** with tolerance of **± 3 °C**.
> - **Recommended Chilled Storage:** **0 °C to +4 °C** for fresh meats, dairy, fish; **+5 °C to +8 °C** for tropical fruits/vegetables.
> - **Thermal Conductivity (λ) of Frozen Food:** ≈ **1.0 to 1.5 W/(m·K)**.
> - **Plank's Equation Constants (P, R):** For an infinite slab — **P = 1/2, R = 1/8**; for infinite cylinder — **P = 1/4, R = 1/16**; for sphere — **P = 1/6, R = 1/24**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle I — Effect of Low Temperature on Microbial Growth

Microbial growth follows the classical *Monod / Logistic kinetics*, with a **specific growth rate (μ)** that is itself a strong function of temperature. The Cardinal Model with Inflection (CMI) describes four cardinal temperatures for each microorganism:

- **Tmin** — Minimum growth temperature
- **Topt** — Optimum growth temperature
- **Tmax** — Maximum growth temperature
- **Tinf** — Inflection temperature

> **Mathematical Form (Rosso/Cardinal Model):**
> 
> $$\mu(T) = \mu_{opt} \cdot \phi(T)$$
> 
> where $\phi(T)$ is a function equal to 0 at $T_{min}$, $T_{max}$, and equal to 1 at $T_{opt}$.

For *psychrotrophs* (e.g., *Pseudomonas*, *Listeria*), Tmin may be as low as **−5 °C**, while for *mesophiles* (e.g., *Salmonella*, *E. coli*), growth essentially halts below **+5 °C**.

---

## 2.2 Principle II — Effect on Enzyme Activity (Arrhenius Relationship)

The rate constant **k** of any chemical or enzymatic reaction is related to temperature by the **Arrhenius Equation**:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

where:

- $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> A 10 °C drop in temperature reduces $k$ by a factor known as **Q₁₀**.

---

## 2.3 Principle III — Freezing Point Depression & Ice Formation

In foods, dissolved solutes (sugars, salts, proteins) lower the freezing point below 0 °C. As ice crystals form, the remaining *unfrozen phase* becomes increasingly concentrated — a phenomenon known as **cryoconcentration**. The freezing point depression follows:

$$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$$

where $K_f$ = cryoscopic constant (1.86 °C·kg/mol for water), $m$ = molality, $i$ = van't Hoff factor.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these 8 equations — they cover ~90 % of numerical questions asked on this topic in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Sensible Heat Removal (Chilling) | $Q_s = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Energy to cool food above freezing |
| 2 | Latent Heat Removal (Freezing) | $Q_L = m \cdot L_f$ | Energy to convert water → ice |
| 3 | Total Refrigeration Load | $Q_{tot} = Q_s^{above} + Q_L + Q_s^{below} + Q_{respiration} + Q_{equip}$ | Cold storage design |
| 4 | Arrhenius Equation | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)}$ | Temperature vs. reaction rate |
| 5 | Q₁₀ Rule | $Q_{10} = \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{10 \, / \, (T_2 - T_1)}$ | Shelf-life prediction |
| 6 | Plank's Freezing Time | $t_f = \dfrac{\rho \, L_f}{\Delta T} \left( \dfrac{P \cdot D}{\lambda_{fr}} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^2}{\lambda_{fr}^2} \right)$ | Freezer design |
| 7 | Freezing Point Depression | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$ | Effect of solutes on freezing |
| 8 | Specific Heat of Food (Siebel's Eq.) | $c_{p,food} = 0.837 + 3.348 \cdot X_w$ | Estimation of cp |
| 9 | Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha = \dfrac{\lambda}{\rho \cdot c_p}$ | Heat transfer inside food |
| 10 | Biot Number Check | $Bi = \dfrac{h \cdot L_c}{\lambda}$ | Internal vs. external resistance |

> **Critical Symbols Reference:**
> - $\rho$ = density of food (kg/m³)
> - $D$ = characteristic thickness (m) — smallest dimension for slabs
> - $\Delta T = T_{fr,initial} - T_{air}$
> - $X_w$ = mass fraction of water in food
> - $L_c$ = characteristic length = volume / surface area

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

| Application Sector | Use of Low-Temperature Preservation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Marine/Seafood** | Tuna, shrimp frozen at **−35 °C** blast freezers, stored at **−25 °C** |
| **Meat Industry** | Carcass chilling at **0 °C to +2 °C**; freezing at **−18 °C** |
| **Dairy** | Pasteurised milk stored at **+4 °C** |
| **Fruits & Vegetables** | Hydro-cooling, vacuum cooling, individual quick freezing (IQF) |
| **Bakery & Ready-to-Eat** | Frozen dough, frozen meals, cold chain logistics |
| **Pharmaceutical / Biotech** | Cryopreservation of biologicals, vaccines |
| **Cold Chain Logistics** | Reefer containers, refrigerated trucks, walk-in cold rooms |

---

## 2.6 Classification of Preservation by Low Temperature

**A. Chilling (0 to +8 °C)** — *Sub-categories:*
- **Cold Storage / Room Chilling**
- **Ice Cooling / Contact Ice**
- **Hydro-cooling** (immersion in chilled water)
- **Vacuum Cooling** (evaporative cooling under vacuum)
- **Cryogenic Cooling** (using liquid N₂ or CO₂ snow)
- **Crushed / Flake Ice Packing**

**B. Freezing (≤ −1 °C, typically −18 °C)** — *Sub-categories:*
- **Slow Freezing** (still air, cold room freezing) — large extracellular ice crystals
- **Quick Freezing (QF)** — small intracellular & extracellular crystals
- **Individual Quick Freezing (IQF)** — product pieces frozen individually
- **Air-Blast Freezing** (forced convection)
- **Contact / Plate Freezing** (conduction)
- **Immersion / Spray Freezing** (cryogens: liquid nitrogen, liquid CO₂)
- **Fluidised Bed Freezing**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Q₁₀ (Temperature Coefficient) Relationship

The **Q₁₀** rule states that for every 10 °C rise in temperature, the rate of a biological/chemical reaction increases by a constant factor.

**Step 1:** Start with the Arrhenius equation applied at two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ (both in Kelvin).

$$k_1 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_1)} \quad \text{and} \quad k_2 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_2)}$$

**Step 2:** Take the ratio $k_2 / k_1$ to eliminate the pre-exponential factor $A$.

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{-\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the exponent using common denominator $T_1 T_2$.

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 \, T_2}}$$

**Step 4:** By definition, when $T_2 - T_1 = 10$ °C (or 10 K), the ratio $k_2/k_1$ becomes $Q_{10}$.

$$Q_{10} = e^{\frac{10 \cdot E_a}{R \cdot T_1 \cdot T_2}}$$

**Step 5:** The generalisation for any temperature difference $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1$ (in °C or K) is:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = Q_{10}^{\, (T_2 - T_1) \, / \, 10}$$

> **Step 6 — Worked Example:** The spoilage rate constant of fresh fish at 0 °C is $k_1 = 0.05$ /day. If $Q_{10} = 2.5$, find the rate at 20 °C.

$$\frac{k_{20}}{k_0} = 2.5^{(20 - 0)/10} = 2.5^{2} = 6.25$$

$$k_{20} = 0.05 \times 6.25 = 0.3125 \text{ per day}$$

**Conclusion:** A 20 °C rise increases the spoilage rate by **6.25×**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Plank's Equation for Freezing Time

This classical equation (E. Plank, 1913) predicts the freezing time of a food item, treating it as a slab, cylinder, or sphere.

**Step 1:** Consider a slab of thickness $2D$ (half-thickness = $D$). Heat is removed from both faces. The frozen layer moves inward as freezing progresses.

**Step 2:** The total freezing time has two distinct contributions:
1. Removal of latent heat from the slab (volume term)
2. Sensible heat conduction through the already-frozen layer (resistance term)

**Step 3:** Plank solved the unsteady-state heat conduction equation for a moving boundary (Stefan problem) and obtained:

$$t_f = \frac{\rho \cdot L_f}{\Delta T} \left( \frac{P \cdot D}{\lambda_{fr}} + \frac{R \cdot D^2}{\lambda_{fr}^2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Meaning of Constants:**

| Geometry | P (linear term) | R (quadratic term) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Infinite Slab | $\frac{1}{2}$ | $\frac{1}{8}$ |
| Infinite Cylinder | $\frac{1}{4}$ | $\frac{1}{16}$ |
| Sphere | $\frac{1}{6}$ | $\frac{1}{24}$ |

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:**

*Given:* Beef slab, $D = 0.04$ m, $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³, $L_f = 250$ kJ/kg (effective, accounting for unfreezable water), $\lambda_{fr} = 1.3$ W/(m·K), initial food temperature = 0 °C, air temperature = −30 °C.

*Solution:*

$$\Delta T = 0 - (-30) = 30 \text{ °C} = 30 \text{ K}$$

$$t_f = \frac{1050 \times 250{,}000}{30} \left( \frac{(1/2) \times 0.04}{1.3} + \frac{(1/8) \times (0.04)^2}{(1.3)^2} \right)$$

**Step 5a — Calculate the bracket term:**

$$\frac{(1/2) \times 0.04}{1.3} = \frac{0.02}{1.3} = 0.01538$$

$$\frac{(1/8) \times 0.0016}{1.69} = \frac{0.0002}{1.69} = 0.0001183$$

$$\text{Sum} = 0.01538 + 0.0001183 = 0.01550 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W}$$

**Step 5b — Compute the prefactor:**

$$\frac{1050 \times 250{,}000}{30} = \frac{262{,}500{,}000}{30} = 8{,}750{,}000 \text{ s·K/m}^2$$

**Step 5c — Final answer:**

$$t_f = 8{,}750{,}000 \times 0.01550 = 135{,}625 \text{ s} \approx 37.7 \text{ hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the *linear term* ($P \cdot D / \lambda_{fr}$) **dominates** (~99 % here). This is why reducing the slab thickness $D$ is the *most effective* way to reduce freezing time. Halving $D$ approximately halves $t_f$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Freezing Point Depression

**Step 1:** At equilibrium between ice and liquid water, the chemical potentials are equal.

$$\mu_{water}^{liquid} = \mu_{water}^{solid}$$

**Step 2:** For an ideal dilute solution, the chemical potential of water in solution is:

$$\mu_{water}^{liquid} = \mu_{water}^{*\,liquid} + R T \ln X_{water}$$

where $X_{water}$ = mole fraction of water.

**Step 3:** Equating and applying the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation gives:

$$\ln X_{water} = -\frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)$$

**Step 4:** For dilute solutions, $\ln X_{water} \approx -X_{solute} \approx -i \cdot m \cdot M_{water}$ (where $M_{water} = 0.018$ kg/mol), leading to:

$$\Delta T_f = T_0 - T = K_f \cdot m \cdot i \quad ; \quad K_f = \frac{R \, T_0^2 \, M_{water}}{1000 \, \Delta H_{fus}} = 1.86 \text{ °C·kg/mol}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Freezing vs Chilling

| Parameter | Chilling | Freezing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Water State** | Liquid | ~80 % as ice |
| **Microbial Growth** | Slowed but not stopped | Inhibited / halted |
| **Enzymatic Activity** | Reduced (5–10×) | Substantially inactivated |
| **Latent Heat Removal** | None | Required (largest energy cost) |
| **Texture** | Retained | Slight structural change |
| **Nutrient Loss** | Minimal | Minimal (if quick frozen) |
| **Storage Energy** | Moderate | High (sustaining −18 °C) |
| **Shelf Life** | Days–Weeks | Months–Years |
| **Ice Crystal Size** | None | 1–100 µm (slow) vs. < 10 µm (quick) |

---

## 3.5 Detailed Step-by-Step Calculation of Refrigeration Load (Cold Store Design)

**Given:** A cold storage room of 5 m × 4 m × 3 m stores **2,000 kg** of apples to be cooled from **+25 °C** to **+2 °C** in **8 hours**. Walls/door infiltration loss = **2 kW**. Equipment/lighting load = **1.5 kW**. Respiration heat of apples at 2 °C ≈ **0.07 W/kg**.

**Step 1 — Sensible heat to be removed from apples:**

$$Q_{apples} = m \cdot c_{p,apple} \cdot \Delta T = 2000 \times 3.6 \times (25 - 2)$$

$$Q_{apples} = 2000 \times 3.6 \times 23 = 165{,}600 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Convert infiltration + equipment + respiration to kJ over 8 h:**

$$Q_{others} = (2 + 1.5) \times 3600 \times 8 + 0.07 \times 2000 \times 3600 \times 8$$

$$Q_{others} = 3.5 \times 28{,}800 + 0.07 \times 2000 \times 28{,}800$$

$$Q_{others} = 100{,}800 + 4{,}032{,}000 = 4{,}132{,}800 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Total cooling load:**

$$Q_{total} = 165{,}600 + 4{,}132{,}800 \approx 4{,}298{,}400 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Required refrigeration capacity in kW:**

$$\text{Capacity} = \frac{Q_{total}}{\text{Time (s)}} = \frac{4{,}298{,}400}{8 \times 3600} = 149.3 \text{ kW}$$

> **Interpretation:** A **~150 kW** refrigeration unit (≈ **43 TR**; 1 TR = 3.517 kW) is required for this cold room.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Diagram — Preservation by Low Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["LOW TEMPERATURE PRESERVATION"] --> B["CHILLING 0C TO 8C"]
    A --> C["FREEZING LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO MINUS 18C"]
    A --> D["CRYOPRESERVATION LESS THAN MINUS 80C"]

    B --> B1["ROOM CHILLING"]
    B --> B2["HYDRO COOLING"]
    B --> B3["VACUUM COOLING"]
    B --> B4["ICE PACKING"]
    B --> B5["CRYOGENIC COOLING LN2 OR CO2"]

    C --> C1["SLOW FREEZING STILL AIR"]
    C --> C2["AIR BLAST FREEZING"]
    C --> C3["PLATE OR CONTACT FREEZING"]
    C --> C4["IMMERSION FREEZING BRINE OR GLYCOL"]
    C --> C5["IQF FLUIDISED BED"]
    C --> C6["CRYOGENIC FREEZING LN2 OR LCO2"]

    D --> D1["ULTRA LOW TEMP STORAGE"]
    D --> D2["VACCINE AND BIO BANK"]
    D --> D3["LYOPHILISATION FEED IN"]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Air-Blast Quick Freezing (Tunnel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1["RAW FOOD ENTRY PLUS 5C"] --> PREP["PREPARATION WASHING PEELING BLANCHING"]
    PREP --> LOAD["LOAD ON TROLLEY OR CONVEYOR"]
    LOAD --> TUNNEL["AIR BLAST TUNNEL MINUS 30 TO MINUS 40C VELOCITY 3 TO 6 M PER SECOND"]
    TUNNEL --> CRYST["ICE NUCLEATION ZONE MINUS 1 TO MINUS 5C FAST"]
    CRYST --> SOLID["SOLIDIFICATION ZONE MINUS 18 TO MINUS 25C"]
    SOLID --> GLAZE["OPTIONAL GLAZING WATER OR SAUCE"]
    GLAZE --> PACK["PACKAGING HERMETIC OR VACUUM"]
    PACK --> COLD["COLD STORE MINUS 18C PLUS OR MINUS 1C"]
    COLD --> OUT["DISTRIBUTION REFRIGERATED TRANSPORT"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    COMP["COMPRESSOR HIGH PRESSURE HIGH TEMP VAPOUR"] --> COND["CONDENSER REJECTS HEAT TO AMBIENT"]
    COND --> EXV["EXPANSION VALVE PRESSURE DROP LIQUID PLUS FLASH GAS"]
    EXV --> EVAP["EVAPORATOR ABSORBS HEAT FROM FOOD Q IN"]
    EVAP --> COMP

    subgraph COLD_SIDE["REFRIGERATED SPACE MINUS 18C TO PLUS 4C"]
        EVAP
    end

    subgraph HOT_SIDE["AMBIENT OR COOLING TOWER"]
        COND
    end
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix — Choosing a Freezing Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"IS PRODUCT IRREGULAR SHAPE OR BULKY?"} -->|YES| A1["USE AIR BLAST FREEZING"]
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2{"IS PRODUCT FLAT OR REGULAR BLOCK?"}
    Q2 -->|YES| A2["USE PLATE OR CONTACT FREEZING HIGH EFFICIENCY"]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q3{"ARE PRODUCT PIECES SMALL AND GRANULAR?"}
    Q3 -->|YES| A3["USE IQF FLUIDISED BED"]
    Q3 -->|NO| Q4{"IS ULTRA RAPID FREEZING NEEDED?"}
    Q4 -->|YES| A4["USE CRYOGENIC LN2 OR CO2"]
    Q4 -->|NO| A5["USE STILL AIR COLD ROOM SLOW FREEZING"]
```

## 4.5 Cause-Effect Diagram — Why Low Temperature Preserves Food

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["LOW TEMPERATURE APPLICATION"] --> R1["REDUCES KINETIC ENERGY OF MOLECULES"]
    ROOT --> R2["CONVERTS FREE WATER TO ICE"]
    ROOT --> R3["DECREASES ENZYME TURNOVER"]

    R1 --> E1["SLOWS MICROBIAL REPRODUCTION"]
    R1 --> E2["RETARDS CHEMICAL OXIDATION"]
    R1 --> E3["REDUCES NON ENZYMATIC BROWNING MAILLARD"]

    R2 --> E4["WATER UNAVAILABLE FOR METABOLISM"]
    R2 --> E5["SOLUTE CRYOCONCENTRATION INHIBITS MICROBES"]

    R3 --> E6["POLYPHENOL OXIDASE INACTIVE PARTIAL"]
    R3 --> E7["LIPASE AND PROTEASE SUPPRESSED"]
```

## 4.6 Schematic — Ice Crystal Formation (Slow vs Quick)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SLOW["SLOW FREEZING MINUS 5 TO MINUS 15C PER HOUR"]
        S1["ICE FORMS OUTSIDE CELLS FIRST"] --> S2["LARGE EXTRACELLULAR CRYSTALS 50 TO 100 UM"]
        S2 --> S3["CELL MEMBRANE DAMAGE DRIP LOSS ON THAW"]
    end

    subgraph QUICK["QUICK FREEZING GREATER THAN 10C PER HOUR"]
        Q1["ICE NUCLEATES INSIDE AND OUTSIDE CELLS SIMULTANEOUSLY"] --> Q2["SMALL INTRACELLULAR CRYSTALS LESS THAN 10 UM"]
        Q2 --> Q3["MINIMAL STRUCTURAL DAMAGE HIGH QUALITY THAW"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between chilling and freezing as methods of food preservation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Chilling | Freezing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Temperature | 0 °C to +8 °C | ≤ −18 °C |
| Physical state of water | Remains liquid | Converts to ice (~80 %) |
| Microbial effect | Slowed growth | Almost completely inhibited |
| Shelf life | Days to weeks | Months to years |
| Energy demand | Lower (sensible heat only) | Higher (latent + sensible heat) |
| Texture change | None | Slight, due to ice crystals |

*(Any 4 distinct points — 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the principle behind preservation by low temperature. Mention the role of Q₁₀.**

**Model Answer:**

1. Lowering temperature reduces the **kinetic energy** of reacting molecules, thereby reducing the frequency of effective collisions → **reaction rate decreases** (Arrhenius law).
2. **Q₁₀** is the factor by which the reaction rate increases for a **10 °C rise** in temperature (typically **2–3** for biological systems). Hence, a 10 °C *drop* reduces rate by 1/Q₁₀ → shelf life is prolonged.
3. Cold also **inhibits microbial growth** (most pathogens have optimum at 20–40 °C) and **retards enzymatic spoilage** (e.g., lipase, PPO).

*(3 marks — 1 for principle, 1 for Q₁₀ definition, 1 for application)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 — Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat diagram the **air-blast freezing method** for preservation of foods. Discuss its advantages and limitations.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Air-blast freezing uses **forced convection of cold air** (typically −30 °C to −40 °C) at high velocity (3–6 m/s) over the food product, achieving quick heat removal.

**Working Principle:**
- Cold air is circulated by fans through a tunnel or chamber.
- Heat flows from the food surface to the air by **convection**, then by **conduction** through the frozen layer to the interior.
- High air velocity increases the surface heat transfer coefficient (h ≈ 20–50 W/m²K), drastically reducing freezing time.

**Diagram:** *(See Section 4.2 Mermaid flowchart)*

**Process Steps:**
1. Pre-cooling and preparation (washing, blanching).
2. Loading onto trolleys/continuous conveyor.
3. Passage through pre-freezing zone (−1 to −5 °C) for nucleation.
4. Solidification zone (−18 to −25 °C).
5. Optional glazing.
6. Packaging and storage.

**Advantages:**
- Suitable for irregular shapes and bulk products.
- Continuous operation possible.
- Uniform freezing if air distribution is well-designed.

**Limitations:**
- High energy consumption (fan power).
- Surface dehydration ("freezer burn") if not packaged.
- Capital cost for insulated tunnels and fans.

*(7 marks: 2 for principle + diagram, 2 for steps, 1 each for advantages and limitations, 1 for neatness)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the principle and mechanism of **ice crystal formation** during slow and quick freezing. How does crystal size affect food quality on thawing?

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** Freezing involves **nucleation** (formation of stable ice nuclei) followed by **crystal growth**. The size and location of crystals depend on the **freezing rate**.

**Slow Freezing:**
- Cooling rate < 1 °C/min.
- Nucleation occurs **extracellularly** (in intercellular spaces) because intracellular fluids require a greater degree of supercooling.
- Water migrates from cells to extracellular ice → **large crystals (50–200 µm)**.
- Osmotic imbalance causes **cell wall rupture**.

**Quick Freezing:**
- Cooling rate > 10 °C/min.
- Nucleation occurs **both intra- and extracellularly** simultaneously.
- Small, uniform crystals (< 10 µm) form → minimal mechanical damage.
- Less free water migration → better retention of cellular integrity.

**Effect on Thawed Quality:**

| Quality Attribute | Slow Freezing | Quick Freezing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Drip loss on thawing | High (10–30 %) | Low (1–5 %) |
| Texture | Mushy, soft | Firm, near-fresh |
| Colour | Bleeding, browning | Retained |
| Nutrient retention | Loss of water-soluble vitamins | Better retention |
| Microbial load post-thaw | Higher (damaged cells release nutrients) | Lower |

**Conclusion:** **Quick freezing is universally preferred** for preserving textural and nutritional quality.

*(7 marks: 2 each for slow and quick mechanisms, 2 for quality comparison, 1 conclusion)*

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Arrhenius equation** and explain how it is applied to predict shelf life of foods stored at low temperatures.

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Basic Statement:** The rate constant $k$ of a chemical reaction increases exponentially with absolute temperature $T$.

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)}$$

**Step 2 — Experimental Basis:** Svante Arrhenius (1889) observed that $\ln k$ plotted versus $1/T$ gives a straight line:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R T}$$

**Step 3 — Slope Interpretation:** The slope of this line equals $-E_a/R$, from which **activation energy** is determined.

**Step 4 — Application to Food Shelf Life:**

If a food spoils at rate $k_1$ at temperature $T_1$ and at $k_2$ at $T_2$, then:

$$\ln \frac{k_2}{k_1} = -\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right) = \frac{E_a \, (T_2 - T_1)}{R \, T_1 \, T_2}$$

**Step 5 — Shelf Life Prediction:** Since shelf life is inversely proportional to rate ($\theta \propto 1/k$):

$$\ln \frac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = \frac{E_a \, (T_2 - T_1)}{R \, T_1 \, T_2}$$

**Worked Numerical:** Milk spoils in 1 day at 25 °C (298 K). Find shelf life at 5 °C (278 K). Take $E_a = 80$ kJ/mol.

$$\ln \frac{\theta_{5}}{\theta_{25}} = \frac{80{,}000 \, (25 - 5)}{8.314 \times 298 \times 278} = \frac{1{,}600{,}000}{688{,}490} = 2.324$$

$$\theta_{5} = 1 \times e^{2.324} \approx 10.2 \text{ days}$$

**Conclusion:** A 20 °C drop extends milk shelf life from 1 day to ~10 days.

*(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for shelf-life application, 2 for numerical example)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **total refrigeration load** required to cool **5,000 kg of fish** from **+10 °C to −20 °C** using the **air-blast method**. Given: $c_{p,fish} = 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K), freezing point = −1 °C, latent heat of fish = 250 kJ/kg, $c_{p,ice} = 2.1$ kJ/(kg·K). Infiltration and equipment losses = 15 kW. Chilling must be completed in 6 hours.

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Sensible Heat Above Freezing Point (10 °C → −1 °C):**

$$Q_1 = m \cdot c_{p,fish} \cdot \Delta T = 5000 \times 3.5 \times (10 - (-1))$$

$$Q_1 = 5000 \times 3.5 \times 11 = 192{,}500 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Latent Heat at Freezing Point:**

$$Q_2 = m \cdot L_f = 5000 \times 250 = 1{,}250{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Sensible Heat Below Freezing Point (−1 °C → −20 °C):**

$$Q_3 = m \cdot c_{p,ice} \cdot \Delta T = 5000 \times 2.1 \times (-1 - (-20))$$

$$Q_3 = 5000 \times 2.1 \times 19 = 199{,}500 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Infiltration + Equipment Losses over 6 h:**

$$Q_4 = P \cdot t = 15 \text{ kW} \times 6 \times 3600 \text{ s} = 324{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Total Refrigeration Load:**

$$Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4$$

$$Q_{total} = 192{,}500 + 1{,}250{,}000 + 199{,}500 + 324{,}000$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = 1{,}966{,}000 \text{ kJ} \approx 1.97 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Step 6 — Required Refrigeration Capacity in kW:**

$$\text{Capacity} = \frac{Q_{total}}{t} = \frac{1{,}966{,}000}{6 \times 3600} = \frac{1{,}966{,}000}{21{,}600} \approx 91 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Conversion to Tons of Refrigeration (TR):**

$$1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$$

$$\text{Required TR} = \frac{91}{3.517} \approx 25.9 \text{ TR}$$

**Conclusion:** A **~26 TR** (≈ 91 kW) refrigeration plant is needed.

*(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1–4, 1 for step 5, 1 for step 6, 1 for conclusion and units)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE (Module 2):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Chilling and Freezing:** Students often write "freezing" when they mean "chilling". Always state the **temperature range explicitly**.
> 2. **Forgetting the 80 % Ice Rule:** Frozen food is NOT 100 % ice. About **20 % water remains unfrozen** even at −18 °C due to bound water and solutes.
> 3. **Missing the Latent Heat Term:** In freezing calculations, the **latent heat (Q₂)** is the dominant term (~70–80 % of total load). Skipping it = lose 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Wrong Plank's Constants:** Using slab constants for a sphere (or vice versa) is a classic error. Memorise: slab → ½, ⅛; cylinder → ¼, 1/16; sphere → ⅙, 1/24.
> 5. **Sign of ΔT in Plank's Equation:** ΔT is **always positive** (T_initial − T_air), irrespective of sign convention.
> 6. **Not Converting to Kelvin** in Arrhenius problems. Kelvin is **mandatory**.
> 7. **Confusing Sensible and Latent Heat:** Sensible = no phase change. Latent = phase change. Food cooling above freezing = sensible only.
> 8. **Forgetting Freezer Burn:** Surface dehydration in air-blast freezing is a **drawback**, not a feature.
> 9. **Q₁₀ Application Direction:** Q₁₀ > 1 means rate INCREASES with temperature. A Q₁₀ = 2.5 means rate at 30 °C = 2.5 × rate at 20 °C.
> 10. **Misnaming Methods:** "IQF" is *Individual* Quick Freezing, not "Instant Quick Freezing".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 2 — Preservation by Low Temperature**
>
> - **Two Regimes:** Chilling (0 to +8 °C) and Freezing (≤ −18 °C).
> - **Three Pillars of Action:** ↓ Microbial growth, ↓ Enzyme activity, ↓ Chemical reactions.
> - **Arrhenius Equation:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)}$; linear plot of ln k vs. 1/T.
> - **Q₁₀ Rule:** Typically **2–3** for biological reactions; halved reaction rate for every 10 °C *drop*.
> - **Q₁₀ Generalised Formula:** $\dfrac{k_2}{k_1} = Q_{10}^{\Delta T / 10}$.
> - **Latent Heat of Water:** **334 kJ/kg**; for foods use **effective** $L_f$ (≈ 250 kJ/kg).
> - **Specific Heat of Food (Siebel):** $c_{p,food} = 0.837 + 3.348 X_w$.
> - **Plank's Freezing Time:** $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L_f}{\Delta T} \left[ \dfrac{P D}{\lambda_{fr}} + \dfrac{R D^2}{\lambda_{fr}^2} \right]$.
> - **Plank's Constants:** Slab (½, ⅛); Cylinder (¼, 1/16); Sphere (⅙, 1/24).
> - **Quick Freezing > 10 °C/min** → small intracellular crystals → minimal damage.
> - **Slow Freezing < 1 °C/min** → large extracellular crystals → drip loss on thaw.
> - **Freezer Burn:** Surface dehydration due to sublimation; prevented by glazing or vapour-proof packaging.
> - **Recommended Frozen Storage:** **−18 °C ± 3 °C** (Codex / FSSAI / KTU norm).
> - **Cold Chain Integrity:** Uninterrupted temperature from processing → retail → consumer.
> - **IQF:** Individual pieces frozen separately; products like peas, shrimp, berries.
> - **Cryogenic Freezing:** Uses LN₂ (−196 °C) or LCO₂ (−78 °C); ultra-rapid; expensive.
> - **Cryoconcentration:** Solutes concentrate in unfrozen phase; inhibits microbes.
> - **Bound Water:** ~ 0.1 g/g dry matter remains unfrozen even at −30 °C.
> - **Freezing Point Depression:** $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$; explains why salted water freezes below 0 °C.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** High Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# High Temperature Processing — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **High Temperature (HT) Processing** in food science refers to the deliberate application of thermal energy, typically in the range of **60 °C to 250 °C**, to raw or semi-processed food materials in order to inactivate spoilage-causing enzymes, eliminate pathogenic and spoilage micro-organisms, alter physico-chemical structure, and extend shelf life.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *OEFTT831 – Fundamentals of Food Processing*, **Module 2 – High Temperature** consolidates the science of thermal preservation, including blanching, pasteurization, sterilization/canning, baking, roasting, frying, extrusion, and emerging electro-thermal methods (ohmic, microwave, infrared).

## 1.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cooked Mango Story"

Imagine you cut a fresh mango and leave it on the counter. Within hours, polyphenol oxidase (PPO) oxidises the surface, microbes multiply, and the fruit softens. Now imagine dunking that mango in **hot water at 90 °C for 4 minutes** — the enzymes denature (PPO is permanently inactivated), most surface microbes are killed, the texture is fixed, and yet the colour and nutrients are largely retained. That thermal "shock" is the essence of **HT processing**: a controlled, time-temperature exposure that hits the *kill-zone* for enzymes/microbes while staying inside the *keep-zone* for nutrients. Industrial HT processing is simply this idea scaled up to thousands of kilograms per hour, with **sterilization (121 °C)** and **UHT (138–150 °C)** being the most aggressive variants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three kill principles of HT processing**
> 1. **Enzyme Inactivation** (denaturation) — usually at **70–95 °C**.
> 2. **Microbial Inactivation** (death) — logarithmic; quantified by **D, z, F values**.
> 3. **Quality Retention** — *cooking value* must be minimised for nutrients, colour, and texture.

## 1.2 Classification of High-Temperature Operations

| Family | Typical Range (°C) | Purpose | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Blanching** | **75 – 95** | Inactivate PPO, catalase, peroxidase | Steam blanching of vegetables |
| **Pasteurization** | **63 – 95** | Pathogen kill (5-D reduction) | HTST milk, fruit juice |
| **Ultra-High Temperature (UHT)** | **135 – 150** | Commercial sterility (aseptic) | UHT milk, cream |
| **Sterilization (in-container)** | **115 – 121** | 12-D reduction of *C. botulinum* | Canned vegetables, meat |
| **Hot-air Dehydration** | **60 – 200** | Moisture removal | Spray drying, tray drying |
| **Baking / Roasting** | **150 – 250** | Structural transformation, Maillard | Bread, biscuits, coffee |
| **Frying** | **150 – 190** | Rapid crust formation, moisture loss | Potato chips, snacks |
| **Extrusion Cooking** | **120 – 200** | Starch gelatinisation, expansion | Ready-to-eat cereals |
| **Ohmic Heating** | **70 – 150** | Volumetric Joule heating | Particulate-liquid aseptic |
| **Microwave / Infrared** | **Internal 80 – 140** | Rapid, surface/volumetric | Blanching, finish-drying |

## 1.3 Key Standard Terms (KTU Glossary)

- **TDP — Thermal Death Point**: The lowest temperature at which a microbial suspension is sterilised (no survivors) in exactly **10 minutes**.
- **TDT — Thermal Death Time**: The time, at a given constant temperature, required to inactivate a specified population of micro-organisms.
- **D-value (Decimal Reduction Time)**: Time, at a constant temperature, needed to reduce the microbial count by **90 %** (one log cycle).
- **z-value**: The temperature increase (°C) required to reduce the D-value by a factor of 10.
- **F-value**: The equivalent time, at a reference temperature, that delivers the same lethality as the actual time-temperature profile.
- **Lethality (L)**: Ratio of D at the reference temperature to D at the actual temperature.
- **Cold Point (C-point)**: The geometrically slowest-heating location inside a product; lethality is calculated at this point.

> [!TIP]
> **Why these parameters matter for KTU Board exams:** The 14-mark numerical/descriptive questions in Module 2 almost always centre on the **D–z–F linkage** and a *lethality calculation*. Memorise the first-order rate law and the three definitions above.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microbial Inactivation Kinetics — First-Order Death

At a constant lethal temperature, microbial inactivation follows **first-order kinetics**. The population $N$ (CFU/g or CFU/mL) decreases as:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k\,N$$

where $k$ is the specific death-rate constant (min$^{-1}$). The solution, after integration between $t=0$ and $t=t$, is:

$$N(t) = N_0\,e^{-k\,t}$$

Taking base-10 logarithm (the standard food-engineering convention):

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{k}{2.303}\,t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why first order?** Empirical evidence: a *constant fraction* (not a constant number) of cells die per unit time. This is a phenomenological law — the underlying biochemistry of membrane/protein damage is far more complex, but the macroscopic decline is log-linear.

## 2.2 Quality Degradation Kinetics

Nutrient loss (e.g., vitamin C, lysine) and sensory deterioration (colour, texture) follow a *parallel* first-order or zero-order kinetic path, with their own **D-quality** and **z-quality** values:

- For most vitamins: **first-order** in concentration.
- For texture softening, non-enzymatic browning: **zero-order** in product properties.
- **z-quality ≈ 25–33 °C** (much larger than microbial z), meaning quality degrades much more slowly with temperature.

## 2.3 Heat-Transfer Modes Relevant to HT Processing

| Mode | Mechanism | Typical Products |
|---|---|---|
| **Conduction** | Molecular vibration transfer | Canned solid foods, sausages |
| **Convection** | Bulk fluid motion | Milk, juices, soups |
| **Radiation (IR / MW)** | Electromagnetic energy | Surface drying, finish-baking |
| **Dielectric / Ohmic** | Internal volumetric heating | Particulate foods, in-line heating |

The slowest-heating point is the **C-point** — for conduction products it is the geometric centre, for convection it is where the flow stagnates.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (with boundary conditions & units)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Markdown Table Rule:** All absolute-value and ratio bars in this table are written as $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking table syntax. Use these forms when copying the formulae into your answer scripts.

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Range | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N(t) = N_0\,e^{-k\,t}$ | First-order microbial death | $N(t) \le N_0$ | CFU/g |
| 2 | $\log\!\left(\dfrac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\dfrac{t}{D}$ | Log-linear form; **D = 2.303 / k** | $D \in [0.1, 1000]$ min | min |
| 3 | $D = \dfrac{2.303}{k}$ | Decimal Reduction Time | $T = \text{const}$ | min |
| 4 | $\log\!\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{z}$ | z-value definition | $z \in [4, 18]$ °C for microbes | °C |
| 5 | $L = 10^{(T - T_{\text{ref}})/z}$ | Instantaneous Lethality | $T \ge T_{\text{ref}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $F = \displaystyle\int_0^{t_{\text{proc}}} 10^{(T(t) - T_{\text{ref}})/z}\,dt$ | Process lethality integral | $T_{\text{ref}} = 121.1$ °C for $F_0$ | min |
| 7 | $F_0 = D_{\text{ref}} \cdot \log\!\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)$ | Required $F_0$ for n-log reduction | Botulinum cook: $F_0 \ge 3$ min | min |
| 8 | $k = A\,e^{-E_a/(RT)}$ | Arrhenius rate law | $R = 8.314$ J mol$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$ | min$^{-1}$ |
| 9 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Two-temperature Arrhenius | K | — |
| 10 | $C = \displaystyle\int_0^t 10^{(T - T_{\text{ref,c}})/z_c}\,dt$ | Cooking Value (quality) | $z_c \approx 25$–$33$ °C | min |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference temperature conventions (KTU 2024 standard)**
> - **$T_{\text{ref}} = 121.1$ °C (250 °F)** → for $F_0$ in *Clostridium botulinum* cook (commercial sterilization).
> - **$T_{\text{ref}} = 65.5$ °C (150 °F)** → for *pasteurization value* $P$ of milk.
> - **$T_{\text{ref,c}} = 100$ °C** → for *cooking value* $C$ of vegetables.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Process Authority / Regulatory Compliance:** $F_0 \ge 3$ min is the legal threshold for low-acid canned foods (FDA / FSSAI).
- **Process Validation:** By integrating the time-temperature profile measured at the C-point, the manufacturer can *prove* commercial sterility.
- **Optimization Trade-off:** Process engineers walk a tightrope — *raise* $F_0$ to ensure safety, *limit* $C$ to retain colour, vitamins and texture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section shows the full algebraic pathway for the three KTU-favourite derivations. Every step is written explicitly; nothing is implicit. Use these to score full marks in any 14-mark derivational question.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The D-value from First-Order Kinetics

**Goal:** Express the **D-value** (time for one log reduction) in terms of the first-order rate constant $k$.

**Step 1 — State the rate equation.**
The decrease of viable cell count $N$ with time at constant temperature follows:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k\,N$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate.**

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k\int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k\,t$$

**Step 4 — Convert natural log to base-10 log** (food-engineering convention; log = log$_{10}$).

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{k}{2.303}\,t$$

**Step 5 — Apply the definition of D-value.** By definition, when $t = D$, the population has dropped by a factor of 10, i.e. $N = N_0 / 10$, so $\log_{10}(N/N_0) = -1$.

$$-1 = -\frac{k}{2.303}\,D$$

**Step 6 — Solve for D.**

$$\boxed{\,D = \frac{2.303}{k}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Units check:** If $k$ is in min$^{-1}$, then $D$ is in min. This validates the derivation dimensionally.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The z-value Relationship

**Goal:** Show that the D-value decreases tenfold for every *z* °C rise in temperature.

**Step 1 — Write the D-value equation at two different temperatures.**

$$D_1 = \frac{2.303}{k_1}, \quad D_2 = \frac{2.303}{k_2}$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio.**

$$\frac{D_1}{D_2} = \frac{k_2}{k_1}$$

**Step 3 — Invoke the empirical log-linear thermal death time (TDT) curve.** Experimental data plotted as $\log_{10}(D)$ vs. $T$ is a straight line whose slope is $-1/z$:

$$\log_{10}(D) = -\frac{T}{z} + C$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the equation at $T_1$ from that at $T_2$.**

$$\log_{10}(D_2) - \log_{10}(D_1) = -\frac{T_2 - T_1}{z}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange.**

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{z}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for z.**

$$\boxed{\,z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal Death Time (TDT) Curve:** A plot of $\log_{10}(D)$ (ordinate) versus $T$ (abscissa) gives a straight line with slope $-1/z$. This is the standard *graphical fingerprint* of microbial heat resistance used in retort process design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Process Lethality F for Non-Isothermal Profiles

**Goal:** Convert a varying temperature profile $T(t)$ into a single equivalent time $F$ at the reference temperature.

**Step 1 — Start with the integrated lethality at constant temperature.** From Derivation 1, the log-reduction is:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{t}{D(T)}$$

**Step 2 — Express $D(T)$ relative to $D_{\text{ref}}$ using the z-relationship.**

$$D(T) = D_{\text{ref}} \cdot 10^{-(T - T_{\text{ref}})/z}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Step 1.**

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{t}{D_{\text{ref}}} \cdot 10^{(T - T_{\text{ref}})/z}$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the ratio $t / D_{\text{ref}}$ as the lethality $F$ at constant $T$.**

**Step 5 — Generalise to a *time-varying* temperature $T(t)$ by integrating:**

$$\boxed{\,F = \int_{0}^{t_{\text{proc}}} 10^{(T(t) - T_{\text{ref}})/z}\,dt\,}$$

For constant $T$, the integrand is constant, and the integral reduces to $F = 10^{(T - T_{\text{ref}})/z}\cdot t$ — exactly Step 3.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Lethality Calculation (KTU Board Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical:** A food product is held at **130 °C** for **4 minutes**. The reference temperature is **121.1 °C** and **z = 10 °C** (typical for *C. botulinum*). Calculate (i) the instantaneous lethality, and (ii) the cumulative $F_0$.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous lethality $L$.**

$$L = 10^{(T - T_{\text{ref}})/z} = 10^{(130 - 121.1)/10}$$

$$L = 10^{8.9 / 10} = 10^{0.89}$$

$$L \approx 7.76$$

> **Interpretation:** Each minute at 130 °C delivers lethality equivalent to **7.76 minutes** at 121.1 °C.

**Step 2 — Cumulative $F_0$.** Since $T$ is constant, integrate the constant lethality over $t_{\text{proc}} = 4$ min:

$$F_0 = L \cdot t_{\text{proc}} = 7.76 \times 4$$

$$F_0 \approx 31.0 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Judge against regulatory threshold.**

$$\boxed{\,F_0 = 31.0 \text{ min} \;\gg\; F_0^{\text{min}} = 3 \text{ min}\,}$$

> The process achieves more than a **10-fold safety margin** above the legal *C. botulinum* cook (12-D reduction), confirming commercial sterility.

**Step 4 — Equivalent time at 121.1 °C for the same lethality.** A 31.0-min hold at 121.1 °C would be required, confirming the design.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Temperature on Kinetic Parameters

| Temperature | D-value | z-value | F-value | L | Practical Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ (Higher) | ↓ (Smaller) | **Constant** (property of organism) | ↑ (Faster lethality) | ↑ (>1) | Faster kill, but quality loss |
| ↓ (Lower) | ↑ (Larger) | **Constant** | ↓ (Slower lethality) | ↓ (<1) | Slower kill, more retention |

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual master key:** "**D** decreases as **T** increases; **z** is a *constant* characteristic of the organism; **F** is the *engineered* parameter that integrates both."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The mermaid diagrams below are rendered as **functional block diagrams**, not freehand drawings. They show process flow, kinetics linkage, and C-point geometry — the three figures that KTU paper-setters reproduce most often.

## 4.1 Diagram A — Functional Block Diagram: A Canning Retort Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[RawFoodIntake] --> nodeB[CanFillingSealing]
    nodeB --> nodeC[RetortLoading]
    nodeC --> nodeD[SteamHeating]
    nodeD --> nodeE[TemperatureMonitoring]
    nodeE --> nodeF{ColdPointReachedTargetT}
    nodeF -->|No| nodeD
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[HoldForF0]
    nodeG --> nodeH[SteamCutCooling]
    nodeH --> nodeI[PressureEquilibration]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[RetortUnloading]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[StorageStability]
    nodeE -.-> nodeL[DataLogger]
    nodeL -.-> nodeM[F0Calculator]
    nodeM -.-> nodeN[QualityCheckF0atleast3]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **retort loop** (nodes nodeD → nodeE → nodeF → nodeD) continues until the **C-point** reaches the target lethality temperature.
- The dashed arrows (nodeE → nodeL → nodeM → nodeN) depict the **off-line F₀ calculation** by the data logger.
- The **decision block** (nodeF) and the **regulatory check** (nodeN) are the two gates that *must* pass for commercial sterility.

## 4.2 Diagram B — Kinetics Linkage: Microbial Death vs. Quality Degradation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[TemperatureTimeProfile] --> p2[MicrobialLogReduction]
    p1 --> p3[QualityLossNutrientTexture]
    p2 --> p4[DValueForMicrobes]
    p2 --> p5[ZValueForMicrobes]
    p3 --> p6[DValueForQuality]
    p3 --> p7[ZValueForQuality]
    p4 --> p8[ProcessF0Lethality]
    p5 --> p8
    p6 --> p9[CookingValueC]
    p7 --> p9
    p8 --> p10[ProcessAuthority]
    p9 --> p11[NutrientRetention]
    p10 --> p12[DesignOptimumF0vsC]
    p11 --> p12
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **left branch** (p2 → p4, p5 → p8 → p10) is the **safety pathway** (microbial kill → $F_0$ → process authority).
- The **right branch** (p3 → p6, p7 → p9 → p11) is the **quality pathway** (sensory/nutrient loss → cooking value $C$ → retention).
- The two pathways converge at **p12**, which is the **design optimum** — the engineer's compromise between *kill* and *keep*.

## 4.3 Diagram C — C-Point Geometry in a Conduction-Heating Can

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1[CanGeometry] --> q2[TopCentre]
    q1 --> q3[SideWallMidHeight]
    q1 --> q4[GeometricCentreColdPoint]
    q2 --> q5[TemperatureSensors]
    q3 --> q5
    q4 --> q5
    q5 --> q6[TimeTemperatureHistoryAtColdPoint]
    q6 --> q7[HeatPenetrationCurve]
    q7 --> q8[SlopeDetermineFhAndJ]
    q8 --> q9[BallFormulaMethod]
    q8 --> q10[StumboMethod]
    q9 --> q11[ComputeF0AtColdPoint]
    q10 --> q11
    q11 --> q12[ValidateAgainstLegalF0]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **C-point** is the **geometric centre** (q4) of a conduction-heating cylindrical can.
- Sensors (q5) record the **T vs. t** at q2, q3, q4.
- The slowest-heating **cold-point curve** (q7) yields the **slope $f_h$** and **lag factor $j$** that feed the **Ball** (q9) or **Stumbo** (q10) formula for $F_0$ at the C-point.
