# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are tagged with simulated KTU University Examination references and follow the **2024 Scheme mark distribution** (Part A: 3 marks each; Part B: 14 marks each with sub-parts a and b, 7 marks each). Model answers and a valuation warning are provided.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

### Q1. Define D-value and z-value in the context of thermal processing of foods. State their units. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- **D-value (Decimal Reduction Time):** The time, in **minutes**, required at a *constant lethal temperature* to reduce the viable microbial population by **90 %** (i.e., one log cycle, or a factor of 10). Unit: **min**.
- **z-value:** The temperature increase, in **°C**, required to reduce the D-value by a factor of 10. Unit: **°C**. For *C. botulinum*, $z \approx 10$ °C; for most quality factors, $z_c \approx 25$–$33$ °C.

> *[1 mark for D-value, 1 mark for z-value, 1 mark for units and example.]*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between HTST and UHT pasteurization with suitable examples. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **HTST** (High-Temperature Short-Time) | **UHT** (Ultra-High Temperature) |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | **72 °C** | **135 – 150 °C** |
| Time | **15 s** | **2 – 5 s** |
| Process | Continuous plate heat exchanger | Direct/indirect steam injection or infusion |
| Shelf-life | **2 – 3 weeks** (refrigerated) | **3 – 6 months** (ambient) |
| Example | Pasteurised market milk | UHT-treated flavoured milk |
| Microbial target | 5-D kill of *Coxiella burnetii* | Commercial sterility (aseptic) |

> *[1 mark for temperature/time, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for shelf-life/process.]*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part is **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** State the first-order kinetic equation governing microbial inactivation at constant temperature. Derive the relationship between D-value and the rate constant $k$. What is the typical D-value of *Bacillus stearothermophilus* spores at 121.1 °C? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A thermal process achieves a cold-point temperature of **128 °C** for a holding time of **5 minutes**. Take the reference temperature $T_{\text{ref}} = 121.1$ °C and $z = 10$ °C. Calculate the **lethality $F_0$**. Comment on whether the process satisfies the *C. botulinum* cook requirement. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (a)

**Step 1 — State the first-order equation.**

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k\,N, \quad N(t) = N_0 e^{-k\,t}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to log form.**

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{k}{2.303}\,t$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of D-value.** When $t = D$, $N = N_0 / 10$ and $\log_{10}(N/N_0) = -1$.

$$-1 = -\frac{k}{2.303}\,D \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{D = \frac{2.303}{k}}$$

**Step 4 — Typical D-value.**

For *Bacillus stearothermophilus*, the most heat-resistant non-pathogenic reference spore, $D_{121.1\,°C} \approx \mathbf{1.5 \text{ min}}$ (commonly cited in the range 1.0 – 2.0 min; it serves as the biological indicator for *UHT* and *in-container sterilization*).

> **Mark split:** [2 — equation; 2 — log conversion; 2 — derivation; 1 — typical D-value.]

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (b)

**Step 1 — Calculate instantaneous lethality $L$.**

$$L = 10^{(T - T_{\text{ref}})/z} = 10^{(128 - 121.1)/10}$$

$$L = 10^{6.9/10} = 10^{0.69}$$

$$L \approx 4.90$$

**Step 2 — Compute $F_0$** (constant T, so $F_0 = L \cdot t$).

$$F_0 = 4.90 \times 5 = \mathbf{24.5 \text{ min}}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the *C. botulinum* cook threshold.**

$$F_0 = 24.5 \text{ min} \;\ge\; F_0^{\text{min}} = 3 \text{ min} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Comment.**

> The process delivers lethality **8.2 × the regulatory minimum** for a botulinum cook. The margin is conservative, but the engineer should *also* verify the cooking value $C$ to ensure acceptable nutrient/colour retention — a 24.5-min equivalent at 121.1 °C will over-cook delicate products. A retort-temperature optimisation is recommended.

> **Mark split:** [2 — L; 2 — F₀; 2 — comparison; 1 — engineering comment.]

---

### **Question B (14 marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the working of a **continuous rotary retort** used in commercial sterilization. Identify the **cold point** and explain why lethality is computed there. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A fruit juice is pasteurised by an HTST exchanger at **85 °C for 25 s**. The reference temperature for pasteurisation value is **65.5 °C** and **$z$ for *Coxiella burnetii* = 5 °C**. Calculate the **pasteurisation value $P$** and the **equivalent time at 65.5 °C**. Comment on whether the process meets the **5-D reduction** target for *C. burnetii* (assume $D_{65.5\,°C} \approx 30$ s). *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (a)

**Step 1 — Block diagram (write in script, examiner-friendly).**

```
INTAKE (cans) → LOADING CRADLE → ROTATING SHELL
         │             │              │
         ▼             ▼              ▼
   PRE-HEAT SOAK    STEAM INLET (≈ 121 °C)   COLD-POINT PROBE (can centre)
         │             │              │
         └─────────────┴──────────────┘
                        │
                        ▼
              HOLD (F₀ integration)
                        │
                        ▼
             STEAM CUT → WATER COOL → AIR COOL
                        │
                        ▼
                  UNLOAD → STORAGE
```

**Step 2 — Cold point (C-point).**

For a conduction-heating product in a metal can, the C-point is the **geometric centre** of the can (along the central axis, at the mid-height of the can). This is the *slowest-heating* location because:

- Heat is transferred *radially* from the can wall.
- The temperature at the centre lags behind the wall.
- The C-point thus experiences the *minimum* time-temperature history, so it *governs* the entire lethality calculation.

**Step 3 — Why lethality is computed at the C-point.**

Because the C-point receives the *least* heat, *if* the microbial inactivation here meets the regulatory $F_0$ (≥ 3 min), then **all other points** in the can receive *more* heat and are *automatically safe*. This is the **worst-case principle** in process validation.

> **Mark split:** [2 — block diagram; 2 — C-point identification; 3 — worst-case justification.]

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (b)

**Step 1 — Instantaneous pasteurisation lethality $L_P$.**

$$L_P = 10^{(T - T_{\text{ref}})/z} = 10^{(85 - 65.5)/5}$$

$$L_P = 10^{19.5/5} = 10^{3.9}$$

$$L_P \approx 7943$$

**Step 2 — Pasteurisation value $P$.**

$$P = L_P \cdot t = 7943 \times 25 \text{ s}$$

$$P \approx \mathbf{1.99 \times 10^5 \text{ s}} \;\approx\; \mathbf{3312 \text{ min}}$$

> *(Express in seconds or minutes; either is acceptable as long as units are stated.)*

**Step 3 — Equivalent time at 65.5 °C.**

$$t_{\text{eq}} = \frac{P}{L_{\text{ref}}} = P \cdot D_{\text{ref}}$$

$$t_{\text{eq}} = 1.99\times 10^5 \text{ s} \times \frac{1}{1} \;\;\text{??}$$

> *Correction:* The equivalent time at $T_{\text{ref}}$ is simply the hold time that gives the *same log reduction* — since $L_{\text{ref}} = 10^{(65.5-65.5)/5} = 1$:

$$t_{\text{eq, ref}} = P / 1 = \mathbf{1.99 \times 10^5 \text{ s}} \approx \mathbf{55.3 \text{ h}}$$

> That is, a hold of **~55 hours** at 65.5 °C would be needed to deliver the same lethality as **25 s at 85 °C**.

**Step 4 — 5-D reduction check.**

The 5-D reduction target requires:

$$F_{\text{req}} = 5 \times D_{65.5\,°C} = 5 \times 30 \text{ s} = 150 \text{ s}$$

Our $P = 1.99\times10^5 \text{ s} \gg 150$ s. **The process meets the 5-D target by a margin of ~1300 ×** — extremely safe from a microbial standpoint, but again the engineer should weigh *over-processing* of flavour and vitamin C against this massive safety margin.

> **Mark split:** [2 — $L_P$; 2 — $P$ and equivalent time; 2 — 5-D check; 1 — design comment.]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the 2.303 conversion factor** in $\log_{10}$ vs. $\ln$. Always write the conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up z and z_c.** Microbial $z \approx 4$–$18$ °C; quality $z_c \approx 25$–$33$ °C. The *numerical* answer changes drastically.
> 3. **Using $T_{\text{ref}} = 121.1$ °C** for *pasteurisation* questions — wrong. Pasteurisation uses $T_{\text{ref}} = 65.5$ °C (or 71.7 °C in some standards).
> 4. **Not identifying the C-point** when a canning/sterilisation question is asked — examiners mark this as a "conceptual lapse".
> 5. **Omitting units** in numerical answers — KTU 2024 valuation strictly deducts ½ mark for missing or wrong units.
> 6. **Reporting $L$ without multiplying by $t$** to get $F_0$ — a classic 2-mark deduction.
> 7. **Confusing $F$ and $F_0$**: $F$ is *generic* (any $T_{\text{ref}}$); $F_0$ specifically refers to $T_{\text{ref}} = 121.1$ °C.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "MUST-REMEMBER" list for Module 2 — High Temperature**

- [x] **HT processing** in food covers **blanching, pasteurisation, sterilisation, baking, roasting, frying, extrusion, ohmic, microwave, IR**.
- [x] **Blanching** (**75 – 95 °C / 1 – 10 min**) targets **PPO, catalase, peroxidase** — *not* commercial sterility.
- [x] **Pasteurisation** — *pathogen reduction*, **5-D** target on *Coxiella burnetii* in milk; HTST = **72 °C / 15 s**.
- [x] **UHT** — **135 – 150 °C / 2 – 5 s**; achieves **commercial sterility** with aseptic packaging; shelf-life **3 – 6 months** at ambient.
- [x] **Commercial sterilisation** — **$F_0 \ge 3$ min** at $T_{\text{ref}} = 121.1$ °C, $z = 10$ °C (botulinum cook).
- [x] **First-order microbial death** $\Rightarrow D = 2.303/k$ at constant $T$.
- [x] **z-value** is the slope parameter of the *log D vs. T* plot.
- [x] **Lethality integral** $F = \int 10^{(T - T_{\text{ref}})/z}\,dt$ — the core equation of *every* thermal-process numerical.
- [x] **C-point (cold point)** — slowest-heating locus (centre of can for conduction products) — lethality is computed *here* under the *worst-case principle*.
- [x] **Quality retention vs. safety** — trade-off between **cooking value $C$** (lower is better) and **$F_0$** (higher is safer).
- [x] **Reference temperature conventions** — $121.1$ °C for $F_0$; $65.5$ °C for pasteurisation $P$; $100$ °C for cooking $C$.
- [x] **Arrhenius** $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ is the *theoretical* underpinning, while $D$–$z$ formulation is the *empirical* shortcut.
- [x] **Heat transfer** — conduction, convection, radiation, dielectric/ohmic — governs the *time-temperature history* at the C-point.
- [x] **HTST vs. UHT vs. in-container retort** — three columns of the *pasteurisation-sterilisation* matrix that always appear in the 14-mark questions.
- [x] **Ball Formula** and **Stumbo's formula method** are the standard *graphical–analytical* routes to compute $F_0$ from heat-penetration data; mention them in descriptive answers for full marks.

> **Final KTU Mnemonic for Process Safety:** *"$F_0 \ge 3$ at the C-point, $P$ in seconds, $C$ in minutes, $z$ in °C, $D$ in min — write units or lose marks."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preservatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Preservatives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Preservative (KTU 2024 Definition):** A food preservative is any naturally occurring or chemically synthesised substance that is intentionally added to food products in controlled quantities to **inhibit, retard, or arrest** the process of microbial spoilage, enzymatic degradation, oxidative rancidity, and other quality deterioration pathways, thereby extending the **shelf-life** and maintaining the **nutritional quality, sensory attributes, and safety** of the food.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a preservative is classified under **Food Additives (Section 3.1 of FSSAI, 2006)** — specifically, the sub-class of substances that prevent or retard the natural processes of food deterioration. The three governing objectives are:

1. **Microbial Control** – Inhibition of bacteria, yeasts, moulds, and fungi.
2. **Chemical Control** – Prevention of oxidation, browning, and rancidity.
3. **Physical Control** – Retardation of enzymatic and textural degradation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Regulatory Body:** In India, the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006** governs the use, dosage, and labelling of all permitted preservatives. Globally, **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** and **FDA (USA)** standards are referenced.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **The "Bodyguard of Food" Analogy**
>
> Think of your freshly prepared food — say, a bowl of homemade mango pickle or a soft loaf of bread — as a **celebrity** who is constantly under threat from "villains" (microbes, oxygen, enzymes, moisture). A preservative is like a **trained bodyguard** standing at the door, performing three jobs:
>
> 1. **Bouncer duty** — physically blocking harmful bacteria and fungi from entering and multiplying.
> 2. **Anti-rust spray** — preventing oxygen from "rusting" the fats (oxidative rancidity).
> 3. **Time freezer** — slowing down the natural ripening/ageing enzymes (like slowing down a fast-forwarded movie).
>
> Without this bodyguard, food "ages" and spoils in hours/days. With it, food can last weeks, months, or even years — exactly what the food industry needs to transport food from Kerala's farms to consumers in Delhi or Dubai.

---

## 1.3 Why Preservatives Are Required — The Spoilage Triad

Food spoils due to three interlocking causes, and preservatives address all three:

| Spoilage Factor | Mechanism | Preservative Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Microbial** | Bacteria, yeasts, moulds metabolise food | Antimicrobial agents (salt, sugar, benzoates) |
| **Chemical** | Oxidation of fats → rancidity | Antioxidants (BHA, BHT, ascorbic acid) |
| **Enzymatic** | Native enzymes break down tissue | Blanching + enzyme inhibitors (citric acid) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Standard Metric:** The legal usage of preservatives is always expressed as parts per million (ppm) or milligrams per kilogram (mg/kg) of the finished food product. The general maximum limit (GMP — Good Manufacturing Practice) ceiling is **1000 ppm (0.1%)** for most Class II preservatives under FSSAI.

---

## 1.4 Scope of the Topic (KTU 2024 Module 2 Boundary)

Within Module 2 of OEFTT831, the topic of **Preservatives** is bounded by the following learning outcomes:

- **CO2:** Identify and apply appropriate preservation techniques based on food type and spoilage mechanism.
- Classification of preservatives (chemical, natural, antioxidant, antimicrobial).
- Mode of action at cellular and molecular level.
- Examples with permitted concentration limits.
- Effects on nutritional and sensory quality.
- Recent trends: bio-preservatives, hurdle technology, natural extracts.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Food Preservatives (KTU High-Yield Framework)

Preservatives are classified along **three primary axes** — origin, function, and regulatory class. The KTU 2024 exam expects a student to know all three classifications cold.

### A. Classification by Origin

1. **Class I — Natural / Traditional Preservatives**
   - Definition: Substances of biological origin (plant, animal, microbial) used since antiquity.
   - Examples: Salt (NaCl), sugar (sucrose), vinegar (acetic acid), honey, spices (clove, cinnamon, turmeric), wood smoke, lemon juice, mustard oil.

2. **Class II — Chemical / Synthetic Preservatives**
   - Definition: Industrially synthesised molecules with proven antimicrobial or antioxidant activity, approved by FSSAI in defined concentrations.
   - Examples: Sodium benzoate (E211), potassium sorbate (E202), BHA (E320), BHT (E321), sodium nitrite (E250), sulphur dioxide (E220), propionic acid (E280).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "Class I = Grandmother's kitchen; Class II = Industrial factory." Class I preservatives are generally regarded as safe (GRAS) at traditional usage levels without strict upper limits, while Class II preservatives are legally capped at specified ppm.

### B. Classification by Function (Mode of Action)

| Function Category | Mechanism | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Antimicrobial** | Disrupt microbial cell wall/membrane, inhibit enzyme systems, denature proteins | NaCl, sugar, vinegar, benzoates, sorbates, nitrites, sulphites |
| **Antioxidant** | Scavenge free radicals, chelate pro-oxidant metals, prevent lipid peroxidation | BHA, BHT, ascorbic acid, tocopherols, EDTA |
| **Antienzymatic** | Inactivate native food enzymes (polyphenol oxidase, lipase) | Citric acid, ascorbic acid, blanching + SO₂ |
| **Chelating Agents** | Bind metal ions (Fe²⁺, Cu²⁺) that catalyse oxidation | EDTA, citric acid, polyphosphates |

### C. Classification by Regulatory Class (FSSAI Schedule)

- **Schedule I** — colours, flavours, etc.
- **Schedule II** — permitted Class II preservatives with maximum levels.
- **Schedule III** — antioxidants, emulsifiers, stabilisers.

---

## 2.2 Mechanism of Action — The "How" Behind Preservation

### 2.2.1 Antimicrobial Preservatives — How They Work

Microbial cells maintain homeostasis through their **cytoplasmic membrane**, **proton gradient (ΔpH)**, and **enzyme systems**. Antimicrobial preservatives disrupt one or more of these:

1. **Membrane Disruption (Surfactant-like action)**
   - Example: Sorbic acid (CH₃-CH=CH-CH=CH-COOH) inserts its hydrophobic tail into the microbial membrane, creating pores.
   - Result: Loss of ion balance → cell death.

2. **Enzyme Inhibition**
   - Example: Sulphite (SO₃²⁻) binds to the disulphide bonds (-S-S-) of microbial enzymes, deactivating them.
   - Result: Microbe cannot metabolise food.

3. **pH Reduction (Acidulants)**
   - Example: Acetic acid (vinegar) lowers pH below 4.5, beyond which most bacteria cannot grow.
   - Result: Metabolic arrest.

4. **Osmotic Pressure (Water Activity Reduction)**
   - Example: Salt and sugar create hypertonic environments; water exits the microbial cell via osmosis.
   - Result: Plasmolysis → microbial death or dormancy.

### 2.2.2 Antioxidant Preservatives — How They Work

Lipid oxidation proceeds via the **free-radical chain mechanism** with three stages: *initiation, propagation, termination*.

Antioxidants act in two ways:

- **Primary (Chain-breaking) antioxidants:** Donate a hydrogen atom to the lipid radical, terminating the chain.
  - Examples: BHA, BHT, tocopherols, ascorbic acid.

- **Secondary (Preventive) antioxidants:** Chelate pro-oxidant metals or decompose hydroperoxides.
  - Examples: EDTA, citric acid, phosphoric acid.

---

## 2.3 Important Chemical Structures & Identities (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!TIP]
> **Study Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to write the chemical formula or E-number of a preservative. Memorise the following master list.

| Preservative | Chemical Formula | E-Number | Function | Typical Use Level (ppm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sodium Chloride (Salt) | NaCl | — | Antimicrobial (osmotic) | 10,000 – 50,000 |
| Sucrose (Sugar) | C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ | — | Antimicrobial (osmotic) | 50,000 – 200,000 |
| Acetic Acid (Vinegar) | CH₃COOH | E260 | Antimicrobial (pH) | 5,000 – 20,000 |
| Sodium Benzoate | C₆H₅COONa | E211 | Antimicrobial (yeast/mould) | 150 – 1,000 |
| Potassium Sorbate | CH₃CH=CHCH=CHCOOK | E202 | Antimicrobial (mould/yeast) | 200 – 1,000 |
| Sodium Nitrite | NaNO₂ | E250 | Antimicrobial (Clostridium botulinum) | 50 – 200 |
| Sulphur Dioxide | SO₂ | E220 | Antimicrobial + antioxidant | 100 – 500 |
| BHA (Butylated Hydroxyanisole) | C₁₁H₁₆O₂ | E320 | Antioxidant | 100 – 200 |
| BHT (Butylated Hydroxytoluene) | C₁₅H₂₄O | E321 | Antioxidant | 100 – 200 |
| Citric Acid | C₆H₈O₇ | E330 | Antioxidant (chelator) + acidulant | 500 – 5,000 |
| Ascorbic Acid (Vitamin C) | C₆H₈O₆ | E300 | Antioxidant | 200 – 1,000 |
| EDTA (Disodium salt) | C₁₀H₁₄N₂Na₂O₈ | E386 | Antioxidant (chelator) | 50 – 250 |
| Propionic Acid | CH₃CH₂COOH | E280 | Antimicrobial (bread mould) | 1,000 – 3,000 |

---

## 2.4 Key Equations & Relationships (KTU Formula Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although food preservation is largely empirical, the following **quantitative relationships** are essential for KTU Part A and Part B numerical/analytical questions.

### 2.4.1 Water Activity ($a_w$) and Microbial Growth

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0} = \frac{\text{Partial pressure of water vapour above food}}{\text{Vapour pressure of pure water at same T}}$$

- Bacteria: inhibited below $a_w \approx 0.91$
- Yeasts: inhibited below $a_w \approx 0.88$
- Moulds: inhibited below $a_w \approx 0.80$
- Halophilic bacteria: can grow down to $a_w \approx 0.75$

### 2.4.2 Shelf-life Estimation (Q₁₀ Rule)

$$Q_{10} = \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

where $k_1$ and $k_2$ are rate constants at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ (°C).

For most spoilage reactions, $Q_{10} \approx 2$ to $3$, meaning a **10 °C temperature rise doubles or triples the spoilage rate**.

### 2.4.3 Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation (for Acidulant Preservatives)

$$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)$$

This is critical when selecting the right acidulant. For example, **benzoic acid** (pKₐ = 4.2) is most effective below pH 4.5, where the undissociated (HA) form dominates and can diffuse into microbial cells.

### 2.4.4 Preservative Concentration in ppm Conversion

$$\text{Concentration (ppm)} = \frac{\text{Mass of preservative (mg)}}{\text{Mass of food (kg)}}$$

Since $1 \text{ kg} = 10^6 \text{ mg}$:

$$1 \text{ ppm} = \frac{1 \text{ mg}}{1 \text{ kg}} = \frac{1 \text{ mg}}{10^6 \text{ mg}}$$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of preservation underpin entire industries:

- **Dairy Industry** — Pasteurised milk + sodium benzoate in flavoured milk.
- **Bakery Industry** — Calcium propionate in bread to prevent rope mould.
- **Beverage Industry** — Sodium benzoate in soft drinks (pH < 4.0).
- **Meat Curing** — Sodium nitrite in sausages to prevent *Clostridium botulinum* toxin and fix colour.
- **Pickle & Condiment Industry** — Vinegar + salt in Kerala-style mango pickle.
- **Marine Products** — Sulphite treatment of prawns/shrimps for export.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Shelf-life Reduction by Preservative Inhibition

> **Problem (Modelled on KTU 2019 University Exam):**
> A fruit juice initially has a yeast count of $N_0 = 10^5$ CFU/mL. Without preservative, the count doubles every **3 hours** at 25 °C. If sodium benzoate is added at 1000 ppm, the doubling time increases to **24 hours**. Calculate the microbial count after 72 hours in (a) untreated juice and (b) benzoate-treated juice. Comment on the effectiveness.

### Step-by-Step Solution

We use the **exponential growth equation**:

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t / t_d}$$

where $N(t)$ is the count at time $t$, $N_0$ is the initial count, and $t_d$ is the doubling time.

**Part (a) — Untreated Juice**

- $N_0 = 10^5$ CFU/mL
- $t_d = 3$ hours
- $t = 72$ hours

The number of doubling cycles in 72 hours:

$$\text{Cycles} = \frac{t}{t_d} = \frac{72 \text{ h}}{3 \text{ h}} = 24$$

Substituting:

$$N_{\text{untreated}} = 10^5 \cdot 2^{24}$$

Computing $2^{24}$:

$$2^{10} = 1024 \approx 1.024 \times 10^3$$

$$2^{20} = (2^{10})^2 \approx (1.024 \times 10^3)^2 \approx 1.0486 \times 10^6$$

$$2^{24} = 2^{20} \cdot 2^4 = 1.0486 \times 10^6 \times 16 \approx 1.6777 \times 10^7$$

Therefore:

$$N_{\text{untreated}} = 10^5 \times 1.6777 \times 10^7 = 1.6777 \times 10^{12} \text{ CFU/mL}$$

**Part (b) — Benzoate-Treated Juice**

- $t_d = 24$ hours
- $t = 72$ hours

$$\text{Cycles} = \frac{72}{24} = 3$$

$$N_{\text{treated}} = 10^5 \cdot 2^{3} = 10^5 \times 8 = 8 \times 10^5 \text{ CFU/mL}$$

**Inference:** Benzoate at 1000 ppm reduces the microbial count by a factor of:

$$\text{Reduction factor} = \frac{1.6777 \times 10^{12}}{8 \times 10^{5}} \approx 2.1 \times 10^6$$

This is a **6-log reduction** — an enormous improvement, demonstrating the high effectiveness of sodium benzoate at legal limits when the juice pH is below 4.5.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Choosing the Right Acidulant via Henderson–Hasselbalch

> **Problem:** A fruit preserve has pH = 3.5. Compare the **undissociated fraction** of (i) benzoic acid (pKₐ = 4.20) and (ii) sorbic acid (pKₐ = 4.76). Which is the better preservative in this product, and why?

### Step-by-Step Solution

The **Henderson–Hasselbalch equation** gives:

$$\text{pH} - \text{p}K_a = \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)$$

The **undissociated fraction** $\alpha_{HA}$ is defined as:

$$\alpha_{HA} = \frac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{HA}] + [\text{A}^-]} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{\text{pH} - \text{p}K_a}}$$

**For Benzoic Acid at pH 3.5:**

$$\text{pH} - \text{p}K_a = 3.5 - 4.20 = -0.70$$

$$\alpha_{HA,\text{benzoic}} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{-0.70}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1995} = \frac{1}{1.1995} \approx 0.834$$

**For Sorbic Acid at pH 3.5:**

$$\text{pH} - \text{p}K_a = 3.5 - 4.76 = -1.26$$

$$\alpha_{HA,\text{sorbic}} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{-1.26}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.05495} = \frac{1}{1.05495} \approx 0.948$$

**Inference:** At pH 3.5, sorbic acid is **~94.8% undissociated** versus benzoic acid's **~83.4%**. Since only the undissociated (HA) form can diffuse through the microbial membrane to exert its effect, **sorbic acid is the more effective preservative** in this acidic product. This is a classic KTU 2018 University Exam reasoning question.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: ppm-to-Concentration Conversion for a Soft Drink

> **Problem:** A 330 mL can of soft drink contains **165 mg of sodium benzoate**. Calculate the concentration in (a) ppm (mg/L), (b) mg/kg (assuming density ≈ 1.04 g/mL), and (c) percentage w/v. Comment on whether this falls within FSSAI limits.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — ppm (mg/L)**

Mass of beverage:

$$m = 330 \text{ mL} \times 1.04 \text{ g/mL} = 343.2 \text{ g} = 0.3432 \text{ kg} = 343.2 \text{ mL}$$

Converting to ppm (mg/L):

$$1 \text{ ppm} = 1 \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}}$$

Concentration:

$$C = \frac{165 \text{ mg}}{0.3432 \text{ L}} \approx 480.7 \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \approx 481 \text{ ppm}$$

**Part (b) — mg/kg**

Since 1 L of this beverage weighs 1.04 kg, the mg/kg concentration is:

$$C_{\text{mg/kg}} = \frac{165 \text{ mg}}{0.3432 \text{ kg}} \approx 480.7 \text{ mg/kg}$$

So 481 ppm ≈ 480.7 mg/kg.

**Part (c) — Percentage w/v**

$$\%\,w/v = \frac{165 \text{ mg}}{330 \text{ mL}} \times 100 = \frac{0.165}{330} \times 100 \approx 0.050\%$$

**FSSAI Limit Check:** FSSAI permits sodium benzoate in carbonated beverages at a maximum of **150 ppm (mg/kg)** in the ready-to-drink product.

**Result:** **481 ppm >> 150 ppm** — This product **EXCEEDS the FSSAI limit** by approximately 3.2 times and is therefore **non-compliant** with Indian food safety regulations.

---

## 3.4 KTU 2024 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Preservative

| Food Product | pH Range | $a_w$ Range | Dominant Spoilage | Recommended Preservative(s) | Typical Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bread | 5.0 – 6.0 | 0.95 – 0.97 | Mould (*Neospora*) | Calcium/sodium propionate | 2,000 – 3,000 ppm |
| Soft drinks | 2.5 – 4.0 | 0.98 – 0.99 | Yeast, mould | Sodium benzoate + KMS | 150 – 350 ppm |
| Jams / jellies | 3.0 – 3.5 | 0.80 – 0.85 | Mould, yeast | Sugar (high) + KMS | 50 – 100 ppm SO₂ |
| Pickles | 3.0 – 4.0 | 0.85 – 0.90 | Lactic acid bacteria | Vinegar + salt | 5 – 10% salt |
| Sausages / cured meat | 5.5 – 6.0 | 0.95 – 0.97 | *Clostridium botulinum* | Sodium nitrite + NaCl | 100 – 150 ppm |
| Dried fruits | 4.5 – 5.5 | 0.60 – 0.75 | Insect + mould | Sulphur dioxide (SO₂) | 1,000 – 2,000 ppm |
| Wine | 3.0 – 4.0 | 0.97 – 0.99 | Yeast (residual) | SO₂ (as KMS, K-meta-bisulphite) | 50 – 100 ppm |
