topics.title: Primary, Secondary and Tertiary Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Primary, Secondary and Tertiary Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Processing** is defined as the set of methods and techniques used to transform raw plant and animal materials into safe, palatable, shelf-stable, and value-added food products suitable for human consumption, distribution, and storage.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme classification framework, food processing is structured hierarchically into **three distinct levels** based on the degree of transformation, the nature of value addition, and the proximity of the final product to the consumer's table.

### The Three Hierarchical Tiers

**1. Primary Processing**
> [!NOTE]
> Primary Processing refers to the **initial conversion** of raw agricultural commodities (harvested crops, slaughtered animals, milked milk, caught fish) into clean, sorted, graded, and minimally altered forms that are suitable for storage, transport, or as inputs to further industrial operations.

**2. Secondary Processing**
> [!NOTE]
> Secondary Processing involves the **substantial transformation** of primary processed raw materials into edible food products through physical, chemical, thermal, or biological means — converting raw ingredients into recognizable consumer products.

**3. Tertiary Processing**
> [!NOTE]
> Tertiary Processing refers to the **final value-addition stage** where secondary processed ingredients are combined, formulated, cooked, packaged, and distributed as ready-to-eat (RTE), ready-to-cook (RTC), or convenience food products with extended shelf life.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Think of food processing like **building a house from raw materials**:

| Stage | House Analogy | Food Processing Equivalent |
|-------|---------------|---------------------------|
| **Primary** | Quarrying stones, cutting timber, mining iron ore | Cleaning wheat, slaughtering cattle, milling rice |
| **Secondary** | Manufacturing bricks, casting steel rods, baking cement | Baking bread, pasteurizing milk, fermenting curd |
| **Tertiary** | Assembling the finished villa, interior decoration, handover | Making a frozen pizza, packing a ready-meal tray, sealing an instant noodle pack |

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Intuition**: Just as you cannot decorate a room without first manufacturing the bricks, you cannot make a frozen pizza without first harvesting the wheat (primary), making the dough and cheese (secondary), and finally assembling, freezing, and packaging the meal (tertiary).

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Terminology

The following **shelf-life, yield, and waste-reduction metrics** are standard benchmarks in food processing:

- **Shelf Life**: typically **3–7 days** (fresh, primary), **weeks–months** (secondary), **6–24 months** (tertiary, packaged)
- **Recovery / Yield Percentage**: defined as **mass of usable output ÷ mass of raw input × 100**
- **Cold Chain Temperature**: **0–4 °C** (chilled) and **−18 °C or lower** (frozen) for tertiary products

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Primary Processing — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Operational Steps in Primary Processing

Primary processing is the **gateway to the entire food value chain**. It involves six core unit operations:

1. **Cleaning & Washing** — Removal of soil, dirt, microbial load, pesticide residues, and extraneous matter using water, brushes, or air jets.
2. **Sorting & Grading** — Separation based on **size, shape, color, weight, ripeness, or defect level** using mechanical, optical, or manual methods.
3. **Peeling / Dehulling / De-skinning** — Removal of inedible outer layers (e.g., paddy → brown rice, sugarcane → cane billets).
4. **Milling / Crushing / Size Reduction** — Particle size reduction using mills, crushers, or grinders.
5. **Chilling / Refrigeration** — Immediate post-harvest cooling to **reduce respiration rate, microbial growth, and enzymatic activity**.
6. **Primary Packaging** — Bulk packaging in gunny bags, crates, or bins for transport to secondary processing plants.

### 2.1.2 Why Primary Processing Matters

- **Reduces post-harvest losses** (which globally stand at **30–40%** for perishables).
- **Standardizes raw material quality** before industrial use.
- **Improves storability** and transportability.
- **Removes non-edible / toxic fractions** (e.g., cyanogenic glycosides in cassava).

---

## 2.2 Secondary Processing — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Operational Methods in Secondary Processing

Secondary processing transforms primary commodities into **intermediate or finished edible products** using four main categories of techniques:

**A. Thermal Processing**
- Pasteurization: Heating to **63 °C for 30 minutes** (LTLT) or **72 °C for 15 seconds** (HTST).
- Sterilization: Heating to **121 °C for 15 minutes** at 15 psi (commercial canned foods).
- Blanching: Short steam/hot-water treatment at **85–100 °C for 1–5 minutes**.
- Baking, Roasting, Frying, Boiling.

**B. Chemical Preservation**
- Addition of salt, sugar, vinegar, acids, or chemical preservatives.
- Smoking, curing, pickling.

**C. Biological / Biochemical Processing**
- Fermentation (lactic acid bacteria, yeast, molds).
- Enzymatic hydrolysis, ageing, ripening.

**D. Mechanical Processing**
- Mixing, emulsification, homogenization, extrusion, membrane filtration.

### 2.2.2 Examples of Secondary Processed Products

| Input (Primary) | Process | Output (Secondary) |
|-----------------|---------|--------------------|
| Wheat grains | Milling | Wheat flour |
| Raw milk | Pasteurization | Pasteurized milk |
| Cocoa beans | Fermentation + Drying | Cocoa nibs |
| Sugarcane | Juice extraction + Boiling | Jaggery / Sugar |
| Mustard seeds | Oil extraction | Mustard oil |
| Green tea leaves | Withering + Rolling + Oxidation | Made tea |

---

## 2.3 Tertiary Processing — Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 Characteristics of Tertiary Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Tertiary processing is **value-engineering at its peak** — it combines multiple secondary ingredients into a single consumer-ready product with controlled shelf life and minimum preparation effort by the end-user.

**Key Operations Include:**
- Formulation and recipe standardization
- Cooking / final heat treatment (retort processing, microwave sterilization)
- Aseptic packaging
- Freezing (IQF — Individual Quick Freezing)
- Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)
- Controlled Atmosphere Packaging (CAP)
- Vacuum packaging
- Ready-to-Eat (RTE) meal assembly

### 2.3.2 Examples of Tertiary Processed Products

- Frozen ready meals (paneer butter masala, pasta)
- Instant noodles, ready mixes (idli batter, dosa batter)
- Tetra Pak fruit juices and UHT milk
- Retort pouched curries (defense / space food)
- Packaged bakery items (bread, cookies, cakes)
- Bottled sauces, ketchups, salad dressings

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following formulas are the **most commonly asked numerical / definitional** items in KTU ESE questions on this topic.

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|-------------------|------------|------------------------------|
| **Process Recovery Yield** | $$ \text{Yield (\%)} = \frac{M_{\text{output}}}{M_{\text{input}}} \times 100 $$ | Expressed as **%**; $M$ in kg or tonnes |
| **Post-Harvest Loss (PHL)** | $$ \text{PHL (\%)} = \frac{M_{\text{initial}} - M_{\text{salvaged}}}{M_{\text{initial}}} \times 100 $$ | Global benchmark: **30–40 %** for perishables |
| **Cold Chain Cooling Rate** | $$ \text{CR} = \frac{T_{\text{initial}} - T_{\text{final}}}{\Delta t} $$ | $T$ in **°C**, $t$ in hours |
| **Shelf Life Extension Ratio** | $$ \text{SLER} = \frac{\text{SL}_{\text{processed}}}{\text{SL}_{\text{fresh}}} $$ | Dimensionless ratio; tertiary products give $\text{SLER} \gg 10$ |
| **Thermal Death Time (F-value)** | $$ F = D \cdot \log \left( \frac{N_{0}}{N} \right) $$ | $D$ = decimal reduction time (min); $N$ = microbial count (CFU/g) |
| **Pasteurization LTLT Reference** | $T = 63 \,^\circ\text{C}, \; t = 30 \, \text{min}$ | For milk; HTST: $72 \,^\circ\text{C} \times 15 \, \text{s}$ |
| **Commercial Sterilization** | $T = 121 \,^\circ\text{C}, \; t = 15 \, \text{min}, \; P = 15 \, \text{psi}$ | Standard retort condition for low-acid canned foods |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cold Chain Logistics Engineers** design primary processing cold rooms with **latent heat storage calculations** at $0.5–1.0 \, \text{kWh/tonne-day}$.
- **Food Plant Design Engineers** size secondary processing equipment based on **throughput, yield %, and Fo (sterilization value) targets**.
- **Packaging Engineers** select tertiary packaging films based on **Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR)** measured in $\text{g/m}^2\text{-day}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example — Process Yield Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> The following illustrative numerical is a **classic KTU 2-mark calculation** that frequently appears in Part A.

### Problem Statement
A fruit processing unit receives **500 kg** of fresh mangoes as raw input. After primary processing (peeling, deseeding, and washing), **420 kg** of usable mango pulp is obtained. The pulp is then subjected to secondary processing (thermal evaporation) to produce mango pulp concentrate, yielding **210 kg** of finished concentrate. Calculate:

**(a)** Primary processing yield
**(b)** Secondary processing yield
**(c)** Overall integrated yield from raw mango to finished concentrate

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{raw input}} &= 500 \, \text{kg of fresh mangoes} \\
M_{\text{after primary}} &= 420 \, \text{kg of mango pulp} \\
M_{\text{after secondary}} &= 210 \, \text{kg of mango pulp concentrate}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute primary processing yield**

Using the formula from the KTU Formula Sheet:
$$ \text{Yield}_{\text{primary}} (\%) = \frac{M_{\text{after primary}}}{M_{\text{raw input}}} \times 100 $$

Substituting the values:
$$ \text{Yield}_{\text{primary}} = \frac{420}{500} \times 100 $$

$$ \text{Yield}_{\text{primary}} = 0.84 \times 100 = \mathbf{84 \, \%} $$

**Step 3 — Compute secondary processing yield**

The input to secondary processing is the output of primary processing ($420 \, \text{kg}$):
$$ \text{Yield}_{\text{secondary}} = \frac{M_{\text{after secondary}}}{M_{\text{after primary}}} \times 100 $$

$$ \text{Yield}_{\text{secondary}} = \frac{210}{420} \times 100 $$

$$ \text{Yield}_{\text{secondary}} = 0.50 \times 100 = \mathbf{50 \, \%} $$

**Step 4 — Compute overall integrated yield**

The overall yield from raw mango to finished concentrate is the **product of individual yields** (since each is referenced to the previous stage):
$$ \text{Yield}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{M_{\text{after secondary}}}{M_{\text{raw input}}} \times 100 $$

$$ \text{Yield}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{210}{500} \times 100 $$

$$ \text{Yield}_{\text{overall}} = \mathbf{42 \, \%} $$

**Step 5 — Verification using multiplication rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Yield}_{\text{overall}} &= \text{Yield}_{\text{primary}} \times \text{Yield}_{\text{secondary}} \\
&= 0.84 \times 0.50 \\
&= 0.42 \\
&= 42 \, \% \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Post-Harvest Loss Estimation

### Problem Statement
A farmer harvests **2000 kg** of tomatoes from his field. After primary processing (cleaning, sorting, and grading), **1700 kg** of marketable tomatoes are obtained. Calculate the **percentage post-harvest loss (PHL)**.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{initial}} &= 2000 \, \text{kg} \\
M_{\text{salvaged}} &= 1700 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the PHL formula**

$$ \text{PHL (\%)} = \frac{M_{\text{initial}} - M_{\text{salvaged}}}{M_{\text{initial}}} \times 100 $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values**

$$ \text{PHL (\%)} = \frac{2000 - 1700}{2000} \times 100 $$

$$ \text{PHL (\%)} = \frac{300}{2000} \times 100 $$

$$ \text{PHL (\%)} = 0.15 \times 100 = \mathbf{15 \, \%} $$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation**

A 15 % PHL is **within the acceptable range** for tomatoes (typically 10–25 %), but still represents a significant **value loss** for the farmer. This justifies the use of **better primary processing infrastructure** (cold rooms, improved crates) to push PHL below 5 %.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Primary Processing | Secondary Processing | Tertiary Processing |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Raw Material Form** | Harvested / slaughtered raw produce | Primary processed commodities | Secondary processed ingredients |
| **Degree of Transformation** | Minimal / cosmetic | Substantial | Final value addition |
| **Typical Operations** | Cleaning, sorting, grading, milling | Pasteurization, fermentation, extraction, baking | Cooking, formulation, aseptic packaging, freezing |
| **Value Addition Level** | Low | Medium–High | Very High |
| **Shelf Life Output** | 3–7 days (fresh) | Weeks–months | 6–24 months |
| **Capital Investment** | Low–Medium | High (plants, machinery) | Very High (automation, packaging) |
| **Skill / Labour** | Semi-skilled | Skilled operators | Highly skilled technicians |
| **Examples** | Milled rice, cleaned fish, chilled milk | Pasteurized milk, flour, refined sugar, jaggery | Frozen pizza, tetra pak juice, retort curry |
| **KTU-typical 2-mark question** | "Define primary processing" | "Explain pasteurization" | "Differentiate between MAP and CAP" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Hierarchical Flow Diagram — Three Levels of Food Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["RAW AGRICULTURAL PRODUCE\nHarvested crops, livestock, fish, milk"]
    node2["PRIMARY PROCESSING\nCleaning, sorting, grading\npeeling, milling, chilling"]
    node3["SECONDARY PROCESSING\nPasteurization, fermentation\nextraction, baking, cooking"]
    node4["TERTIARY PROCESSING\nFormulation, aseptic packaging\nfreezing, MAP, retort packing"]
    node5["READY-TO-EAT CONSUMER\nEnd user table / market shelf"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5

    node2 -.->|"By-products\nbran, husk, whey"| nodeB1["Industrial Byproducts\nAnimal feed, biofuel, compost"]
    node3 -.->|"Whey, molasses,\nspent grain"| nodeB1
    node4 -.->|"Package waste\nfilms, cartons"| nodeB1
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Inputs, Operations & Outputs per Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph subgraphA["PRIMARY PROCESSING BLOCK"]
        a1["INPUT\nRaw farm produce"] --> a2["OPERATIONS\nClean / Sort / Grade\nPeel / Mill / Chill"] --> a3["OUTPUT\nCleaned raw material"]
    end

    subgraph subgraphB["SECONDARY PROCESSING BLOCK"]
        b1["INPUT\nPrimary processed material"] --> b2["OPERATIONS\nPasteurize / Sterilize\nFerment / Extract / Bake"] --> b3["OUTPUT\nEdible intermediate product"]
    end

    subgraph subgraphC["TERTIARY PROCESSING BLOCK"]
        c1["INPUT\nSecondary processed ingredients"] --> c2["OPERATIONS\nFormulate / Cook / Pack\nFreeze / Aseptic fill"] --> c3["OUTPUT\nReady-to-eat convenience food"]
    end

    a3 --> b1
    b3 --> c1
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix Diagram — Choosing the Right Processing Level

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start{"What is the desired\nproduct form?"}
    start -->|"Just clean raw material\nfor transport"| p1["PRIMARY"]
    start -->|"Edible processed product\nbut needs cooking"| p2["SECONDARY"]
    start -->|"Eat directly from pack\nno further cooking"| p3["TERTIARY"]

    p1 --> ex1["Examples:\nMilled rice, chilled meat,\ncleaned fish, raw milk"]
    p2 --> ex2["Examples:\nPasteurized milk, refined sugar,\nbaked bread, fruit jam"]
    p3 --> ex3["Examples:\nFrozen meals, tetra pak juice,\ninstant noodles, retort curry"]
```

## 4.4 Cold Chain & Storage Temperature Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    temp1["Room Temperature\n25 to 35 deg C\nPrimary fresh produce shelf life 1 to 3 days"]
    temp2["Chilled Storage\n0 to 4 deg C\nSecondary dairy shelf life 7 to 14 days"]
    temp3["Frozen Storage\nminus 18 deg C and below\nTertiary RTE shelf life 6 to 24 months"]
    temp4["Retort Sterilized\nAmbient stable\nTertiary shelf life 12 to 36 months"]

    temp1 --> temp2
    temp2 --> temp3
    temp3 --> temp4
```

> [!TIP]
> **Note for students**: The Mermaid diagrams above use **block-level functional flow** to represent the interactions of each processing stage, ensuring clean rendering and avoiding any reserved-keyword conflicts.
