<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Differentiate between Primary and Secondary food processing with two suitable examples of each.**

#### Model Answer (Valuation Key Pattern):

| Parameter | Primary Processing | Secondary Processing |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------------|
| **Definition** | Initial cleaning, sorting, grading of raw produce | Substantial transformation into edible product |
| **Form change** | Cosmetic / minimal | Structural / chemical |
| **Shelf life** | 3–7 days | Weeks–months |
| **Examples** | (i) Cleaning and grading of apples, (ii) De-hulling of paddy to brown rice | (i) Pasteurization of milk, (ii) Milling of wheat to maida |

> **KTU Marking Pattern**: 1 mark for definition contrast, 1 mark for two primary examples, 1 mark for two secondary examples.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What is Tertiary Processing? Mention any four tertiary processed food products available in the Indian retail market.**

#### Model Answer:

**Definition (1 mark):**
Tertiary processing is the final stage of food processing where secondary processed ingredients are combined, formulated, cooked, and packaged into ready-to-eat (RTE) or ready-to-cook (RTC) convenience foods with extended shelf life.

**Any Four Examples (2 marks):**
1. **Tetra Pak UHT milk** — aseptically packaged milk with 6-month shelf life.
2. **Frozen ready meals** (e.g., ITC Aashirvaad frozen parathas).
3. **Instant noodles** (e.g., Maggi).
4. **Retort pouch curries** (e.g., MTR ready-to-eat sambar).
5. **Bottled fruit juices** (e.g., Tropicana, Real).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various unit operations involved in **Primary Processing of food** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the importance and applications of **Secondary Processing** in the food industry. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — Primary Processing Unit Operations (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Primary processing is the foundational stage that prepares harvested raw produce for further industrial use by removing impurities, standardizing quality, and improving shelf stability.

**Key Unit Operations (5 marks):**

1. **Cleaning & Washing** — Removal of soil, dust, pesticide residues, and microbial load using water, brushes, or air-jet blowers. Example: Washing of fruits and vegetables in chlorinated water (50–200 ppm chlorine).

2. **Sorting & Grading** — Separation based on size, color, ripeness, or defect using manual sorting tables, vibratory screens, or **electronic optical sorters**. Example: Grading of apples into Extra Class, Class I, and Class II based on diameter.

3. **Peeling / Dehulling** — Removal of inedible outer layers using mechanical abrasion, lye treatment, or steam. Example: Caustic peeling of tomatoes using **NaOH at 8–10 % concentration and 95 °C**.

4. **Size Reduction (Milling/Crushing)** — Particle size reduction using hammer mills, roller mills, or ball mills. Example: Wheat milling into flour and semolina.

5. **Chilling / Pre-cooling** — Rapid cooling of perishables to **0–4 °C** within 2–4 hours of harvest using forced-air cooling or hydro-cooling.

6. **Primary Packaging** — Bulk packing in crates, gunny bags, or bins for transport.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Efficient primary processing reduces post-harvest losses (currently 30–40 % for perishables), enhances food safety, and ensures uniform raw material quality for downstream industries.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — Secondary Processing Importance & Applications (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Secondary processing converts primary processed commodities into edible food products through thermal, chemical, biological, or mechanical means.

**Importance (3 marks):**
1. **Enhances shelf life** — Pasteurization extends milk shelf life from **2 days (raw) to 15 days (pasteurized)**.
2. **Improves palatability and digestibility** — Cooking gelatinizes starch and denatures proteins.
3. **Ensures food safety** — Destruction of pathogens like *Salmonella*, *E. coli*, and *Listeria*.
4. **Adds economic value** — Raw mango sells at ₹20/kg, but secondary processed pulp concentrate sells at ₹150/kg.
5. **Enables product diversification** — Single raw material yields multiple products (e.g., milk → paneer, ghee, yogurt, cheese).

**Applications (3 marks):**
1. **Dairy industry** — Pasteurized milk, cheese, butter, ghee, condensed milk.
2. **Grain processing** — Flour, semolina, breakfast cereals, pasta.
3. **Fruit & vegetable industry** — Juice, jam, jelly, ketchup, pickles, canned products.
4. **Bakery** — Bread, biscuits, cakes.
5. **Beverage industry** — Beer, wine, packaged drinking water.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the concept of **Tertiary Processing** with its key operations. Mention any **five examples** of tertiary processed foods. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary Processing** in a tabular form on the basis of any **seven parameters**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — Tertiary Processing Concept & Examples (7 Marks)

**Concept (3 marks):**
Tertiary processing is the **culmination stage** of the food processing value chain, in which secondary processed ingredients are systematically combined, formulated, cooked (if required), and packaged into **ready-to-eat (RTE), ready-to-cook (RTC), or ready-to-serve (RTS)** convenience foods. The hallmark of tertiary processing is the delivery of **maximum consumer convenience** combined with **long ambient or chilled shelf life**.

**Key Operations (2 marks):**
1. Formulation and recipe standardization
2. Retort thermal sterilization (121 °C, 15 psi, 15 min)
3. Aseptic packaging in Tetra Pak cartons
4. Individual Quick Freezing (IQF)
5. Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) and Vacuum packaging
6. Microwave-assisted sterilization

**Five Examples (2 marks):**
1. **Retort pouched paneer butter masala** (MTR / Kohinoor brand)
2. **Tetra Pak mango juice** (Real / Tropicana)
3. **Frozen parathas** (Aashirvaad / McCain)
4. **Instant noodles** (Maggi / Top Ramen)
5. **Ready-to-eat idli / dosa batter** (iD Fresh, MTR)

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — Tabular Comparison on Seven Parameters (7 Marks)

| S.No. | Parameter | Primary Processing | Secondary Processing | Tertiary Processing |
|-------|-----------|--------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Definition** | Initial cleaning & preparation | Conversion to edible product | Final value-added RTE product |
| 2 | **Nature of Change** | Cosmetic / physical | Chemical / biological | Formulation-based |
| 3 | **Capital Investment** | Low–Medium | High | Very High |
| 4 | **Shelf Life of Output** | 3–7 days | Weeks–months | 6–24 months |
| 5 | **Example** | Cleaned rice | Pasteurized milk | Frozen pizza |
| 6 | **Skill Level Required** | Semi-skilled | Skilled | Highly skilled |
| 7 | **Consumer Effort** | Must cook/process further | Needs cooking | Minimal / no cooking |

> **Marking Pattern**: 1 mark per correctly filled row × 7 rows = 7 marks.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing Secondary with Tertiary Processing**: Many students incorrectly classify **Tetra Pak milk** as "secondary" — it is actually **tertiary** because it involves formulation + aseptic packaging.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Yield Calculations**: Always write **%** symbol after yield numbers. A bare number without unit **loses 0.5 mark**.
> 3. **Writing Only One Example When Two Are Asked**: In "give two examples" questions, listing only one **deducts 0.5 mark** per missing example.
> 4. **Skipping the Conclusion**: Long answer (14-mark) questions without a concluding paragraph lose **0.5–1 mark**.
> 5. **Incorrect Pasteurization Temperature**: Writing **"72 °C for 30 minutes"** instead of **"72 °C for 15 seconds (HTST)" or "63 °C for 30 minutes (LTLT)"** is a frequently penalized error.
> 6. **Mixing up MAP vs CAP**: MAP = Modified Atmosphere Packaging (gas mix adjusted once); CAP = Controlled Atmosphere Packaging (gas composition continuously monitored and adjusted). Examiners **love testing this distinction**.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Primary Processing** = cleaning + sorting + grading + peeling + milling + chilling. Examples: cleaned rice, chilled fish, graded apples.
- ✅ **Secondary Processing** = pasteurization + sterilization + fermentation + extraction + baking + chemical preservation. Examples: pasteurized milk, refined sugar, fruit jam.
- ✅ **Tertiary Processing** = formulation + cooking + aseptic packaging + freezing + MAP. Examples: frozen pizza, Tetra Pak juice, retort curry, instant noodles.
- ✅ **Standard yields**: Primary = 70–90 %, Secondary = 30–60 %, Overall integrated yield = product of individual stage yields.
- ✅ **Key temperatures to memorize**: Chilled storage **0–4 °C**, Frozen storage **−18 °C or below**, HTST pasteurization **72 °C × 15 s**, LTLT pasteurization **63 °C × 30 min**, Commercial sterilization **121 °C × 15 min at 15 psi**.
- ✅ **Global PHL benchmark** for perishables = **30–40 %**; well-designed cold chains can reduce this to **below 5 %**.
- ✅ **Distinction mnemonic**: **P**rimary = **P**reparation, **S**econdary = **S**ubstance change, **T**ertiary = **T**able-ready.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 hot topics** for Part A: (i) Define any one tier, (ii) Differentiate between MAP and CAP, (iii) Give two examples of tertiary foods.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 hot topics** for Part B: (i) Describe all three tiers in detail, (ii) Tabular comparison on ≥7 parameters, (iii) Numerical on yield calculation.
- ✅ **Always write units** in numerical answers — bare numbers are penalized.
- ✅ **Conclusion paragraph** is mandatory in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processed Food Products - Types and Methods of Preparation - Cereals and Pulses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Processed Food Products - Types and Methods of Preparation - Cereals and Pulses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Cereals and Pulses (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Cereals** are monocotyledonous starchy grains belonging to the grass family *Poaceae (Gramineae)*, harvested for their edible carbohydrate-rich endosperm. The major cereals include **rice (Oryza sativa)**, **wheat (Triticum aestivum)**, **maize (Zea mays)**, **barley (Hordeum vulgare)**, **oats (Avena sativa)**, **sorghum (Sorghum bicolor)**, **pearl millet (Pennisetum glaucum)**, and **finger millet (Eleusine coracana)**.

**Pulses** are dry, edible seeds of plants belonging to the family *Fabaceae (Leguminosae)*, characterized by a high protein content (typically **20–30%**) and harvested exclusively for their grain legume. Common pulses include **chickpea (Bengal gram)**, **pigeon pea (red gram)**, **green gram (mung bean)**, **black gram (urad dal)**, **lentil (masoor)**, and **cowpea**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Focus**
> The 2024 Scheme explicitly maps this sub-topic to **CO3 (Apply knowledge of processing methods to cereals and pulses)** and **CO4 (Analyze quality changes during processing)**. Students must link each processing method to a measurable change in **moisture, texture, color, or nutritional profile**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Lunchbox" Model

Imagine each cereal grain as a **sealed lunchbox with a tough outer shell (husk)**. The kernel inside contains the **starch (main course)**, **protein (side dish)**, **vitamins and minerals (condiments)**, and a **tiny embryo (germ)** that could grow into a new plant. Processing cereals is essentially about:

1. **Opening the lunchbox** (dehusking/decortication)
2. **Choosing what to keep** (whole grain vs. refined flour)
3. **Re-cooking the contents** in new ways (parboiling, puffing, flaking)

Pulses, on the other hand, are like **double-sealed containers** — they have a fibrous seed coat and a tightly attached seed leaf (cotyledon) that must be split to release the inner nutrients. This is why pulse processing is dominated by **dehusking + splitting (dhal making)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the difference matters**: Cereals are starch-dominant (60–80% carbs), while pulses are protein-dominant (20–30% protein). This fundamentally dictates their processing — cereals undergo **starch gelatinization and modification**, while pulses undergo **protein denaturation and hydration**.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bold for Board Recall)

- **Moisture Content (Safe Storage Limit)**: Cereals ≤ **14%**, Pulses ≤ **10–12%**
- **Gelatinization Temperature Range**: Starch granules of cereals typically gel between **55–75°C**
- **Standard Dehusking Efficiency (KTU definition)**: Ratio of dehusked grains to total grains, expressed in **%**
- **Protein Content (Pulse Benchmark)**: Average **20–25%** on a dry weight basis
- **1000-Kernel Weight (TKW)**: Used as a grading index, often between **25–50 g** for cereals
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Processed Cereal & Pulse Products

The processing hierarchy in KTU 2024 is divided into **Primary Processing** (post-harvest handling) and **Secondary Processing** (value addition).

#### 2.1.1 Primary Processing of Cereals

| Stage | Operation | Purpose | Typical Equipment |
|-------|-----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Cleaning** | Remove dust, stones, chaff, foreign matter | Air-screen cleaner, magnetic separator |
| 2 | **Grading** | Sort by size, density, color | Gravity separator, indented cylinder, optical sorter |
| 3 | **Drying** | Reduce moisture to safe storage level | Flatbed dryer, fluidized bed dryer |
| 4 | **Parboiling** (rice-specific) | Gelatinize starch within the husk | Soaking tank + steam boiler |
| 5 | **Dehusking/Hulling** | Remove outer inedible husk | Rubber roll sheller, impact huller |
| 6 | **Polishing/Whitening** | Remove bran layer (for white rice) | Friction-type polisher, abrasive polisher |
| 7 | **Grading/Sizing** | Separate broken from head grains | Length grader, indent disk separator |

#### 2.1.2 Primary Processing of Pulses

| Stage | Operation | Purpose | Typical Equipment |
|-------|-----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Cleaning & Grading** | Remove foreign matter | Air-screen cleaner, gravity separator |
| 2 | **Soaking** | Hydrate seed coat (8–12 h) | Soaking tanks with water circulation |
| 3 | **Dehusking/Decortication** | Remove seed coat (testa) | Roller mill, attrition mill, Under-runner disc sheller |
| 4 | **Splitting** | Separate cotyledons into dhal | Pulse splitter (impact / shearing) |
| 5 | **Polishing** | Improve appearance of dhal | Emery roll polisher |
| 6 | **Grading** | Sort by size | Indent cylinder, vibrating screen |

#### 2.1.3 Secondary Processing — Value-Added Products

**From Cereals:**
- **Atta, Maida, Suji (Rawa)** → Wheat flour milling products
- **Rice Flakes (Poha / Aval)** → Flaked rice
- **Puffed Rice (Muri / Marmalade rice)** → Gun puffing
- **Popcorn** → High-temperature popping of maize
- **Cornflakes** → Flaking of cooked maize grits
- **Bread, Biscuits, Cakes** → Bakery from refined wheat flour
- **Pasta, Noodles, Vermicelli** → Extrusion / sheeting of wheat dough
- **Breakfast Cereals (Ready-to-Eat)** → Flaking + toasting + sugar coating

**From Pulses:**
- **Dhal (split pulse)** → Primary processed form
- **Besan (Bengal gram flour)** → Fine milling of chana dal
- **Papad / Appalam** → Seasoned, sun-dried thin wafers
- **Sprouts** → Germinated pulses (vitamin C rich)
- **Fermented Products** → Idli/Dosa batter (urad + rice), Dhokla (besan)
- **Protein Concentrates/Isolates** → Industrial extraction of pulse protein

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU-High-Yield** — expect them in numerical problems, derivations, or short derivations during ESE (End Semester Evaluation).

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Standard Units |
|---|---------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\text{DM\%} = \dfrac{W_{\text{dry}}}{W_{\text{initial}}} \times 100$ | Dry Matter content (basis of all cereal/pulse calculations) | % |
| 2 | $\text{MC\% (w.b.)} = \dfrac{W_{\text{water}}}{W_{\text{initial}}} \times 100$ | Moisture content on **wet basis** (commonly used) | % |
| 3 | $\text{MC\% (d.b.)} = \dfrac{W_{\text{water}}}{W_{\text{dry}}} \times 100$ | Moisture content on **dry basis** (used in drying calcs) | % |
| 4 | $\text{MC}_{\text{w.b.}} = \dfrac{\text{MC}_{\text{d.b.}}}{1 + \text{MC}_{\text{d.b.}}} \times 100$ | Conversion between wet and dry basis | % |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{husk}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{dehusked}}}{M_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | Dehusking efficiency | % |
| 6 | $\text{BRE} = \dfrac{M_{\text{head rice}}}{M_{\text{paddy}}} \times 100$ | Brown rice recovery / Head rice yield | % |
| 7 | $\text{MRE} = \dfrac{M_{\text{head rice}}}{M_{\text{paddy milled}}} \times 100$ | **Milling Recovery** (KTU-favorite 2-mark question) | % |
| 8 | $H = 1.0065\,T_{db} + 0.0245\,T_{db}^2$ (approx.) | Hygroscopic equilibrium relation, rough dry-bulb correction | varies |
| 9 | $\text{Capillary rise: } h = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | Explains water uptake during soaking of pulses | m |
| 10 | $\text{Protein Estimate} = N \times 6.25$ | **Jones factor** for cereals/pulses (N → protein) | % |
| 11 | $\Delta T = T_{\text{gelatinization}} - T_{\text{room}}$ | Net temperature rise to gelatinize starch | °C |
| 12 | $\text{Yield}_{\text{dhal}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{dhal}}}{M_{\text{pulse feed}}} \times 100$ | Dhal yield from pulse processing | % |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Jones factor 6.25** is used for cereals and pulses because their protein contains ~16% nitrogen ($100/16 = 6.25$). For wheat, some texts use **5.7** (gluten-rich), but KTU boards generally accept **6.25** as standard.

---

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" of Each Processing Step

- **Why parboil rice?** → Parboiling drives vitamins (thiamine, niacin) from the bran into the endosperm, reducing nutrient loss during subsequent polishing. It also **hardens the grain**, reducing breakage during milling.
- **How does puffing work?** → Grains contain trapped moisture. When heated rapidly in a sealed gun, internal water pressure builds to **~9–10 kg/cm²**. Sudden release causes **explosive vaporization** and the kernel expands to **8–10 times** its original volume.
- **Why soak pulses before dehusking?** → Hydration **loosens the bond** between seed coat and cotyledon (the **mucilaginous layer** softens), making mechanical decortication possible with minimum breakage.
- **How is dhal split?** → Cotyledons of pulses have a **natural cleavage plane** along the embryonic axis. Mechanical impact along this plane splits the seed into two halves (dhal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility**: The dhal yield of 70–78% is the **economic benchmark** for any pulse processing plant in India. A 1% increase translates to multi-crore gains for industrial-scale operations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between Wet-Basis and Dry-Basis Moisture

This is the **most frequently asked numerical** in KTU Module 3 assessments.

Let:
- $W_i$ = Initial weight of grain (containing water + dry matter)
- $W_w$ = Weight of water in the grain
- $W_d$ = Weight of dry matter

By definition:

$$M_w = \frac{W_w}{W_i} \times 100 \quad \text{(wet basis)}$$

$$M_d = \frac{W_w}{W_d} \times 100 \quad \text{(dry basis)}$$

Step 1 — Write $W_i$ in terms of $W_w$ and $W_d$.

Since $W_i = W_w + W_d$, we substitute into the wet-basis formula:

$$M_w = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d} \times 100$$

Step 2 — Divide numerator and denominator by $W_d$:

$$M_w = \frac{\frac{W_w}{W_d}}{\frac{W_w}{W_d} + \frac{W_d}{W_d}} \times 100 = \frac{\frac{M_d}{100}}{\frac{M_d}{100} + 1} \times 100$$

Step 3 — Simplify the fraction. Let $x = M_d / 100$:

$$M_w = \frac{x}{x + 1} \times 100$$

Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by 100:

$$M_w = \frac{M_d}{M_d + 100} \times 100$$

Step 5 — Final working form:

$$\boxed{\,M_w = \frac{100 \cdot M_d}{100 + M_d}\,}$$

**Reverse conversion (dry basis from wet basis):**

$$\boxed{\,M_d = \frac{100 \cdot M_w}{100 - M_w}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters**: Drying calculations in food engineering almost always use **dry basis**, while quality control and FSSAI regulations report moisture in **wet basis**. Engineers MUST be able to convert instantly to avoid reporting errors.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU 2024 Style

**Problem:** A paddy sample weighs **200 g** before drying and **172 g** after drying. Calculate:
1. Moisture content on **wet basis**.
2. Moisture content on **dry basis**.
3. Amount of water that must be removed to bring the sample to a safe storage moisture of **14% (w.b.)**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1** — Initial moisture lost during drying.

$$\Delta W = 200 - 172 = 28 \text{ g (this is the water removed)}$$

**Step 2** — Moisture content on **wet basis (initial)**.

$$M_w = \frac{28}{200} \times 100 = 14\%$$

**Step 3** — Moisture content on **dry basis**.

$$M_d = \frac{28}{172} \times 100 = 16.28\%$$

**Step 4** — Water to be removed to reach safe 14% w.b.

Let final weight be $W_f$. Then:

$$M_{w,\text{final}} = \frac{W_f - 172}{W_f} \times 100 = 14$$

Solving:

$$0.14\, W_f = W_f - 172$$

$$0.86\, W_f = 172$$

$$W_f = 200 \text{ g}$$

Wait — this means the sample is **already at 14% w.b.** No further drying is required. Therefore, the **amount of water to be removed = 0 g**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Error**: Students often confuse the *dry mass* (172 g) with the *original mass* (200 g) when applying the formula. Always re-derive $W_d$ from the post-drying weight.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Processing Methods vs. Nutrient/Quality Effect

This matrix is **high-yield** for KTU Part-A 3-mark questions.

| Processing Method | Applied To | Key Change in Starch | Key Change in Protein | Texture Outcome | Shelf Life |
|-------------------|------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------------|------------|
| **Parboiling** | Rice | Pre-gelatinized (α-starch) | Partial denaturation | Harder, glassier | ↑ Increased |
| **Dry Milling** | Wheat → Atta/Maida | Granular disruption | Gluten network forms on hydration | Variable | Neutral |
| **Wet Milling** | Maize | Starch separation from germ/gluten | Concentrated in gluten fraction | Smooth slurry | ↑ Increased |
| **Puffing / Popping** | Rice, Maize | α-starch, porous structure | Denatured, expanded | Crispy, expanded 8–10× | ↓ Decreased (re-staling) |
| **Flaking** | Maize, Rice | Pre-gelatinized + dried | Denatured | Thin, crisp sheets | ↑ Increased |
| **Malting** | Barley, Ragi | Starch partially hydrolyzed to maltose | Limited change | Soft, sweet | Neutral |
| **Decortication** | Pulses | Minimal direct effect | Concentrated (testa removed) | Coarse dhal | ↑ Increased |
| **Sprouting** | Pulses | Starch → simple sugars | Digestibility ↑↑ (anti-nutrients reduced) | Soft, fresh | ↓ Decreased (perishable) |
| **Fermentation** | Rice + Urad (Idli) | Hydrolyzed by amylase | Anti-nutrients (phytate) ↓ | Soft, porous | ↑ Increased (acidic pH) |
| **Roasting** | Chana, Soyabean | Dextrinization begins | Lysine may reduce (Maillard) | Crunchy | ↑↑ Significantly increased |

---

### 3.4 Energy & Mass Balance in Drying (Simplified KT-Level Form)

For a grain dryer, the mass balance over a time interval $\Delta t$ gives:

$$\frac{dM_d}{dt} = -\frac{\dot{m}_{\text{evap}}}{M_{\text{dry}}}$$

where $\dot{m}_{\text{evap}}$ is the rate of moisture evaporation. The energy balance is:

$$Q = \dot{m}_{\text{evap}} \cdot \lambda_s + M_s \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- $Q$ = total heat supplied (kJ)
- $\lambda_s$ = latent heat of vaporization of water (≈ **2260 kJ/kg**)
- $M_s$ = mass of solid grain (kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of grain (≈ **1.5–2.0 kJ/kg·K**)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature rise of grain (K)

These are the **core equations** a KTU student should be able to write and apply in a 14-mark Part-B question on **drying of cereals/pulses**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Processing Flowchart of Paddy (Cereal)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Paddy Intake] --> A1[Pre-Cleaning Air Screen]
    A1 --> A2[De-stoning]
    A2 --> A3[Magnetic Separation]
    A3 --> B1{Parboiling Required?}
    B1 -- Yes --> B2[Soaking 12 to 24 h]
    B2 --> B3[Steam Pressure Cooking]
    B3 --> C1[Sun Drying or Mechanical Drying]
    B1 -- No --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Husking with Rubber Roll Sheller]
    D1 --> D2[Paddy Separator]
    D2 --> D3[Whitening and Polishing]
    D3 --> D4[Grading by Length]
    D4 --> E1[Head Rice A]
    D4 --> E2[Broken Rice B]
    D4 --> E3[Bran and Husk C]
    E1 --> F1[Quality Check Color Size Moisture]
    F1 --> G1[Packaging and Storage]
    E2 --> G2[Value Added Flour or Poha Line]
    E3 --> G3[Oil Extraction and Fuel]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU answer writing**: The output streams (A = head rice, B = broken rice, C = bran + husk) are the basis of **mass balance questions**. Total input mass = sum of A + B + C + losses.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Pulse to Dhal Processing (Decortication Line)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Raw Pulse Intake] --> P1[Dry Cleaning with Air Screen]
    P1 --> P2[Stone and Metal Removal]
    P2 --> P3[Grading by Size]
    P3 --> P4[Soaking Tank 8 to 12 h]
    P4 --> P5[Surface Drying Sun or Mechanical]
    P5 --> P6[Conditioning Moisture 10 to 12 percent]
    P6 --> P7[Dehusking Roller or Under Runner Disc]
    P7 --> P8[Separation of Husk by Aspiration]
    P8 --> P9[Pretreatment with Oil and Water for Tight Coats]
    P9 --> P10[Splitting into Dhal]
    P10 --> P11[Polishing Emery Roll]
    P11 --> P12[Final Grading and Quality Inspection]
    P12 --> P13[Packaging 25 to 50 kg]
    P7 --> P14[Undehusked Fraction Return Loop]
    P14 --> P7
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the return loop (P14 → P7)?** In commercial dhal mills, **15–20% of pulses remain unhusked** after the first pass. They are recycled through the dehusker to achieve a final dhal yield of 70–78%. This is a **typical KTU 2-mark question**.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Value-Added Secondary Products Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CEREALS
    C0[Whole Cereal Grains] --> C1[Rice]
    C0 --> C2[Wheat]
    C0 --> C3[Maize]
    C1 --> C1A[Parboiled Rice]
    C1 --> C1B[Poha Flakes]
    C1 --> C1C[Puffed Rice Muri]
    C2 --> C2A[Atta Whole Wheat Flour]
    C2 --> C2B[Maida Refined Flour]
    C2 --> C2C[Suji Semolina]
    C2 --> C2D[Bread Biscuit Pasta]
    C3 --> C3A[Cornflakes]
    C3 --> C3B[Popcorn]
    C3 --> C3C[Starch and Corn Syrup]
    end
    subgraph PULSES
    Q0[Whole Pulse Seeds] --> Q1[Chickpea]
    Q0 --> Q2[Red Gram]
    Q0 --> Q3[Black Gram]
    Q1 --> Q1A[Chana Dal]
    Q1 --> Q1B[Besan Flour]
    Q2 --> Q2A[Toor Dal]
    Q3 --> Q3A[Urad Dal Whole]
    Q3 --> Q3B[Idli Dosa Batter Fermented]
    Q1A --> Q1C[Roasted Dal Snacks]
    Q1B --> Q1D[Dhokla Khaman]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical use**: This tree can be reproduced in a 7-mark answer for the question *"List the major secondary processed products from cereals and pulses and explain any two."* Each leaf becomes a sub-bullet.

---

### 4.4 Functional Schematic — Batch Parboiling Unit (KTU Drawing Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1[Soaking Tank with Water Heater] --> T2[Drainage Screen]
    T2 --> T3[Steam Boiling Kettle Pressure Vessel]
    T3 --> T4[Mechanical Drying Yard or LSB Dryer]
    T4 --> T5[Tempering Bin 4 to 6 h]
    T5 --> T6[Storage Silo for Parboiled Paddy]
    T6 --> T7[Milling Section Husking Polishing]
    H1[Boiler Steam Supply] --> T3
    H2[Water Pump] --> T1
    H3[Air Blower] --> T4
    H4[Conveyor Chain] --> T6
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define parboiling of rice. List the three main changes that occur in the grain during parboiling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — award structure):**

*Definition (1 mark):*
Parboiling is a hydrothermal treatment of paddy in which the grain is first **soaked in water**, then **steam-cooked**, and finally **dried** before milling. The process gelatinizes the starch *in situ*, hardens the kernel, and migrates nutrients from the bran into the endosperm.

*Three changes (1 mark each, total 2 marks):*
1. **Starch gelatinization** — the compact starch granules absorb water and swell, forming a hard, translucent endosperm.
2. **Nutrient migration** — water-soluble vitamins (B-complex) and minerals from the aleurone layer diffuse into the starchy endosperm, improving the milled rice's nutritional value.
3. **Reduction in breakage** — the gelatinized starch acts as a binder, making the grain tougher, thus reducing the percentage of broken kernels during dehusking and polishing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (1-mark loss risk)**: Students often write "rice becomes softer" — this is **WRONG**. Parboiled rice is **harder** than raw rice after cooling. Only the *un-milled* paddy becomes easier to dehusk, not the cooked rice.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the function of the Jones factor in cereal/pulse analysis? What value is used and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — award structure):**

*Function (1 mark):*
The Jones factor is a **multiplication constant** used to convert the **nitrogen content** of a food sample (determined by the Kjeldahl or Dumas method) into an **estimated crude protein content**, on the assumption that all nitrogen in the food originates from protein.

*Value (1 mark):*
For cereals and pulses, the standard Jones factor is **6.25**.

*Reason (1 mark):*
Proteins contain an average of **16% nitrogen** by mass. Therefore, the protein equivalent of measured nitrogen is calculated as $100 / 16 = 6.25$. The relation is:

$$\text{Crude Protein (\%)} = N (\%) \times 6.25$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus for full marks (1 extra line)**: Mention that for *wheat flour*, a factor of **5.7** is sometimes used because wheat proteins are *nitrogen-richer* (specific gluten composition), but KTU accepts 6.25 as default.

---

### 5.2 PART-B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Cereals Focus**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — Part-B Module 3]**

**(a) Describe the primary processing of paddy into milled rice. List the main unit operations and explain the role of parboiling. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*1. Cleaning (1 mark):*
Raw paddy contains straw, stones, dust, weed seeds, and metallic impurities. **Air-screen cleaners** remove light impurities by aspiration; **indent cylinders** separate by length; **magnetic separators** trap ferrous metals.

*2. Parboiling (2 marks):*
Paddy is soaked in water at **65–70°C for 3–4 hours** (or 25–30°C for 12–24 hours), drained, then **steam-cooked at 1.0–1.5 kg/cm² for 10–20 minutes**, and finally **dried to 14% moisture**.
- **Role**: (i) hardens the kernel, (ii) migrates vitamins from bran to endosperm, (iii) reduces breakage during milling, (iv) improves storability of milled rice.

*3. Dehusking / Hulling (1 mark):*
The husk is removed by **rubber roll shellers** (two rollers rotating at different speeds create shear force, removing husk without breaking the kernel).

*4. Paddy separation (1 mark):*
A **paddy separator** (tray type or compartment type) uses **differential specific gravity and size** of brown rice vs. unhulled paddy to separate them; unhulled grains are returned for re-hulling.

*5. Polishing / Whitening (1 mark):*
**Friction polishers** or **abrasive polishers** remove the bran layer (pericarp, seed coat, aleurone) to produce white rice. Whiteness is controlled by residence time and pressure.

*6. Grading (1 mark):*
**Indented cylinders** or **disc separators** sort kernels by length into **head rice (>3/4 length)**, **broken rice**, and **brewers rice (<1/4 length)**.

---

**(b) Explain the principle and methods of puffing of cereals. Describe gun puffing and popping with a neat diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Principle (2 marks):*
Cereal grains contain a small amount of internal moisture (10–14%) trapped in the starch matrix. When the grain is heated rapidly in a sealed environment, this moisture turns to **superheated steam**, raising the **internal pressure to 9–10 kg/cm²** at temperatures around **230–250°C**. The **starch matrix** has become **amorphous and rubbery** at this temperature. When the pressure is suddenly released, the **superheated steam expands explosively**, the starch matrix ruptures and **the kernel inflates to 8–10 times** its original volume, producing a porous, crisp structure.

*Gun Puffing Method (3 marks):*
1. Grains are preconditioned to ~14% moisture.
2. They are loaded into a **heavy-duty sealed metal cylinder (gun)**.
3. The gun is rotated while being heated externally (gas or oil-fired) for **3–5 minutes**, building pressure to ~**9–10 kg/cm²**.
4. A quick-release valve (the "gun") is opened. The sudden depressurization **explodes** the grains into a collection chamber.
5. Puffed grains are separated, cooled, and packaged.

*Popping Method (2 marks):*
1. Used mainly for **popcorn (maize)** and **pop sorghum**.
2. Grains are heated in a **hot air popper** or **microwave** at 200–250°C.
3. The pericarp acts as a pressure vessel; once the internal steam pressure exceeds the pericarp's rupture strength, the kernel **flips inside-out**, producing a fluffy white expanded mass.
4. **Popping ratio** (volume after popping / volume before) for popcorn is typically **30–40×**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (2-mark loss risk)**: Students often confuse **gelatinization** with **puffing**. They are NOT the same:
> - **Gelatinization** = starch granules absorb water and swell in a *moist* environment (irreversible order–disorder transition).
> - **Puffing** = dry-thermal process using *internal steam pressure* to physically expand the kernel.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Pulses Focus
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Part-B Module 3]**

**(a) Explain the step-by-step processing of pulses to produce dhal. Highlight the role of soaking, conditioning, and pretreatment. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*1. Cleaning (1 mark):*
Pulses contain dust, stones, stems, and other seeds. **Air-screen cleaners** and **gravity separators** remove foreign matter based on size and density.

*2. Soaking (1.5 marks):*
Pulses are soaked in water for **8–12 hours** (sometimes up to 24 hours for hard-coated varieties). This:
- Hydrates the **mucilaginous layer** between the seed coat (testa) and the cotyledons.
- **Loosens the bond**, making dehusking easier.
- Increases the seed weight by **30–60%**.
- Softens the cotyledon, reducing breakage during splitting.

*3. Surface Drying (1 mark):*
After soaking, the seeds are **surface-dried in the sun or by mechanical dryer** to **10–12% moisture**. This:
- Reduces the **slipperiness** for better handling.
- Allows the husk to become **brittle**, easing its removal.

*4. Conditioning (1.5 marks):*
Conditioning is a **controlled tempering** step where seeds are held in bins for **2–4 hours** to allow **uniform moisture distribution** between the surface and the interior. This step **minimizes breakage** during the next operation.

*5. Pretreatment (1.5 marks):*
This is a critical step for pulses with **tight seed coats** (e.g., pigeon pea, black gram). Seeds are **mixed with 0.5–1.0% edible oil** (linseed, castor, or groundnut oil) and a small amount of water. The oil:
- **Penetrates the seed coat** and weakens the husk–cotyledon bond.
- Reduces powdering and breakage.
- Improves the **lustre of the dhal** during the final polishing step.

*6. Dehusking (0.5 mark):*
Performed in **under-runner disc shellers** or **roller mills**. The abrasive action rubs off the seed coat.

---

**(b) Describe the preparation of the following value-added pulse products: (i) Besan, (ii) Idli/Dosa batter, (iii) Papad. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Besan (1.5 marks):**
- **Raw material**: Bengal gram (*Cicer arietinum*) — chana dal or whole chana.
- **Process**: Cleaned chana dal is **dry-milled in a plate grinder or hammer mill** to a fine powder.
- **Fineness**: Passed through a **60–80 mesh sieve**.
- **Packing**: Immediately packed in moisture-proof packaging to prevent lumping.
- **Yield**: ~95–97% flour from cleaned chana dal.

**(ii) Idli/Dosa Batter (3 marks):**
- **Ratio**: Traditional ratio is **3 parts parboiled rice : 1 part urad dal (black gram)**.
- **Step 1**: Both ingredients are **cleaned and soaked separately** for 4–6 hours.
- **Step 2**: Wet-ground using a **stone grinder or wet grinder**. Urad dal is ground first (with salt and water) to a **fluffy, aerated paste** due to its high mucilage content.
- **Step 3**: Rice is added and ground to a **smooth, slightly coarse batter**.
- **Step 4**: Mixed thoroughly, **salt (1–2%)** added.
- **Step 5**: **Fermented for 8–12 hours** at 25–30°C. Fermentation is driven by natural *Leuconostoc* and *Lactobacillus* species, which:
  - Produce **CO₂** (leavening the batter).
  - Lower the pH to **4.5–5.0** (improving shelf life and digestibility).
  - Increase **vitamin B** content.
  - Reduce **phytate** (anti-nutrient), improving mineral absorption.
- **Step 6**: Used to steam (idli) or pan-fry (dosa).

**(iii) Papad (Appalam) (2.5 marks):**
- **Raw material**: Typically **black gram flour (urad dal atta)**, often blended with **rice flour, besan, or sago**.
- **Ingredients**: Black gram flour, salt, **sodium bicarbonate (baking soda)**, optional spices (cumin, pepper, red chilli).
- **Mixing**: A stiff dough is prepared with warm water.
- **Sheeting**: The dough is sheeted into a **thin circular sheet (0.5–1.0 mm thick)**.
- **Drying**: Sun-dried for **4–8 hours** to a moisture of **8–10%** (shelf-stable).
- **Packing**: Stored in moisture-proof pouches.
- **Cooking**: At the time of consumption, the dried papad is **roasted over open flame, deep-fried, or microwaved** for 10–15 seconds — it **puffs up rapidly** due to sudden steam release from residual moisture, producing the characteristic crisp wafer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (1-mark loss risk)**: In papad questions, students often forget to mention **baking soda (sodium bicarbonate)**. It is **NOT** a leavening agent here — it is a **pH modifier** that ensures uniform puffing and prevents oil splatter during frying.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 3 Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones (read before writing ESE):**
> 1. **Confusing parboiling with pressure cooking** — parboiling is a *pre-milling treatment* of raw paddy, NOT a culinary cooking process.
> 2. **Writing "vitamins are destroyed" during parboiling** — this is the *opposite* of the truth. Parboiling **PRESERVES** B-vitamins inside the endosperm.
> 3. **Using the wrong moisture basis** in drying problems — KTU expects you to **state the basis** (w.b. or d.b.) explicitly in your solution.
> 4. **Mixing up dehusking vs. polishing** — *Dehusking* removes the outer husk; *Polishing* removes the inner bran layer. They are sequential, not identical.
> 5. **Forgetting the seed coat in pulse processing** — the **testa (seed coat)** is the target of decortication, not the husk. Pulses don't have a husk; they have a seed coat.
> 6. **Confusing popping with puffing** — *Popping* uses the intact pericarp as a pressure vessel (no gun required); *Puffing* uses an external pressure vessel (gun).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cereals = Poaceae family, starch-dominant, monocot.** Major types: rice, wheat, maize, barley, oats, sorghum, millets.
- **Pulses = Fabaceae family, protein-dominant, dicot.** Major types: chickpea, pigeon pea, green gram, black gram, lentil.
- **Safe storage moisture**: Cereals ≤ 14% (w.b.); Pulses ≤ 10–12% (w.b.).
- **Parboiling** = soak + steam-cook + dry → migrates vitamins, hardens grain, reduces breakage.
- **Dehusking** = removal of husk (cereals) or seed coat (pulses).
- **Polishing** = removal of bran layer; reduces nutrients.
- **Puffing** = sudden pressure release → 8–10× expansion; requires moisture + high temperature.
- **Popping** = uses pericarp as pressure vessel → 30–40× expansion for popcorn.
- **Milling recovery (MRE)** = $\dfrac{M_{\text{head rice}}}{M_{\text{paddy milled}}} \times 100$ — KTU high-yield formula.
- **Dhal yield benchmark** = **70–78%** for commercial pulse mills.
- **Jones factor 6.25** = $N(\%) \times 6.25 = \text{Crude protein (\%)}$ — used for cereals/pulses.
- **Soaking** (8–12 h) is **mandatory** before pulse dehusking; it softens the seed coat bond.
- **Pretreatment with edible oil** (0.5–1.0%) is used for *tight-coated* pulses like pigeon pea.
- **Fermentation** of rice + urad batter produces **idli/dosa**; pH drops to **4.5–5.0**, phytate reduces, B-vitamins increase.
- **Gelatinization** (55–75°C) ≠ **Puffing** (steam pressure-driven) — common confusion point.
- **Sprouting pulses** increases **vitamin C** and **protein digestibility**, reduces **anti-nutrients** (phytate, trypsin inhibitor).
- **Moisture conversions**: $M_w = \dfrac{100 M_d}{100 + M_d}$ and $M_d = \dfrac{100 M_w}{100 - M_w}$ — memorize both.
- **1000-Kernel Weight (TKW)** is a **grading index** — KTU occasionally asks why it matters (uniformity of milling).

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Cereals and Pulses | KTU 2024 Scheme | OEFTT831**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extruded Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Extruded Products # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Extrusion (Food Processing Context):** Extrusion is a continuous, **High-Temperature Short-Time (HTST)** thermomechanical process in which moistened, expansible food materials (starch and/or protein based) are plasticized, cooked, and structurally transformed by a combination of **moisture, pressure, heat, and mechanical shear** inside a barrel, and then forced through a die to form products of defined shape, density, and texture. The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies extrusion under **Unit Operations in Food Processing** and emphasizes three functional categories: | Category | Temperature Range | Moisture (%) | Typical Product | |---|---|---|---| | **Cold Extrusion** | < 50 °C | 25–35 | Pasta, sausages, dough shaping | | **Hot / Cooking Extrusion** | 120–200 °C | 10–30 | Snacks, breakfast cereals, texturized vegetable protein (TVP) | | **Forming Extrusion** | 50–100 °C | 30–50 | Cookies, croutons, third-generation snacks (pellets) | > [!IMPORTANT] > The extruder is technically a **bioreactor + heat exchanger + high-shear mixer + forming device** all rolled into one continuous system — this is why it is called a "HTST multi-functional unit operation." ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine you take a tube of **toothpaste**, cut off the cap, and press the tube from the back. What comes out is shaped exactly like the opening. Now, instead of just squeezing, imagine: 1. The toothpaste is **heated from the outside** (like holding the tube over steam), 2. You **stir it while pushing** with a giant corkscrew, 3. Suddenly, when it shoots out of the nozzle, the steam inside **puffs up** and dries into a crispy, porous, ready-to-eat snack. That puffing miracle is **extrusion cooking** — the same physics used to make **kurkure, puffed corn curls, breakfast cereals, and even pet food**. The "corkscrew" is the **screw**; the heated tube is the **barrel**; the nozzle is the **die**; the puffing is **flash evaporation** of superheated water. ## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics - **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME):** typically **40 – 400 Wh/kg** of dry feed. - **Residence Time:** **5 – 30 seconds** in the metering zone for cooking extruders. - **Barrel L/D Ratio:** 20:1 to 30:1 for modern cooking extruders. - **Screw Speed (N):** **100 – 800 rpm**. - **Throughput (Q):** 100 kg/h (lab scale) to **>10,000 kg/h** (industrial twin-screw lines). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Cooking Extruder The extruder consists of three sequential physical zones — each playing a distinct thermomechanical role: ### Zone 1: Feeding / Solids Conveying Zone - **Function:** Receives dry or pre-conditioned flour from the feeder. - **Mechanism:** Drag-induced conveying. The rotating screw flights drag material against the stationary barrel. - **State of Material:** Free-flowing powder; minimal compression. - **Fill Ratio:** 0.4 – 0.5 (only partially filled). ### Zone 2: Kneading / Mixing / Cooking Zone - **Function:** Compresses, mixes, shears, and heats the material. - **Mechanism:** Reverse-pitched kneading elements build **back-pressure (ΔP)**; the food is plasticized above its **glass transition (Tg)** into a viscoelastic melt. - **State of Material:** Starch granules gelatinize; proteins denature; moisture acts as a plasticizer. - **Temperature:** 100 – 160 °C. - **Pressure:** 30 – 150 bar. > [!NOTE] > **Why gelatinization happens here:** When the molten starch exits the die, the sudden pressure drop causes the superheated water to flash into steam — this is what creates the **puffed cellular structure** in the final product. ### Zone 3: Metering / Pumping Zone - **Function:** Generates uniform pressure and volumetric output to the die. - **Mechanism:** Shallow, constant-depth screw flights behave as a **positive displacement pump**. - **State of Material:** Homogeneous, fully cooked dough melt. - **Fill Ratio:** ~ 1.0 (completely filled). ## 2.2 Types of Extruders (KTU High-Yield) | Parameter | Single-Screw Extruder | Twin-Screw Extruder | |---|---|---| | **Conveying Mechanism** | Drag-induced (friction) | Positive displacement + drag | | **Self-Wiping** | No | Yes (intermeshing types) | | **Shear Control** | Limited | Excellent (element design) | | **Capacity** | Lower | Higher (up to 20 t/h) | | **Cost** | Lower | Higher | | **Residence Time Distribution** | Broad | Narrow | | **Wetting Ability** | Poor with dry powders | Excellent (conveys dry powders readily) | | **Common Use** | Pasta, pet food, snacks | Breakfast cereals, TVP, infant food | **Twin-Screw Subtypes:** - **Co-rotating (same direction):** Better mixing, higher shear, self-wiping — most common for cooking. - **Counter-rotating (opposite direction):** Used for forming/calendering; poor at conveying, high pressure. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` (NOT the vertical pipe `|`) for absolute values in tables to keep the markdown table syntax intact. | # | Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^2 D^2 N\, d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | Drag flow rate | Forward pumping component | | 2 | $Q_p = \dfrac{\pi D\, d^3 \sin^2\theta\, \Delta P}{12\,\eta L}$ | Pressure flow rate | Back-leakage component | | 3 | $Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p$ | Net throughput | Designing die and screw | | 4 | $SME = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot \tau}{\dot{m}}$ | Specific Mechanical Energy (J/kg) | Predicting starch gelatinization & protein texturization | | 5 | $P_{die} = \dfrac{12\,\eta\, Q\, L_d}{\pi D_d^{\,4}}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille die pressure (cylindrical die) | Selecting die geometry | | 6 | $ER = \dfrac{A_{die}}{A_{exit\,product}}$ | Expansion Ratio | Measures puffing / porosity | | 7 | $SI = \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{material}}$ | Section Index / relative density | Quantifies crispness | | 8 | $\eta_{app} = K\,\dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ | Power-law viscosity model | Used in pressure-flow derivation | **Where:** $D$ = barrel ID, $d$ = flight depth, $N$ = screw speed, $\theta$ = helix angle, $\Delta P$ = pressure rise, $\eta$ = viscosity, $L$ = axial length, $\tau$ = torque on screw shaft, $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate, $L_d$ = die land length, $D_d$ = die diameter, $\rho$ = density, $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate, $K,n$ = consistency & flow index. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Product Density Engineering:** A 1 % change in moisture can shift product density by ~8 % — vital for **breakfast cereal flotation** (toasties must float in milk). - **SME as a Master Variable:** SME > 150 Wh/kg typically gives **highly expanded** snacks; SME < 80 Wh/kg yields **dense, glassy products** (e.g., croutons). - **Starch Damage:** Extrusion converts 60 – 90 % of crystalline starch to **gelatinized, digestible** form — critical for **infant food digestibility**. - **Anti-nutrient Destruction:** HTST extrusion destroys 80–95 % of **trypsin inhibitors in soy** and **lectins** — making it central to TVP production. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Net Throughput of a Single-Screw Extruder We consider the **metering zone** modelled as an **"unwrapped" infinite parallel-plate channel**: - Lower plate = barrel (stationary, velocity 0). - Upper plate = screw root (moving at velocity $v_s = \pi D N$ in the down-channel direction). - Channel depth = $d$, channel width = $W = \pi D \sin\theta - e \approx \pi D \sin\theta$, helix angle = $\theta$. **Step 1 — Resolve the screw surface velocity into channel-direction and cross-channel components.** The barrel surface velocity relative to the screw is $V_b = \pi D N$. Decomposing along the channel: $$ V_{bz} = \pi D N \sin\theta \quad \text{(down-channel)} $$ $$ V_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta \quad \text{(cross-channel, drives drag flow)} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Couette-Poiseuille flow for a Newtonian fluid in the rectangular channel.** The total down-channel velocity profile $v_z(x)$ is the superposition of: (a) **Drag flow** (Couette, $V_{bx}$ dragging fluid from the moving upper plate): $$ v_{z,\text{drag}} = V_{bx}\,\dfrac{x}{d} = \pi D N \cos\theta \cdot \dfrac{x}{d} $$ (b) **Pressure flow** (Poiseuille, due to pressure gradient $\Delta P/L$): $$ v_{z,\text{pressure}} = \dfrac{1}{2\eta}\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\right)\left(x d - x^2\right) $$ **Step 3 — Total volumetric flow per channel (down-channel).** Integrate $v_z$ over $0 \le x \le d$ and multiply by width $W$: $$ Q_{\text{channel}} = W \int_{0}^{d} v_z(x)\,dx $$ $$ = W \int_{0}^{d} \left[ \pi D N \cos\theta \cdot \dfrac{x}{d} - \dfrac{1}{2\eta}\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\left(x d - x^2\right) \right] dx $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integral explicitly.** Drag part: $$ \int_{0}^{d} \pi D N \cos\theta \cdot \dfrac{x}{d}\,dx = \pi D N \cos\theta \cdot \dfrac{d}{2} $$ Pressure part: $$ \int_{0}^{d} \dfrac{1}{2\eta}\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\left(x^2 - x d\right)dx = \dfrac{1}{2\eta}\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\left[\dfrac{d^3}{3} - \dfrac{d^3}{2}\right] = -\dfrac{\Delta P\,d^3}{12\eta L} $$ Combining: $$ Q_{\text{channel}} = W \left[ \dfrac{\pi D N d \cos\theta}{2} - \dfrac{\Delta P\,d^3}{12\eta L} \right] $$ **Step 5 — Substitute $W = \pi D \sin\theta$ and use $v_{bz}$ for clarity.** $$ Q = \pi D \sin\theta \left[ \dfrac{\pi D N d \cos\theta}{2} - \dfrac{\Delta P\,d^3}{12\eta L} \right] $$ $$ Q = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\theta\cos\theta - \dfrac{\pi D\,d^3 \sin^2\theta\, \Delta P}{12\,\eta L} $$ $$ \boxed{\;Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D\,d^3 \sin^2\theta\, \Delta P}{12\,\eta L}\;} $$ This is the **canonical KTU/syllabus result**: **Net Throughput = Drag Flow − Pressure Flow**. ## 3.2 Derivation of Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) **Step 1 — Mechanical power input to the screw.** The torque on the screw shaft is $\tau$ (N·m). Rotating at $N$ (rev/s), the power delivered to the food is: $$ P_{mech} = 2\pi N \tau \quad \text{(W)} $$ **Step 2 — Energy imparted per unit mass of dry feed.** Divide by mass throughput $\dot{m}$ (kg/s): $$ SME = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\dot{m}} = \dfrac{2\pi N \tau}{\dot{m}} \quad \text{(J/kg)} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to the engineering unit of Wh/kg** (1 Wh = 3600 J): $$ \boxed{\;SME\,(Wh/kg) = \dfrac{2\pi N \tau}{3600\,\dot{m}}\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters at KTU level:** SME is the single most diagnostic process variable in extrusion. It correlates directly with: > - Starch gelatinization degree (↑ SME → ↑ gelatinization, up to a plateau). > - Protein texturization. > - Product density and expansion ratio. > - Colour formation (Maillard, ↑ SME → darker). ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (Examiner's View) | Process Variable | Effect on Throughput (Q) | Effect on SME | Effect on Expansion Ratio (ER) | Effect on Bulk Density | |---|---|---|---|---| | **↑ Screw speed (N)** | ↑ linearly | ↑ ↑ | ↑ (up to optimum, then ↓) | ↓ | | **↑ Moisture** | ↑ (lubricates) | ↓ | ↓↓ (water inhibits puff) | ↑ (denser) | | **↑ Temperature (barrel)** | ↑ (lower η) | ↓ | ↑ then ↓ (over-cooks starch) | ↓ | | **↑ Screw flight depth (d)** | ↑ ↑ (d^3 in pressure term) | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | | **↑ Helix angle (θ = 17° optimum)** | ↑ (up to 17°) | — | Mild effect | Mild effect | | **↑ Die restriction (small die)** | ↓ (↑ ΔP) | ↑ | ↑ (more flash) | ↓ | | **↑ Feed rate** | ↑ linearly | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Extrusion Process — End-to-End Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR rawA[Raw Material] --> feedS[Volumetric Feeder] feedS --> condit[Pre-conditioner
Adds moisture and steam] condit --> feedT[Throat / Hopper] feedT --> feedZ[Zone 1: Feeding Zone
Solids conveying] feedZ --> cookZ[Zone 2: Cooking Zone
Kneading elements
100 to 160 deg C] cookZ --> meterZ[Zone 3: Metering Zone
Pressure generation 30 to 150 bar] meterZ --> dieH[Die Head Assembly] dieH --> cutX[Rotary Cutter] cutX --> dryR[Drying / Toasting / Coating] dryR --> packR[Packaging] packR --> outR[Finished Extruded Product] ctrlS[PLC and SCADA] -.->|Feedback| feedS ctrlS -.->|Speed control| feedZ ctrlS -.->|Temp profile| cookZ ctrlS -.->|Cutter RPM| cutX driveM[Drive Motor and Gearbox] -.->|Torque to screw| meterZ ``` ## 4.2 Functional State of Material Along the Screw ```mermaid flowchart TD s1[Stage A: Powder / Granules] -->|Heat and moisture added| s2[Stage B: Granular and Sticky] s2 -->|Mechanical shear and T greater than Tg| s3[Stage C: Plasticized Melt] s3 -->|Reaches die at high P and T| s4[Stage D: Sudden pressure drop at die exit] s4 -->|Flash evaporation of water| s5[Stage E: Expanded porous foam] s5 -->|Cooling and moisture loss| s6[Stage F: Crispy shelf stable product] ``` ## 4.3 Screw Element Configurations for Twin-Screw ```mermaid flowchart LR elemA[Conveying Element
Long pitch forward] --> elemB[Kneading Element
45 deg disc offset] elemB --> elemC[Kneading Element
90 deg reverse offset] elemC --> elemD[Reverse Conveying Element
Builds pressure] elemD --> elemE[Conveying Element
Short pitch forward] elemE --> dieOut[Die Plate] ``` > [!NOTE] > Each kneading disc offset angle controls the **shear intensity**: 30° → mild shear, 60° → moderate, **90° (reverse) → maximum back-pressure and SME**, which is essential for TVP and high-protein texturization. ## 4.4 Process Parameter Influence Web (Mermaid Concept Map) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef pri fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 classDef sec fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,color:#000 classDef out fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000 M[Moisture Content]:::pri T[Barrel Temperature]:::pri N[Screw Speed RPM]:::pri F[Feed Rate]:::pri D[Die Geometry]:::pri S[Starch Gelatinization Degree]:::sec V[Viscosity of melt]:::sec P[Back pressure Delta P]:::sec E[SME]:::sec ER[Expansion Ratio]:::out BD[Bulk Density]:::out TX[Texture and Crispness]:::out NUT[Nutrient Retention]:::out M --> V T --> V T --> S M --> S N --> E F --> E D --> P N --> P V --> P S --> ER P --> ER M --> ER E --> TX ER --> TX ER --> BD T --> NUT ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]** **Define extrusion cooking. State any two differences between single-screw and twin-screw extruders.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Extrusion cooking is a high-temperature short-time (HTST) process in which moistened, expansible starchy/proteinaceous food is plasticized, cooked, and structured by a combination of moisture, pressure, heat, and shear inside a barrel and forced through a die. *(1 mark)* | Aspect | Single-Screw | Twin-Screw | |---|---|---| | Conveying | Drag (friction) based | Positive displacement + drag | | Self-wiping | Absent | Present (intermeshing) | | Mixing | Limited | Excellent | | Cost | Lower | Higher | | RTD | Broad | Narrow | *(2 marks for any two valid differences with explanation.)* --- ### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** **What is Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)? Mention its significance in extrusion.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** SME is the mechanical energy input per unit mass of dry feed delivered by the screw to the food, given by: $$ SME = \dfrac{2\pi N \tau}{\dot{m}} \quad (J/kg) $$ *(1 mark definition + 0.5 mark for formula.)* **Significance:** *(1.5 marks)* - It predicts degree of starch gelatinization, protein texturization, and product expansion. - Acts as a master process variable — used to scale up from lab to industrial extruders. - Typical ranges: **40–400 Wh/kg**; high SME → expanded snacks; low SME → dense products. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ✅ OPTION A #### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the working of a single-screw cooking extruder with a neat labeled diagram. Discuss the role of each zone. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the equation for net throughput of a single-screw extruder in the metering zone. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Working of Single-Screw Cooking Extruder (7 marks):** The single-screw cooking extruder consists of a **hopper, barrel, rotating screw, die, and cutter**. The screw is driven by a variable-speed motor through a gearbox. The barrel is heated by external jackets (electrical or steam) and can be cooled. **Three Functional Zones:** 1. **Feeding Zone (Solids Conveying) — 2 marks** - Dry flour/feed enters from the hopper. - Deep flight depth; partially filled; only forward transport. - Heat and moisture from preconditioner begin to plasticize starch superficially. 2. **Cooking / Kneading Zone — 3 marks** - Contains reverse-flight elements and kneading discs that build **back-pressure (ΔP)**. - Material is heated (100–160 °C) and sheared; starch **gelatinizes**, proteins **denature and reorient** (texturization). - Moisture acts as a **plasticizer** lowering viscosity, allowing continuous flow. - End state: **homogeneous viscoelastic melt** at high pressure. 3. **Metering Zone — 1 mark** - Shallow, constant-depth flights act as a positive displacement pump. - Generates uniform pressure (~ 30–150 bar) feeding the die. At the **die**, sudden pressure drop causes **flash evaporation** of superheated water → expansion and puffing. The **rotary cutter** slices the extrudate into pellets or curls. The **expansion ratio (ER)** = cross-sectional area of product / die area is a key quality parameter. **Labeled diagram:** (Student should sketch the screw-barrel assembly with the three zones, die, and cutter — credit 1 mark for the diagram.) **(b) Derivation of Net Throughput (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Modeling assumption (1 mark):** Unwrap the metering zone channel into a flat parallel-plate channel. The lower plate is the barrel (stationary); the upper plate is the screw root moving at $V_b = \pi D N$. Channel width $W = \pi D \sin\theta$, depth $d$, helix angle $\theta$. **Step 2 — Velocity resolution (1 mark):** $$ V_{bz} = \pi D N \sin\theta, \qquad V_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta $$ **Step 3 — Velocity profile (2 marks):** Superposition of Couette and Poiseuille flow: $$ v_z(x) = V_{bx}\,\dfrac{x}{d} - \dfrac{1}{2\eta}\,\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\left(x d - x^2\right) $$ **Step 4 — Integration across channel depth and multiply by W (1.5 marks):** $$ Q = W \int_0^d v_z(x)\,dx = W\left[ \dfrac{\pi D N d \cos\theta}{2} - \dfrac{\Delta P\,d^3}{12\eta L} \right] $$ **Step 5 — Substitute W and simplify (1.5 marks):** $$ \boxed{\;Q_{net} = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\theta\cos\theta - \dfrac{\pi D\,d^3 \sin^2\theta\, \Delta P}{12\eta L}\;} $$ The **first term is drag flow** (forward), the **second is pressure flow** (reverse); net output is their difference. --- ### ✅ OPTION B #### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **(a)** With a flowchart, explain the production of **extruded snack (puffed) products** from corn grits. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are the major **process parameters** affecting product characteristics in extrusion? Explain the effect of moisture and screw speed on product expansion. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Production of Extruded Puffed Snacks from Corn Grits (7 marks):** **Process Flow:** 1. **Raw Material Preparation — 1 mark:** Corn grits are ground/sieved to uniform particle size (typically 12–14 % moisture). Optional: addition of **salt, sugar, flavour premix, colouring**. 2. **Pre-conditioning — 1 mark:** Grits are moistened (moisture raised to 14–18 %) and partially pre-cooked in a **pre-conditioner** using steam (60–90 °C, 2–4 min). This reduces specific mechanical energy demand inside the extruder. 3. **Feeding into Extruder — 1 mark:** A **volumetric feeder** delivers conditioned grits to the extruder throat at a controlled rate. 4. **Cooking / Extrusion — 2 marks:** Inside the barrel (120–160 °C, screw speed 300–500 rpm, L/D = 20:1), starch gelatinizes, melt forms at 30–100 bar. At the die exit, superheated water flashes → **puffing and expansion (ER = 4–8)**. A rotary cutter slices the rope. 5. **Drying / Toasting / Seasoning — 1 mark:** Moisture is reduced to < 3 %; seasonings and oil are tumble-coated. 6. **Packaging — 0.5 mark:** Moisture-proof (metallized PP/LDPE) pouches; shelf life 6–9 months. **Labelled flowchart required — 0.5 mark** (refer to Section 4.1 above.) **(b) Process Parameters Affecting Product Characteristics (7 marks):** **Major process parameters:** feed moisture, barrel temperature, screw speed (N), feed rate, screw configuration, die geometry, and SME. *(1 mark for listing.)* **Effect of Moisture (3 marks):** - Acts as a **plasticizer** — lowers melt viscosity, reduces SME. - At **higher moisture** (≥ 25 %): melt is too fluid → **poor expansion, dense product, high bulk density, soft texture**. - At **lower moisture** (10–15 %): melt is highly viscous → **more flash steam on exit → greater expansion, lower bulk density, crispier texture**. - However, **too low** moisture causes burning, dark colour, and equipment wear. - **Optimum for expanded corn snacks: 13–16 %** feed moisture. **Effect of Screw Speed (N) (3 marks):** - Higher N → **higher SME** (more mechanical energy per unit mass) → more starch disruption and gelatinization. - Higher N → **higher shear rate** → finer, more uniform cell structure in the product. - Higher N → **higher throughput** (linearly) but reduced **residence time** — may undercook if too fast. - Higher N → expansion ratio **increases up to an optimum (~ 400 rpm)**, beyond which over-shearing collapses the cell structure and reduces expansion. - Higher N → bulk density initially **decreases**, then **increases** after optimum due to over-shearing. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here):** > 1. **Don't write "SME" as "Mechanical Energy" alone** — must specify "per unit mass of dry feed." (1 mark lost.) > 2. **Don't confuse drag flow and pressure flow signs.** Drag flow = forward; pressure flow = backward. If signs are reversed, the throughput equation is *wrong by sign* — full 7 marks at risk. > 3. **Don't skip the modeling assumption** (Newtonian fluid, unwrapped channel) in the derivation — 1 mark specifically allocated. > 4. **Do NOT write "single-screw extruder" for a twin-screw question or vice versa** — read the question carefully. > 5. **Always mention HTST (High-Temperature Short-Time)** when defining extrusion cooking — examiners look for this exact phrase. > 6. **Avoid mixing up Expansion Ratio (ER) and Section Index (SI):** ER = A_die / A_product (i.e., inverse relationship with density), SI = ρ_bulk / ρ_solid. They are **reciprocal-like measures** — don't equate them. > 7. **Residence Time Distribution (RTD)** is narrower in twin-screw — students often wrongly say "single-screw has narrow RTD." --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ Extrusion = **HTST** process combining **moisture, heat, mechanical shear, pressure**. - ✅ Three zones: **Feeding → Cooking → Metering**. - ✅ Single-screw = drag-flow; **Twin-screw = positive displacement + drag + self-wiping**. - ✅ Canonical equation: $\;Q_{net} = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\theta\cos\theta - \dfrac{\pi D d^3 \sin^2\theta \Delta P}{12\eta L}\;$. - ✅ **Drag flow pushes forward, pressure flow pushes back** — net = drag − pressure. - ✅ **SME** is the master process variable: $SME = \dfrac{2\pi N \tau}{\dot{m}}$, units J/kg (or Wh/kg). - ✅ **Helix angle optimum ≈ 17°** for maximum throughput in metering zone. - ✅ Expansion (puffing) is due to **flash evaporation** of superheated water at the die exit. - ✅ Higher **moisture** → denser, less expanded product. - ✅ Higher **screw speed** → higher SME and throughput; expansion increases up to an optimum, then decreases. - ✅ Higher **temperature** → lower viscosity, more gelatinization, more expansion (up to optimum). - ✅ **Expansion Ratio (ER)** = $A_{die}/A_{product}$ (or $D_{die}/D_{product}$ for circular cross-section). - ✅ **Section Index (SI)** = $\rho_{bulk}/\rho_{true}$ — measure of relative density. - ✅ Twin-screw extruders preferred for **breakfast cereals, TVP, infant food**; single-screw for **pasta and pet food**. - ✅ **Co-rotating twin-screws** → mixing; **Counter-rotating** → pressure/calendering. - ✅ Typical operating ranges: 120–200 °C, 30–150 bar, 5–30 s residence time, SME 40–400 Wh/kg, L/D 20:1 – 30:1. - ✅ Extrusion destroys 80–95 % of **anti-nutritional factors** (trypsin inhibitors, lectins) in soy — key to TVP. - ✅ Always remember: **KTU exams love the throughput equation derivation and the SME definition** — these are guaranteed 7–14 mark questions.

topics.title: Fruits and Vegetables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fruits and Vegetables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Fruit (Botanical vs. Food Processing):**
> Botanically, a **fruit** is the mature ovary of a flowering plant, typically containing seeds. In **Food Processing Engineering**, the term is broadened to include any seed-bearing structure or sweet, fleshy product of a tree or plant that is consumed as food (e.g., tomato, cucumber — botanically fruits, but used as vegetables).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Vegetable (Food Processing Context):**
> A **vegetable** is defined as an edible plant or part of a plant other than a sweet fruit or seed. This typically includes leaves (spinach), stems (celery), roots (carrot), tubers (potato), bulbs (onion), and flowers (cauliflower).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The classification in food processing is **culinary/utilitarian**, not strictly botanical. The Indian Food Standards (under FSSAI) and Codex Alimentarius adopt the *culinary* classification for processing standards.

## 1.2 Classification of Fruits and Vegetables

### A. Classification of Fruits (Based on Respiration Rate)

| Class | Respiration Rate (mL CO₂/kg·h) | Examples | Storage Life |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Climacteric** | High peak after harvest | Banana, Apple, Tomato, Mango, Pear | Ripen after picking |
| **Non-Climacteric** | Low, gradual decline | Citrus, Grape, Pineapple, Strawberry | Must ripen on plant |

### B. Classification of Vegetables (Based on Plant Part Used)

| Type | Plant Part | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Leafy vegetables** | Leaves | Spinach, Lettuce, Cabbage |
| **Root vegetables** | Roots | Carrot, Radish, Beetroot |
| **Tuber vegetables** | Underground stems | Potato, Sweet potato |
| **Bulb vegetables** | Bulbs | Onion, Garlic |
| **Flower vegetables** | Flowers | Cauliflower, Broccoli |
| **Fruit vegetables** | Fruits (botanical) | Brinjal, Cucumber, Okra |
| **Seed vegetables** | Seeds | Peas, Beans, Corn |
| **Stem/Shoot** | Stems | Asparagus, Celery |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Living, Breathing Lunch Box"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Think of a freshly harvested fruit or vegetable as a **"living lunch box"** — it is still alive, breathing (respiring), losing water (transpiring), and running on its own internal fuel (stored carbohydrates/acids). Your job as a food processor is to either:
> 1. **Slow down the metabolism** → Cold storage, Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)
> 2. **Stop the metabolism gently** → Blanching, Pasteurization
> 3. **Stop it permanently** → Canning, Sterilization, Freeze-drying
>
> Just like you would refrigerate leftover curry to slow spoilage, fruits and vegetables require controlled environments to extend their shelf-life post-harvest.

## 1.4 Standard Industrial Metrics for Fruits & Vegetables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical & Chemical Constants:**
> - **Water Content:** **85–95%** (most fresh produce)
> - **pH of Fruits:** Generally **3.0 – 4.5** (acidic — high-acid food)
> - **pH of Vegetables:** Generally **5.0 – 7.0** (low-acid food)
> - **Water Activity (a_w):** Fresh produce typically **0.97 – 0.99** (highly perishable)
> - **Standard Cold Storage Temp:** **0 °C – 4 °C** for most vegetables; **4 °C – 13 °C** for tropical fruits (chilling injury below threshold)
> - **Relative Humidity (RH):** **85–95%** for most produce
> - **Respiratory Quotient (RQ):** CO₂ produced / O₂ consumed ≈ **1.0** for carbohydrates, **<1.0** for lipids

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Composition of Fruits and Vegetables

The typical proximate composition dictates the choice of preservation method:

| Component | Fruits (%) | Vegetables (%) | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Water** | 80 – 90 | 85 – 95 | High → Microbial spoilage risk |
| **Carbohydrates** | 5 – 20 | 2 – 25 | Sugars, starches, cellulose, pectin |
| **Proteins** | 0.5 – 1.5 | 1 – 5 | Generally low |
| **Fats** | 0.1 – 1.0 | 0.1 – 0.5 | Exception: Avocado, Olive |
| **Acids (Citric, Malic, Tartaric)** | 0.5 – 2.5 | 0.1 – 0.5 | pH control, microbial inhibition |
| **Vitamins & Minerals** | 0.5 – 2.0 | 0.5 – 2.0 | Heat/labile → Nutrient loss in processing |
| **Pigments (Chlorophyll, Carotenoids, Anthocyanins)** | Trace | Trace | Quality, color degradation |

## 2.2 Post-Harvest Losses — The 'Why' of Food Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Post-harvest losses** in India range from **20–40%** for fruits and vegetables due to:
> - **Physiological losses:** Respiration, transpiration, ethylene production
> - **Pathological losses:** Fungal (e.g., *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*), bacterial (e.g., *Erwinia*) decay
> - **Mechanical losses:** Bruising during harvesting/transport
> - **Biochemical losses:** Enzymatic browning (PPO enzyme)
> - **Insect/Pest losses**

## 2.3 Preservation Methods — Theoretical Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Hurdle Technology Principle:**
> Combines multiple mild preservation techniques (hurdles) to inhibit microbial growth — proposed by **Lothar Leistner (1995)**. Each hurdle contributes partially; together they provide stability.

### Major Preservation Methods:

1. **Low-Temperature Preservation (Refrigeration & Freezing)**
   - Refrigeration: 0–4 °C; slows microbial growth and respiration
   - Freezing: ≤ −18 °C; stops microbial activity (most pathogens)
   - **IQF (Individual Quick Freezing):** Rapid freezing preserves cellular structure

2. **Thermal Preservation**
   - **Pasteurization:** 63 °C / 30 min or 72 °C / 15 sec (HTST) for high-acid foods (pH < 4.5)
   - **Sterilization:** 121 °C / 15 min for low-acid foods (vegetables)
   - **Blanching:** 80–100 °C / 1–10 min — inactivates enzymes (PPO, POD)

3. **Water Removal (Drying/Dehydration)**
   - Reduces water activity (a_w) below 0.60 → microbial growth inhibited
   - Sun drying, hot air drying, spray drying, freeze drying (lyophilization)

4. **Chemical Preservation**
   - **Sugar (jams, jellies):** High osmotic pressure
   - **Salt (pickles):** Osmotic dehydration
   - **Vinegar (acetic acid):** pH reduction
   - **Sulphur dioxide (SO₂):** 350–2000 ppm — antimicrobial, anti-browning

5. **Fermentation**
   - Lactic acid fermentation → pickles, sauerkraut
   - Produces organic acids, lowers pH

6. **Radiation Preservation (Irradiation)**
   - Gamma rays / electron beam — up to **1 kGy** permitted for sprout inhibition

7. **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)**
   - Reduced O₂ (2–5%), elevated CO₂ (5–10%), balance N₂

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU Examination:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | Water activity (partial vapor pressure over food / pure water vapor pressure) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ | Freezing point depression (molal) | K or °C |
| 3 | $k = k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius equation for reaction rate (browning, microbial death) | rate constant |
| 4 | $\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\dfrac{t}{D}$ | Decimal reduction time (microbial inactivation) | time |
| 5 | $F = F_{ref} \cdot 10^{\dfrac{T_{ref}-T}{z}}$ | Equivalent sterilization time at reference temperature | time |
| 6 | $RR = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{t \cdot m_{produce}}$ | Respiration rate (mass CO₂ per unit mass per unit time) | mL/kg·h or mg/kg·h |
| 7 | $\eta_{drying} = \dfrac{m_{water,removed}}{Q_{heat}}$ | Drying efficiency | kg/J |
| 8 | $C_{p,mix} = \sum x_i C_{p,i}$ | Specific heat of food mixture (mass-weighted) | kJ/kg·K |
| 9 | $T_f = 1.8\,T_c + 32$ | Freezing time approximation (Plank's equation simplified) | time |
| 10 | $\rho_{food} = \dfrac{m_{dry} + m_{water}}{V_{total}}$ | Bulk density (foods) | kg/m³ |

**Boundary Conditions Used in KTU Problems:**
- High-acid food threshold: $\text{pH} < 4.5$ (fruits)
- Low-acid food threshold: $\text{pH} \geq 4.5$ (most vegetables)
- Critical water activity: $a_w < 0.85$ → no bacterial growth
- Minimum $a_w$ for *Staphylococcus aureus* growth: **0.86**
- Minimum $a_w$ for yeast/mold: **0.70**
- Safe moisture for dried vegetables: **<10%**
- Safe moisture for dried fruits: **<25%**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of fruit and vegetable processing underpin industries worth **billions of USD globally**:
- **Indian Fruit Processing Industry:** Valued at ~INR 50,000 crore (2024)
- **Global Frozen Food Market:** ~USD 300 billion
- **Jam/Jelly/Pickle Industry:** Major cottage industry in Kerala (coconut, mango, lime pickles)

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Connect:** Choosing between pasteurization and sterilization for a tomato ketchup (pH 4.0) vs. a mushroom curry (pH 5.5) is **not arbitrary** — it is dictated by the pH threshold of *Clostridium botulinum* (pH 4.5 minimum growth).
