# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Freezing Point Depression for Fruits/Vegetables

The freezing point of a fruit/vegetable is **below 0 °C** because dissolved sugars, salts, and acids depress the freezing point. This is critical for designing freezers and predicting ice formation.

### Starting Point — Raoult's Law (Ideal Dilute Solution)

For a dilute aqueous solution of solutes in fruit juice:

$$\Delta T_f = T_f^{pure} - T_f^{solution}$$

where:
- $T_f^{pure}$ = Freezing point of pure water = **0 °C**
- $T_f^{solution}$ = Freezing point of fruit juice (typically **−1 °C to −3 °C**)

### Step 1 — Apply the Cryoscopic Equation

$$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$$

where:
- $K_f$ = Cryoscopic constant for water = **1.86 K·kg/mol**
- $m$ = Molality of solution (mol solute / kg solvent)

### Step 2 — Express Molality in Terms of Mass Fractions

For a fruit juice containing sugars (sucrose: $M = 342$ g/mol), salts, and acids:

$$m = \sum_{i} \dfrac{w_i \times 1000}{M_i \times W_{water}}$$

where:
- $w_i$ = Mass of solute $i$ (grams)
- $M_i$ = Molar mass of solute $i$
- $W_{water}$ = Mass of water (grams)
- The factor 1000 converts kg → g

### Step 3 — Worked Example (Orange Juice)

A sample of orange juice contains:
- Total Soluble Solids (TSS) = 12 °Brix ≈ 12 g sugar / 100 g juice
- Predominant sugar: Sucrose ($M_{sucrose}$ = 342 g/mol)
- Water content = 88 g per 100 g juice

**Compute molality:**

$$m = \dfrac{12 \times 1000}{342 \times 88}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{12000}{30096} \\
m &= 0.3987 \;\; \text{mol/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute freezing point depression:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_f &= K_f \cdot m \\
\Delta T_f &= 1.86 \times 0.3987 \\
\Delta T_f &= 0.7416 \;\; \text{°C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Hence, freezing point of orange juice:**

$$T_f^{juice} = 0 - 0.7416 = -0.74 \;\; \text{°C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A typical orange juice (12 °Brix) freezes at approximately **−0.7 °C to −1.0 °C**. This explains why domestic freezers (−18 °C) freeze juice solidly — the freezing point depression is small compared to freezer temperature.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Preservation Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Matrix for KTU Problems — "Given this fruit/vegetable, what is the ideal preservation method?"**

| Food Product | pH | $a_w$ | Recommended Method | Justification (KTU Logic) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apple Juice | 3.5 | 0.99 | Pasteurization (HTST) + Refrigeration | High acid → inhibits *Clostridium*; pasteurization sufficient |
| Mango Pulp | 4.0 | 0.98 | Hot Break + Pasteurization + Aseptic Packaging | High acid; enzymatic inactivation needed |
| Green Peas | 6.2 | 0.98 | Blanching + Freezing (−18 °C) | Low acid → cannot rely on pH; freezing safe |
| Tomato Ketchup | 3.8 | 0.85 | Hot-fill + Hold + Vinegar addition | Acidified; lower thermal load |
| Potato (dehydrated) | 6.0 | < 0.30 | Hot Air Drying | Low $a_w$ inhibits microbes |
| Pickle (Mango) | 3.5 | 0.80 | Salt + Vinegar + Oil | Acid + low $a_w$ = hurdle |
| Mushroom (canned) | 5.8 | 0.99 | Sterilization (121 °C) | Low acid → botulism risk; full sterilization needed |

## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Penetration in Canning (Simplified)

For a conduction-heated food in a cylindrical can, the time-temperature history at the **critical (coldest) point** (geometric center for conduction foods) is given by:

$$\log\left(\dfrac{T_c - T_{cooling\,medium}}{T_{initial} - T_{cooling\,medium}}\right) = -\dfrac{t}{f_h}$$

where:
- $T_c$ = Temperature at center at time $t$
- $f_h$ = Heating rate index (time for one log cycle reduction)
- $j$ factor (lag factor) is often added for thermal process calculation

### Sterilizing Value ($F_0$) Calculation

$$F_0 = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{\dfrac{T(t) - T_{ref}}{z}} dt$$

For constant retort temperature:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_0 &= t \cdot 10^{\dfrac{T_{retort} - 121.1}{10}} \\
\end{aligned}$$

where $T_{ref} = 121.1 °C$ and $z = 10 °C$ for *C. botulinum* spores.

**Example:** Retort at 115 °C for 30 min:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_0 &= 30 \times 10^{\dfrac{115 - 121.1}{10}} \\
F_0 &= 30 \times 10^{-0.61} \\
F_0 &= 30 \times 0.2455 \\
F_0 &= 7.36 \;\; \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

This $F_0$ is **inadequate** for low-acid canned vegetables (need $F_0 \geq 3$ min for *C. botulinum*, but commercial sterility needs $F_0 = 6-12$ min).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Drying Efficiency

> **Problem:** A batch dryer processes **200 kg** of sliced mango from initial moisture **80% (w.b.)** to final moisture **15% (w.b.)** using **450 MJ** of heat. Compute drying efficiency.

**Step 1 — Moisture Removed:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{initial} &= 200 \;\; \text{kg} \\
m_{water,initial} &= 200 \times 0.80 = 160 \;\; \text{kg} \\
m_{dry\,matter} &= 200 \times 0.20 = 40 \;\; \text{kg} \\
m_{final} &= \dfrac{40}{1 - 0.15} = \dfrac{40}{0.85} = 47.06 \;\; \text{kg} \\
m_{water,removed} &= 200 - 47.06 = 152.94 \;\; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Latent Heat of Vaporization (~2260 kJ/kg):**

$$Q_{latent} = 152.94 \times 2260 = 345{,}644 \;\; \text{kJ} = 345.64 \;\; \text{MJ}$$

**Step 3 — Drying Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{drying} = \dfrac{Q_{latent}}{Q_{supplied}} = \dfrac{345.64}{450} = 0.768 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 76.8\%$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between climacteric and non-climacteric fruits with two examples each. Why is this distinction important in food processing?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Climacteric Fruits | Non-Climacteric Fruits |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Respiration Pattern** | Sharp peak in CO₂ & ethylene during ripening | Low, steady respiration |
> | **Ripening** | Continues after harvest | Must ripen on plant |
> | **Ethylene** | Self-produced burst | Low/insignificant |
> | **Examples** | Banana, Mango, Apple, Tomato | Citrus, Grape, Pineapple, Strawberry |
>
> **Importance in processing (1 mark):**
> - Climacteric fruits can be harvested mature-green and ripened during storage/transport → flexible logistics
> - Non-climacteric fruits must be harvested fully ripe → shorter shelf life, immediate processing needed
> - Ethylene management is critical — exposure accelerates ripening of climacteric fruits and damages non-climacteric ones (e.g., banana ethylene damages tomato)

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]
**Define water activity (a_w). Why is it a better indicator of perishability than total moisture content?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1.5 marks):**
> Water activity ($a_w$) is the ratio of the partial vapor pressure of water in a food ($p$) to the vapor pressure of pure water ($p_0$) at the same temperature:
>
> $$a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$$
>
> **Why better than moisture content (1.5 marks):**
> - Microorganisms do not "count" water molecules — they require water **available** for biochemical reactions
> - Bound water (held by proteins, sugars) is unavailable → high moisture with low $a_w$ (e.g., honey, 17% moisture, $a_w$ ≈ 0.55) is shelf-stable
> - Two foods with identical moisture can have different $a_w$ — only $a_w$ predicts microbial growth
> - Critical thresholds: bacteria need $a_w > 0.86$, yeasts > 0.70, molds > 0.70

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Pattern (2024 Scheme):**
> Part B question carries **14 marks**. Must attempt either Option A or Option B. Each option has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ Option A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the various methods of preservation of fruits and vegetables. Explain in detail the **canning process** with a neat flow diagram.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Classification of Preservation Methods (3 marks):**
>
> 1. **By Temperature:** Refrigeration, Freezing, Pasteurization, Sterilization
> 2. **By Water Removal:** Concentration, Drying, Dehydration, Freeze-drying
> 3. **By Chemical Means:** Sugar (osmotic), Salt, Acids, Sulphitation, Chemical preservatives
> 4. **By Fermentation:** Lactic acid, Alcoholic, Acetic acid
> 5. **By Radiation:** UV, Gamma irradiation, Microwave
> 6. **By Packaging:** MAP, Vacuum packaging, Aseptic packaging
>
> **Canning Process in Detail (4 marks):**
>
> The canning process for fruits and vegetables follows these steps:
>
> 1. **Selection & Sorting:** Sound, mature produce only
> 2. **Washing:** Removes dirt, microbes, pesticide residues
> 3. **Peeling:** Mechanical, chemical (lye peeling for mango), or steam peeling
> 4. **Cutting/Size Reduction:** Uniform size for even heat penetration
> 5. **Blanching:** 80–100 °C / 2–5 min for vegetables (inactivates enzymes PPO, POD)
> 6. **Can Filling:** Hot fill for high-acid foods; cold fill for vegetables
> 7. **Exhausting:** Heating to 80 °C to remove headspace air (prevents can bulging)
> 8. **Sealing:** Hermetic seal with double-seam can
> 9. **Thermal Processing:**
>    - Fruits (pH < 4.5): Pasteurization at 100 °C / 10–30 min in boiling water
>    - Vegetables (pH > 4.5): Sterilization at 121 °C / 15–60 min in retort (pressure cooker-type)
> 10. **Cooling:** Rapid cooling to 35–40 °C to prevent over-cooking
> 11. **Labeling & Storage:** Store in cool, dry place
>
> **Engineering Rationale:** Low-acid vegetables require sterilization because *Clostridium botulinum* spores (pH tolerance ≥ 4.5) are not destroyed by boiling.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **enzymatic browning** in fruits and vegetables. List the methods used to prevent it.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Mechanism (3 marks):**
>
> Enzymatic browning is catalyzed by the enzyme **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)**, also called tyrosinase, in the presence of molecular oxygen:
>
> $$\text{Phenol (substrate)} + O_2 \xrightarrow{PPO} \text{Quinone} \rightarrow \text{Melanin (brown pigment)}$$
>
> The reaction occurs in damaged tissues (cut apple, peeled potato). PPO uses Cu²⁺ as a cofactor. Substrates include tyrosine, chlorogenic acid, catechins.
>
> **Factors Affecting PPO Activity:**
> - pH optimum: 6.0–7.0
> - Temperature optimum: 25–35 °C (inactivated at >90 °C)
> - Requires O₂ and Cu²⁺
>
> **Prevention Methods (4 marks):**
>
> 1. **Thermal inactivation:** Blanching (100 °C, 3–5 min)
> 2. **Chemical inhibition:**
>    - **Sulphitation:** SO₂ (350–2000 ppm) — most effective; binds to PPO active site
>    - **Acidification:** Citric acid, ascorbic acid (lowers pH, chelates Cu²⁺)
>    - **Sodium chloride:** 1–2% (reduces O₂ solubility, inhibits PPO)
> 3. **Oxygen exclusion:** Vacuum packaging, immersion in water/syrup
> 4. **Use of inhibitors:** 4-Hexylresorcinol, EDTA
> 5. **Genetic/Varietal selection:** PPO-deficient cultivars (e.g., non-browning apple varieties)

---

### ⭐ Option A — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **post-harvest losses** in fruits and vegetables. What are the major causes and how can they be minimized?

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Magnitude of Losses (1 mark):**
> Post-harvest losses in India for fruits and vegetables range from **20% to 40%** — translating to ~INR 60,000 crore annually. Globally, ~14% of food is lost between harvest and retail (FAO).
>
> **Major Causes (4 marks):**
>
> 1. **Physiological Losses (Respiration & Transpiration)**
>    - Continues post-harvest → consumes sugars, produces CO₂ and heat
>    - Water loss → shrivelling, weight loss (5–10% weight loss makes produce unsaleable)
>
> 2. **Pathological Losses (Microbial Decay)**
>    - Fungi: *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*, *Botrytis*, *Alternaria*
>    - Bacteria: *Erwinia carotovora* (soft rot in vegetables), *Pseudomonas*
>    - Entry via wounds, natural openings (lenticels)
>
> 3. **Mechanical Damage**
>    - Bruising during harvest, transport, handling
>    - Creates entry points for pathogens, accelerates respiration
>
> 4. **Enzymatic Degradation**
>    - PPO → browning; PME → texture loss; Cellulase, Pectinase → softening
>
> 5. **Insect/Pest Infestation**
>    - Fruit flies, weevils, sucking pests
>
> 6. **Environmental Stress**
>    - Chilling injury in tropical fruits (banana, mango below 12 °C)
>    - Freezing injury; sunburn
>
> **Minimization Strategies (2 marks):**
>
> - **Pre-cooling:** Rapid field heat removal (hydro-cooling, forced-air cooling)
> - **Cold Chain Maintenance:** 0–4 °C, 90–95% RH throughout distribution
> - **MAP / CAP Storage:** Reduced O₂, elevated CO₂ atmosphere
> - **Edible coatings:** Chitosan, wax coatings reduce transpiration
> - **Improved handling:** Padded crates, gentle harvesting
> - **Irradiation:** 0.5–1 kGy for sprout inhibition
> - **Training & Infrastructure:** Especially for small farmers in Kerala (jackfruit, banana, mango)

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **osmotic dehydration** process. Compare it with hot air drying.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Principle (2 marks):**
> Osmotic dehydration (OD) involves immersing fruits/vegetables in a hypertonic solution (sugar or salt) → water flows out of the food due to osmotic pressure difference, while some solute enters the food. It is a **partial water removal** technique — typically achieves 30–50% moisture reduction.
>
> **Process Flow:**
> 1. Selection and sorting
> 2. Peeling and slicing (uniform 5–10 mm thick)
> 3. Pre-treatment (blanching/soaking optional)
> 4. Immersion in osmotic solution (60–70 °Brix sucrose or 10–15% NaCl) at 30–50 °C for 1–6 hours
> 5. Fruit-to-syrup ratio typically 1:4
> 6. Drain and rinse
> 7. Optional complementary drying (hot air or freeze)
>
> **Driving force:** Difference in water activity:
> $$\Delta a_w = a_w^{food} - a_w^{solution}$$
>
> **Comparison Table (4 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Osmotic Dehydration | Hot Air Drying |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Mechanism** | Osmotic pressure difference | Heat + mass transfer by convection |
> | **Temperature** | Mild (30–50 °C) | High (50–80 °C) |
> | **Energy Use** | Low (no phase change) | High (latent heat of vaporization) |
> | **Product Quality** | Better color, texture, vitamins | Heat damage, shrinkage, case hardening |
> | **Moisture Removal** | Partial (30–50%) | Complete (to <10%) |
> | **Shelf Life** | Intermediate (with complementary drying) | Long |
> | **Capital Cost** | Low | Moderate-High |
> | **Time** | 1–6 hours | 6–24 hours |
>
> **Engineering Significance (1 mark):**
> OD preserves heat-sensitive nutrients (vitamin C retention >90% vs ~60% in hot air drying). In Kerala, OD is used for **tropical fruits** like mango, pineapple, jackfruit bulbs to produce intermediate-moisture products.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusion between pH and $a_w$:** Students often mix up acidity and water activity. Remember — **pH** measures acid concentration; **$a_w$** measures available water. Vinegar (pH 3) has high $a_w$ (~0.99); honey (pH 4) has low $a_w$ (~0.55).
> 2. **Freezing vs. Chilling Injury:** Writing that all fruits should be stored at 0 °C is **WRONG**. Tropical fruits (banana, mango, papaya) suffer chilling injury below 12–13 °C.
> 3. **Canning Logic:** Stating "canning destroys all bacteria" is incomplete. **Commercial sterility** is the target, not absolute sterility — thermophilic spores may survive but cannot grow at storage temperatures.
> 4. **Formula Mistakes in Numerical Problems:** Forgetting that moisture content on **wet basis (w.b.)** and **dry basis (d.b.)** are different. KTU problems often test this conversion. Use:
> $$M_{w.b.} = \dfrac{M_{d.b.}}{1 + M_{d.b.}}$$
> 5. **Skipping Diagrams:** KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for neat flow diagrams in process questions. Always include one, even if rough.
> 6. **Ethylene Effects:** Failing to mention ethylene's role as a plant hormone — it accelerates ripening in climacteric fruits even at 0.1 ppm.
