- [x] Canning: selection → washing → peeling → blanching → filling → exhausting → sealing → thermal processing → cooling
- [x] Osmotic dehydration (sugar/salt solution)
- [x] Drying to $a_w < 0.60$ for shelf-stable products
- [x] MAP: 2–5% O₂, 5–10% CO₂, balance N₂

### ✅ Must-Know Chemistry
- [x] Enzymatic browning: Phenol + O₂ → Quinone → Melanin (via PPO enzyme)
- [x] Methods to prevent browning: heat, SO₂, citric acid, ascorbic acid, vacuum
- [x] Freezing point depression: $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$; for water, $K_f = 1.86$ K·kg/mol
- [x] Decimal reduction time (D-value) and z-value for thermal processing

### ✅ Must-Know Numericals (Practice These)
- [x] Moisture content conversion (w.b. ↔ d.b.)
- [x] Freezing point depression calculation
- [x] Drying efficiency computation
- [x] Respiration rate calculation
- [x] $F_0$ value calculation for sterilization

### ✅ Key Examples (Likely Short Notes)
- [x] Pectin and jelly formation (high- methoxyl pectin + sugar + acid)
- [x] Vinegar pickling of mango/lime (Kerala context)
- [x] Tender coconut water preservation
- [x] Banana chips (Kerala specialty — frying vs. drying)
- [x] Jackfruit processing (chips, bulbs, seeds)
- [x] Pineapple processing (juice, canned slices)

### ✅ Industry & Sustainability Connect
- [x] Kerala's spice/fruit processing industry (Kerala State Food Processing Mission)
- [x] Cold storage infrastructure gap in India
- [x] Food loss vs. food waste (post-harvest losses are quantitative; food waste is qualitative)
- [x] FSSAI standards for processed fruits and vegetables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:**
> In 14-mark answers, structure as: **Definition → Classification → Mechanism → Process Flow (with diagram) → Advantages/Limitations → Examples**. This matches KTU's marking scheme and ensures full marks allocation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimally Processed Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Minimally Processed Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimally Processed Products (MPPs)** are fruits, vegetables, or other raw plant materials that have been subjected to **mild physical treatments** (such as washing, peeling, trimming, cutting, slicing, or shredding) and packaged in a manner that maintains their **fresh-like, living state**, while ensuring **microbial safety, extended shelf life, and convenience of use**. They are also referred to in the literature as **"fresh-cut," "lightly processed," "partially processed,"** or **"ready-to-eat"** products.

According to the **IFT (Institute of Food Technologists)** and the **Codex Alimentarius guidelines** that KTU syllabi reference, the defining criteria of a minimally processed product are:

1. The product retains its **living cellular respiration** (it is alive — not blanched or cooked).
2. Processing involves **non-thermal or mild operations only** (no sterilization, no deep frying, no fermentation).
3. The product is **ready for direct consumption** or requires minimal kitchen preparation.
4. **Cold-chain integrity** is mandatory from processor to consumer.
5. Shelf life is **typically 5–21 days** under refrigeration (**2–5 °C**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Pre-Washed Salad Bag" Analogy** 🥗
>
> Imagine buying a sealed plastic bag of salad leaves from a supermarket. You open it, pour it onto a plate, and eat it directly — no washing, no chopping, no cooking. That bag of salad is a **Minimally Processed Product**.
>
> Compare this to:
> - 🥕 A whole, unpeeled carrot from a farm (raw commodity).
> - 🥫 A canned carrot (heavily processed, sterile, dead tissue).
> - 🥦 The salad bag in the middle — **just cleaned, cut, washed, and packed**. The cells are still alive and respiring, but it is convenient and safe.
>
> Minimally processed products sit **on a spectrum between raw agricultural produce and fully processed preserved food**. The processor's job is to keep the produce alive and fresh-looking for as long as possible, while keeping spoilage microbes and browning enzymes in check.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot

| Category | Examples | Typical Shelf Life | Storage Temp |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fresh-cut fruits | Watermelon cubes, pineapple chunks, melon balls | 5–10 days | 2–4 °C |
| Fresh-cut vegetables | Packaged salad mixes, shredded cabbage, carrot sticks | 7–14 days | 2–4 °C |
| Washed & whole | Whole washed apples, bell peppers | 14–21 days | 4–7 °C |
| Sprouted seeds | Mung bean sprouts, alfalfa sprouts | 5–7 days | 2–4 °C |
| Minimally processed tubers | Peeled & vacuum-packed potatoes | 7–10 days | 4–6 °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics (must be memorised for KTU ESE)**
> - **Recommended refrigeration temperature:** **2–5 °C**
> - **Maximum shelf life for MPPs:** **up to 21 days** (industry benchmark)
> - **Q₁₀ value for respiration of fresh produce:** typically **2.0–3.0**
> - **Surface sanitizer concentration (chlorine):** **50–200 ppm** free chlorine
> - **Modified Atmosphere Packaging target O₂:** **2–5 %**; **CO₂:** **5–15 %**; **N₂:** balance

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Minimal Processing

Minimal processing is built on **five engineering principles**. Understanding these is essential for both theory questions and the 14-mark ESE problems.

> **Pillar 1 — Gentle Unit Operations**
> Only mild physical steps are used. No heat sterilization, no high-pressure homogenization. Typical operations: chlorinated water washing, abrasive peeling, sharp blade slicing, centrifugal drying.

> **Pillar 2 — Microbial Reduction on the Cut Surface**
> Cutting ruptures cells, releasing **nutrients (sugars, proteins)** that become a feast for **spoilage bacteria and pathogens** (e.g., *Listeria monocytogenes*, *E. coli* O157:H7, *Salmonella*). Sanitation with **50–200 ppm chlorine** or alternatives (ozone, organic acids, electrolyzed water) is critical.

> **Pillar 3 — Browning and Texture Control**
> Cutting exposes **PPO (Polyphenol Oxidase)** enzyme to oxygen, causing **enzymatic browning**. Control strategies:
> - Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C) dip (**0.5–1 %**)
> - Citric acid (**0.5–2 %**)
> - Sodium erythorbate
> - Blanching (only for vegetables — not fruit, as it kills the "fresh-like" quality)

> **Pillar 4 — Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)**
> Replacing the in-pack atmosphere with a controlled gas mixture to slow respiration and inhibit aerobic spoilage microbes. Common gas composition: **2–5 % O₂, 5–15 % CO₂, balance N₂**.

> **Pillar 5 — Cold Chain Integrity**
> Temperature is the **single most important factor**. A rise from 4 °C to 10 °C roughly **doubles** the respiration rate and **quadruples** microbial growth rate. Strict unbroken refrigeration is non-negotiable.

## 2.2 Why Minimal Processing is Hard (The "Why" Behind the Steps)

| Phenomenon | Engineering Consequence | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Wounding stress (cutting) | ↑ Respiration rate, ↑ ethylene production | MAP with low O₂, refrigeration |
| Cellular leakage | Microbial substrate on cut surface | Chlorine wash, cold storage |
| PPO activity | Brown/black discoloration | Acid dips, ascorbic acid |
| Texture softening | Pectin breakdown by **PME & PG** enzymes | Calcium chloride dip (**0.5–1 %**) |
| Microbial proliferation | Reduced shelf life, food safety risk | Sanitizers + MAP + refrigeration |
| Moisture loss | Wilting, weight loss, shrivelling | Perforated or anti-fog films |

## 2.3 The Two Major Methods in MPP

### Method A: **Minimal Processing of Fruits**
- Wash → sort → peel (if needed) → core/seed removal → slice/cube → anti-browning dip → drain → **MAP pack** → refrigerate.
- Critical: anti-browning is **mandatory** because fruits are rich in PPO substrates (polyphenols).
- Calcium fortification of dip maintains firmness.

### Method B: **Minimal Processing of Vegetables**
- Wash → trim → cut/shred → sanitize (chlorine) → rinse → **mild blanching (optional, 60–90 °C for 1–2 min)** → cool → centrifuge dry → **MAP or vacuum pack** → refrigerate.
- Vegetables tolerate mild blanching because texture is **cellulose-based**, not pectin-based.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside any table cell to preserve Markdown table integrity.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Arrhenius Equation** (shelf-life prediction) | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R \cdot T)}$ | $k$ = rate constant, $A$ = pre-exponential, $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = temperature (K) | Predicts how reaction rates (respiration, browning, microbial growth) change with temperature |
| 2 | **Q₁₀ Temperature Coefficient** | $Q_{10} = \left( \dfrac{r_2}{r_1} \right)^{\dfrac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | $r_1, r_2$ = rates at $T_1, T_2$ (°C) | Quick rule: Q₁₀ ≈ 2–3 for produce; doubles rate per 10 °C rise |
| 3 | **Respiration Rate (Clausen & Workman model)** | $R = R_0 \cdot e^{\alpha \cdot T}$ | $R$ = CO₂ production (mL/kg·h), $R_0$ = baseline, $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient, $T$ = °C | Models O₂ consumption / CO₂ evolution of packaged produce |
| 4 | **Michaelis–Menten Respiration** | $R = \dfrac{V_{max} \cdot [O_2]}{K_m + [O_2]}$ | $V_{max}$ = max rate, $K_m$ = half-saturation constant (≈ 2–5 % O₂ for most produce) | Used to design optimal O₂ level in MAP |
| 5 | **Henry's Law for CO₂ solubility in tissue** | $C = k_H \cdot p_{CO_2}$ | $C$ = dissolved CO₂, $k_H$ = Henry's constant, $p_{CO_2}$ = partial pressure | Explains CO₂ inhibition of microbes inside MAP |
| 6 | **Decimal Reduction Time (D-value)** | $\log(N / N_0) = - t / D$ | $N$ = survivors, $N_0$ = initial, $t$ = time, $D$ = D-value (min) | Microbial inactivation kinetics during sanitization |
| 7 | **Water Activity Depression by MAP** | $a_w^{eff} \approx a_w \cdot f(O_2, CO_2)$ | $a_w$ = water activity, $f$ = empirical function of gas composition | Explains combined hurdle effect of MAP + refrigeration |
| 8 | **Heat Generation by Respiration** | $Q_{resp} = R \cdot \Delta H_{CO_2}$ | $Q_{resp}$ = W/kg, $\Delta H_{CO_2}$ ≈ 10.7 kJ/L CO₂ for glucose oxidation | Predicts self-heating inside bulk MPP packages |
| 9 | **Fick's First Law (Gas diffusion through film)** | $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | $J$ = flux, $D$ = diffusion coeff., $dC/dx$ = concentration gradient | Designs film permeability for MAP |
| 10 | **Modified Atmosphere Steady State** | $\dfrac{dO_2}{dt} = 0 \Rightarrow R_{O_2} = P_{film} \cdot (O_2^{ext} - O_2^{int})$ | $P_{film}$ = film permeability, $O_2^{ext}$ = external O₂ (≈ 21 %) | Sets target O₂ inside MAP package |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The MAP + refrigeration system is the **backbone of the global fresh-cut produce industry**, which is valued at over **USD 30 billion** worldwide. In India, companies like **Safal (Mother Dairy)**, **Choupik**, and **VegEx** produce minimally processed salads, peeled garlic, diced watermelon, and grated coconut — all reliant on the principles above. Cold-chain logistics companies (Snowman, ColdEx) support this segment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Connection to Other Modules**
> - **Module 1**: Food chemistry of browning (PPO) and texture (PME/PG).
> - **Module 2**: Water activity ($a_w$) and hurdle technology from preservation science.
> - **Module 4 (Packaging)**: Detailed MAP film selection and permeability engineering.
> - **Module 5 (Quality)**: Sensory, microbial, and nutritional testing of MPPs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shelf Life from Arrhenius + Q₁₀

**Problem:** A minimally processed lettuce has a shelf life of **14 days at 4 °C**. If it is accidentally stored at **10 °C**, what is the new shelf life? Assume $Q_{10} = 2.5$.

### Step 1 — Write the Q₁₀ relationship

The general Q₁₀ equation is:

$$
Q_{10} = \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{\dfrac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}
$$

where $k_1$ and $k_2$ are the deterioration rates at $T_1$ and $T_2$.

### Step 2 — Identify the temperatures

We are given:
- $T_1 = 4 \;°C$ (reference)
- $T_2 = 10 \;°C$ (accidental)
- $Q_{10} = 2.5$

### Step 3 — Solve for the rate ratio

Substitute the values:

$$
2.5 = \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{\dfrac{10}{10 - 4}}
$$

$$
2.5 = \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{\dfrac{10}{6}}
$$

$$
2.5 = \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{1.6667}
$$

Take natural log of both sides:

$$
\ln(2.5) = 1.6667 \cdot \ln\left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right)
$$

$$
0.9163 = 1.6667 \cdot \ln\left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right)
$$

$$
\ln\left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = 0.5498
$$

$$
\dfrac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{0.5498} \approx 1.733
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret: shelf life is **inversely proportional** to rate

Shelf life $\theta \propto 1/k$, so:

$$
\dfrac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = \dfrac{k_1}{k_2} = \dfrac{1}{1.733} \approx 0.577
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the new shelf life

$$
\theta_2 = 0.577 \times 14 \; \text{days} \approx 8.08 \; \text{days}
$$

> **Result:** A 6 °C rise in storage temperature **reduces shelf life from 14 days to ~8 days** — a **42 % loss**. This single calculation is the most important reason the cold chain must never break.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal O₂ Level in MAP (Steady-State Mass Balance)

**Problem:** Design the steady-state O₂ level inside a MAP package containing shredded carrots, where:
- Respiration follows Michaelis–Menten with $V_{max} = 12$ mL O₂/(kg·h) and $K_m = 3$ % O₂.
- Film permeability to O₂ is such that the steady-state equation holds.
- External O₂ = 21 %, target internal O₂ = 3 %.

### Step 1 — Set up the steady-state mass balance

At steady state, **O₂ entering through the film = O₂ consumed by respiration**:

$$
P_{film} \cdot (O_2^{ext} - O_2^{int}) = V_{max} \cdot \dfrac{[O_2^{int}]}{K_m + [O_2^{int}]}
$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate Michaelis–Menten at [O₂] = 3 %

$$
R = \dfrac{12 \cdot 3}{3 + 3} = \dfrac{36}{6} = 6 \; \text{mL O}_2 / (\text{kg} \cdot \text{h})
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for required film permeability

$$
P_{film} \cdot (21 - 3) = 6
$$

$$
P_{film} = \dfrac{6}{18} = 0.333 \; \text{mL O}_2 / (\text{kg} \cdot \text{h} \cdot \%)
$$

> **Interpretation:** The film must have a permeability of **0.333 mL O₂/(kg·h·%)** to maintain 3 % O₂ in steady state. This guides the packaging engineer in selecting films (e.g., **PE / PP / multilayer EVOH laminates**).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Decimal Reduction during Chlorine Sanitation

**Problem:** A shredded cabbage has an initial microbial load of $N_0 = 10^6$ CFU/g. It is dipped in **100 ppm chlorine** for **5 minutes**. The D-value at this chlorine concentration is $D = 1.5$ min. Calculate the final microbial load $N$.

### Step 1 — D-value equation

$$
\log\left( \dfrac{N_0}{N} \right) = \dfrac{t}{D}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$
\log\left( \dfrac{10^6}{N} \right) = \dfrac{5}{1.5} = 3.333
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for N

$$
\dfrac{10^6}{N} = 10^{3.333} \approx 2154
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{10^6}{2154} \approx 464 \; \text{CFU/g}
$$

> **Result:** A 5-minute 100 ppm chlorine dip reduces the microbial load from **10⁶ to ~464 CFU/g** — a **>3-log reduction**. This is the engineering basis for sanitizer concentration and contact time design.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: MPP Methods vs. Engineering Parameters

| Engineering Parameter | Fresh-Cut Lettuce | Fresh-Cut Apple | Peeled Carrot | Sprouts | Whole Washed Bell Pepper |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wash sanitizer** | 100 ppm Cl₂ | 50 ppm Cl₂ + 0.5 % citric | 100 ppm Cl₂ | 50 ppm Cl₂ | 50 ppm Cl₂ |
| **Anti-browning agent** | Not needed | 1 % ascorbic acid | Not needed | Not needed | Not needed |
| **Firming agent** | None | 0.5 % CaCl₂ | None | None | None |
| **Mild blanching** | No | No | Optional (1 min, 80 °C) | No | No |
| **MAP gas** | 3 % O₂, 10 % CO₂, 87 % N₂ | 2 % O₂, 5 % CO₂, 93 % N₂ | 5 % O₂, 10 % CO₂, 85 % N₂ | Air packaging | 5 % O₂, 5 % CO₂, 90 % N₂ |
| **Storage temp (°C)** | 2–4 | 1–4 | 2–4 | 2–4 | 4–7 |
| **Typical shelf life (days)** | 10–14 | 7–14 | 7–10 | 5–7 | 14–21 |
| **Main spoilage mechanism** | Browning + microbial | PPO browning | White blush + softening | Microbial | Wilting |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of MPP vs. Other Preservation Methods

| Property | Minimally Processed | Thermal Preservation (Canning) | Frozen | Fermented |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cell viability | **Living** | Dead | Dead (ice-damaged) | Dead |
| Texture | Fresh, crisp | Soft, cooked | Variable | Soft |
| Nutrient retention | High | Moderate (heat loss) | High (except vit. C) | Variable |
| Shelf life | 5–21 days | 1–5 years | 6–12 months | Weeks–months |
| Energy cost | Moderate (cold chain) | High (retort) | Very high (freezing) | Low |
| Use of chemicals | Mild (sanitizers, acids) | None | None | None |
| Major spoilage | Microbial, browning | None (sterile) | Freezer burn | Surface yeast |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — General Process Flow for MPP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Produce Intake] --> B[Sorting and Grading]
    B --> C[Pre-Washing in Chlorinated Water 50 to 100 ppm]
    C --> D[Peeling Trimming and Inspection]
    D --> E[Cutting Slicing or Shredding]
    E --> F[Sanitizing Wash 100 to 200 ppm Chlorine]
    F --> G[Anti-Browning Dip 0.5 to 1 percent Ascorbic Acid]
    G --> H[Optional Mild Blanching 60 to 90 deg C 1 to 2 min]
    H --> I[Cooling in Iced Water 0 to 4 deg C]
    I --> J[Centrifugal Drying]
    J --> K[Weighing and Portioning]
    K --> L[Modified Atmosphere Packaging MAP]
    L --> M[Metal Detection and Labelling]
    M --> N[Cold Storage 2 to 4 deg C]
    N --> O[Distribution via Refrigerated Truck]
    O --> P[Retail Display at 2 to 5 deg C]
    P --> Q[Consumer End Use]
```

> **Reading guide:** Each rectangular block is a unit operation. The arrows show the direction of material flow. Quality and cold-chain checkpoints can be inserted between any two blocks.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — MAP System Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Gas Supply System]
        G1[N2 Cylinder] --> MIX[Gas Mixer with Flow Meters]
        G2[O2 Cylinder] --> MIX
        G3[CO2 Cylinder] --> MIX
    end
    MIX --> ANA[Gas Analyzer verifies composition]
    ANA --> INJ[Gas Injection Nozzle into Package]
    subgraph PACK[Sealed MAP Package]
        INJ --> HEAD[Package Headspace Gases]
        PROD[Shredded Produce Inside] <-->|Respiration CO2 release O2 uptake| HEAD
    end
    HEAD --> FILM[Permeable Film O2 in CO2 out]
    FILM --> EXT[External Atmosphere 21 percent O2]
```

> **Engineering meaning:** The gas mixer sets the *initial* atmosphere; the steady-state atmosphere is governed by the **balance between produce respiration and film permeability**. Engineers tune either the film or the initial mix to hit the target $O_2^{int}$.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right MPP Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Choose MPP Strategy] --> Q1{Is the produce fruit or vegetable?}
    Q1 -->|Fruit| Q2{High PPO activity?}
    Q1 -->|Vegetable| Q3{Need firm texture?}
    Q2 -->|Yes apple banana mushroom| F1[Apply Ascorbic Acid plus Citric Acid Dip]
    Q2 -->|No watermelon melon| F2[Skip Anti Browning Use MAP with High CO2]
    Q3 -->|Yes carrot celery| V1[Calcium Chloride Dip 0.5 percent]
    Q3 -->|No leafy greens| V2[Skip Firming Use Sanitizer only]
    F1 --> END[Pack in MAP and Refrigerate at 2 to 4 deg C]
    F2 --> END
    V1 --> END
    V2 --> END
```

---

## 4.4 Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA) Block for MPP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FAIL[Critical Failure Modes in MPP] --> M1[Enzymatic Browning due to PPO]
    FAIL --> M2[Microbial Spoilage Listeria E coli]
    FAIL --> M3[Texture Softening due to PME and PG]
    FAIL --> M4[White Blush on cut carrot surface]
    FAIL --> M5[Off Odor from Anaerobic Respiration]
    M1 --> C1[Control Ascorbic Acid 1 percent plus Citric Acid 0.5 percent plus MAP]
    M2 --> C2[Control Chlorine 100 ppm plus Cold Chain plus MAP]
    M3 --> C3[Control Calcium Chloride 0.5 percent plus Refrigeration]
    M4 --> C4[Control Mild Blanching 80 deg C 1 min]
    M5 --> C5[Control Avoid below 1 percent O2 use balanced MAP]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define minimally processed products. List any four unit operations commonly used in their preparation.**

**Model Answer (12–15 lines):**
Minimally processed products (MPPs) are raw fruits or vegetables that have been subjected to **mild physical treatments** such as washing, peeling, slicing or shredding, and packaged under refrigeration to retain their **fresh-like quality** while ensuring safety and convenience. The product cells remain **alive and respiring**, distinguishing MPPs from canned or cooked products.

Four common unit operations are:
1. **Chlorinated water washing** (50–200 ppm free Cl₂) to reduce surface microbial load.
2. **Peeling and trimming** using abrasive or knife methods.
3. **Cutting/slicing/shredding** to desired particle size.
4. **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)** with controlled O₂, CO₂, and N₂ ratios.

Other optional steps include anti-browning dips, mild blanching, and centrifugal drying.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark characteristics + 1 mark unit ops)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Explain the role of Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) in extending the shelf life of fresh-cut produce.**

**Model Answer (12–15 lines):**
MAP replaces the air inside a package with a **controlled gas mixture**, typically **2–5 % O₂, 5–15 % CO₂, balance N₂**. Its shelf-life-extending roles are:

- **Low O₂ (2–5 %)** slows aerobic respiration of the living plant tissue and delays senescence.
- **Elevated CO₂ (5–15 %)** inhibits the growth of aerobic spoilage bacteria and molds (e.g., *Pseudomonas*, *Botrytis*).
- **N₂ as a filler** is inert and prevents package collapse.
- Combined with **refrigeration (2–5 °C)**, MAP can extend shelf life of fresh-cut produce from 3–5 days (unpacked) to **14–21 days**.

The gas composition is chosen such that the produce does not shift to **anaerobic respiration** (which causes off-odors and ethanol accumulation), so O₂ must not fall below **~1 %**.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark gas roles + 1 mark combined effect with cold chain)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> *As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer ONE of two choices. Each question is split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **principles and steps involved in the minimal processing of fresh-cut fruits**. Add a note on **enzymatic browning** and its control.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Discuss the **parameters to be controlled in Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)**. How do you arrive at the **optimum gas composition** for a given produce? Support with a suitable calculation.

---

### **Model Answer for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Principles and Steps for Fresh-Cut Fruits (7 marks)

**Principles (3 marks):**
1. The fruit must remain **alive and respiring** — no thermal kill.
2. **Microbial load** on cut surfaces must be reduced without harming tissue.
3. **Enzymatic browning** (PPO-mediated) must be controlled using chemical dips.
4. **Texture** must be preserved using calcium salts.
5. **Cold chain** must be unbroken.

**Process Steps (3 marks):**
1. **Receipt and sorting** — remove damaged, over-ripe fruits.
2. **Washing** in chlorinated water (50–100 ppm Cl₂).
3. **Peeling and coring** — manual or mechanical.
4. **Slicing/cubing** — sharp blades minimise cell damage.
5. **Anti-browning dip** — 1 % ascorbic acid + 0.5 % citric acid, 2–5 min.
6. **Calcium dip** — 0.5 % CaCl₂, 1–2 min (firmness).
7. **Drain and dry** — gentle centrifuge.
8. **MAP packaging** — typically 2 % O₂, 5 % CO₂, 93 % N₂.
9. **Refrigerated storage** at 2–4 °C.

**Note on Enzymatic Browning (1 mark):**
When cells are cut, **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)** enzyme contacts **phenolic substrates** and **O₂**, producing **o-quinones** that polymerise into **melanins** (brown pigments). Control: exclude O₂ via MAP, acidify tissue (citric acid, pH < 4), reduce PPO activity (ascorbic acid reduces quinones back to phenols), or use **4-hexylresorcinol** as a competitive inhibitor.

---

#### Part (b) — Parameters & Optimum Gas Composition in MAP (7 marks)

**Parameters Controlled (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| O₂ | 2–5 % | Below respiration threshold, slows senescence |
| CO₂ | 5–15 % | Inhibits spoilage microbes, suppresses ethylene perception |
| N₂ | Balance | Inert filler, prevents pack collapse |
| Temperature | 2–5 °C | Slows all rates by Q₁₀ ≈ 2–3 |
| Film permeability | Tuned to produce respiration rate | Maintains steady-state atmosphere |
| Package headspace | 1–2 mL/g produce | Determines how quickly gases equilibrate |
| Sanitizer residual | < 1 ppm Cl₂ in final product | Regulatory & sensory limit |

**Calculation of Optimum Composition (3 marks):**

For shredded carrots with $V_{max} = 12$ mL O₂/(kg·h), $K_m = 3$ % O₂, and chosen film permeability $P_{film} = 0.333$ mL O₂/(kg·h·%), the steady-state O₂ inside the pack is found from:

$$
0.333 \cdot (21 - O_2^{int}) = \dfrac{12 \cdot O_2^{int}}{3 + O_2^{int}}
$$

Solving iteratively with $O_2^{int} = 3$ %:
- LHS = $0.333 \times 18 = 6.0$
- RHS = $\dfrac{12 \times 3}{6} = 6.0$ ✓

Hence, **3 % O₂, 10 % CO₂, 87 % N₂** is the optimum MAP composition for shredded carrots at 4 °C.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Students often confuse **headspace gas composition** (the *initial* mix injected) with **steady-state gas composition** (what develops inside the pack after 24–48 h due to respiration). Marks are awarded only when the answer **clearly distinguishes** the two.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a **neat flowchart**, describe the **general process flow for minimal processing of vegetables**. Mention the role of **mild blanching** and **calcium chloride** in such products.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **minimally processed cabbage** has a shelf life of **12 days at 4 °C**. Compute its shelf life if stored at **12 °C**, given $Q_{10} = 2.2$. Comment on the engineering significance of the result for cold-chain design.

---

### **Model Answer for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Process Flow for Fresh-Cut Vegetables (7 marks)

**Flowchart (3 marks):**
Raw vegetable → Sorting → Pre-wash (50–100 ppm Cl₂) → Trim → Cut/shred → Sanitize (100–200 ppm Cl₂) → Rinse → **Mild blanch (optional, 80 °C, 1 min)** → Cool (iced water 0–4 °C) → Centrifuge dry → **Calcium chloride dip (0.5 %, 1 min)** → MAP pack (5 % O₂, 10 % CO₂, 85 % N₂) → Refrigerate (2–4 °C) → Distribute.

**Role of Mild Blanching (2 marks):**
- Inactivates **PPO** and **peroxidase** enzymes that cause browning.
- Reduces surface microbial load by 1–2 log cycles.
- Drives out intracellular gases, reducing oxidative browning.
- *Caution:* over-blanching (> 2 min at > 90 °C) destroys the "fresh-like" texture — defeats the purpose of MPP.

**Role of Calcium Chloride (2 marks):**
- Calcium ions **cross-link with pectin** in the middle lamella, forming **calcium pectate** that strengthens cell walls.
- Maintains **firmness and crispness** of cut vegetables (carrot, celery, potato).
- Inhibits **pectin methyl esterase (PME)** softening action.
- Typical dip: 0.5–1 % CaCl₂ for 1–5 minutes at room temperature.

---

#### Part (b) — Q₁₀ Shelf Life Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $\theta_1 = 12$ days at $T_1 = 4 \;°C$
- $T_2 = 12 \;°C$
- $Q_{10} = 2.2$

**Step 1 — Write the Q₁₀ relation (1 mark):**
$$
Q_{10} = \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{\dfrac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (1 mark):**
$$
2.2 = \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{\dfrac{10}{8}} = \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{1.25}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the rate ratio (2 marks):**
$$
\ln(2.2) = 1.25 \cdot \ln\left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right)
$$

$$
0.7885 = 1.25 \cdot \ln\left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right)
$$

$$
\ln\left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = 0.6308
$$

$$
\dfrac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{0.6308} \approx 1.879
$$

**Step 4 — Shelf life is inversely proportional to rate (1 mark):**
$$
\dfrac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = \dfrac{k_1}{k_2} = \dfrac{1}{1.879} \approx 0.532
$$

**Step 5 — Compute new shelf life (1 mark):**
$$
\theta_2 = 0.532 \times 12 \approx 6.39 \; \text{days}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Significance (1 mark):**
An 8 °C rise from 4 °C to 12 °C **halves the shelf life** of minimally processed cabbage. This justifies the **strict cold-chain requirement** of $T \leq 5 \;°C$ for MPPs. Even short breaks in refrigeration (e.g., 4 hours at retail loading docks in Kerala summer) can cumulatively cause **spoilage and food-safety risks**. The design implication: every link in the cold chain must be monitored with **time-temperature indicators (TTIs)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> - A common error is **forgetting to invert the rate ratio** to get the shelf-life ratio. Always remember: $\theta \propto 1/k$, so shelf life *decreases* when rate *increases*.
> - Another common error is **mixing °C and K** in the Arrhenius equation. Use Kelvin for $T$ in $e^{-E_a/RT}$ and Celsius for $T$ in $Q_{10}$.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Minimally Processed Products**

- **Definition** — MPPs are *living* fresh produce subjected to *mild* physical operations and kept under refrigeration.
- **Scope** — Includes fresh-cut fruits, fresh-cut vegetables, washed whole produce, and sprouted seeds.
- **Core philosophy** — Keep cells **alive**; **delay** senescence; **suppress** microbes and browning; **never break** the cold chain.
- **Five pillars** — (1) Gentle unit ops, (2) Sanitation, (3) Browning control, (4) MAP, (5) Cold chain.
- **Key chemicals** — Chlorine 50–200 ppm, Ascorbic acid 0.5–1 %, Citric acid 0.5–2 %, CaCl₂ 0.5–1 %.
- **Standard MAP gas** — **2–5 % O₂, 5–15 % CO₂, balance N₂** (exact values produce-specific).
- **Standard storage temperature** — **2–5 °C** (industry cold-chain spec).
- **Typical shelf life** — **5–21 days** depending on produce and treatment.
- **Q₁₀ for produce** — typically **2.0–3.0**; **doubles** rate per 10 °C rise.
- **Arrhenius equation** — $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/(RT)}$ — used to predict shelf life at any temperature.
- **Michaelis–Menten respiration** — $R = V_{max}[O_2]/(K_m + [O_2])$ — used to design optimal MAP.
- **D-value equation** — $\log(N_0/N) = t/D$ — used to design sanitizer contact time.
- **Browning chemistry** — PPO + O₂ + phenols → o-quinones → melanin (brown pigment).
- **Browning controls** — Ascorbic acid (reducing), Citric acid (pH), MAP (low O₂), 4-HR (inhibitor), mild blanch (vegetables only).
- **Texture softener** — PME & PG enzymes; **controlled by** CaCl₂ dip.
- **White blush on carrot** — surface lignification; controlled by **mild blanching** (80 °C, 1 min).
- **Critical DO NOTs**:
  - ❌ Do not let O₂ fall below **1 %** → anaerobic respiration → off-odors.
  - ❌ Do not exceed **5 °C** in storage — Q₁₀ risk.
  - ❌ Do not over-blanch — destroys fresh-like quality.
  - ❌ Do not skip sanitation — pathogens (*Listeria*, *E. coli*) love cut surfaces.
- **Industrial examples** — Pre-cut salad mixes, peeled garlic, diced watermelon, grated coconut, packaged sprouts.
- **Key Indian brands** — Safal (Mother Dairy), Choupik, VegEx, Freshtrop, Sam Agritech.
- **Standard references (Codex/ISO)** — Codex Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CAC/RCP 53-2003); ISO 21807 (microbiology of fresh produce).
- **Distinguish carefully**:
  - MPP vs Canning — MPP is **living**; canned is **sterile and dead**.
  - MPP vs Frozen — MPP is **refrigerated and fresh**; frozen has **ice-damaged cells**.
  - MAP vs Vacuum — MAP **replaces** gas mix; vacuum **removes** air only.
- **Exam mantra** — Always pair every claim with a number (e.g., "shelf life 14 days at 4 °C, Q₁₀ 2.5"). Vague answers lose marks in KTU evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dairy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dairy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Dairy Processing?

**Dairy Processing** is the branch of food engineering that deals with the treatment of raw milk and its conversion into a wide range of safe, shelf-stable, and consumable dairy products through a sequence of physical, thermal, biochemical, and microbiological operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Dairy processing encompasses the industrial handling of milk — from reception at the dairy plant, through standardization, pasteurization, homogenization, separation, fermentation, and packaging — to the manufacture of fluid milk, butter, cheese, yogurt, ghee, milk powder, and other value-added dairy products.*

In the KTU 2024 framework, "Dairy" as a topic under **Module 3 (Processing of Specific Food Commodities)** focuses on the *unit operations* applied to milk and the *principles* behind the manufacture of major dairy products.

---

## 1.2 Why Study Dairy? — Intuitive Analogy

**Real-World Analogy: "Milk is Nature's Most Complete Raw Material"**

Imagine milk as a **"chemical factory in a glass"** — it is the only single food that nature designed specifically to *completely nourish a newborn mammal* in the first months of life. Because of this, milk contains:

| Milk Component | Analogy (Plain English) |
|----------------|------------------------|
| **Water (~87%)** | The "solvent" — the river in which everything else is dissolved/suspended. |
| **Lactose (4.8%)** | The "fuel" — the carbohydrate that provides energy. |
| **Fat (3.5–5%)** | The "energy reserve" — also carries fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, K. |
| **Proteins (3.2%)** | The "building blocks" — primarily **casein** (curds) and **whey** proteins. |
| **Minerals (0.7%)** | The "regulators" — calcium, phosphorus for bones. |
| **Vitamins** | The "spark plugs" — co-factors for metabolism. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Hook:** Because milk is *perishable* (it spoils within hours at room temperature) and *nutrient-rich for bacteria*, dairy processing is essentially a **race against microbial spoilage** combined with a *value-addition strategy* to convert surplus milk into stable products (cheese, powder, ghee) that can be stored and traded.

---

## 1.3 Standard Composition of Cow's Milk (KTU High-Yield Data)

The following is the *standard reference composition* every KTU student must memorize:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Cow's Milk Composition (per 100 g)**
> - **Water:** **87.0 g**
> - **Total Solids (TS):** **13.0 g**
> - **Solids-Not-Fat (SNF):** **9.0 g**
> - **Fat:** **4.0 g** (varies 3.5–5.0 %)
> - **Protein:** **3.4 g** (casein ~2.7 g + whey ~0.6 g)
> - **Lactose:** **4.8 g**
> - **Ash (Minerals):** **0.7 g**
> - **Energy:** ~**62 kcal** per 100 mL

The *Food Safety and Standards Authority of India* (FSSAI) and the *Prevention of Food Adulteration Act (PFA)* legally define milk standards in India.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Dairy Products

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 3 — Product Categories You Must Know**

**1. Fluid / Market Milk Products**
- Toned milk, double-toned milk, standardized milk, full-cream milk, skim milk.

**2. Fermented / Cultured Products**
- Yogurt (Dahi), Lassi, Buttermilk (Chaas), Kefir, Cheese.

**3. Concentrated Products**
- Sweetened Condensed Milk (SCM), Evaporated Milk, Khoa.

**4. Dried / Dehydrated Products**
- Skim Milk Powder (SMP), Whole Milk Powder (WMP), Infant Formula, Whey Powder.

**5. Fat-Rich Products**
- Butter, Ghee, Cream (table cream, whipping cream), Butter Oil (Anhydrous Milk Fat).

**6. Frozen Products**
- Ice cream, Frozen yogurt, Kulfi.

**7. By-Products**
- Whey, Casein, Lactose, Whey Protein Concentrate (WPC).

---

## 1.5 Flow of Milk — From Farm to Consumer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The complete chain of custody is called the **"Cold Chain"** — every break in the cold chain compromises quality.

**Step 1:** Milking at farm → **Step 2:** Immediate cooling to **4 °C** → **Step 3:** Insulated tanker transport → **Step 4:** Reception dock at dairy plant → **Step 5:** Quality testing (acidity, fat%, SNF%, sediment) → **Step 6:** Chilling/Storage → **Step 7:** Processing (pasteurization, homogenization, separation) → **Step 8:** Packaging → **Step 9:** Cold distribution → **Step 10:** Consumer refrigerator.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Unit Operations in Dairy Processing

The dairy industry uses a *sequence of unit operations*, each governed by specific engineering principles.

### 2.1.1 Reception and Chilling
- Raw milk is tested for **acidity** (titratable acidity, normal range **0.13–0.16 % lactic acid**), **fat %**, **SNF %**, **specific gravity** (1.028–1.032 at 15.6 °C), and **microbial load** (Standard Plate Count < 1×10⁵ CFU/mL for good quality).
- It is then chilled using **plate heat exchangers (PHE)** to **4 °C or below** to suppress bacterial growth.

### 2.1.2 Filtration and Clarification
- Removes **sediment, somatic cells, dust, and foreign particles**.
- A **clarifier** (centrifugal separator) operates at **6,000–8,000 rpm** and uses the difference in specific gravity to remove impurities.

### 2.1.3 Standardization
- Adjusting the **fat-to-SNF ratio** of milk to a target legal standard (e.g., **3.5 % fat + 8.5 % SNF** for standardized milk in India).
- Done mathematically using the **Pearson Square Method** or by an **in-line proportional blender**.

### 2.1.4 Cream Separation
- Uses a **centrifugal cream separator** based on Stoke's law.
- **Cream** (lighter, fat-rich, SG ~ 0.99) rises to the centre, while **skim milk** (heavier, fat-free, SG ~ 1.035) is thrown outward.
- **Separation efficiency** depends on:
  - Disc bowl **angular velocity (ω)**
  - Disc **radius (r)**
  - **Viscosity (μ)** of milk
  - **Density difference (Δρ)** between fat and skim phases

### 2.1.5 Pasteurization
The most critical safety step — eliminates **pathogenic vegetative bacteria** (especially *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, *Brucella*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria*) while preserving nutritional/sensory quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legal Pasteurization Standards (KTU Favourite Question)**
> - **LTLT (Low-Temperature Long-Time):** **63 °C for 30 min** (Batch, "Holder" method)
> - **HTST (High-Temperature Short-Time):** **72 °C for 15 sec** (Continuous, plate exchanger)
> - **UHT (Ultra-High Temperature):** **138–142 °C for 2–4 sec** (Sterilization — gives shelf-stable milk)
> - **Thermization:** 57–68 °C for 15–20 s (sub-pasteurization to extend raw milk shelf life)

**Why 72 °C / 15 s?**
- The thermal death point of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* is approximately **60 °C / 30 min** or equivalently **72 °C / 15 s** (by thermal death time kinetics — z-value of the organism is ~7 °C in milk). The HTST standard is set with a **5-log reduction safety margin** beyond this.

### 2.1.6 Homogenization
- Reduces **fat globule size** from **3–8 μm** down to **< 1 μm** (uniformly).
- Done at pressures of **150–250 bar (15–25 MPa)** in a high-pressure homogenizer.
- **Purpose:** Prevent creaming (fat separation), improve whiteness, mouthfeel, and digestibility.
- **Mechanism:** Milk is forced through a narrow valve gap (10–20 μm). Turbulent flow, cavitation, and shear forces rupture fat globules.

### 2.1.7 Fermentation (for Yogurt, Cheese, Buttermilk)
- **Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB)** ferment lactose → lactic acid.
- The drop in pH (from ~6.7 to ~4.5) causes **casein micelles to coagulate** → gel network forms.
- Common starter cultures:
  - *Streptococcus thermophilus* + *Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus* (yogurt)
  - *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis* and *cremoris* (cheese)
  - *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* (buttermilk)

### 2.1.8 Chilling and Packaging
- Pasteurized milk is immediately cooled to **4 °C** to arrest bacterial growth.
- Packaging is in **glass bottles, LDPE pouches, Tetra Pak (multi-layer cartons), or PET bottles** — all designed for the cold chain.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the *core numerical relationships* tested in KTU examinations. Memorize these units and constants — they are the most frequently asked derivations.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Key | Typical Unit | Used For |
|---|------------------------|------------|--------------|----------|
| 1 | **TS = Fat + SNF** | TS = Total Solids, SNF = Solids-Not-Fat | % by mass | Composition check |
| 2 | **SNF % = (0.25 × LR reading) + (0.22 × Fat %) + 0.72** | LR = Lactometer Reading at 27 °C (corrected) | % | SNF estimation |
| 3 | **Corrected LR = LR + (Temp °C − 27) × 0.2** | For temperatures other than 27 °C | — | Temperature correction |
| 4 | **Pearson Square (Standardization)** | Mass ratios solved by cross-multiplication | kg of cream / skim | Fat % adjustment |
| 5 | **Stoke's Law (Cream separation)** $v = \dfrac{d^{2}\,(\Delta\rho)\,g}{18\,\mu}$ | $v$ = terminal velocity, $d$ = globule diameter, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\mu$ = viscosity | m/s | Separation theory |
| 6 | **Centrifugal force** $F_c = m\,\omega^{2}\,r$ | $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $r$ = radius | N | Separator bowl design |
| 7 | **Pasteurization: 72 °C / 15 s** | HTST standard | — | Microbial kill |
| 8 | **D-value** (decimal reduction time) | Time to kill 90 % of a microbial population at a given T | min | Sterilization kinetics |
| 9 | **z-value** (thermal resistance) | °C change to alter D-value by 10× | °C | Thermal process design |
| 10 | **F₀ value** (sterilization equivalent) | Equivalent minutes at 121.1 °C (250 °F) | min | UHT / in-container sterilization |
| 11 | **Homogenization pressure** $P_{h}$ | Typical 15–25 MPa | MPa | Globule size reduction |
| 12 | **Moisture in Butter** = **≤ 16 %** (FSSAI legal) | Quality standard | % | Butter grading |
| 13 | **Yield of Cheese** ≈ **10 kg milk → 1 kg hard cheese** (rule of thumb) | Mass basis | kg | Process economics |
| 14 | **Ghee yield** ≈ **25 kg cream (40 % fat) → 1 kg ghee** | Mass basis | kg | Fat concentration |
| 15 | **Energy for pasteurization** $Q = m\,c_p\,\Delta T$ | $c_p$ of milk ≈ **3.93 kJ/(kg·K)** | kJ | Heat exchanger design |

---

## 2.3 The Pearson Square Method — Worked Reference

The Pearson Square (also called the **Pearson's Box** or **Rectangle**) is the **standard KTU method** to compute the proportions of two milk streams (e.g., whole milk and skim milk) needed to obtain a target fat %.

**General Procedure:**
1. Place the **desired fat %** at the centre of the square.
2. Place the **fat % of the two streams** at the **left corners**.
3. Subtract diagonally (always take **larger − smaller**).
4. The numbers on the right are the **mass parts** of each stream.

**Example — Target 3.0 % fat from 4.5 % whole milk and 0.5 % skim milk:**

```
            4.5 % (Whole milk)
                  |
        +---------+---------+
        |         |         |
    3.0 % (Target)         |
        |         |         |
        +---------+---------+
                  |
            0.5 % (Skim milk)
```

- Diagonal difference 1:  $\vert 3.0 - 0.5 \vert = 2.5$ parts of **whole milk**
- Diagonal difference 2:  $\vert 4.5 - 3.0 \vert = 1.5$ parts of **skim milk**
- **Total parts** = $2.5 + 1.5 = 4.0$ parts
- To prepare 100 kg of standardized milk: use $62.5$ kg whole milk + $37.5$ kg skim milk.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Dairy processing is one of the **largest industries globally** (worth ~USD 900 billion in 2024). In India, Operation Flood (the White Revolution, 1970–1996) transformed India into the world's **largest milk producer** (~230 million tonnes/year). Every operation in the dairy chain — from milking machines, plate heat exchangers, centrifugal separators, homogenizers, spray dryers, and CIP (Clean-In-Place) systems — is governed by **food engineering principles** (heat transfer, mass transfer, fluid mechanics, kinetics) that a B.Tech student must master.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: SNF Estimation Using the Lactometer Reading

The **Quevenne Lactometer** is a hydrometer calibrated to read correctly at **15.6 °C (60 °F)**. In India, the lactometer is usually calibrated for **27 °C**. The relationship between specific gravity and solids is empirically derived.

**Given:**

- Lactometer reading (LR) at temperature $T$
- Fat % of milk
- Specific gravity of milk $SG$

**Empirical formula (Richer-Gossling form):**

$$
\text{SNF \%} = \frac{\text{LR}_{27} \times 0.25 + \text{Fat \%} \times 0.22 + 0.72}{1}
$$

For temperatures other than 27 °C, **add 0.2 LR units for every 1 °C above 27**, or subtract similarly below:

$$
\text{LR}_{\text{corrected}} = \text{LR}_{\text{observed}} + (T - 27) \times 0.2
$$

**Worked Numerical — KTU Style:**

A milk sample at **30 °C** shows a lactometer reading of **28**. Fat % by Gerber method is **4.0 %**. Find the **SNF %**.

**Step 1 — Temperature Correction**

$$
\text{LR}_{\text{corrected}} = 28 + (30 - 27) \times 0.2 = 28 + 0.6 = 28.6
$$

**Step 2 — Apply SNF formula**

$$
\text{SNF \%} = (28.6 \times 0.25) + (4.0 \times 0.22) + 0.72
$$

$$
\text{SNF \%} = 7.15 + 0.88 + 0.72 = 8.75 \,\%
$$

**Step 3 — Total Solids**

$$
\text{TS} = \text{Fat \%} + \text{SNF \%} = 4.0 + 8.75 = 12.75 \,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A normal cow milk should have SNF between **8.5–9.0 %** and TS between **12.5–14.5 %**. Values below this suggest **adulteration with water**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Standardization Using the Algebraic Method (Cross-Verification of Pearson Square)

For a deeper, equation-based derivation (often asked as 7-mark problem):

**Let:**
- $m_1$ = mass of stream 1 (e.g., whole milk) with fat $F_1$
- $m_2$ = mass of stream 2 (e.g., skim milk) with fat $F_2$
- $m_3$ = mass of standardized milk with target fat $F_3$

**Two governing equations:**

$$
m_1 + m_2 = m_3
$$

$$
m_1 \, F_1 + m_2 \, F_2 = m_3 \, F_3
$$

**Solve for $m_1$ and $m_2$:**

$$
m_1 = m_3 \times \frac{F_3 - F_2}{F_1 - F_2}
$$

$$
m_2 = m_3 \times \frac{F_1 - F_3}{F_1 - F_2}
$$

**Worked Numerical:**

Prepare **500 kg** of standardized milk at **3.5 % fat** from **whole milk (4.2 %)** and **skim milk (0.1 %)**.

$$
m_1 = 500 \times \frac{3.5 - 0.1}{4.2 - 0.1} = 500 \times \frac{3.4}{4.1} = 414.6 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_2 = 500 \times \frac{4.2 - 3.5}{4.2 - 0.1} = 500 \times \frac{0.7}{4.1} = 85.4 \,\text{kg}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
414.6 + 85.4 = 500 \,\text{kg} \;\;\checkmark
$$

$$
\text{Fat balance: } (414.6 \times 4.2 + 85.4 \times 0.1) / 500 = 1750 / 500 = 3.5 \,\% \;\;\checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat Load in an HTST Pasteurizer

In an HTST plant, raw milk is heated from $T_1$ to $T_2$ in a **plate heat exchanger**, using hot water as the heating medium.

**Energy balance:**

$$
Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, (T_2 - T_1)
$$

**Where:**
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate of milk (kg/s)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of milk ≈ **3.93 kJ/(kg·K)**
- $T_2 - T_1$ = temperature rise

**Worked Numerical:**

A dairy plant pasteurizes milk at a rate of **10,000 L/h**. Milk enters at **6 °C** and is heated to **72 °C**. Calculate the **heat duty (kW)** required.

**Step 1 — Convert flow to mass flow**

$$
\dot{m} = 10{,}000 \,\frac{\text{L}}{\text{h}} \times 1.03 \,\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{L}} = 10{,}300 \,\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}} = 2.86 \,\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}}
$$

(Density of milk ≈ 1.03 kg/L)

**Step 2 — Apply energy balance**

$$
Q = 2.86 \times 3.93 \times (72 - 6)
$$

$$
Q = 2.86 \times 3.93 \times 66
$$

$$
Q = 741.8 \,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical allowance**

Add **10 %** for heat losses:

$$
Q_{\text{design}} = 741.8 \times 1.10 \approx 815.9 \,\text{kW}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Centrifugal Separator — Separation Factor

A cream separator uses **centrifugal force** instead of gravity. The ratio of centrifugal force to gravitational force is called the **separation factor (Z)**:

$$
Z = \frac{F_c}{F_g} = \frac{m \, \omega^{2} \, r}{m \, g} = \frac{\omega^{2} \, r}{g}
$$

For a typical milk separator:
- Bowl radius $r = 0.15$ m
- Rotational speed $N = 7{,}000$ rpm
- $\omega = 2\pi N / 60 = 2\pi \times 7000 / 60 = 733$ rad/s

$$
Z = \frac{733^2 \times 0.15}{9.81} = \frac{80{,}617}{9.81} \approx 8{,}218
$$

This means the **centrifugal force is ~8,200 times gravity**, allowing rapid separation of fat from skim milk.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Adulterants in Milk and Their Detection (KTU Favourite)

| Adulterant | How Added | Detection Method | Legal Status |
|------------|-----------|------------------|--------------|
| **Water** | Increases volume | ↓ Lactometer reading, ↓ Fat, ↓ SNF, ↓ Freezing Point | Illegal (FSSAI) |
| **Starch** (cheap filler) | Increases TS | Iodine test (blue-black color) | Illegal |
| **Urea** | Raises apparent SNF | DMAB (para-dimethyl amino benzaldehyde) test | Illegal |
| **Synthetic milk** | Detergent + urea + oil + starch | Detergent foam test, urea test, oil test | Highly illegal |
| **Formalin / H₂O₂** | Preservative, extends shelf life | Sulphuric acid + ferric chloride test, starch-KI test | Illegal (toxic) |
| **Skim milk powder + vegetable oil** (reconstituted) | Imitation of whole milk | Reichert-Meissl value, fatty acid profile | Legal only if labelled |
| **Melamine** (nitrogen fraud) | Falsely raises "protein" | HPLC-MS, colorimetric assay | Illegal (toxic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Question Alert:** "Discuss common adulterants of milk and their detection." — This is a 7- or 14-mark classic. Master the table above.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Problem Set (Practice for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Try these problems yourself** — model answers are provided below each.

### Problem A — Ghee Yield
A dairy plant processes **1,000 kg of cream** with **40 % fat** into ghee. If the ghee yield (mass efficiency) is **92 %** of the fat content, calculate the **kg of ghee produced**.

**Model Solution:**

$$
\text{Fat in cream} = 1000 \times 0.40 = 400 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Ghee} = 400 \times 0.92 = 368 \,\text{kg}
$$

### Problem B — Paneer Yield
**100 kg** of milk with **3.5 % fat + 8.5 % SNF** is converted to paneer (Indian cottage cheese) with **70 %** yield on the casein + fat basis. Casein is **80 %** of total protein. Find the **kg of paneer**.

**Model Solution:**

$$
\text{Total protein} = 100 \times 0.034 = 3.4 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Casein} = 3.4 \times 0.80 = 2.72 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Fat} = 100 \times 0.035 = 3.5 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Casein + Fat} = 2.72 + 3.5 = 6.22 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Paneer} = 6.22 \times 0.70 = 4.354 \,\text{kg}
$$

### Problem C — Energy for Spray Drying Milk Powder
**500 kg/h** of milk concentrate (**50 % TS**) is spray-dried to powder with **3 % moisture**. Find the **kg/h of powder** and the **water to be evaporated**.

**Model Solution:**

$$
\text{Solids in feed} = 500 \times 0.50 = 250 \,\text{kg/h}
$$

Powder has 3 % moisture → 97 % solids:

$$
\text{Powder} = 250 / 0.97 = 257.7 \,\text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\text{Water evaporated} = 500 - 257.7 = 242.3 \,\text{kg/h}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete HTST Pasteurization Plant

The following schematic illustrates the **interactions of equipment** in a typical HTST pasteurization line. Mermaid's *graph TD* syntax is used to ensure all node IDs are alphanumeric and non-reserved.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Raw Milk Reception Dock] --> A2[Chilled Storage Tank 4 degC]
    A2 --> A3[Milk Silo Buffer Tank]
    A3 --> A4[Centrifugal Clarifier]
    A4 --> A5[Plate Heat Exchanger Regeneration Section]
    A5 --> A6[Heating Section Hot Water 72 degC]
    A6 --> A7[Holding Tube 15 sec at 72 degC]
    A7 --> A8[Flow Diversion Valve FDV]
    A8 -->|Acceptable| A9[Regeneration Cooling Section]
    A8 -->|Re divert | A3
    A9 --> A10[Chilled Water Cooling to 4 degC]
    A10 --> A11[Homogenizer 200 bar]
    A11 --> A12[Packaging Machine Pouch or Carton]
    A12 --> A13[Cold Storage 4 degC Dispatch]
    H1[Hot Water Boiler 85 degC] --> A6
    W1[Chilled Water Unit 1 degC] --> A10
    CIP[CIP Clean In Place Loop] -.-> A5
    CIP -.-> A6
    CIP -.-> A9
    CIP -.-> A10
    CIP -.-> A11
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** is the legal safety device — if milk does not reach 72 °C, it automatically diverts flow back to the raw milk silo. This is **mandatory** in HTST plants.
- The **regeneration section** recovers heat from already-pasteurized milk to pre-warm incoming milk → **saves up to 85 % of energy**.
- **CIP (Clean-In-Place)** is a closed-loop circulation of cleaning solutions (NaOH 1–2 %, HNO₃ 0.5–1 %) without dismantling the plant.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Butter & Ghee Manufacturing

```mermaid
graph TD
    B1[Whole Milk] --> B2[Centrifugal Cream Separator]
    B2 --> B3[Cream 40 percent fat]
    B2 --> B4[Skim Milk By product]
    B3 --> B5[Pasteurization 72 degC 15 s]
    B5 --> B6[Cooling to 4 degC Aging 4 to 8 hours]
    B6 --> B7[Churning in Butter Churn]
    B7 --> B8[Butter Grains plus Buttermilk]
    B8 --> B9[Butter 80 percent fat]
    B8 --> B10[Buttermilk By product]
    B9 --> B11[Working and Salting]
    B11 --> B12[Packaged Butter]
    B9 --> B13[Heat Clarification 110 to 120 degC]
    B13 --> B14[Sediment Removal]
    B14 --> B15[Moisture Removal Final Stage]
    B15 --> B16[Clarified Ghee 99.5 percent fat]
    B16 --> B17[Cooling and Packaging]
```

**Engineering Notes:**
- **Churning** is a *phase inversion* process — water-in-oil emulsion (cream) → oil-in-water emulsion (butter). The fat globule membranes rupture, releasing liquid fat which entraps water and air.
- **Ghee** manufacture is essentially **moisture removal** from butter until < 0.5 % moisture remains, plus thermal development of flavour compounds (lactones, methyl ketones, free fatty acids).

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cheese Manufacturing

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1[Standardized Pasteurized Milk] --> C2[Addition of Starter Culture Lactococcus]
    C2 --> C3[Incubation 30 to 32 degC for 30 to 45 min]
    C3 --> C4[Addition of Rennet Enzyme Chymosin]
    C4 --> C5[Curd Formation Coagulation 30 to 45 min]
    C5 --> C6[Cutting of Curd into Cubes]
    C6 --> C7[Cooking and Stirring 38 to 40 degC]
    C7 --> C8[Whey Drainage]
    C8 --> C9[Curd Hooping Moulding]
    C9 --> C10[Pressing to Remove Whey]
    C10 --> C11[Salting Brine or Dry Salt]
    C11 --> C12[Ripening Aging 1 to 12 months]
    C12 --> C13[Packaged Cheese]
    C8 --> C14[Whey By product for WPC or Lactose]
```

**Engineering Notes:**
- **Rennet** cleaves the $\kappa$-casein from the casein micelle → calcium bridges form between micelles → gel (coagulum).
- **Cutting** controls the rate of whey expulsion — smaller cubes = drier curd.
- **Ripening** is a controlled *biochemical maturation* by residual enzymes, starter bacteria, and moulds (in blue cheese). pH, moisture, salt, and temperature define the final cheese variety.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Yogurt Manufacturing

```mermaid
graph TD
    Y1[Standardized Milk 3.5 percent fat 8.5 percent SNF] --> Y2[Fortification Skim Milk Powder 2 to 3 percent]
    Y2 --> Y3[Homogenization 200 bar 65 degC]
    Y3 --> Y4[Heat Treatment 85 to 95 degC for 5 to 10 min]
    Y4 --> Y5[Cooling to 42 to 45 degC]
    Y5 --> Y6[Inoculation Streptococcus thermophilus plus Lactobacillus bulgaricus]
    Y6 --> Y7[Incubation 42 degC for 4 to 6 hours until pH 4.5]
    Y7 --> Y8[Cooling to 5 degC to Arrest Fermentation]
    Y8 --> Y9[Fruit Addition and Packaging]
```

**Engineering Notes:**
- The **high heat treatment (85–95 °C)** denatures whey proteins → they complex with casein → firmer, smoother gel (less syneresis).
- **Streptococcus thermophilus** grows first (faster), lowering pH; the **lactobacilli** then take over, producing acetaldehyde (the characteristic yogurt aroma).

---

## 4.5 Comparative Architecture: Batch vs. Continuous Processing

| Process | Batch (e.g., Cheese Vat) | Continuous (e.g., HTST Milk) |
|---------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Capacity** | Low (1000–5000 L) | High (10,000–50,000 L/h) |
| **Labour** | High | Low (automated) |
| **Control** | Manual, operator skill | PLC / SCADA controlled |
| **CIP** | Difficult (open vessels) | Easy (closed loop) |
| **Uniformity** | Batch-to-batch variation | High uniformity |
| **Capital cost** | Low | High |
| **Energy** | Inefficient | Heat recovery possible |
| **Example products** | Paneer, artisanal cheese | Fluid milk, ice cream mix, SMP |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define pasteurization. List the legal time-temperature combinations for LTLT and HTST pasteurization.**

**Model Answer:**
Pasteurization is the process of heating every particle of milk or milk product to a specific temperature for a definite time, such that it destroys all pathogenic vegetative microorganisms and most of the spoilage organisms, with minimal chemical, physical, and organoleptic changes.

- **LTLT:** **63 °C for 30 minutes** (Holder method, batch process)
- **HTST:** **72 °C for 15 seconds** (continuous, plate heat exchanger)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is the difference between homogenization and pasteurization?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Homogenization | Pasteurization |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Purpose** | Reduce fat globule size | Kill pathogens |
| **Process** | Mechanical (high pressure) | Thermal (heat treatment) |
| **Conditions** | 150–250 bar, 60–70 °C | 72 °C / 15 s (HTST) or 63 °C / 30 min (LTLT) |
| **Effect on microbes** | None | Lethal |
| **Effect on fat** | Physical breakdown of globules | No change in globule size |
| **Equipment** | High-pressure homogenizer | Plate Heat Exchanger |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **composition of milk** in detail, listing the major constituents and their typical percentage ranges. Explain why milk is considered a **complete food**. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **steps in the manufacture of cheese** from standardized milk. Mention the role of rennet and starter culture. (7 marks)

**Model Answer (a):**

Milk is a complex colloidal system composed of:
- **Water (87.0 %)** — the continuous phase
- **Fat (3.5–5.0 %)** — present as an **emulsion** of fat globules (diameter 3–8 μm) coated with a milk fat globule membrane (MFGM)
- **Proteins (3.2–3.5 %)** — present as a **colloidal suspension** of casein micelles (80 %) and a true solution of whey proteins (20 %)
- **Lactose (4.5–5.0 %)** — present as a **true solution** in the aqueous phase; the primary carbohydrate
- **Minerals/Ash (0.7–0.8 %)** — calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, sodium, potassium
- **Vitamins** — both water-soluble (B-complex, C) and fat-soluble (A, D, E, K)

**Milk as a complete food:** It contains *all essential nutrients* required for growth and development of the neonate — proteins of high biological value, all essential amino acids, balanced fats with essential fatty acids, lactose as an energy source, calcium and phosphorus in a 1.2:1 ratio ideal for bone growth, and a wide spectrum of vitamins.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **casein** with **whey protein**. Casein is *precipitable* at pH 4.6; whey remains in solution. Don't write "milk contains casein and albumin" — albumin is a minor component. Use "**whey proteins (β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin)**" instead.

**Model Answer (b):**

*(See Block Diagram 4.3 for the flow.)*

The steps are:
1. **Standardization and pasteurization** of milk (optional for raw-milk cheeses).
2. **Starter culture addition** — *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis* and *cremoris* ferment lactose → lactic acid → pH drops to 6.0–6.3 → favours rennet action and curd formation.
3. **Rennet addition** — chymosin (or microbial coagulant) cleaves $\kappa$-casein → loss of stabilizing layer → casein micelles aggregate → **coagulum** forms in 30–45 min at 30 °C.
4. **Cutting** the curd into 1–2 cm cubes releases **whey**.
5. **Cooking and stirring** at 38–40 °C expels more whey and reduces moisture.
6. **Whey drainage**.
7. **Hooping and pressing** — moulds the curd and removes residual whey.
8. **Salting** — controls microbial growth, enhances flavour, draws out more moisture.
9. **Ripening** — biochemical maturation by enzymes (lipase, protease), producing flavour and texture characteristic of the variety (Cheddar 3–12 months; Parmesan 12–36 months).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Marks are *deducted* if you do not mention the **role of rennet** (cleaves $\kappa$-casein) and **role of starter culture** (produces lactic acid). Both are compulsory sub-points for full marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU Style]

**(a)** What is **standardization of milk**? Explain the **Pearson Square method** with a suitable example. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **principle, working, and applications of a centrifugal cream separator**. State Stoke's law. (7 marks)

**Model Answer (a):**

**Standardization** is the process of adjusting the fat content of milk to a desired legal level by mixing high-fat and low-fat streams.

**Pearson Square Method** (see Section 2.3) — a graphical tool to find the proportions of two streams.

**Example:** To prepare **1000 kg** of standardized milk at **3.5 % fat** from whole milk (4.2 %) and skim milk (0.1 %):

Place desired fat in centre: 3.5
Top-left: 4.2 (whole); Bottom-left: 0.1 (skim)

- Diagonal 1 (top-right) = $3.5 - 0.1 = 3.4$ parts of **whole milk**
- Diagonal 2 (bottom-right) = $4.2 - 3.5 = 0.7$ parts of **skim milk**
- Total parts = 3.4 + 0.7 = 4.1

$$
\text{Whole milk} = 1000 \times \frac{3.4}{4.1} = 829.3 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Skim milk} = 1000 \times \frac{0.7}{4.1} = 170.7 \,\text{kg}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to put the *higher* value on the *bottom* left of the square. Always place the streams in a way that the diagonal differences are *positive*. The parts of the top stream equal the **bottom diagonal difference**, and vice versa.

**Model Answer (b):**

**Principle of Centrifugal Cream Separator:** It works on the principle of **density difference** between fat globules (~0.93 g/cm³) and skim milk (~1.035 g/cm³). Under high centrifugal force (separation factor ~5000–10,000 × g), the heavier skim milk moves outward and the lighter cream moves towards the centre.

**Working:**
- Milk enters the rotating bowl (6,000–8,000 rpm) through a central feed.
- A stack of conical **discs** (60–120 discs, gap ~0.4 mm) shortens the settling distance → rapid separation.
- Cream exits through the top outlet; skim milk exits through the bottom outlet.
- The fat content of cream can be controlled by the **cream screw** (back-pressure).

**Stoke's Law:**

$$
v = \frac{d^{2}\,(\Delta\rho)\,g}{18\,\mu}
$$

In a centrifuge, the gravity $g$ is replaced by $\omega^{2} r$, so the settling velocity becomes:

$$
v_c = \frac{d^{2}\,(\Delta\rho)\,\omega^{2} r}{18\,\mu}
$$

**Applications:**
- Separation of **cream** and **skim milk**.
- **Clarification** of milk (removal of sediment and somatic cells).
- **Bactofugation** (removal of bacterial spores, especially *Bacillus cereus*).
- Standardization of fat % in continuous in-line systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write **"centrifugal force"** as the answer to "principle". The *correct* principle is **density difference under high centrifugal force**, not centrifugal force itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Reference Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **Standard milk composition:** Water 87 %, Fat 4 %, SNF 9 %, TS 13 %, Lactose 4.8 %, Protein 3.4 %, Ash 0.7 %.
- **Legal pasteurization:** LTLT 63 °C/30 min; HTST 72 °C/15 s; UHT 138–142 °C/2–4 s.
- **HTST** uses a **plate heat exchanger** with a **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** as a legal safety device.
- **Homogenization:** 150–250 bar, fat globule size reduces from 3–8 μm → < 1 μm.
- **Pearson Square** is a graphical method to compute proportions for **standardization**.
- **Stoke's Law** governs cream separation in a centrifugal separator: $v = d^{2} \Delta\rho g / 18\mu$.
- **Centrifugal separation factor** $Z = \omega^{2} r / g$ — typically 5000–10,000 in milk separators.
- **SNF formula:** $SNF = 0.25 \times LR_{27} + 0.22 \times Fat + 0.72$ (LR corrected to 27 °C).
- **Rennet** cleaves $\kappa$-casein → curd formation. **Starter culture** produces lactic acid.
- **Yogurt culture:** *S. thermophilus* + *L. bulgaricus*. **Cheese culture:** *Lactococcus* spp.
- **Cheese ripening** is biochemical — enzymes, pH, moisture, salt define variety.
- **Ghee** manufacture: butter → clarification (110–120 °C) → moisture removal → < 0.5 % moisture.
- **Spray drying** for milk powder: feed with 50 % TS → powder with 3 % moisture.
- **Adulterants** in milk: water (low LR), starch (iodine test), urea (DMAB), formalin, melamine.
- **FSSAI legal SNF:** min **8.5 %** for cow milk; min **3.5 % fat** for standardized milk.
- **Engineering energy balance** for pasteurizer: $Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$, with $c_p \approx 3.93$ kJ/(kg·K).
- **Butter** contains ≥ 80 % fat, ≤ 16 % moisture (FSSAI).
- **Cold chain** must be maintained at **4 °C ± 2 °C** from reception to consumer.
- **CIP** (Clean-In-Place) uses NaOH (1–2 %) and HNO₃ (0.5–1 %) for closed-loop cleaning.
- **India** is the **world's largest milk producer** (~230 MT/year) — Operation Flood context is a KTU viva favourite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Mantra for KTU Viva / Exam:**
> *"Milk is a colloidal system of fat-in-water emulsion, casein colloidal suspension, and lactose solution. Pasteurize at 72/15, homogenize at 200 bar, separate by density, ferment with LAB, and keep the cold chain alive at 4 °C."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flesh Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flesh Foods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flesh Foods (Perishable Animal-Derived Foods):**
> Flesh foods are edible, protein-rich animal tissues obtained from the muscular flesh of livestock (cattle, sheep, goat, pig), poultry (chicken, turkey, duck), and aquatic animals (fish, crustaceans, mollusks). They are characterized by high biological value protein (containing all essential amino acids), high moisture content (**~60–80%**), high perishability, and susceptibility to microbial, enzymatic, and oxidative spoilage.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, flesh foods are studied under the umbrella of **perishable, high-water-activity (a<sub>w</sub> ≈ 0.99)** foods that require immediate post-slaughter preservation to maintain quality and ensure food safety.

## 1.2 Classification of Flesh Foods

| Class | Examples | Muscle Myoglobin | Typical Color |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Red Meat** | Beef, mutton, pork, venison | High | Bright cherry-red to dark red |
| **White Meat** | Chicken, turkey, rabbit, veal (young) | Low | Pale pink to off-white |
| **Fish & Seafood** | Salmon, tuna, prawns, oysters, squid | Variable (high in tuna) | White, pink, or red |
| **Game Meat** | Deer, boar, pheasant | Very high | Dark red / purplish |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The distinction between *red* and *white* meat is **NOT** based on the color of the cooked meat, but on the **concentration of myoglobin** in the muscle at the time of slaughter. A working muscle (leg of a chicken) has more myoglobin than a breast muscle.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Living Battery":**
> Think of an animal's muscle as a **biological rechargeable battery**. While the animal is alive, the muscle is constantly being "charged" with energy (glycogen → ATP) and "discharged" for movement (ATP → motion). When the animal is slaughtered, the "charger" (blood supply and oxygen) is cut off, but the battery keeps discharging until it dies completely. The discharge path is **glycogen → lactic acid**, and the dead battery state is called **rigor mortis**. Understanding this "battery discharge" concept unlocks nearly all post-slaughter biochemistry you'll meet in KTU exams.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Flesh Food Science

1. **Structure** — How muscle is built (fibers, connective tissue, fat).
2. **Composition** — What is inside (water, protein, fat, vitamins, minerals).
3. **Post-mortem Change** — What happens after death (rigor mortis, aging, spoilage).

These three pillars are the recurring motif for every Part B (14-mark) question on this topic in KTU ESE papers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Composition of Flesh Foods (Approximate, per 100 g edible portion)

| Component | Beef (lean) | Chicken (breast) | Fish (lean) | Pork (lean) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water (g) | 70–75 | 74 | 70–80 | 70–75 |
| Protein (g) | 20–22 | 22–24 | 18–22 | 20–22 |
| Fat (g) | 4–8 | 1–3 | 1–5 | 4–10 |
| Minerals (g) | 1.0 | 1.0 | 1.2 | 1.0 |
| Energy (kcal) | 150–220 | 110–165 | 80–150 | 150–220 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point for KTU:** Flesh foods are the **most bioavailable source of heme iron (Fe²⁺)**, vitamin B<sub>12</sub>, zinc, and complete protein. The heme iron has absorption efficiency of **15–35%**, compared to only **2–20%** for non-heme plant iron — a frequently asked comparison question.

## 2.2 Structure of Skeletal Muscle (The "Building Block")

Skeletal muscle is the primary edible portion. A whole muscle is built in a hierarchical fashion:

1. **Muscle** → bundle of **fasciculi**
2. **Fasciculi** → bundles of **muscle fibers (cells)**
3. **Muscle fiber** → multinucleated cell (**10–100 µm diameter, 1–30 cm length**)
4. **Myofibril** → contractile unit inside the fiber
5. **Sarcomere** → repeating unit of **actin (thin)** and **myosin (thick)** filaments

### 2.2.1 Connective Tissue Components (Crucial for Tenderness)

| Component | Location | Solubility | Effect on Tenderness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Epimysium** | Outer muscle sheath | Insoluble | Tough |
| **Perimysium** | Around fasciculi | Slightly soluble on heating | Binds fiber bundles |
| **Endomysium** | Around each fiber | Soluble on prolonged heating | Affects texture |
| **Collagen → Gelatin** | All three layers | Converts to gelatin on moist heat (>70 °C) | Tenderizes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Collagen content directly dictates cooking method:**
> - **High collagen** (e.g., shank, chuck, brisket) → **moist, slow cooking** (braising, stewing).
> - **Low collagen** (e.g., tenderloin, pork loin) → **dry, fast cooking** (grilling, roasting).

## 2.3 Muscle Proteins — Three Functional Groups

| Group | Examples | % of Total Protein | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sarcoplasmic (water-soluble)** | Myoglobin, enzymes, hemoglobin | ~30–35% | Color, metabolism |
| **Myofibrillar (salt-soluble)** | Actin, myosin, tropomyosin | ~50–55% | Contraction, texture |
| **Stromal/Connective (insoluble)** | Collagen, elastin, reticulin | ~10–15% | Structural support |

## 2.4 Post-Mortem Biochemistry — The Core of Flesh Food Science

After slaughter, the muscle undergoes three sequential phases:

### Phase 1: Pre-Rigor (Immediately after death)
- Circulation stops → no O₂ supply.
- Cell shifts to **anaerobic glycolysis**.
- Muscle is **soft, pliable, and stretchable** (ideal for hot-boning in ham processing).

### Phase 2: Rigor Mortis (Stiffening)
- ATP reserves deplete → actin-myosin cross-bridges **cannot detach**.
- Muscle becomes **rigid, inextensible**.
- Occurs within **2–6 hours** in poultry, **6–12 hours** in beef, **12–24 hours** in pork.

### Phase 3: Post-Rigor (Aging / Resolution of Rigor)
- Proteolytic enzymes (**cathepsins, calpains**) degrade myofibrillar proteins.
- Muscle becomes **tender again** — this is the basis of **dry/wet aging** in meat industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Trick:** "Why is meat tough immediately after rigor mortis but tender after aging?"
> **Answer skeleton:** During rigor, actin and myosin are permanently cross-linked. Endogenous proteases (cathepsins from lysosomes and calpains activated by Ca²⁺) selectively cleave Z-disc proteins (e.g., titin, nebulin) and degrade myofibrillar integrity, restoring tenderness over **7–28 days** (beef dry aging) or **4–7 days** (wet aging at 0–4 °C).

## 2.5 pH Decline Curve — Quantitative Anchor

Muscle pH is the single most important post-mortem quality indicator.

| Stage | Time | pH | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **At slaughter** | 0 h | **7.0–7.2** (live muscle) | Pre-rigor |
| **Normal decline endpoint** | 24 h | **5.4–5.7** | Normal rigor |
| **PSE (Pale, Soft, Exudative)** | <1 h | pH < 5.8 while still hot | Stress before slaughter |
| **DFD (Dark, Firm, Dry)** | 24 h | pH > 6.0 | Exhausted glycogen pre-slaughter |

> [!WARNING]
> **DFD meat** has high ultimate pH (>6.0) → closer to isoelectric point of proteins is NOT reached → water-holding capacity INCREASES → dark color, sticky surface, **highly susceptible to microbial spoilage** (short shelf life). Common in stressed beef and young bulls.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Relation | Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Water-Holding Capacity (WHC)** $\propto \dfrac{1}{\vert pH - pI \vert}$ | pI ≈ 5.4 for myofibrillar proteins | Lowest WHC at pH 5.4 |
| 2 | **PSE condition:** pH<sub>1h</sub> < **5.8** | pH measured 1 h post-mortem | Indicator of pre-slaughter stress |
| 3 | **DFD condition:** pH<sub>24h</sub> > **6.0** | pH measured 24 h post-mortem | Glycogen-depleted animal |
| 4 | **Heat shortening:** onset < **10 °C** pre-rigor | Cold-induced contraction | Avoid in hot-boned meat |
| 5 | **Cold shortening threshold** | Length reduction up to **50%** of sarcomere | Tough meat |
| 6 | **Thaw rigor** | Muscle frozen pre-rigor → thaw → extreme contraction | Severe drip loss |
| 7 | **Collagen → Gelatin** transition | Occurs at **>70 °C** in presence of water | Time-temperature dependent |
| 8 | **Maillard Reaction onset** | Temperature **>140 °C**, low a<sub>w</sub>, reducing sugars + amines | Crust browning on grilling |
| 9 | **Microbial doubling time at 4 °C** | Psychrotrophs: **6–12 h**; Mesophiles: inhibited | Determines chilled shelf life |
| 10 | **Heme Iron % in flesh foods** | Beef ≈ 60–70% heme; Chicken ≈ 30–40% heme | Bioavailability advantage |

## 2.7 Preservation Methods for Flesh Foods (One-Line Anchors)

| Method | Core Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Chilling (0–4 °C)** | Inhibits mesophiles | Short-term (3–7 days fish; 1–3 weeks red meat) |
| **Freezing (-18 to -40 °C)** | Stops microbial & enzymatic activity | Long-term (months) |
| **Curing (NaCl + NaNO₂)** | Lowers a<sub>w</sub> + antimicrobial + color fixation | Ham, bacon, corned beef |
| **Smoking** | Antimicrobial phenols + drying | Sausages, fish |
| **Thermal processing (canning)** | Commercial sterilization (F<sub>0</sub> ≥ 3 min) | Tuna, sardines, corned beef |
| **Vacuum / MAP packaging** | Inhibits aerobic spoilage bacteria | Retail cuts |
| **Irradiation** | DNA damage in microbes (1–7 kGy) | Patis, chicken, spices |
| **High-Pressure Processing (HPP)** | 400–600 MPa, inactivates microbes | Cooked meats, oysters |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Part B:** If asked "Discuss preservation methods for flesh foods," always link each method to **one specific spoilage mechanism** (microbial, enzymatic, or oxidative) — this is how examiners award full marks.

---
