- $PHL = \dfrac{M_{lost}}{M_{produced}} \times 100$ (Post-Harvest Loss).
- $VA = \dfrac{V_{processed} - V_{raw}}{V_{raw}} \times 100$ (Value Addition).

### H. One-Line Industry Buzzwords for Marks
- **"Farm-to-Fork"** — entire F&V value chain.
- **"Operation Greens TOP to ALL"** — extended from 3 to 23 perishables.
- **"ODOP — One District One Product"** — under PMFME.
- **"Mega Food Park"** — flagship PMKSY infrastructure.
- **"PLISFPI"** — Production Linked Incentive Scheme for Food Processing Industry.
- **"Cold Chain — the missing link"** — most-cited F&V processing challenge.
- **"From Plate to Plough"** — NITI Aayog's flagship food policy column.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dairy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dairy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Dairy Processing?

**Dairy Processing** is the branch of food engineering that deals with the treatment of raw milk and its conversion into a wide range of safe, shelf-stable, and consumable dairy products through a sequence of physical, thermal, biochemical, and microbiological operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Dairy processing encompasses the industrial handling of milk — from reception at the dairy plant, through standardization, pasteurization, homogenization, separation, fermentation, and packaging — to the manufacture of fluid milk, butter, cheese, yogurt, ghee, milk powder, and other value-added dairy products.*

In the KTU 2024 framework, "Dairy" as a topic under **Module 3 (Processing of Specific Food Commodities)** focuses on the *unit operations* applied to milk and the *principles* behind the manufacture of major dairy products.

---

## 1.2 Why Study Dairy? — Intuitive Analogy

**Real-World Analogy: "Milk is Nature's Most Complete Raw Material"**

Imagine milk as a **"chemical factory in a glass"** — it is the only single food that nature designed specifically to *completely nourish a newborn mammal* in the first months of life. Because of this, milk contains:

| Milk Component | Analogy (Plain English) |
|----------------|------------------------|
| **Water (~87%)** | The "solvent" — the river in which everything else is dissolved/suspended. |
| **Lactose (4.8%)** | The "fuel" — the carbohydrate that provides energy. |
| **Fat (3.5–5%)** | The "energy reserve" — also carries fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, K. |
| **Proteins (3.2%)** | The "building blocks" — primarily **casein** (curds) and **whey** proteins. |
| **Minerals (0.7%)** | The "regulators" — calcium, phosphorus for bones. |
| **Vitamins** | The "spark plugs" — co-factors for metabolism. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Hook:** Because milk is *perishable* (it spoils within hours at room temperature) and *nutrient-rich for bacteria*, dairy processing is essentially a **race against microbial spoilage** combined with a *value-addition strategy* to convert surplus milk into stable products (cheese, powder, ghee) that can be stored and traded.

---

## 1.3 Standard Composition of Cow's Milk (KTU High-Yield Data)

The following is the *standard reference composition* every KTU student must memorize:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Cow's Milk Composition (per 100 g)**
> - **Water:** **87.0 g**
> - **Total Solids (TS):** **13.0 g**
> - **Solids-Not-Fat (SNF):** **9.0 g**
> - **Fat:** **4.0 g** (varies 3.5–5.0 %)
> - **Protein:** **3.4 g** (casein ~2.7 g + whey ~0.6 g)
> - **Lactose:** **4.8 g**
> - **Ash (Minerals):** **0.7 g**
> - **Energy:** ~**62 kcal** per 100 mL

The *Food Safety and Standards Authority of India* (FSSAI) and the *Prevention of Food Adulteration Act (PFA)* legally define milk standards in India.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Dairy Products

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 3 — Product Categories You Must Know**

**1. Fluid / Market Milk Products**
- Toned milk, double-toned milk, standardized milk, full-cream milk, skim milk.

**2. Fermented / Cultured Products**
- Yogurt (Dahi), Lassi, Buttermilk (Chaas), Kefir, Cheese.

**3. Concentrated Products**
- Sweetened Condensed Milk (SCM), Evaporated Milk, Khoa.

**4. Dried / Dehydrated Products**
- Skim Milk Powder (SMP), Whole Milk Powder (WMP), Infant Formula, Whey Powder.

**5. Fat-Rich Products**
- Butter, Ghee, Cream (table cream, whipping cream), Butter Oil (Anhydrous Milk Fat).

**6. Frozen Products**
- Ice cream, Frozen yogurt, Kulfi.

**7. By-Products**
- Whey, Casein, Lactose, Whey Protein Concentrate (WPC).

---

## 1.5 Flow of Milk — From Farm to Consumer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The complete chain of custody is called the **"Cold Chain"** — every break in the cold chain compromises quality.

**Step 1:** Milking at farm → **Step 2:** Immediate cooling to **4 °C** → **Step 3:** Insulated tanker transport → **Step 4:** Reception dock at dairy plant → **Step 5:** Quality testing (acidity, fat%, SNF%, sediment) → **Step 6:** Chilling/Storage → **Step 7:** Processing (pasteurization, homogenization, separation) → **Step 8:** Packaging → **Step 9:** Cold distribution → **Step 10:** Consumer refrigerator.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Unit Operations in Dairy Processing

The dairy industry uses a *sequence of unit operations*, each governed by specific engineering principles.

### 2.1.1 Reception and Chilling
- Raw milk is tested for **acidity** (titratable acidity, normal range **0.13–0.16 % lactic acid**), **fat %**, **SNF %**, **specific gravity** (1.028–1.032 at 15.6 °C), and **microbial load** (Standard Plate Count < 1×10⁵ CFU/mL for good quality).
- It is then chilled using **plate heat exchangers (PHE)** to **4 °C or below** to suppress bacterial growth.

### 2.1.2 Filtration and Clarification
- Removes **sediment, somatic cells, dust, and foreign particles**.
- A **clarifier** (centrifugal separator) operates at **6,000–8,000 rpm** and uses the difference in specific gravity to remove impurities.

### 2.1.3 Standardization
- Adjusting the **fat-to-SNF ratio** of milk to a target legal standard (e.g., **3.5 % fat + 8.5 % SNF** for standardized milk in India).
- Done mathematically using the **Pearson Square Method** or by an **in-line proportional blender**.

### 2.1.4 Cream Separation
- Uses a **centrifugal cream separator** based on Stoke's law.
- **Cream** (lighter, fat-rich, SG ~ 0.99) rises to the centre, while **skim milk** (heavier, fat-free, SG ~ 1.035) is thrown outward.
- **Separation efficiency** depends on:
  - Disc bowl **angular velocity (ω)**
  - Disc **radius (r)**
  - **Viscosity (μ)** of milk
  - **Density difference (Δρ)** between fat and skim phases

### 2.1.5 Pasteurization
The most critical safety step — eliminates **pathogenic vegetative bacteria** (especially *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, *Brucella*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria*) while preserving nutritional/sensory quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legal Pasteurization Standards (KTU Favourite Question)**
> - **LTLT (Low-Temperature Long-Time):** **63 °C for 30 min** (Batch, "Holder" method)
> - **HTST (High-Temperature Short-Time):** **72 °C for 15 sec** (Continuous, plate exchanger)
> - **UHT (Ultra-High Temperature):** **138–142 °C for 2–4 sec** (Sterilization — gives shelf-stable milk)
> - **Thermization:** 57–68 °C for 15–20 s (sub-pasteurization to extend raw milk shelf life)

**Why 72 °C / 15 s?**
- The thermal death point of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* is approximately **60 °C / 30 min** or equivalently **72 °C / 15 s** (by thermal death time kinetics — z-value of the organism is ~7 °C in milk). The HTST standard is set with a **5-log reduction safety margin** beyond this.

### 2.1.6 Homogenization
- Reduces **fat globule size** from **3–8 μm** down to **< 1 μm** (uniformly).
- Done at pressures of **150–250 bar (15–25 MPa)** in a high-pressure homogenizer.
- **Purpose:** Prevent creaming (fat separation), improve whiteness, mouthfeel, and digestibility.
- **Mechanism:** Milk is forced through a narrow valve gap (10–20 μm). Turbulent flow, cavitation, and shear forces rupture fat globules.

### 2.1.7 Fermentation (for Yogurt, Cheese, Buttermilk)
- **Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB)** ferment lactose → lactic acid.
- The drop in pH (from ~6.7 to ~4.5) causes **casein micelles to coagulate** → gel network forms.
- Common starter cultures:
  - *Streptococcus thermophilus* + *Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus* (yogurt)
  - *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis* and *cremoris* (cheese)
  - *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* (buttermilk)

### 2.1.8 Chilling and Packaging
- Pasteurized milk is immediately cooled to **4 °C** to arrest bacterial growth.
- Packaging is in **glass bottles, LDPE pouches, Tetra Pak (multi-layer cartons), or PET bottles** — all designed for the cold chain.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the *core numerical relationships* tested in KTU examinations. Memorize these units and constants — they are the most frequently asked derivations.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Key | Typical Unit | Used For |
|---|------------------------|------------|--------------|----------|
| 1 | **TS = Fat + SNF** | TS = Total Solids, SNF = Solids-Not-Fat | % by mass | Composition check |
| 2 | **SNF % = (0.25 × LR reading) + (0.22 × Fat %) + 0.72** | LR = Lactometer Reading at 27 °C (corrected) | % | SNF estimation |
| 3 | **Corrected LR = LR + (Temp °C − 27) × 0.2** | For temperatures other than 27 °C | — | Temperature correction |
| 4 | **Pearson Square (Standardization)** | Mass ratios solved by cross-multiplication | kg of cream / skim | Fat % adjustment |
| 5 | **Stoke's Law (Cream separation)** $v = \dfrac{d^{2}\,(\Delta\rho)\,g}{18\,\mu}$ | $v$ = terminal velocity, $d$ = globule diameter, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\mu$ = viscosity | m/s | Separation theory |
| 6 | **Centrifugal force** $F_c = m\,\omega^{2}\,r$ | $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $r$ = radius | N | Separator bowl design |
| 7 | **Pasteurization: 72 °C / 15 s** | HTST standard | — | Microbial kill |
| 8 | **D-value** (decimal reduction time) | Time to kill 90 % of a microbial population at a given T | min | Sterilization kinetics |
| 9 | **z-value** (thermal resistance) | °C change to alter D-value by 10× | °C | Thermal process design |
| 10 | **F₀ value** (sterilization equivalent) | Equivalent minutes at 121.1 °C (250 °F) | min | UHT / in-container sterilization |
| 11 | **Homogenization pressure** $P_{h}$ | Typical 15–25 MPa | MPa | Globule size reduction |
| 12 | **Moisture in Butter** = **≤ 16 %** (FSSAI legal) | Quality standard | % | Butter grading |
| 13 | **Yield of Cheese** ≈ **10 kg milk → 1 kg hard cheese** (rule of thumb) | Mass basis | kg | Process economics |
| 14 | **Ghee yield** ≈ **25 kg cream (40 % fat) → 1 kg ghee** | Mass basis | kg | Fat concentration |
| 15 | **Energy for pasteurization** $Q = m\,c_p\,\Delta T$ | $c_p$ of milk ≈ **3.93 kJ/(kg·K)** | kJ | Heat exchanger design |

---

## 2.3 The Pearson Square Method — Worked Reference

The Pearson Square (also called the **Pearson's Box** or **Rectangle**) is the **standard KTU method** to compute the proportions of two milk streams (e.g., whole milk and skim milk) needed to obtain a target fat %.

**General Procedure:**
1. Place the **desired fat %** at the centre of the square.
2. Place the **fat % of the two streams** at the **left corners**.
3. Subtract diagonally (always take **larger − smaller**).
4. The numbers on the right are the **mass parts** of each stream.

**Example — Target 3.0 % fat from 4.5 % whole milk and 0.5 % skim milk:**

```
            4.5 % (Whole milk)
                  |
        +---------+---------+
        |         |         |
    3.0 % (Target)         |
        |         |         |
        +---------+---------+
                  |
            0.5 % (Skim milk)
```

- Diagonal difference 1:  $\vert 3.0 - 0.5 \vert = 2.5$ parts of **whole milk**
- Diagonal difference 2:  $\vert 4.5 - 3.0 \vert = 1.5$ parts of **skim milk**
- **Total parts** = $2.5 + 1.5 = 4.0$ parts
- To prepare 100 kg of standardized milk: use $62.5$ kg whole milk + $37.5$ kg skim milk.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Dairy processing is one of the **largest industries globally** (worth ~USD 900 billion in 2024). In India, Operation Flood (the White Revolution, 1970–1996) transformed India into the world's **largest milk producer** (~230 million tonnes/year). Every operation in the dairy chain — from milking machines, plate heat exchangers, centrifugal separators, homogenizers, spray dryers, and CIP (Clean-In-Place) systems — is governed by **food engineering principles** (heat transfer, mass transfer, fluid mechanics, kinetics) that a B.Tech student must master.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: SNF Estimation Using the Lactometer Reading

The **Quevenne Lactometer** is a hydrometer calibrated to read correctly at **15.6 °C (60 °F)**. In India, the lactometer is usually calibrated for **27 °C**. The relationship between specific gravity and solids is empirically derived.

**Given:**

- Lactometer reading (LR) at temperature $T$
- Fat % of milk
- Specific gravity of milk $SG$

**Empirical formula (Richer-Gossling form):**

$$
\text{SNF \%} = \frac{\text{LR}_{27} \times 0.25 + \text{Fat \%} \times 0.22 + 0.72}{1}
$$

For temperatures other than 27 °C, **add 0.2 LR units for every 1 °C above 27**, or subtract similarly below:

$$
\text{LR}_{\text{corrected}} = \text{LR}_{\text{observed}} + (T - 27) \times 0.2
$$

**Worked Numerical — KTU Style:**

A milk sample at **30 °C** shows a lactometer reading of **28**. Fat % by Gerber method is **4.0 %**. Find the **SNF %**.

**Step 1 — Temperature Correction**

$$
\text{LR}_{\text{corrected}} = 28 + (30 - 27) \times 0.2 = 28 + 0.6 = 28.6
$$

**Step 2 — Apply SNF formula**

$$
\text{SNF \%} = (28.6 \times 0.25) + (4.0 \times 0.22) + 0.72
$$

$$
\text{SNF \%} = 7.15 + 0.88 + 0.72 = 8.75 \,\%
$$

**Step 3 — Total Solids**

$$
\text{TS} = \text{Fat \%} + \text{SNF \%} = 4.0 + 8.75 = 12.75 \,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A normal cow milk should have SNF between **8.5–9.0 %** and TS between **12.5–14.5 %**. Values below this suggest **adulteration with water**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Standardization Using the Algebraic Method (Cross-Verification of Pearson Square)

For a deeper, equation-based derivation (often asked as 7-mark problem):

**Let:**
- $m_1$ = mass of stream 1 (e.g., whole milk) with fat $F_1$
- $m_2$ = mass of stream 2 (e.g., skim milk) with fat $F_2$
- $m_3$ = mass of standardized milk with target fat $F_3$

**Two governing equations:**

$$
m_1 + m_2 = m_3
$$

$$
m_1 \, F_1 + m_2 \, F_2 = m_3 \, F_3
$$

**Solve for $m_1$ and $m_2$:**

$$
m_1 = m_3 \times \frac{F_3 - F_2}{F_1 - F_2}
$$

$$
m_2 = m_3 \times \frac{F_1 - F_3}{F_1 - F_2}
$$

**Worked Numerical:**

Prepare **500 kg** of standardized milk at **3.5 % fat** from **whole milk (4.2 %)** and **skim milk (0.1 %)**.

$$
m_1 = 500 \times \frac{3.5 - 0.1}{4.2 - 0.1} = 500 \times \frac{3.4}{4.1} = 414.6 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_2 = 500 \times \frac{4.2 - 3.5}{4.2 - 0.1} = 500 \times \frac{0.7}{4.1} = 85.4 \,\text{kg}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
414.6 + 85.4 = 500 \,\text{kg} \;\;\checkmark
$$

$$
\text{Fat balance: } (414.6 \times 4.2 + 85.4 \times 0.1) / 500 = 1750 / 500 = 3.5 \,\% \;\;\checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat Load in an HTST Pasteurizer

In an HTST plant, raw milk is heated from $T_1$ to $T_2$ in a **plate heat exchanger**, using hot water as the heating medium.

**Energy balance:**

$$
Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, (T_2 - T_1)
$$

**Where:**
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate of milk (kg/s)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of milk ≈ **3.93 kJ/(kg·K)**
- $T_2 - T_1$ = temperature rise

**Worked Numerical:**

A dairy plant pasteurizes milk at a rate of **10,000 L/h**. Milk enters at **6 °C** and is heated to **72 °C**. Calculate the **heat duty (kW)** required.

**Step 1 — Convert flow to mass flow**

$$
\dot{m} = 10{,}000 \,\frac{\text{L}}{\text{h}} \times 1.03 \,\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{L}} = 10{,}300 \,\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}} = 2.86 \,\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}}
$$

(Density of milk ≈ 1.03 kg/L)

**Step 2 — Apply energy balance**

$$
Q = 2.86 \times 3.93 \times (72 - 6)
$$

$$
Q = 2.86 \times 3.93 \times 66
$$

$$
Q = 741.8 \,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical allowance**

Add **10 %** for heat losses:

$$
Q_{\text{design}} = 741.8 \times 1.10 \approx 815.9 \,\text{kW}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Centrifugal Separator — Separation Factor

A cream separator uses **centrifugal force** instead of gravity. The ratio of centrifugal force to gravitational force is called the **separation factor (Z)**:

$$
Z = \frac{F_c}{F_g} = \frac{m \, \omega^{2} \, r}{m \, g} = \frac{\omega^{2} \, r}{g}
$$

For a typical milk separator:
- Bowl radius $r = 0.15$ m
- Rotational speed $N = 7{,}000$ rpm
- $\omega = 2\pi N / 60 = 2\pi \times 7000 / 60 = 733$ rad/s

$$
Z = \frac{733^2 \times 0.15}{9.81} = \frac{80{,}617}{9.81} \approx 8{,}218
$$

This means the **centrifugal force is ~8,200 times gravity**, allowing rapid separation of fat from skim milk.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Adulterants in Milk and Their Detection (KTU Favourite)

| Adulterant | How Added | Detection Method | Legal Status |
|------------|-----------|------------------|--------------|
| **Water** | Increases volume | ↓ Lactometer reading, ↓ Fat, ↓ SNF, ↓ Freezing Point | Illegal (FSSAI) |
| **Starch** (cheap filler) | Increases TS | Iodine test (blue-black color) | Illegal |
| **Urea** | Raises apparent SNF | DMAB (para-dimethyl amino benzaldehyde) test | Illegal |
| **Synthetic milk** | Detergent + urea + oil + starch | Detergent foam test, urea test, oil test | Highly illegal |
| **Formalin / H₂O₂** | Preservative, extends shelf life | Sulphuric acid + ferric chloride test, starch-KI test | Illegal (toxic) |
| **Skim milk powder + vegetable oil** (reconstituted) | Imitation of whole milk | Reichert-Meissl value, fatty acid profile | Legal only if labelled |
| **Melamine** (nitrogen fraud) | Falsely raises "protein" | HPLC-MS, colorimetric assay | Illegal (toxic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Question Alert:** "Discuss common adulterants of milk and their detection." — This is a 7- or 14-mark classic. Master the table above.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Problem Set (Practice for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Try these problems yourself** — model answers are provided below each.

### Problem A — Ghee Yield
A dairy plant processes **1,000 kg of cream** with **40 % fat** into ghee. If the ghee yield (mass efficiency) is **92 %** of the fat content, calculate the **kg of ghee produced**.

**Model Solution:**

$$
\text{Fat in cream} = 1000 \times 0.40 = 400 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Ghee} = 400 \times 0.92 = 368 \,\text{kg}
$$

### Problem B — Paneer Yield
**100 kg** of milk with **3.5 % fat + 8.5 % SNF** is converted to paneer (Indian cottage cheese) with **70 %** yield on the casein + fat basis. Casein is **80 %** of total protein. Find the **kg of paneer**.

**Model Solution:**

$$
\text{Total protein} = 100 \times 0.034 = 3.4 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Casein} = 3.4 \times 0.80 = 2.72 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Fat} = 100 \times 0.035 = 3.5 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Casein + Fat} = 2.72 + 3.5 = 6.22 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Paneer} = 6.22 \times 0.70 = 4.354 \,\text{kg}
$$

### Problem C — Energy for Spray Drying Milk Powder
**500 kg/h** of milk concentrate (**50 % TS**) is spray-dried to powder with **3 % moisture**. Find the **kg/h of powder** and the **water to be evaporated**.

**Model Solution:**

$$
\text{Solids in feed} = 500 \times 0.50 = 250 \,\text{kg/h}
$$

Powder has 3 % moisture → 97 % solids:

$$
\text{Powder} = 250 / 0.97 = 257.7 \,\text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\text{Water evaporated} = 500 - 257.7 = 242.3 \,\text{kg/h}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete HTST Pasteurization Plant

The following schematic illustrates the **interactions of equipment** in a typical HTST pasteurization line. Mermaid's *graph TD* syntax is used to ensure all node IDs are alphanumeric and non-reserved.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Raw Milk Reception Dock] --> A2[Chilled Storage Tank 4 degC]
    A2 --> A3[Milk Silo Buffer Tank]
    A3 --> A4[Centrifugal Clarifier]
    A4 --> A5[Plate Heat Exchanger Regeneration Section]
    A5 --> A6[Heating Section Hot Water 72 degC]
    A6 --> A7[Holding Tube 15 sec at 72 degC]
    A7 --> A8[Flow Diversion Valve FDV]
    A8 -->|Acceptable| A9[Regeneration Cooling Section]
    A8 -->|Re divert | A3
    A9 --> A10[Chilled Water Cooling to 4 degC]
    A10 --> A11[Homogenizer 200 bar]
    A11 --> A12[Packaging Machine Pouch or Carton]
    A12 --> A13[Cold Storage 4 degC Dispatch]
    H1[Hot Water Boiler 85 degC] --> A6
    W1[Chilled Water Unit 1 degC] --> A10
    CIP[CIP Clean In Place Loop] -.-> A5
    CIP -.-> A6
    CIP -.-> A9
    CIP -.-> A10
    CIP -.-> A11
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** is the legal safety device — if milk does not reach 72 °C, it automatically diverts flow back to the raw milk silo. This is **mandatory** in HTST plants.
- The **regeneration section** recovers heat from already-pasteurized milk to pre-warm incoming milk → **saves up to 85 % of energy**.
- **CIP (Clean-In-Place)** is a closed-loop circulation of cleaning solutions (NaOH 1–2 %, HNO₃ 0.5–1 %) without dismantling the plant.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Butter & Ghee Manufacturing

```mermaid
graph TD
    B1[Whole Milk] --> B2[Centrifugal Cream Separator]
    B2 --> B3[Cream 40 percent fat]
    B2 --> B4[Skim Milk By product]
    B3 --> B5[Pasteurization 72 degC 15 s]
    B5 --> B6[Cooling to 4 degC Aging 4 to 8 hours]
    B6 --> B7[Churning in Butter Churn]
    B7 --> B8[Butter Grains plus Buttermilk]
    B8 --> B9[Butter 80 percent fat]
    B8 --> B10[Buttermilk By product]
    B9 --> B11[Working and Salting]
    B11 --> B12[Packaged Butter]
    B9 --> B13[Heat Clarification 110 to 120 degC]
    B13 --> B14[Sediment Removal]
    B14 --> B15[Moisture Removal Final Stage]
    B15 --> B16[Clarified Ghee 99.5 percent fat]
    B16 --> B17[Cooling and Packaging]
```

**Engineering Notes:**
- **Churning** is a *phase inversion* process — water-in-oil emulsion (cream) → oil-in-water emulsion (butter). The fat globule membranes rupture, releasing liquid fat which entraps water and air.
- **Ghee** manufacture is essentially **moisture removal** from butter until < 0.5 % moisture remains, plus thermal development of flavour compounds (lactones, methyl ketones, free fatty acids).

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cheese Manufacturing

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1[Standardized Pasteurized Milk] --> C2[Addition of Starter Culture Lactococcus]
    C2 --> C3[Incubation 30 to 32 degC for 30 to 45 min]
    C3 --> C4[Addition of Rennet Enzyme Chymosin]
    C4 --> C5[Curd Formation Coagulation 30 to 45 min]
    C5 --> C6[Cutting of Curd into Cubes]
    C6 --> C7[Cooking and Stirring 38 to 40 degC]
    C7 --> C8[Whey Drainage]
    C8 --> C9[Curd Hooping Moulding]
    C9 --> C10[Pressing to Remove Whey]
    C10 --> C11[Salting Brine or Dry Salt]
    C11 --> C12[Ripening Aging 1 to 12 months]
    C12 --> C13[Packaged Cheese]
    C8 --> C14[Whey By product for WPC or Lactose]
```

**Engineering Notes:**
- **Rennet** cleaves the $\kappa$-casein from the casein micelle → calcium bridges form between micelles → gel (coagulum).
- **Cutting** controls the rate of whey expulsion — smaller cubes = drier curd.
- **Ripening** is a controlled *biochemical maturation* by residual enzymes, starter bacteria, and moulds (in blue cheese). pH, moisture, salt, and temperature define the final cheese variety.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Yogurt Manufacturing

```mermaid
graph TD
    Y1[Standardized Milk 3.5 percent fat 8.5 percent SNF] --> Y2[Fortification Skim Milk Powder 2 to 3 percent]
    Y2 --> Y3[Homogenization 200 bar 65 degC]
    Y3 --> Y4[Heat Treatment 85 to 95 degC for 5 to 10 min]
    Y4 --> Y5[Cooling to 42 to 45 degC]
    Y5 --> Y6[Inoculation Streptococcus thermophilus plus Lactobacillus bulgaricus]
    Y6 --> Y7[Incubation 42 degC for 4 to 6 hours until pH 4.5]
    Y7 --> Y8[Cooling to 5 degC to Arrest Fermentation]
    Y8 --> Y9[Fruit Addition and Packaging]
```

**Engineering Notes:**
- The **high heat treatment (85–95 °C)** denatures whey proteins → they complex with casein → firmer, smoother gel (less syneresis).
- **Streptococcus thermophilus** grows first (faster), lowering pH; the **lactobacilli** then take over, producing acetaldehyde (the characteristic yogurt aroma).

---

## 4.5 Comparative Architecture: Batch vs. Continuous Processing

| Process | Batch (e.g., Cheese Vat) | Continuous (e.g., HTST Milk) |
|---------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Capacity** | Low (1000–5000 L) | High (10,000–50,000 L/h) |
| **Labour** | High | Low (automated) |
| **Control** | Manual, operator skill | PLC / SCADA controlled |
| **CIP** | Difficult (open vessels) | Easy (closed loop) |
| **Uniformity** | Batch-to-batch variation | High uniformity |
| **Capital cost** | Low | High |
| **Energy** | Inefficient | Heat recovery possible |
| **Example products** | Paneer, artisanal cheese | Fluid milk, ice cream mix, SMP |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define pasteurization. List the legal time-temperature combinations for LTLT and HTST pasteurization.**

**Model Answer:**
Pasteurization is the process of heating every particle of milk or milk product to a specific temperature for a definite time, such that it destroys all pathogenic vegetative microorganisms and most of the spoilage organisms, with minimal chemical, physical, and organoleptic changes.

- **LTLT:** **63 °C for 30 minutes** (Holder method, batch process)
- **HTST:** **72 °C for 15 seconds** (continuous, plate heat exchanger)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is the difference between homogenization and pasteurization?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Homogenization | Pasteurization |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Purpose** | Reduce fat globule size | Kill pathogens |
| **Process** | Mechanical (high pressure) | Thermal (heat treatment) |
| **Conditions** | 150–250 bar, 60–70 °C | 72 °C / 15 s (HTST) or 63 °C / 30 min (LTLT) |
| **Effect on microbes** | None | Lethal |
| **Effect on fat** | Physical breakdown of globules | No change in globule size |
| **Equipment** | High-pressure homogenizer | Plate Heat Exchanger |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **composition of milk** in detail, listing the major constituents and their typical percentage ranges. Explain why milk is considered a **complete food**. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **steps in the manufacture of cheese** from standardized milk. Mention the role of rennet and starter culture. (7 marks)

**Model Answer (a):**

Milk is a complex colloidal system composed of:
- **Water (87.0 %)** — the continuous phase
- **Fat (3.5–5.0 %)** — present as an **emulsion** of fat globules (diameter 3–8 μm) coated with a milk fat globule membrane (MFGM)
- **Proteins (3.2–3.5 %)** — present as a **colloidal suspension** of casein micelles (80 %) and a true solution of whey proteins (20 %)
- **Lactose (4.5–5.0 %)** — present as a **true solution** in the aqueous phase; the primary carbohydrate
- **Minerals/Ash (0.7–0.8 %)** — calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, sodium, potassium
- **Vitamins** — both water-soluble (B-complex, C) and fat-soluble (A, D, E, K)

**Milk as a complete food:** It contains *all essential nutrients* required for growth and development of the neonate — proteins of high biological value, all essential amino acids, balanced fats with essential fatty acids, lactose as an energy source, calcium and phosphorus in a 1.2:1 ratio ideal for bone growth, and a wide spectrum of vitamins.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **casein** with **whey protein**. Casein is *precipitable* at pH 4.6; whey remains in solution. Don't write "milk contains casein and albumin" — albumin is a minor component. Use "**whey proteins (β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin)**" instead.

**Model Answer (b):**

*(See Block Diagram 4.3 for the flow.)*

The steps are:
1. **Standardization and pasteurization** of milk (optional for raw-milk cheeses).
2. **Starter culture addition** — *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis* and *cremoris* ferment lactose → lactic acid → pH drops to 6.0–6.3 → favours rennet action and curd formation.
3. **Rennet addition** — chymosin (or microbial coagulant) cleaves $\kappa$-casein → loss of stabilizing layer → casein micelles aggregate → **coagulum** forms in 30–45 min at 30 °C.
4. **Cutting** the curd into 1–2 cm cubes releases **whey**.
5. **Cooking and stirring** at 38–40 °C expels more whey and reduces moisture.
6. **Whey drainage**.
7. **Hooping and pressing** — moulds the curd and removes residual whey.
8. **Salting** — controls microbial growth, enhances flavour, draws out more moisture.
9. **Ripening** — biochemical maturation by enzymes (lipase, protease), producing flavour and texture characteristic of the variety (Cheddar 3–12 months; Parmesan 12–36 months).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Marks are *deducted* if you do not mention the **role of rennet** (cleaves $\kappa$-casein) and **role of starter culture** (produces lactic acid). Both are compulsory sub-points for full marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU Style]

**(a)** What is **standardization of milk**? Explain the **Pearson Square method** with a suitable example. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **principle, working, and applications of a centrifugal cream separator**. State Stoke's law. (7 marks)

**Model Answer (a):**

**Standardization** is the process of adjusting the fat content of milk to a desired legal level by mixing high-fat and low-fat streams.

**Pearson Square Method** (see Section 2.3) — a graphical tool to find the proportions of two streams.

**Example:** To prepare **1000 kg** of standardized milk at **3.5 % fat** from whole milk (4.2 %) and skim milk (0.1 %):

Place desired fat in centre: 3.5
Top-left: 4.2 (whole); Bottom-left: 0.1 (skim)

- Diagonal 1 (top-right) = $3.5 - 0.1 = 3.4$ parts of **whole milk**
- Diagonal 2 (bottom-right) = $4.2 - 3.5 = 0.7$ parts of **skim milk**
- Total parts = 3.4 + 0.7 = 4.1

$$
\text{Whole milk} = 1000 \times \frac{3.4}{4.1} = 829.3 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Skim milk} = 1000 \times \frac{0.7}{4.1} = 170.7 \,\text{kg}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to put the *higher* value on the *bottom* left of the square. Always place the streams in a way that the diagonal differences are *positive*. The parts of the top stream equal the **bottom diagonal difference**, and vice versa.

**Model Answer (b):**

**Principle of Centrifugal Cream Separator:** It works on the principle of **density difference** between fat globules (~0.93 g/cm³) and skim milk (~1.035 g/cm³). Under high centrifugal force (separation factor ~5000–10,000 × g), the heavier skim milk moves outward and the lighter cream moves towards the centre.

**Working:**
- Milk enters the rotating bowl (6,000–8,000 rpm) through a central feed.
- A stack of conical **discs** (60–120 discs, gap ~0.4 mm) shortens the settling distance → rapid separation.
- Cream exits through the top outlet; skim milk exits through the bottom outlet.
- The fat content of cream can be controlled by the **cream screw** (back-pressure).

**Stoke's Law:**

$$
v = \frac{d^{2}\,(\Delta\rho)\,g}{18\,\mu}
$$

In a centrifuge, the gravity $g$ is replaced by $\omega^{2} r$, so the settling velocity becomes:

$$
v_c = \frac{d^{2}\,(\Delta\rho)\,\omega^{2} r}{18\,\mu}
$$

**Applications:**
- Separation of **cream** and **skim milk**.
- **Clarification** of milk (removal of sediment and somatic cells).
- **Bactofugation** (removal of bacterial spores, especially *Bacillus cereus*).
- Standardization of fat % in continuous in-line systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write **"centrifugal force"** as the answer to "principle". The *correct* principle is **density difference under high centrifugal force**, not centrifugal force itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Reference Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **Standard milk composition:** Water 87 %, Fat 4 %, SNF 9 %, TS 13 %, Lactose 4.8 %, Protein 3.4 %, Ash 0.7 %.
- **Legal pasteurization:** LTLT 63 °C/30 min; HTST 72 °C/15 s; UHT 138–142 °C/2–4 s.
- **HTST** uses a **plate heat exchanger** with a **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** as a legal safety device.
- **Homogenization:** 150–250 bar, fat globule size reduces from 3–8 μm → < 1 μm.
- **Pearson Square** is a graphical method to compute proportions for **standardization**.
- **Stoke's Law** governs cream separation in a centrifugal separator: $v = d^{2} \Delta\rho g / 18\mu$.
- **Centrifugal separation factor** $Z = \omega^{2} r / g$ — typically 5000–10,000 in milk separators.
- **SNF formula:** $SNF = 0.25 \times LR_{27} + 0.22 \times Fat + 0.72$ (LR corrected to 27 °C).
- **Rennet** cleaves $\kappa$-casein → curd formation. **Starter culture** produces lactic acid.
- **Yogurt culture:** *S. thermophilus* + *L. bulgaricus*. **Cheese culture:** *Lactococcus* spp.
- **Cheese ripening** is biochemical — enzymes, pH, moisture, salt define variety.
- **Ghee** manufacture: butter → clarification (110–120 °C) → moisture removal → < 0.5 % moisture.
- **Spray drying** for milk powder: feed with 50 % TS → powder with 3 % moisture.
- **Adulterants** in milk: water (low LR), starch (iodine test), urea (DMAB), formalin, melamine.
- **FSSAI legal SNF:** min **8.5 %** for cow milk; min **3.5 % fat** for standardized milk.
- **Engineering energy balance** for pasteurizer: $Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$, with $c_p \approx 3.93$ kJ/(kg·K).
- **Butter** contains ≥ 80 % fat, ≤ 16 % moisture (FSSAI).
- **Cold chain** must be maintained at **4 °C ± 2 °C** from reception to consumer.
- **CIP** (Clean-In-Place) uses NaOH (1–2 %) and HNO₃ (0.5–1 %) for closed-loop cleaning.
- **India** is the **world's largest milk producer** (~230 MT/year) — Operation Flood context is a KTU viva favourite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Mantra for KTU Viva / Exam:**
> *"Milk is a colloidal system of fat-in-water emulsion, casein colloidal suspension, and lactose solution. Pasteurize at 72/15, homogenize at 200 bar, separate by density, ferment with LAB, and keep the cold chain alive at 4 °C."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cereals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cereals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cereal (KTU 2024 Definition):** Cereals are edible members of the *Gramineae* (grass) family, characterized by a **caryopsis** — a dry, single-seeded fruit in which the seed coat (pericarp) is firmly fused with the fruit wall (testa). The principal cereals of economic and nutritional importance are **wheat, rice, maize, barley, oats, rye, sorghum, and millets**, which collectively supply over **50%** of the global caloric intake.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *OEFTT831 — Fundamentals of Food Processing*, cereals are classified as **staple food crops** cultivated primarily for their **starchy endosperm**, harvested as **grains**, and processed through both traditional and industrial unit operations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Egg-Shaped Lunchbox"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for a beginner:** Imagine a tiny, naturally sealed egg-shaped lunchbox called a *kernel*. The outer hard shell is the **husk**, the thin brown wrapper is the **bran**, the tiny unborn plant is the **germ**, and the big white starchy middle is the **endosperm** — the actual "food" packed inside. When you eat rice or chapati, you are essentially opening this lunchbox and consuming the packed carbohydrates, proteins, vitamins, and minerals stored for the next plant generation.

## 1.3 Botanical & Structural Identity

Every cereal grain is a **caryopsis** with three primary anatomical regions:

| Anatomical Region | Sub-layer | Approximate % of Kernel | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pericarp (Husk)** | Outer husk, inner pericarp | 5–8% | Physical protection |
| **Seed Coat (Testa)** | Aleurone layer | 6–7% | Pigment, enzyme reserve |
| **Endosperm** | Starchy endosperm | 80–85% | Starch & protein storage |
| **Germ (Embryo)** | Embryonic axis, scutellum | 2–3% | New plant growth |

> [!NOTE]
> The **aleurone layer** is a single layer of cells just beneath the seed coat, rich in proteins, lipids, minerals (especially phosphorus), and B-vitamins. During malting, this layer is responsible for synthesizing **α-amylase** and **proteases** that mobilize stored reserves.

## 1.4 Major Cereals — Quick Reference Table

| Cereal | Botanical Name | Primary Global Use | Key Starch Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wheat** | *Triticum aestivum* | Bread, chapati | A + B starch granules |
| **Rice** | *Oryza sativa* | Cooked grain, flour | Amylose (15–30%) + Amylopectin |
| **Maize** | *Zea mays* | Corn flour, starch, syrup | Amylose (25%) + Amylopectin |
| **Barley** | *Hordeum vulgare* | Malt, beer | β-glucan rich |
| **Oats** | *Avena sativa* | Porridge, rolled oats | Lipid-rich (6–9%) |
| **Rye** | *Secale cereale* | Bread (rye), whiskey | Pentosan-rich |
| **Sorghum** | *Sorghum bicolor* | Roti, porridge, animal feed | Waxy & non-waxy varieties |
| **Millets** | *Pennisetum, Eleusine, Setaria, Panicum* | Ragi, jowar, bajra | Gluten-free |

## 1.5 Why Cereals Matter in Food Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Cereals form the backbone of food processing industries because they are:
> 1. **Renewable** — annual harvest cycle.
> 2. **Storable** — low moisture (<14%) allows long shelf life.
> 3. **Versatile** — converted by milling, malting, extrusion, popping, flaking, and fermentation into countless products.
> 4. **Nutrient-dense** — carbohydrates (70–80%), proteins (7–15%), lipids (2–7%), fiber, vitamins, minerals.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Chemical Composition of Cereal Grains

The proximate composition of cereals on a **dry-weight basis (db)** is central to KTU 2024 evaluation. Memorize the ranges below.

| Component | Wheat | Rice (brown) | Maize | Sorghum | Barley |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Starch** | 60–75% | 65–75% | 65–75% | 60–75% | 55–65% |
| **Protein** | 10–15% | 7–9% | 8–11% | 9–13% | 10–12% |
| **Lipid (Fat)** | 2–3% | 2–3% | 3–5% | 2–4% | 2–3% |
| **Fiber** | 2–3% | 1–2% | 2–3% | 2–3% | 4–6% |
| **Ash (Minerals)** | 1.5–2% | 1.2–1.8% | 1.2–1.5% | 1.5–2% | 2–3% |
| **Moisture (as harvested)** | 10–14% | 12–14% | 12–15% | 10–14% | 10–14% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Wheat proteins** are uniquely classified into four fractions based on **Osborne's solubility classification**:
> - **Albumins** — water-soluble (enzymes).
> - **Globulins** — salt-soluble (e.g., legumin-like).
> - **Prolamins** — alcohol-soluble (in wheat = **gliadin**).
> - **Glutelins** — acid/alkali-soluble (in wheat = **glutenin**).
>
> **Gliadin + Glutenin = Gluten** — the viscoelastic protein complex responsible for the gas-holding capacity of bread dough.

## 2.2 Classification of Cereals (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

Cereals are classified based on **three different criteria**:

### A. Based on Growing Season
- **Kharif (Monsoon) Crops** — sown June–July, harvested Sept–Oct: Rice, Maize, Sorghum, Pearl Millet.
- **Rabi (Winter) Crops** — sown Oct–Nov, harvested Mar–Apr: Wheat, Barley, Oats, Rye.

### B. Based on Grain Structure
- **Husked (covered) cereals** — Rice, Barley, Oats (lemma & palea attached).
- **Naked (free-threshing) cereals** — Wheat, Maize, Rye, Sorghum (lemma & palea loosely attached, easily removed).

### C. Based on Use
- **Bread cereals** — Wheat, Rye.
- **Rice (food) cereals** — Rice.
- **Feed/Fodder cereals** — Maize, Sorghum, Oats, Barley.
- **Malting cereals** — Barley, Sorghum.
- **Gluten-free cereals** — Rice, Maize, Millets.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are tested in KTU 2024 numerical problems. Each formula uses the symbol convention adopted in standard food engineering textbooks.

### 2.3.1 Moisture Content Equations

$$
M_{wb} \;=\; \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100
$$

$$
M_{db} \;=\; \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100
$$

### 2.3.2 Inter-conversion Between wb and db Moisture Content

$$
M_{db} \;=\; \frac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100
$$

### 2.3.3 Hundred-Grain Weight (Test Weight)

$$
TW \;=\; \frac{\text{Mass of 100 grains (g)}}{\text{Count}} \times 100
$$

### 2.3.4 Milling Yield (for Rice)

$$
\eta_{\text{mill}} \;=\; \frac{W_{\text{white rice}}}{W_{\text{paddy}}} \times 100 \;(\%)
$$

### 2.3.5 Protein Conversion (Nitrogen → Protein)

$$
\%\text{ Protein} \;=\; \%N \times F
$$

where $F$ is the **Nitrogen-to-Protein conversion factor** (also called **Jones factor**):

| Cereal | Conversion Factor $F$ |
|---|---|
| Wheat | 5.70 |
| Rice | 5.95 |
| Maize | 6.25 |
| Sorghum | 6.25 |
| Barley | 5.83 |
| Oats | 5.83 |
| Millets | 6.25 |

### 2.3.6 Gelatinization Temperature of Starch

$$
T_{\text{gel}} \;=\; T_{\text{onset}} + 0.5 \,(T_{\text{conclusion}} - T_{\text{onset}})
$$

Typical gelatinization ranges:
- **Rice starch**: 65 – 78 °C
- **Wheat starch**: 58 – 64 °C
- **Maize starch**: 62 – 72 °C
- **Waxy maize**: 63 – 72 °C

### 2.3.7 Water Absorption Index (WAI)

$$
WAI \;=\; \frac{W_{\text{gel}}}{W_{\text{dry sample}}}
$$

### 2.3.8 Degree of Gelatinization

$$
DG \;(\%) \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{sample}}}{V_{\text{fully cooked}}} \times 100
$$

where $V$ is the absorbance or viscosity reading of iodine complex at **620 nm**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Students often use $F = 6.25$ for all cereals. This is **incorrect** because each cereal protein has a different nitrogen content (e.g., wheat gluten is nitrogen-poor, hence $F = 5.70$). Always use the cereal-specific factor.

## 2.4 Cereals — Engineering Utility

Cereal processing is governed by the following **unit operations**:

| Unit Operation | Industrial Application | Product |
|---|---|---|
| **Cleaning & Grading** | Destoning, aspiration | Whole grain |
| **Drying** | Reducing moisture to <14% | Safe storage grain |
| **Milling** | Roller milling / Hammer milling | Flour, semolina |
| **Parboiling** | Soak–steam–dry | Parboiled rice |
| **Malting** | Steep–germinate–kiln | Malt for beer |
| **Popping / Puffing** | Sudden HTST heating | Popcorn, puffed rice |
| **Flaking** | Steam + roller | Corn flakes, oats |
| **Extrusion cooking** | HTST shear through die | Ready-to-eat snacks |
| **Fermentation** | Microbial action | Idli, dosa batter, beer |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Inter-conversion of Moisture Content (wb ↔ db)

This is a frequently tested **KTU 2024 3-mark derivation**.

**Step 1 — Define the wet-basis moisture.**

Let $W_w$ = mass of wet sample (g) and $W_d$ = mass of dry sample (g).

$$
M_{wb} \;=\; \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the dry-basis moisture.**

$$
M_{db} \;=\; \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide the two equations.**

$$
\frac{M_{db}}{M_{wb}} \;=\; \frac{(W_w - W_d)/W_d}{(W_w - W_d)/W_w}
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the common numerator.**

$$
\frac{M_{db}}{M_{wb}} \;=\; \frac{W_w}{W_d}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the identity $W_w/W_d = 1/(1 - M_{wb})$.** Because $W_d = W_w (1 - M_{wb})$ from rearranging Step 1.

$$
\frac{M_{db}}{M_{wb}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - M_{wb}}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $M_{db}$.**

$$
M_{db} \;=\; \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}
$$

**Step 7 — Express as a percentage.**

$$
M_{db} \;(\%) \;=\; \frac{M_{wb} \;(\%)}{100 - M_{wb} \;(\%)} \times 100
$$

**Verification of logic:** When the grain is wet, $M_{wb} \to 100\%$, and $M_{db} \to \infty$ (logically correct — infinite water relative to zero dry matter). When dry, $M_{wb} \to 0$, and $M_{db} \to 0$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A sample of wheat has a wet-basis moisture content of **14%**. The grain is dried until the moisture content (wb) reaches **10%**. If the original mass is **1000 kg**, calculate:
> (a) Initial mass of dry matter.
> (b) Final mass of grain.
> (c) Moisture removed in kg.

### Solution

**Step (a) — Initial mass of dry matter.**

$$
W_d \;=\; W_w \times (1 - M_{wb})
$$

$$
W_d \;=\; 1000 \times (1 - 0.14) \;=\; 860 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step (b) — Final mass of grain.**

Dry matter remains conserved during drying:

$$
W_{w,f} \;=\; \frac{W_d}{1 - M_{wb,f}}
$$

$$
W_{w,f} \;=\; \frac{860}{1 - 0.10} \;=\; \frac{860}{0.90}
$$

$$
W_{w,f} \;=\; 955.56 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step (c) — Moisture removed.**

$$
\Delta W \;=\; 1000 - 955.56 \;=\; 44.44 \text{ kg of water}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Drying 1000 kg of wheat from 14% to 10% wb removes **only 44.44 kg of water** — a small amount, but it increases shelf life from ~3 months to >12 months under proper storage.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Protein Calculation

> **Problem:** A wheat sample shows **2.20% nitrogen** on dry-basis analysis. Calculate the protein content using the correct KTU-specified factor.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the factor.** For wheat, $F = 5.70$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Jones-factor equation.**

$$
\%\text{ Protein} \;=\; 2.20 \times 5.70
$$

$$
\%\text{ Protein} \;=\; 12.54\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> If a student incorrectly uses $F = 6.25$, they get **13.75%**, an over-estimation of **~9.6%**. The examiner will deduct marks.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Milling Yield

> **Problem:** 2000 kg of paddy yields **1280 kg of milled white rice**, **120 kg of bran**, and the rest is broken grains + husk. Calculate the milling yield percentage and head-rice recovery if **950 kg** is whole-grain rice.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Milling yield.**

$$
\eta_{\text{mill}} \;=\; \frac{1280}{2000} \times 100 \;=\; 64.0\%
$$

**Step 2 — Head-rice recovery.**

$$
HRR \;=\; \frac{950}{2000} \times 100 \;=\; 47.5\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Industry Standard: A modern rice mill targets **65–72% total yield** and **>55% head-rice recovery** for export-grade parboiled rice.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Starch Gelatinization Across Cereals

| Cereal | Gelatinization Onset (°C) | Peak (°C) | Conclusion (°C) | Enthalpy ΔH (J/g) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wheat** | 58 | 62 | 64 | 9.5 |
| **Rice (normal)** | 65 | 73 | 78 | 11.5 |
| **Rice (waxy)** | 63 | 70 | 75 | 14.0 |
| **Maize (normal)** | 62 | 67 | 72 | 12.0 |
| **Maize (waxy)** | 63 | 70 | 72 | 15.5 |
| **Sorghum** | 68 | 75 | 80 | 10.0 |
| **Barley** | 56 | 62 | 68 | 9.0 |
| **Millet (Ragi)** | 65 | 72 | 78 | 11.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **enthalpy of gelatinization** quantifies the energy required to disrupt crystalline starch granules. Waxy cereals (near-zero amylose) require **higher enthalpy** than normal starches because of tighter amylopectin packing.

## 3.6 Parboiling of Rice — Mass & Energy Balance (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** 1000 kg of paddy at **22% wb** is soaked to **30% wb**, then steamed (moisture rises to **38% wb**), and finally dried to **12% wb**. Calculate water added during soaking and water removed during drying.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Dry matter (constant).**

$$
W_d \;=\; 1000 \times (1 - 0.22) \;=\; 780 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass after soaking (wb = 30%).**

$$
W_{w,s} \;=\; \frac{780}{1 - 0.30} \;=\; \frac{780}{0.70} \;=\; 1114.29 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Water added in soaking.**

$$
\Delta W_{\text{soak}} \;=\; 1114.29 - 1000 \;=\; 114.29 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass after steaming (wb = 38%).**

$$
W_{w,st} \;=\; \frac{780}{1 - 0.38} \;=\; \frac{780}{0.62} \;=\; 1258.06 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Final dried mass (wb = 12%).**

$$
W_{w,f} \;=\; \frac{780}{1 - 0.12} \;=\; \frac{780}{0.88} \;=\; 886.36 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Water removed during drying.**

$$
\Delta W_{\text{dry}} \;=\; 1258.06 - 886.36 \;=\; 371.70 \text{ kg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Parboiling **adds 114 kg** of water but **removes 372 kg** during drying. The net energy economy and the gelatinization of starch inside the kernel make parboiled rice nutritionally superior (B-vitamins diffuse inward from the aleurone) and harder (lower breakage during milling).

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Inter-conversion of Moisture Content (wb ↔ db)

This is a frequently tested **KTU 2024 3-mark derivation**.

**Step 1 — Define the wet-basis moisture.**

Let $W_w$ = mass of wet sample (g) and $W_d$ = mass of dry sample (g).

$$
M_{wb} \;=\; \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the dry-basis moisture.**

$$
M_{db} \;=\; \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide the two equations.**

$$
\frac{M_{db}}{M_{wb}} \;=\; \frac{(W_w - W_d)/W_d}{(W_w - W_d)/W_w}
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the common numerator.**

$$
\frac{M_{db}}{M_{wb}} \;=\; \frac{W_w}{W_d}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the identity $W_w/W_d = 1/(1 - M_{wb})$.** Because $W_d = W_w (1 - M_{wb})$ from rearranging Step 1.

$$
\frac{M_{db}}{M_{wb}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - M_{wb}}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $M_{db}$.**

$$
M_{db} \;=\; \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}
$$

**Step 7 — Express as a percentage.**

$$
M_{db} \;(\%) \;=\; \frac{M_{wb} \;(\%)}{100 - M_{wb} \;(\%)} \times 100
$$

**Verification of logic:** When the grain is wet, $M_{wb} \to 100\%$, and $M_{db} \to \infty$ (logically correct — infinite water relative to zero dry matter). When dry, $M_{wb} \to 0$, and $M_{db} \to 0$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A sample of wheat has a wet-basis moisture content of **14%**. The grain is dried until the moisture content (wb) reaches **10%**. If the original mass is **1000 kg**, calculate:
> (a) Initial mass of dry matter.
> (b) Final mass of grain.
> (c) Moisture removed in kg.

### Solution

**Step (a) — Initial mass of dry matter.**

$$
W_d \;=\; W_w \times (1 - M_{wb})
$$

$$
W_d \;=\; 1000 \times (1 - 0.14) \;=\; 860 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step (b) — Final mass of grain.**

Dry matter remains conserved during drying:

$$
W_{w,f} \;=\; \frac{W_d}{1 - M_{wb,f}}
$$

$$
W_{w,f} \;=\; \frac{860}{1 - 0.10} \;=\; \frac{860}{0.90}
$$

$$
W_{w,f} \;=\; 955.56 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step (c) — Moisture removed.**

$$
\Delta W \;=\; 1000 - 955.56 \;=\; 44.44 \text{ kg of water}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Drying 1000 kg of wheat from 14% to 10% wb removes **only 44.44 kg of water** — a small amount, but it increases shelf life from ~3 months to >12 months under proper storage.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Protein Calculation

> **Problem:** A wheat sample shows **2.20% nitrogen** on dry-basis analysis. Calculate the protein content using the correct KTU-specified factor.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the factor.** For wheat, $F = 5.70$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Jones-factor equation.**

$$
\%\text{ Protein} \;=\; 2.20 \times 5.70
$$

$$
\%\text{ Protein} \;=\; 12.54\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> If a student incorrectly uses $F = 6.25$, they get **13.75%**, an over-estimation of **~9.6%**. The examiner will deduct marks.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Milling Yield

> **Problem:** 2000 kg of paddy yields **1280 kg of milled white rice**, **120 kg of bran**, and the rest is broken grains + husk. Calculate the milling yield percentage and head-rice recovery if **950 kg** is whole-grain rice.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Milling yield.**

$$
\eta_{\text{mill}} \;=\; \frac{1280}{2000} \times 100 \;=\; 64.0\%
$$

**Step 2 — Head-rice recovery.**

$$
HRR \;=\; \frac{950}{2000} \times 100 \;=\; 47.5\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Industry Standard: A modern rice mill targets **65–72% total yield** and **>55% head-rice recovery** for export-grade parboiled rice.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Starch Gelatinization Across Cereals

| Cereal | Gelatinization Onset (°C) | Peak (°C) | Conclusion (°C) | Enthalpy ΔH (J/g) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wheat** | 58 | 62 | 64 | 9.5 |
| **Rice (normal)** | 65 | 73 | 78 | 11.5 |
| **Rice (waxy)** | 63 | 70 | 75 | 14.0 |
| **Maize (normal)** | 62 | 67 | 72 | 12.0 |
| **Maize (waxy)** | 63 | 70 | 72 | 15.5 |
| **Sorghum** | 68 | 75 | 80 | 10.0 |
| **Barley** | 56 | 62 | 68 | 9.0 |
| **Millet (Ragi)** | 65 | 72 | 78 | 11.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **enthalpy of gelatinization** quantifies the energy required to disrupt crystalline starch granules. Waxy cereals (near-zero amylose) require **higher enthalpy** than normal starches because of tighter amylopectin packing.

## 3.6 Parboiling of Rice — Mass & Energy Balance (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** 1000 kg of paddy at **22% wb** is soaked to **30% wb**, then steamed (moisture rises to **38% wb**), and finally dried to **12% wb**. Calculate water added during soaking and water removed during drying.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Dry matter (constant).**

$$
W_d \;=\; 1000 \times (1 - 0.22) \;=\; 780 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass after soaking (wb = 30%).**

$$
W_{w,s} \;=\; \frac{780}{1 - 0.30} \;=\; \frac{780}{0.70} \;=\; 1114.29 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Water added in soaking.**

$$
\Delta W_{\text{soak}} \;=\; 1114.29 - 1000 \;=\; 114.29 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass after steaming (wb = 38%).**

$$
W_{w,st} \;=\; \frac{780}{1 - 0.38} \;=\; \frac{780}{0.62} \;=\; 1258.06 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Final dried mass (wb = 12%).**

$$
W_{w,f} \;=\; \frac{780}{1 - 0.12} \;=\; \frac{780}{0.88} \;=\; 886.36 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Water removed during drying.**

$$
\Delta W_{\text{dry}} \;=\; 1258.06 - 886.36 \;=\; 371.70 \text{ kg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Parboiling **adds 114 kg** of water but **removes 372 kg** during drying. The net energy economy and the gelatinization of starch inside the kernel make parboiled rice nutritionally superior (B-vitamins diffuse inward from the aleurone) and harder (lower breakage during milling).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Anatomy of a Cereal Caryopsis (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CEREAL CARYOPSIS KERNEL] --> B[PERICARP AND HUSK LAYER]
    A --> C[TESTA SEED COAT]
    A --> D[ENDOSPERM]
    A --> E[GERM EMBRYO]
    B --> B1[Outer protective husk 5 to 8 percent]
    B --> B2[Inner pericarp cellulose fiber]
    C --> C1[Aleurone layer rich in enzymes proteins lipids minerals B vitamins]
    D --> D1[Starchy endosperm starch granules 80 to 85 percent]
    D --> D2[Subaleurone protein matrix]
    E --> E1[Embryonic axis future shoot and root]
    E --> E2[Scutellum transfers nutrients to embryo]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Cereal Processing Flow-Chart (Milling to End Products)

```mermaid

    P1[HARVESTED GRAIN 18 to 22 percent wb] --> P2[Drying to below 14 percent wb]

    P3 --> P4[Conditioning Tempering water addition]
    P4 --> P5{Choose cereal type}
    P5 --> Q1[Wheat]
    P5 --> Q2[Rice]
    P5 --> Q3[Maize]
    P5 --> Q4[Barley]
    P5 --> Q5[Oats]
    Q1 --> R1[Roller milling] --> S1[Flour Semolina Bran Germ]
    Q2 --> R2[Soaking] --> R3[Steaming] --> R4[Drying] --> R5[Dehusking and Polishing] --> S2[Parboiled White Rice]
    Q3 --> R6[Degerming] --> R7[Dry milling] --> S3[Corn grits Corn flour]
    Q4 --> R8[Steeping] --> R9[Germination] --> R10[Kilning] --> S4[Malt]
    Q5 --> R11[Steaming] --> R12[Roller Flaking] --> S5[Rolled Oats Oat Flour]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Osborne Classification of Wheat Proteins

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WHEAT[WHEAT PROTEIN 10 to 15 percent] --> OSB[OSBORNE FRACTIONATION]
    OSB --> O1[Albumins Water Soluble]
    OSB --> O2[Globulins Salt Soluble]
    OSB --> O3[Prolamins Ethanol Soluble GLIADIN]
    OSB --> O4[Glutelins Acid Alkali Soluble GLUTENIN]
    O3 --> GLU[GLUTEN COMPLEX]
    O4 --> GLU
    GLU --> FUNC[VISCOELASTIC NETWORK Gas retention in dough Bread loaf volume]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Parboiling Process State Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[State 0 Paddy 22 percent wb] --> S1[State 1 Soaked 30 percent wb]
    S1 --> S2[State 2 Steamed 38 percent wb]
    S2 --> S3[State 3 Dried 12 percent wb]
    S3 --> S4[State 4 Milled White Rice 64 percent yield]
    S0 -- Water plus 114 kg --> S1
    S1 -- Water plus 144 kg --> S2
    S2 -- Water minus 372 kg --> S3
    S3 -- Husk and bran removed --> S4
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Comparative Cereal Composition (Radial Architecture Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    H --> H2[RICE]
    H --> H3[MAIZE]

```

Diagram 4.4 is a **state-transition diagram** for moisture changes. Diagram 4.5 is a **radial composition map** for quick revision.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define cereals. List any four major cereals with their botanical names.**

**Model Answer:**
Cereals are members of the *Gramineae* family producing a **caryopsis** — a dry, one-seeded fruit where pericarp is fused with the seed coat.

| Cereal | Botanical Name |
|---|---|
| Wheat | *Triticum aestivum* |
| Rice | *Oryza sativa* |
| Maize | *Zea mays* |
| Barley | *Hordeum vulgare* |

**Mark split (KTU pattern):** Definition = 2 marks; listing 4 cereals with bot. names = 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is gluten? Why is it important in bread-making?**

**Model Answer:**
Gluten is a **viscoelastic protein complex** formed when water is added to wheat flour, comprising **gliadin (prolamin fraction)** and **glutenin (glutelin fraction)** in approximately 1:1 ratio.

During dough mixing:
- **Gliadin** contributes **viscosity and extensibility**.
- **Glutenin** contributes **elasticity and strength**.

When yeast ferments sugars into CO₂, the gluten network **traps the gas**, allowing the dough to rise and produce a spongy crumb structure. Without gluten (e.g., rice, maize), bread cannot be made by conventional methods.

**Mark split:** Gluten definition = 1 mark; gliadin & glutenin roles = 1 mark; CO₂ retention = 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3 Choice-1]`

**a)** Classify cereals based on (i) growing season, (ii) grain structure, and (iii) end-use. Give suitable examples for each class. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the **Osborne classification** of wheat proteins. Describe the role of each fraction in determining bread-making quality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Classification of cereals (7 marks):**

**(i) Based on growing season (3 marks):**

| Class | Sowing / Harvest | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Kharif (Monsoon)** | June–July / Sept–Oct | Rice, Maize, Sorghum, Pearl millet |
| **Rabi (Winter)** | Oct–Nov / Mar–Apr | Wheat, Barley, Oats, Rye |

**(ii) Based on grain structure (2 marks):**
- **Husked (covered) cereals:** lemma and palea remain attached to the kernel after threshing. Examples: **Rice, Barley, Oats**. Require dehusking before consumption.
- **Naked (free-threshing) cereals:** lemma and palea are loosely attached and easily removed. Examples: **Wheat, Maize, Rye, Sorghum**. Can be directly milled.

**(iii) Based on end-use (2 marks):**
- **Bread-making cereals:** Wheat, Rye (contain gluten-forming proteins).
- **Rice cereals:** Rice (consumed as whole grain, flour, flakes).
- **Feed/Fodder cereals:** Maize, Sorghum, Oats, Barley.
- **Malting cereals:** Barley, Sorghum (used for beer and breakfast malt drinks).
- **Gluten-free cereals:** Rice, Maize, Millets (used for celiac patients).

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Osborne classification of wheat proteins (7 marks):**

In 1907, T.B. Osborne classified cereal proteins based on **solubility in different solvents**:

| Fraction | Solvent | Approx. % of Total Protein | Example Component |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Albumins** | Water | 10–15% | Enzymes (e.g., amylase) |
| **Globulins** | Dilute salt solution | 5–10% | Structural proteins |
| **Prolamins** | 70% aqueous ethanol | 30–40% | **Gliadin** |
| **Glutelins** | Dilute acid / alkali | 30–40% | **Glutenin** |

**Roles in bread-making:**

1. **Gliadin (Prolamin):** Provides **extensibility and viscosity** to the dough. A dough made from pure gliadin would stretch but not spring back.

2. **Glutenin (Glutelin):** Provides **elasticity and strength**. Its inter-molecular disulfide bonds (S–S) form a continuous network.

3. **Combined role of Gluten:** When flour and water are mixed and kneaded, gliadin and glutenin hydrate, align, and form a **three-dimensional viscoelastic network**. During fermentation, CO₂ from yeast is trapped, causing the dough to rise. On baking, the network **sets** via protein denaturation, fixing the loaf structure.

4. **Albumins and Globulins** are largely enzymatic or structural; they do not contribute to dough rheology but influence nutritional quality.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **gliadin and glutenin roles**. Examiner expects you to state: **gliadin = extensibility (viscous flow); glutenin = elasticity (spring-back)**. Writing only "they form gluten" will fetch **partial credit only**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice-2]`

**a)** What is **parboiling of rice**? Describe the **three stages** of parboiling with their moisture targets. **(7 marks)**

**b)** 2000 kg of paddy is parboiled. Initial moisture (wb) is **22%**, after soaking **30%**, after steaming **38%**, and after final drying **12%**. Calculate: (i) initial dry matter; (ii) mass of water added in soaking; (iii) mass of water removed during drying. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Parboiling of rice (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Parboiling is a **hydro-thermal treatment** of paddy rice prior to milling, in which the starch inside the endosperm is **gelatinized** by soaking in water and steaming, followed by drying. The word *parboil* literally means **"partially boiled."**

**Three stages of parboiling:**

| Stage | Operation | Moisture (wb) |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Soaking** | Paddy immersed in water at 65–70 °C for 3–4 h | 22% → 30% |
| **2. Steaming** | Soaked paddy exposed to live steam at ~100 °C for 10–20 min | 30% → 35–38% |
| **3. Drying** | Sun-drying or mechanical drying in two stages | 38% → 12–14% |

**Effect of parboiling:**
- **Nutritional:** B-vitamins from the aleurone layer diffuse inward into the endosperm.
- **Physical:** Starch granules gelatinize → harder grain → **less breakage** during milling (higher head-rice recovery).
- **Storage:** Reduced insect infestation due to harder texture.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step (i) — Initial dry matter.**

$$
W_d \;=\; W_w \times (1 - M_{wb,i})
$$

$$
W_d \;=\; 2000 \times (1 - 0.22) \;=\; 2000 \times 0.78
$$

$$
W_d \;=\; 1560 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step (ii) — Water added during soaking.**

Mass of soaked grain at 30% wb:

$$
W_{w,s} \;=\; \frac{W_d}{1 - 0.30} \;=\; \frac{1560}{0.70} \;=\; 2228.57 \text{ kg}
$$

Water added:

$$
\Delta W_{\text{soak}} \;=\; 2228.57 - 2000 \;=\; 228.57 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step (iii) — Water removed during drying.**

Mass of steamed grain at 38% wb:

$$
W_{w,st} \;=\; \frac{1560}{1 - 0.38} \;=\; \frac{1560}{0.62} \;=\; 2516.13 \text{ kg}
$$

Final dried mass at 12% wb:

$$
W_{w,f} \;=\; \frac{1560}{1 - 0.12} \;=\; \frac{1560}{0.88} \;=\; 1772.73 \text{ kg}
$$

Water removed:

$$
\Delta W_{\text{dry}} \;=\; 2516.13 - 1772.73 \;=\; 743.40 \text{ kg}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly subtract moisture percentages directly (e.g., 38 − 12 = 26 kg per 100 kg). This is **dimensionally wrong** because the moisture refers to **mass of water ÷ mass of wet grain**, not to a fixed 100 kg. Always convert using the **mass-balance equation** shown above.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Cereals (KTU 2024):**

- ⭐ **Cereal definition:** grass family *Gramineae*, fruit is a **caryopsis** (fused pericarp + testa).
- ⭐ **Eight major cereals:** Wheat, Rice, Maize, Barley, Oats, Rye, Sorghum, Millets — supply >50% global calories.
- ⭐ **Kernel anatomy:** Pericarp / Testa / Aleurone / Endosperm / Germ (scutellum + embryonic axis).
- ⭐ **Endosperm is 80–85%** of the kernel — the actual food reserve (starch + protein).
- ⭐ **Aleurone layer** synthesizes **α-amylase** during malting.
- ⭐ **Wheat protein fractions (Osborne):** Albumins, Globulins, **Prolamins (gliadin)**, **Glutelins (glutenin)**.
- ⭐ **Gluten = gliadin + glutenin** → gliadin = extensibility; glutenin = elasticity.
- ⭐ **Gluten-forming cereals:** Wheat and Rye ONLY.
- ⭐ **Milling yield of rice:** typically 64–70%; head-rice recovery >55% for export grade.
- ⭐ **Parboiling:** Soaking (22→30% wb) → Steaming (30→38% wb) → Drying (38→12% wb).
- ⭐ **Moisture equations:**
  - $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$
  - $M_{db} = \dfrac{W_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$
- ⭐ **Protein calculation:** % Protein = %N × **Jones factor** (wheat 5.70; rice 5.95; maize 6.25; barley 5.83).
- ⭐ **Starch gelatinization temperatures:** Wheat 58–64 °C; Rice 65–78 °C; Maize 62–72 °C.
- ⭐ **Waxy cereals** (near-zero amylose): Waxy rice, Waxy maize — higher gelatinization enthalpy.
- ⭐ **Hundred-grain weight (TW)** is a quality indicator; higher TW = bolder grain = better milling yield.
- ⭐ **Safe storage moisture for cereals:** **< 14% wb** (below this, fungal activity halts).
- ⭐ **Kharif cereals** = rice, maize, sorghum, bajra; **Rabi cereals** = wheat, barley, oats, rye.
- ⭐ **Industrial unit operations** on cereals: Cleaning → Drying → Milling / Malting / Parboiling / Popping / Flaking / Extrusion.
- ⭐ **Enzymatic conversion** during germination produces malt with high **diastatic power** — essential for brewing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT831 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pulses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — PULSES

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> Pulses are the dried edible seeds of plants belonging to the family **Fabaceae (Leguminosae)**, harvested solely for their dry grain. They are characterized by a **low fat content (1–2%)** and a **high protein content (20–30%)**, making them a primary source of plant-based protein in the vegetarian diet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Pulse"?**
> The English word *pulse* originates from the Latin word **"puls"**, meaning a thick soup or porridge. Historically, these legumes were boiled into thick porridges, hence the nomenclature.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Engineer's Battery Pack"

Imagine the human body as an **electrical machine**. Carbohydrates are the immediate current (glucose), fats are the long-duration fuel tank, and **pulses are the high-density, rechargeable battery pack**. They release energy slowly, repair tissues (proteins), and stabilize the system (fiber, micronutrients) — exactly the role a battery pack plays in an engineering system.

### 1.1.2 Botanical Classification

Pulses belong to the order **Fabales** and family **Fabaceae**, which is the third-largest family of flowering plants. The seed-bearing fruit is technically a **legume (pod)**, but the term "pulse" is restricted to dried seeds (not fresh beans or peas).

| Botanical Term | Common Term | Example |
|----------------|-------------|---------|
| Legume | The fruit (pod) itself | Green bean pod |
| Pulse | Dried mature seeds | Lentil, Chickpea |
| Vegetable | Immature fresh seeds | Green peas |

### 1.1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Seed Coat (Husk/Testa):** Outer fibrous layer, high in **dietary fiber**.
- **Cotyledons:** Two large storage tissue parts containing starch and protein.
- **Germ (Embryo):** Tiny structure that germinates into a new plant.
- **Hilum:** Scar where the seed was attached to the pod.
- **Standard Moisture Content for Safe Storage:** **≤ 12%** (wet basis).
- **Standard Protein Conversion Factor for Pulses:** **5.70** (general Kjeldahl equivalent for legumes, compared to 6.25 for general proteins).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always remember that pulses have a **low glycemic index (GI < 55)**, making them highly recommended for diabetic diets — a frequent 3-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Pulses (High-Yield)

### 2.1.1 Major Pulses (Account for >85% of global pulse production)

| Common Name | Scientific Name | India Production Rank |
|-------------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Chickpea (Bengal gram / Chana) | *Cicer arietinum* | 1st |
| Pigeon pea (Red gram / Tur / Arhar) | *Cajanus cajan* | 2nd |
| Mung bean (Green gram) | *Vigna radiata* | 3rd |
| Black gram (Urad dal) | *Vigna mungo* | — |
| Lentil (Masoor) | *Lens culinaris* | — |

### 2.1.2 Minor Pulses

Horse gram (*Macrotyloma uniflorum*), Moth bean, Cowpea, Field pea, Kidney bean, etc.

## 2.2 Nutritional Composition — The "Why" Behind Pulse Importance

Pulses are nutritionally unique because they provide a **complementary protein** when combined with cereals.

- **Protein:** 20–30% (Globulins — legumin & vicilin are the major fractions).
- **Carbohydrates:** 55–65% (mostly starch; low glycemic).
- **Fiber:** 5–25% (high insoluble fiber in seed coat).
- **Fat:** 1–2% (exception: soybean and groundnut, which are oilseeds).
- **Minerals:** Rich in **Iron (Fe), Zinc (Zn), Potassium (K), Phosphorus (P)**.
- **Vitamins:** B-complex (Thiamine, Niacin, Folic acid).

> [!NOTE]
> **Complementary Protein Principle:**
> Cereals are deficient in **Lysine** but rich in **Methionine**. Pulses are deficient in **Methionine** but rich in **Lysine**. When consumed together (e.g., **Rice + Dal**, **Roti + Chana**), they provide a complete amino acid profile — a key concept in food science.

## 2.3 Anti-Nutritional Factors (ANFs) — Engineering Concerns

ANFs are natural compounds in pulses that interfere with nutrient absorption or digestion. They are the primary reason pulses require processing.

| ANF | Chemical Nature | Effect on Humans |
|-----|------------------|------------------|
| **Phytic Acid** | Myo-inositol hexaphosphate | Chelates Zn, Fe, Ca → reduces bioavailability |
| **Trypsin Inhibitors** | Proteins (Bowman-Birk, Kunitz) | Inhibit trypsin → reduces protein digestion |
| **Tannins** | Polyphenols | Bitter taste; bind proteins |
| **Lectins** | Glycoproteins | Hemagglutination; GI toxicity |
| **Saponins** | Glycosides | Bitter taste; membrane permeabilization |
| **Oligosaccharides** (Raffinose, Stachyose) | α-Galactosides | Flatulence (gas production in colon) |
| **L-DOPA** | Non-protein amino acid | Found in *Mucuna* species |

## 2.4 Primary Processing of Pulses

The processing chain converts the harvested dry seed into **dhal** (split cotyledon) — the edible form.

### Step 1: Cleaning & Grading
Removal of foreign matter (stones, chaff, broken seeds) using **air classifiers, gravity separators, magnetic separators**, and **sieves**.

### Step 2: Drying / Conditioning (Pitting)
Moisture content is raised from **~10% to 12–14%** by water sprinkling or steam treatment. The seed coat is loosened by **pitting** (mechanical scarification). This is called **"Pitting & Oil Treatment"** — an important KTU topic.

### Step 3: Dehusking & Splitting
Removal of husk (seed coat) using:
- **Roller mills** (modern)
- **Under-runner disc sheller (URDS)** (traditional)
- **Emery roller** (industry standard)

The husk is separated by aspiration; splits (dhal) are graded by size.

### Step 4: Polishing (Optional)
Removal of the **aleurone layer** using **emery rollers + oil/water spray**. This gives the characteristic glossy appearance (e.g., polished tur dal) but **reduces fiber and micronutrient content**.

### Step 5: Grading & Packaging
Sized using **slot sieves**, sorted by color (photo-optic sorters), and packed in moisture-proof bags.

## 2.5 Secondary Processing & Value Addition

| Product | Process | Application |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Besan | Fine grinding of dehusked chana | Batter, sweets |
| Papad | Dough (dal + salt + spices) → rolled → sun-dried | Snack |
| Dal Makhani (ready-to-eat) | Wet cooking + retort sterilization | Convenience food |
| Sprouted pulses | Germination (24–48 h) | Increased Vitamin C, reduced ANFs |
| Fermented products (Idli/Dosa batter) | Natural fermentation | Improved digestibility |
| Protein concentrates/isolates | Alkaline extraction + isoelectric precipitation | Food formulation |
| Weaning foods | Extrusion cooking | Infant nutrition |

> [!TIP]
> **Germination** increases **Vitamin C** from zero to **~15 mg/100g** in 48 hours and reduces phytic acid by **30–50%** — a very high-frequency question.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Equation | Units | Notes |
|-----------|-------------------|-------|-------|
| Moisture Content (Wet Basis) | $MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | $W_w$ = wet weight, $W_d$ = dry weight |
| Moisture Content (Dry Basis) | $MC_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}$ | kg water/kg dry solid | Used in drying calculations |
| Protein Content (Kjeldahl) | $\%N = \frac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 14}{W} \times 100$ | % | Standard Kjeldahl formula |
| Protein from Nitrogen | $\% Protein = \%N \times F$ | % | **F = 5.70 for pulses** |
| Water Absorption Index (WAI) | $WAI = \frac{W_w \text{ (gel)}}{W_d \text{ (sample)}}$ | g/g | Indicator of starch hydration |
| Cooking Time Index | $CTI = \frac{T_{cooked}}{T_{control}}$ | dimensionless | Quality indicator |
| Hardness (Texture) | $H = \frac{F_{max}}{A}$ | N/mm² or kPa | From texture profile analysis |
| Bulk Density | $\rho_b = \frac{m}{V_{bulk}}$ | kg/m³ | Storage silo design |
| True Density | $\rho_t = \frac{m}{V_{particle}}$ | kg/m³ | Porosity $= 1 - (\rho_b/\rho_t)$ |
| Angle of Repose | $\tan\theta = \frac{2h}{D}$ | degrees | $h$ = heap height, $D$ = heap diameter |
| Cooking Yield | $\%CY = \frac{W_{after} - W_{before}}{W_{before}} \times 100$ | % | Indicates water uptake |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion:** To convert between wet and dry basis moisture:
> $$MC_{wb} = \frac{MC_{db}}{1 + MC_{db}} \times 100$$
> Always verify the basis used in KTU numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Protein Content of a Pulse Sample (Kjeldahl Method)

This is the **most expected numerical** in the KTU ESE paper for Module 3.

### Problem Statement
A **2.0 g** sample of dehusked chana dal is digested and distilled. The liberated ammonia is trapped in **50 mL of 0.1 N H₂SO₄**. The excess acid is back-titrated with **0.1 N NaOH**, and the burette reading is **20 mL**. A blank titration requires **50 mL of NaOH**. Calculate the protein content using **F = 5.70**.

### Given Data
- Sample weight, $W = 2.0 \text{ g}$
- Acid normality, $N = 0.1 \text{ N}$
- Volume of acid taken, $V_a = 50 \text{ mL}$
- Sample titre volume, $V_s = 20 \text{ mL}$
- Blank titre volume, $V_b = 50 \text{ mL}$
- Protein factor, $F = 5.70$

### Step 1 — Write the General Kjeldahl Formula

$$\%N = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 1.4}{W}$$

Here, the constant **14** (atomic weight of N) is combined with the factor **100 (for percentage)** and **10 (mL → L conversion)**, giving **1.4** per mL of 0.1 N acid.

### Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$\%N = \frac{(50 - 20) \times 0.1 \times 1.4}{2.0}$$

$$\%N = \frac{30 \times 0.14}{2.0}$$

$$\%N = \frac{4.2}{2.0}$$

$$\%N = 2.1\%$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Protein Content

$$\% Protein = \%N \times F$$

$$\% Protein = 2.1 \times 5.70$$

$$\% Protein = 11.97\%$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> The dehusked chana dal contains **~12% protein**, which is lower than the typical 20% for whole chana. This is because the **protein is concentrated in the seed coat and cotyledon periphery**, which is partially removed during dehusking. This is a very common observation-based question.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Moisture Content Interconversion

### Problem
A pulse sample has a moisture content of **0.20 kg water / kg dry solid** on a dry basis. Find the wet basis moisture percentage.

### Step 1 — Identify the Given

$$MC_{db} = 0.20 \text{ kg/kg}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Conversion Formula

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{MC_{db}}{1 + MC_{db}}$$

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{0.20}{1 + 0.20}$$

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{0.20}{1.20}$$

$$MC_{wb} = 0.1667 \text{ (decimal)}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Percentage

$$MC_{wb} \ (\%)\ = 0.1667 \times 100 = 16.67\%$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation

> [!WARNING]
> A moisture content of **16.67% (wb)** is **unsafe for storage**! It exceeds the safe limit of **12% wb**. This sample is prone to **fungal infestation (Aspergillus)** and **insect attack (bruchid beetles)**. The sample must be dried before storage.

## 3.3 Worked Derivation: Hardness Reduction During Soaking

### Concept
Pulse hardness decreases exponentially during soaking, following first-order kinetics:

$$H_t = H_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$$

Where:
- $H_t$ = hardness at time $t$
- $H_0$ = initial hardness
- $k$ = rate constant (h⁻¹)
- $t$ = soaking time (h)

### Example
If initial hardness is **100 N** and after **5 hours** of soaking it reduces to **60 N**, calculate the rate constant $k$.

$$\frac{H_t}{H_0} = e^{-kt}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{60}{100}\right) = -k \cdot 5$$

$$-0.5108 = -5k$$

$$k = 0.1022 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

### Time for 90% Softening

$$H_t = 0.10 \times H_0$$

$$\ln(0.10) = -kt$$

$$-2.3026 = -0.1022 \times t$$

$$t = 22.53 \text{ hours}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Processing on Nutritional Quality

| Processing Method | Protein | Fiber | Vitamins | ANFs (Phytic Acid) | Bioavailability |
|-------------------|---------|-------|----------|--------------------|-----------------|
| Soaking (12 h) | Slight ↓ | No change | B-vitamins leach | ↓ 15–25% | ↑ |
| Germination (48 h) | ↑ Free amino acids | No change | ↑ Vitamin C | ↓ 30–50% | ↑↑ |
| Fermentation | ↑↑ Bioactive peptides | ↓ | ↑ B-vitamins | ↓ 40–60% | ↑↑ |
| Roasting | No change | ↓ | ↓ Heat-labile | ↓ 20–30% | ↑ |
| Extrusion Cooking | Slight ↓ | ↓↓ (soluble ↑) | ↓ Variable | ↓ 40–60% | ↑↑↑ |
| Pressure Cooking | Slight ↓ | No change | ↓ Heat-labile | ↓ 50–70% | ↑↑↑ |

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Insight:** Among household cooking methods, **pressure cooking** is the most effective at destroying **trypsin inhibitors** and reducing **flatulence-causing oligosaccharides**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pulse Grain Anatomy (Block-Level Schematic)

flowchart TD
